topics.title: Classification of Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy (KTU 2024 Definition):** Energy is defined as the fundamental physical quantity representing the **capacity of a system to perform work or to transfer heat**. In the context of energy engineering, it is the primary resource that powers all natural and human-made processes, quantified in standard units of **Joules (J)**, **kilo-Watt-hours (kWh)**, **Mega-Watt-hours (MWh)**, or **Tera-Watt-hours (TWh)**.

The SI unit of energy is the Joule (J), where:
$$ 1 \text{ J} = 1 \text{ N·m} = 1 \text{ W·s} $$

For practical large-scale measurement:
$$ 1 \text{ TWh} = 10^{12} \text{ Wh} = 3.6 \times 10^{15} \text{ J} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to understand the **classification of energy sources**, the global and Indian **energy scenario**, the **energy chain**, and the various **renewable energy alternatives** that exist as substitutes for conventional fossil fuels.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy: Energy as "Money in a Bank"

Think of energy the same way you think of **money**:

| Money Analogy | Energy Equivalent |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Cash in your wallet (ready to spend) | **Primary Energy** (coal, oil, sunlight in raw form) |
| Money deposited in a bank (usable later) | **Secondary Energy** (electricity, hydrogen) |
| Earned income (renewable) | **Renewable Energy** (solar, wind, hydro) |
| Limited savings (finite) | **Non-Renewable Energy** (fossil fuels, uranium) |
| Local currency (used in villages) | **Non-Commercial Energy** (firewood, dung cakes) |
| International currency (market-traded) | **Commercial Energy** (coal, electricity, LPG) |

> [!TIP]
> Just as you would not spend all your savings at once, humanity cannot indefinitely consume non-renewable energy without securing sustainable alternatives. This is the foundational logic of the **Non-Conventional Energy Systems** course.

## 1.3 Why is Classification Important?

Energy sources are classified to:

1. **Plan generation portfolios** — A country needs a balanced mix for energy security.
2. **Estimate reserves and depletion rates** — Critical for non-renewable sources.
3. **Identify sustainable alternatives** — Drives policy and research into renewables.
4. **Quantify environmental impact** — CO₂ footprint varies drastically by source.
5. **Design conversion systems** — Engineers design systems based on the type of primary input.

## 1.4 Primary vs Secondary Energy (Foundational Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Energy** is the energy found in nature **before any human-conversion process** (e.g., crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium ore, firewood).
>
> **Secondary Energy** is the energy obtained **after conversion from a primary form** (e.g., electricity from coal, gasoline from crude oil, hydrogen from water electrolysis).

**Conversion Losses:** During primary → secondary conversion, a significant portion is lost. For example:
- A typical coal-fired thermal power plant operates at **30%–40% efficiency**.
- A combined-cycle gas plant reaches **55%–60% efficiency**.
- This means **40%–70%** of primary energy is rejected as waste heat.

![Energy Classification Visual Map - See SECTION_4 for Mermaid block]

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Energy Sources

Energy sources can be classified along **multiple axes** as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme. Below is the structured taxonomy:

### A. Classification by **Origin / Nature of Source**

1. **Fossil Fuels** (Chemical origin, finite, conventional)
   - Coal, Lignite, Peat
   - Crude Oil (Petroleum)
   - Natural Gas
2. **Nuclear Fuels** (Nuclear binding energy, finite, conventional)
   - Uranium-235, Plutonium-239, Thorium-232
3. **Renewable / Non-Conventional Sources** (Continuously replenished)
   - Solar Energy (Direct + Indirect)
   - Wind Energy
   - Biomass & Biofuels
   - Geothermal Energy
   - Hydropower (Tidal, Wave, Ocean Thermal)
   - Hydrogen (as an energy carrier)

### B. Classification by **Replenishment Rate**

| Criterion | Renewable Energy | Non-Renewable Energy |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Replenishment | Continuously replenished by nature | Finite stock, takes millions of years to form |
| Examples | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium |
| Carbon footprint | Low / Zero | High (CO₂ released on combustion) |
| Stock vs Flow | **Flow** resource (rate-based) | **Stock** resource (quantity-based) |
| Sustainability | Sustainable in long term | Unsustainable beyond reserves |
| KTU Exam Tag | **Non-Conventional** | **Conventional** |

### C. Classification by **Commercial Use**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Energy:** Sources that are **bought and sold in the organized market** at a measurable price (e.g., coal, electricity, LPG, natural gas, kerosene).
>
> **Non-Commercial Energy:** Sources that are **not traded in the market** — typically collected from nature or agricultural residue for **subsistence use** (e.g., firewood, cow-dung cakes, agricultural waste, straw, charcoal).

In **developing countries like India**, non-commercial energy still accounts for a substantial portion of rural energy consumption (historically ~25–30% of total).

### D. Classification by **End-Use Form**

1. **Heat Energy** — Combustion of fuels, geothermal
2. **Mechanical Energy** — Wind, hydropower
3. **Electrical Energy** — Generated from all above
4. **Radiant Energy** — Solar radiation
5. **Chemical Energy** — Batteries, fuel cells, hydrogen

### E. Classification by **Environmental Impact**

- **Clean / Green Energy** — Solar PV, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Geothermal
- **Polluting Energy** — Coal, Oil, Natural Gas (CO₂, SOₓ, NOₓ emissions)

## 2.2 The Energy Chain (KTU 2024 High-Priority Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Energy Chain** traces the journey of energy from its natural source to its end-use application. This is a frequently-asked KTU exam topic.

$$\boxed{\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Conversion} \rightarrow \text{Transmission/Distribution} \rightarrow \text{End Use}}$$

**Stage-by-stage description:**

1. **Source/Reservoir** — The natural form in which energy exists (sun, coal mine, oil well, uranium ore).
2. **Extraction/Collection** — Mining coal, drilling oil, harvesting solar radiation.
3. **Conversion** — Burning coal → heat → steam → turbine → electricity; or PV cell directly converting sunlight to electricity.
4. **Transmission & Distribution** — High-voltage grid lines carry electricity to consumers.
5. **End-Use Application** — Lighting, heating, motor-driven mechanical work, electronics.

> At every stage, there is a **loss** (thermal, mechanical, electrical). The total efficiency of the chain is the **product of all stage efficiencies**:
$$ \eta_{\text{chain}} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \dots \times \eta_n $$

## 2.3 The Solar Constant and Terrestrial Energy Availability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Solar Constant (G_sc)** is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere, on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the mean Earth–Sun distance.
> $$ G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.361 \text{ kW/m}^2 $$

After atmospheric attenuation (due to scattering, absorption by ozone, water vapor, and dust), the maximum value at the Earth's surface on a clear day is approximately:
$$ G_{on} \approx 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 = 1 \text{ kW/m}^2 $$

This is the **standard test condition (STC)** used universally for rating solar PV panels.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|----------|----------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** | $PCEC = \dfrac{E_{\text{total annual}}}{P_{\text{population}}}$ | kWh/person/year |
| 2 | **Energy Density (by mass)** | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | MJ/kg |
| 3 | **Energy Density (by volume)** | $\rho_V = \dfrac{E}{V}$ | MJ/L |
| 4 | **Solar Constant** | $G_{sc}$ at outer atmosphere | **1367 W/m²** |
| 5 | **Terrestrial Peak Solar Irradiance** | After atmospheric loss | **~1000 W/m²** (1 kW/m²) |
| 6 | **Annual Solar Energy on Earth** | $E_{\odot} = G_{sc} \times A_{\text{cross}} \times t$ | $\sim 1.74 \times 10^{17}$ Wh/day |
| 7 | **Power Output of a Wind Turbine** | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | **Hydropower Potential** | $P = \rho g Q H$ | Watts (W) |
| 9 | **Calorific Value (CV)** | Heat released per unit mass/volume | kJ/kg or kJ/m³ |
| 10 | **Chain Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{chain}} = \prod \eta_i$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!TIP]
> In tables above, $\rho$ = air/water density, $A$ = swept area, $v$ = wind speed, $C_p$ = power coefficient (Betz limit = 0.593), $Q$ = discharge, $H$ = head, $g$ = 9.81 m/s².

## 2.5 Energy Density Comparison (Critical for KTU Problems)

| Fuel / Source | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | Form |
|--------------|------------------------|------|
| Uranium-235 (fission) | **80,000,000** | Nuclear |
| Hydrogen (LHV) | 120 | Chemical |
| Natural Gas | 55 | Chemical |
| Diesel / Petrol | 45 | Chemical |
| Coal (Bituminous) | 24–30 | Chemical |
| Wood (dry) | 16 | Chemical |
| Lead-acid Battery | 0.17 | Electrochemical |
| Lithium-ion Battery | 0.5–0.8 | Electrochemical |

> This table is fundamental to understanding **why fossil fuels dominate** despite pollution: they pack enormous energy per unit mass, making them easy to transport and use.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Planners** use classification to balance baseload (coal, nuclear) with peak load (gas, hydro, solar).
- **Policy Makers** use PCEC to compare development indices across countries.
- **Engineers** use the Betz limit ($C_p = 0.593$) to design wind turbine blades.
- **Solar Designers** use the solar constant to size PV arrays.
- **Energy Auditors** use chain efficiency to identify and minimize losses at every stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Identifying the Type of Energy Source

The following structured decision matrix is the most reliable way to **classify any given energy source** in KTU exam questions.

| Step | Question to Ask | If YES | If NO |
|------|----------------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Is it traded in the open market at a price? | Commercial | Non-Commercial |
| 2 | Is it replenished by nature within a human lifetime? | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
| 3 | Does it release CO₂ on combustion? | Polluting (carbon-emitting) | Clean / Green |
| 4 | Has it been used for >100 years in industry? | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
| 5 | Is it found directly in nature without conversion? | Primary | Secondary |

**Worked Example 1:** LPG cylinder at your home
- Commercial? **YES** (bought in market)
- Renewable? **NO** (refined from crude oil)
- Polluting? **YES** (releases CO₂)
- Conventional? **YES** (used for decades)
- Primary? **NO** (refined from natural gas) → **Secondary**

**Worked Example 2:** Solar PV electricity
- Commercial? **YES** (sold by DISCOMs in India)
- Renewable? **YES**
- Polluting? **NO**
- Conventional? **NO** → **Non-Conventional**
- Primary? **NO** (sunlight is primary, electricity is secondary) → **Secondary**

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Solar Power Intercepted by Earth

The Earth intercepts a fraction of the Sun's total radiated power. The derivation is a frequent KTU Module 1 question.

**Step 1:** Total power radiated by the Sun (using Stefan-Boltzmann law):
$$ L_{\odot} = 4 \pi R_{\odot}^2 \sigma T_{\odot}^4 $$

**Step 2:** At the mean orbital distance $d$ from the Sun, this power is spread uniformly over a sphere of surface area $4\pi d^2$.

**Step 3:** The Earth presents a **circular cross-section** (not a full sphere) of area $\pi R_E^2$ to intercept the Sun's rays.

**Step 4:** The fraction of the Sun's total power intercepted by Earth is therefore:
$$ f = \frac{\pi R_E^2}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{1}{4} \left(\frac{R_E}{d}\right)^2 $$

**Step 5:** The total solar power intercepted by Earth:
$$ P_{\text{intercepted}} = f \cdot L_{\odot} = \frac{L_{\odot}}{4} \left(\frac{R_E}{d}\right)^2 $$

**Step 6:** Substituting standard values ($L_{\odot} = 3.846 \times 10^{26}$ W, $R_E = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m, $d = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m):
$$ P_{\text{intercepted}} \approx 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W} = 174 \text{ PW} $$

**Step 7:** Converting to daily energy:
$$ E_{\text{daily}} = 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W} \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 1.5 \times 10^{22} \text{ J} = 4.2 \times 10^{15} \text{ kWh} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This energy received in **one day** is more than the **total annual energy consumption of the entire human civilization** (~25,000 TWh/year ≈ $9 \times 10^{13}$ kWh). This is the mathematical proof that solar energy can, in principle, power all of humanity — the challenge is conversion and storage.

## 3.3 Derivation: Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)

The PCEC is a standard development indicator used by the World Bank, IEA, and Ministry of Power, India.

**Step 1:** Define total annual energy consumption of a country.
$$ E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i} E_i \quad \text{(sum of all energy forms)} $$

**Step 2:** Define total population.
$$ P = \text{Total population} $$

**Step 3:** Compute PCEC:
$$ PCEC = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{P} \quad \text{[kWh/person/year]} $$

**Worked Example 3:** India's data (approximate, 2023):
- Total Primary Energy Consumption: ~35,000 TWh/year
- Population: ~1.4 billion = $1.4 \times 10^9$
$$ PCEC = \frac{35{,}000 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 25{,}000 \text{ kWh/person/year} $$

**Worked Example 4:** USA:
- Total Primary Energy Consumption: ~95,000 TWh/year
- Population: ~330 million = $3.3 \times 10^8$
$$ PCEC = \frac{95{,}000 \times 10^9}{3.3 \times 10^8} \approx 287{,}879 \text{ kWh/person/year} $$

> This simple calculation shows why developing nations aim to increase PCEC to match developed nations — it is directly linked to quality of life, industrial capacity, and economic growth.

## 3.4 Derivation: Wind Turbine Power

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a Module-2 preview but is included here because it uses the **classification framework** to derive the power available in a flow resource.

The Betz analysis of wind power:

**Step 1:** Kinetic energy of a mass of air $m$ moving at velocity $v$:
$$ KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 $$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate of air through the turbine's swept area $A$:
$$ \dot{m} = \rho A v \quad \text{[kg/s]} $$

**Step 3:** Total available wind power:
$$ P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 $$

**Step 4:** Real turbine captures only a fraction $C_p$ (Betz limit = 0.593):
$$ P_{\text{turbine}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p \quad \text{with} \quad C_p \leq 0.593 $$

**Step 5:** Numerical example: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $A = 50$ m², $v = 12$ m/s, $C_p = 0.4$
$$ P = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 50 \times (12)^3 \times 0.4 $$
$$ P = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 50 \times 1728 \times 0.4 $$
$$ P = 21{,}168 \text{ W} \approx 21.2 \text{ kW} $$

> Notice the **cubic dependence on wind speed** — doubling wind speed increases power **8-fold**. This is why wind farm siting depends critically on accurate wind speed data.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Energy Content of Coal (Classification Use)

A thermal power plant burns **10,000 tonnes of coal per day**. The calorific value of the coal is **25 MJ/kg**. Calculate:
(a) Total chemical energy input per day
(b) Electrical energy output if the plant efficiency is 35%
(c) The number of 100-W bulbs that can be lit continuously from this plant.

**Solution:**

(a) Mass per day:
$$ m = 10{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 10^7 \text{ kg} $$
$$ E_{\text{input}} = m \times CV = 10^7 \times 25 \times 10^6 = 2.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ J/day} $$

(b) Electrical output:
$$ E_{\text{elec}} = 0.35 \times 2.5 \times 10^{14} = 8.75 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/day} $$

Converting to kWh:
$$ E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{8.75 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 2.43 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh/day} = 24{,}306 \text{ MWh/day} $$

(c) Number of 100-W bulbs for 24 hours:
$$ \text{Total power} = \frac{24{,}306 \text{ MWh}}{24 \text{ h}} = 1012.75 \text{ MW} = 1.01275 \times 10^9 \text{ W} $$
$$ N = \frac{1.01275 \times 10^9}{100} = 10.13 \text{ million bulbs} $$

> This illustrates the staggering energy density of fossil fuels — but also the CO₂ emitted per day, which justifies the shift to renewables.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree of Energy Sources

The following Mermaid block represents the **complete classification hierarchy** required for KTU Module 1. It is rendered as a top-down tree for maximum readability.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENERGY SOURCES] --> B[BY REPLENISHMENT]
    A --> C[BY COMMERCIAL USE]
    A --> D[BY CONVERSION STAGE]
    A --> E[BY ORIGIN]

    B --> B1[RENEWABLE Non-Conventional]
    B --> B2[NON-RENEWABLE Conventional]

    B1 --> R1[Solar Energy]
    B1 --> R2[Wind Energy]
    B1 --> R3[Hydropower]
    B1 --> R4[Biomass and Biofuels]
    B1 --> R5[Geothermal Energy]
    B1 --> R6[Tidal and Wave Energy]
    B1 --> R7[Hydrogen Energy]

    B2 --> N1[Fossil Fuels]
    B2 --> N2[Nuclear Fuels]

    N1 --> F1[Coal]
    N1 --> F2[Crude Oil]
    N1 --> F3[Natural Gas]

    N2 --> U1[Uranium 235]
    N2 --> U2[Plutonium 239]
    N2 --> U3[Thorium 232]

    C --> C1[COMMERCIAL Traded in Market]
    C --> C2[NON-COMMERCIAL Subsistence Use]

    C1 --> CM1[Electricity]
    C1 --> CM2[LPG and Natural Gas]
    C1 --> CM3[Kerosene and Diesel]
    C1 --> CM4[Coal]

    C2 --> NC1[Firewood]
    C2 --> NC2[Cow Dung Cakes]
    C2 --> NC3[Agricultural Residue]
    C2 --> NC4[Straw and Charcoal]

    D --> D1[PRIMARY Energy]
    D --> D2[SECONDARY Energy]

    D1 --> P1[Crude Oil]
    D1 --> P2[Sunlight]
    D1 --> P3[Wind]
    D1 --> P4[Coal in situ]
    D1 --> P5[Uranium Ore]

    D2 --> S1[Electricity]
    D2 --> S2[Refined Petrol and Diesel]
    D2 --> S3[Hydrogen Fuel]
    D2 --> S4[Steam]

    E --> E1[CHEMICAL ORIGIN Fossil]
    E --> E2[NUCLEAR ORIGIN]
    E --> E3[THERMAL ORIGIN Solar Geo]
    E --> E4[MECHANICAL ORIGIN Wind Hydro Tidal]
    E --> E5[ELECTROMAGNETIC ORIGIN Solar Radiation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Energy Chain (Source to End-Use)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[PRIMARY SOURCE Sun Coal Wind Uranium Biomass] --> node2[EXTRACTION AND COLLECTION Mining Drilling Harvesting]
    node2 --> node3[CONVERSION STAGE Thermal Mechanical Photoelectric]
    node3 --> node4[TRANSMISSION AND DISTRIBUTION Grid Lines Pipelines]
    node4 --> node5[END USE APPLICATION Lighting Heating Motoring Electronics]
    node5 -.Loss Heat.-> node6[WASTE HEAT AND LOSSES]
    node5 -.CO2 Emissions.-> node7[ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT]

    style node1 fill:#FFD700
    style node2 fill:#FFA500
    style node3 fill:#FF6347
    style node4 fill:#4682B4
    style node5 fill:#32CD32
    style node6 fill:#808080
    style node7 fill:#A52A2A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows represent the **forward flow of energy**. The dotted lines from End Use to Losses and Environmental Impact represent **inefficiencies and side effects** that engineers seek to minimize. The 5-stage block is the most commonly tested energy chain in KTU exams.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Classify energy sources based on (a) replenishment rate and (b) commercial use. Give two examples for each category.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Based on Replenishment Rate:**

> **Renewable Energy Sources:** These are energy sources that are **continuously replenished by natural processes** within a human timescale and do not get exhausted with use.
> - **Examples:** Solar energy, Wind energy, Hydropower, Biomass.
>
> **Non-Renewable Energy Sources:** These exist in **finite reserves** in the Earth's crust and take **millions of years to form**, hence they get depleted with use.
> - **Examples:** Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium.

**(b) Based on Commercial Use:**

> **Commercial Energy:** Sources that are **bought and sold in the organized market** at a measurable price, used in industries, transport, and urban households.
> - **Examples:** Electricity, LPG, Diesel, Kerosene, Coal.
>
> **Non-Commercial Energy:** Sources that are **not traded in the market**; they are collected by individuals (mostly in rural areas) for domestic consumption.
> - **Examples:** Firewood, Cow-dung cakes, Agricultural residues, Straw.

*(Examiner's Note: 3-mark answers require 2 sub-categories with 2 examples each. Drawing a small tree or list is recommended.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, KTU Model QP]`

**Define the term "energy chain" and list its stages with a one-line description of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **energy chain** is the **sequence of stages through which energy flows** from its natural primary source to its final end-use application.

The five stages of the energy chain are:

1. **Source / Reservoir** — Natural occurrence of energy (sun, coal seam, oil well, wind stream, uranium ore).
2. **Extraction / Collection** — Mining, drilling, pumping, or harvesting the primary resource.
3. **Conversion** — Transforming primary energy into a useful secondary form (e.g., combustion → heat → mechanical → electrical).
4. **Transmission / Distribution** — Carrying the converted energy to consumers via grids, pipelines, or transport.
5. **End Use** — Final application in lighting, heating, transportation, industrial processes, or electronics.

> At every stage, there are **conversion and transmission losses**, so the overall chain efficiency is the **product of stage efficiencies**, often yielding only 30–40% overall.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, Module 1 of OECHT837 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Below are TWO complete alternatives.

---

### PART B - OPTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

#### (a) Classify the various energy sources with a neat diagram and explain renewable and non-renewable sources in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for Classification (1 mark):**
Energy sources are classified to understand their availability, environmental impact, replenishment rate, and commercial viability. This helps in energy planning, policy-making, and selecting appropriate conversion technologies.

**2. Classification Diagram (2 marks):**
*(See the Mermaid classification tree in SECTION 4.1 for the complete diagram. Students should redraw as a labeled flow chart in the exam.)*

**3. Renewable Energy Sources (2 marks):**
- Sources that are **continuously replenished** by natural processes.
- **Solar Energy** — From the sun via PV or thermal systems.
- **Wind Energy** — Kinetic energy of moving air, captured by turbines.
- **Hydropower** — Potential/kinetic energy of water at elevation.
- **Biomass** — Organic material (wood, agricultural waste) used as fuel.
- **Geothermal** — Heat from Earth's interior.
- **Tidal/Wave** — Gravitational and oceanic kinetic energy.
- **Hydrogen** — Carrier, not a primary source, but renewable when made from water electrolysis using renewables.

**4. Non-Renewable Energy Sources (2 marks):**
- Sources with **finite reserves**, formed over millions of years.
- **Fossil Fuels** — Coal, Oil, Natural Gas. Formed from ancient biomass under heat and pressure. Highest commercial use.
- **Nuclear Fuels** — Uranium, Thorium, Plutonium. Release energy via fission.

---

#### (b) Discuss the global and Indian energy scenario with reference to renewable energy potential. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Global Energy Scenario (2 marks):**
- World primary energy consumption: ~606 EJ/year (≈168,500 TWh) as of 2023.
- **Fossil fuels** (coal + oil + gas) account for ~**80%** of global primary energy.
- **Renewables** (excluding hydro) contribute ~**7–8%**, hydropower ~**2.5%**, and nuclear ~**4%**.
- **Per capita consumption** is highly skewed: USA ~290,000 kWh/year vs. India ~25,000 kWh/year vs. Sub-Saharan Africa ~5,000 kWh/year.
- Major concerns: **Climate change, CO₂ emissions, energy security**.

**2. Indian Energy Scenario (3 marks):**
- **Installed electricity capacity (2024):** ~430 GW.
  - Thermal (coal + gas): ~205 GW (47%)
  - Renewables (solar + wind + biomass + small hydro): ~190 GW (44%)
  - Hydro (large): ~47 GW (11%)
  - Nuclear: ~8 GW (2%)
- **Per capita consumption:** ~1,200 kWh/year (electrical) — among the lowest globally.
- **Total primary energy:** ~35,000 TWh/year; 55% from coal.
- **Renewable targets:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 (Panchamrit pledges).
- **Solar potential:** ~750 GW (assuming 3% wasteland); current installed ~85 GW.
- **Wind potential:** ~302 GW onshore, 174 GW offshore.

**3. Renewable Energy Potential in India (2 marks):**

| Source | Estimated Potential (MW) |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Solar | 750,000 |
| Wind (onshore) | 302,000 |
| Small Hydro | 21,000 |
| Biomass | 25,000 |
| Tidal | 9,000 |
| Geothermal | 10,000 |
| **Total** | **~1,117,000 MW ≈ 1.1 TW** |

**Conclusion:** India has the technical potential to meet its entire projected 2030 electricity demand (estimated 350 GW peak) entirely from renewable sources, but faces challenges of **storage, grid integration, land acquisition, and financing**.

---

### PART B - OPTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

#### (a) Explain the energy chain in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the various losses occurring at each stage. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition of Energy Chain (1 mark):**
The energy chain represents the **complete flow path of energy** from the primary source through conversion and delivery to the final consumer. It is a system-level view that helps identify where losses occur and where efficiency improvements can be made.

**2. Block Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.2. Students must redraw the 5-stage linear chain: Source → Extraction → Conversion → Transmission → End Use.)*

**3. Stage-by-Stage Description and Losses (4 marks):**

| Stage | Activity | Typical Efficiency | Major Loss Type |
|-------|----------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1. Source | Natural availability (sun, coal) | — | — |
| 2. Extraction | Mining, drilling, harvesting | 85–95% | Spillage, leakage, unrecoverable reserves |
| 3. Conversion | Heat → Mechanical → Electrical | 30–60% | Heat rejected to cooling water/air, friction |
| 4. Transmission | High-voltage grid, pipelines | 90–95% | $I^2R$ losses, transformer losses |
| 5. End Use | Light, heat, motion | 50–90% | Inefficient appliances, standby losses |

**4. Overall Chain Efficiency:**
$$ \eta_{\text{chain}} = 0.9 \times 0.4 \times 0.93 \times 0.7 \approx 0.234 \approx 23.4\% $$

> This means out of every 100 units of primary energy, only **~23 units reach the end user as useful work** — the rest is lost as waste heat, line losses, and inefficient conversion. Renewable systems like solar PV (bypass the heat → mechanical chain) achieve higher end-to-end efficiencies.

---

#### (b) A thermal power station burns 5,000 tonnes of coal per day. The calorific value of the coal is 20 MJ/kg. The plant operates at an overall efficiency of 30%. (i) Calculate the daily electrical energy output in MWh. (ii) Find the per capita energy consumption if this plant serves a city of 5 lakh population. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Mass of coal burned per day: $m = 5{,}000$ tonnes $= 5 \times 10^6$ kg
- Calorific value: $CV = 20$ MJ/kg
- Plant efficiency: $\eta = 30\% = 0.30$
- Population served: $P = 5 \text{ lakh} = 5 \times 10^5$

**Step 1: Daily Chemical Energy Input (1 mark)**
$$ E_{\text{input}} = m \times CV = 5 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 1.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ J/day} $$

**Step 2: Daily Electrical Energy Output (2 marks)**
$$ E_{\text{elec}} = \eta \times E_{\text{input}} = 0.30 \times 1.0 \times 10^{14} = 3.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/day} $$

**Step 3: Convert to MWh (1 mark)**
$$ E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}}{3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ J/MWh}} = 8{,}333.33 \text{ MWh/day} $$

**Step 4: Per Capita Energy Consumption (2 marks)**
$$ PCEC = \frac{E_{\text{elec}}}{P} = \frac{8{,}333.33 \text{ MWh}}{5 \times 10^5} = 0.01667 \text{ MWh/person/day} $$

Converting to kWh/person/year:
$$ 0.01667 \times 1000 \text{ kWh} \times 365 \text{ days} = 6{,}083.33 \text{ kWh/person/year} $$

**Final Answers:**
- Daily electrical energy output: **8,333.33 MWh/day**
- Per capita energy consumption: **~6,083 kWh/person/year**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert tonnes → kg (×1000) and J → MWh (÷3.6×10⁹) is the #1 mistake. Always show both conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Calorific value units:** Coal CV is usually in kJ/kg or MJ/kg — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Per capita definition trap:** Some questions ask per *electrical* energy served, some ask per *primary* energy — confirm before dividing.
> 4. **Numerical answer without units:** A numerical answer without "MWh/day" or "kWh/person/year" will lose 1 mark in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Not stating formulas:** Always write the formula first, then substitute values — KTU examiners award step-marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and use it as a last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- **Energy:** Capacity to do work; SI unit is Joule.
- **Primary Energy:** Found in nature (coal, sunlight, wind, uranium).
- **Secondary Energy:** Converted from primary (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).
- **Commercial Energy:** Traded in market (LPG, electricity, coal).
- **Non-Commercial Energy:** Not traded (firewood, dung cakes).
- **Renewable Energy:** Replenished within human lifetime.
- **Non-Renewable Energy:** Finite stock, takes millions of years to form.
- **Energy Chain:** Source → Extraction → Conversion → Transmission → End Use.
- **Solar Constant (G_sc):** 1367 W/m² at the outer atmosphere.
- **Terrestrial peak irradiance:** 1000 W/m² = 1 kW/m² (STC).
- **Betz Limit:** Maximum $C_p$ for wind turbine = 0.593 (59.3%).

### ✅ Must-Know Numerical Formulas
- **PCEC** = Total Energy ÷ Population [kWh/person/year]
- **Wind Power** = $0.5 \times \rho \times A \times v^3 \times C_p$
- **Hydro Power** = $\rho \times g \times Q \times H$
- **Energy output** = $\eta \times \text{Fuel mass} \times CV$
- **Chain efficiency** = $\prod \eta_i$ (product of stage efficiencies)

### ✅ Must-Know Numerical Values
- Solar constant = **1367 W/m²** ≈ 1.361 kW/m²
- Earth's intercepted solar power ≈ **1.74 × 10¹⁷ W = 174 PW**
- India's per capita energy consumption ≈ **25,000 kWh/person/year** (primary)
- India's per capita electricity consumption ≈ **1,200 kWh/person/year**
- India's total installed capacity (2024) ≈ **430 GW**
- India renewable target: **500 GW by 2030**
- 1 TWh = **10⁹ kWh** = **3.6 × 10¹⁵ J**

### ✅ Must-Know Classification Facts
- **Renewable examples:** Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, Hydrogen.
- **Non-Renewable examples:** Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium, Thorium.
- **Conventional = Non-Renewable** (fossil + nuclear)
- **Non-Conventional = Renewable** (most are, excluding large hydro sometimes)
- **Flow resources** = renewable; **Stock resources** = non-renewable
- **Secondary examples:** Electricity, refined petroleum, hydrogen, steam

### ✅ Must-Know Indian Energy Data
- Solar potential: **750 GW** (3% wasteland assumption)
- Wind onshore potential: **302 GW**
- Coal share in primary energy: **~55%**
- Renewables share in installed capacity: **~44%** (2024)
- Major renewable policy: **National Solar Mission**, **PM-KUSUM**, **Panchamrit pledges**

### ✅ KTU Exam-Writing Tips
1. Always draw a **labeled diagram** for classification questions.
2. For 14-mark answers, spend **~25 minutes** — about 1.5 minutes per mark.
3. Use **bullet points and tables** to structure long answers.
4. Always **state the formula** before substitution.
5. Mention **units at every step** — marks are deducted otherwise.
6. For definitions, write **2–3 lines minimum** with at least one example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and Importance of Non-Conventional Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Importance of Non-Conventional Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Conventional Energy** refers to energy sources that are **renewable**, **inexhaustible** on a human time scale, **environmentally benign** (low-to-zero greenhouse gas emissions), and **continuously replenished** by natural processes. These sources are also termed **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** or **Alternative Energy Sources**, and they stand in direct contrast to **conventional (fossil-based) energy**, which is finite and polluting.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECHT837 – Module 1)**, the umbrella term "non-conventional energy" officially encompasses:
- Solar Energy (Photovoltaic + Thermal)
- Wind Energy
- Biomass and Biofuels
- Geothermal Energy
- Tidal and Wave Energy (Ocean energy)
- Hydrogen Energy (Green Hydrogen)
- Fuel Cells
- Small/Micro/Mini Hydro Power

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Household Money Analogy**
> Imagine your monthly household expenses. If your *only* source of income is a **fixed monthly salary** that can be exhausted (like a coal mine that finishes), you live in constant anxiety about the future. But if you *also* earn money from **renting a spare room** (solar panels on the roof), **growing vegetables in your garden** (biomass), and **collecting rainwater** (hydropower), then your energy budget becomes resilient. *Non-conventional energy sources are exactly these "side incomes" of the planet* — they replenish naturally, cost nothing to "refill," and keep the ecological house running even after the finite resources (coal, oil, gas) run out.

### 1.3 Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Energy — The Defining Contrast

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Conventional Energy** = Stock-type resources (finite reserve, mined/ extracted) — Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (fission).
> - **Non-Conventional Energy** = Flow-type resources (continuously supplied) — Solar, Wind, Hydrokinetic, Geothermal.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the Energy Sector

The following internationally accepted units and metrics will be used throughout this course:

- **1 TWh (Terawatt-hour) = $10^{9}$ kWh = $10^{12}$ Wh**
- **1 GW (Gigawatt) = $10^9$ W = $10^6$ kW**
- **TOE (Tonnes of Oil Equivalent):** 1 TOE ≈ 11.63 MWh (standard energy conversion)
- **CO₂ Emission Factor:** Coal ≈ **0.95 kg CO₂/kWh**, Natural Gas ≈ **0.49 kg CO₂/kWh**, Solar PV ≈ **0.04 kg CO₂/kWh** (life-cycle average).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is Non-Conventional Energy "Important"? — Structured Logic

The importance of non-conventional energy rests on **five pillars**, each of which has been a **repeated KTU examination theme** (often asked as 7-mark descriptive questions):

#### Pillar 1 — Depletion of Fossil Reserves
- Proven global **coal reserves** may last only **~150 years** at current extraction rates.
- **Petroleum and natural gas** reserves are projected to be **exhausted within 50–60 years**.
- **India** imports **> 85% of its crude oil** and **~50% of its natural gas** — a massive **foreign-exchange drain** (energy security issue).

#### Pillar 2 — Environmental Degradation
- Combustion of fossil fuels releases **CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter (PM 2.5)**.
- Global atmospheric **CO₂ concentration** has crossed **420 ppm** (vs. pre-industrial baseline of 280 ppm).
- **Paris Agreement (2015):** Limit global warming to **1.5 °C above pre-industrial levels**.

#### Pillar 3 — Energy Security & Import Independence
- Decentralized renewable generation reduces a nation's **geopolitical vulnerability**.
- For India, every **1 GW** of renewable capacity displaces roughly **1.5–2 million tonnes of coal imports annually**.

#### Pillar 4 — Sustainable Development & Decentralization
- Solar, wind, and biomass enable **distributed generation** (even in remote off-grid villages).
- Supports **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)** of the UN 2030 Agenda.

#### Pillar 5 — Economic & Employment Multiplier
- The renewable energy sector creates **~7–10 jobs per MW** installed (vs. ~2 jobs/MW in coal-based plants).
- **LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy)** of solar PV has fallen from **₹17.9/kWh (2010)** to **₹2.5/kWh (2024)** — making it the cheapest new electricity source in India.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $E_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | kWh/person/year |
| 2 | Power of a Resource | $P = \dfrac{E}{t}$ | W, kW, MW |
| 3 | Energy from Fuel | $E = m \times CV$ | $m$ = mass (kg), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg), $E$ in kJ |
| 4 | Primary Energy Demand Projection | $E_{n} = E_{0}(1+r)^{n}$ | $r$ = annual growth rate (fraction) |
| 5 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Dimensionless, $\leq 1$ |
| 6 | $\text{CO}_{2}$ Avoided Annually | $\Delta CO_{2} = E_{gen} \times EF_{fossil} \times \dfrac{1}{1000}$ | tonnes/year |
| 7 | Energy Payback Time (EPBT) | $EPBT = \dfrac{E_{lifecycle}}{E_{annual\_gen}}$ | Years |
| 8 | LCOE (simplified) | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t} \dfrac{C_{t}}{(1+d)^{t}}}{\sum_{t} \dfrac{E_{t}}{(1+d)^{t}}}$ | ₹/kWh or \$/kWh |
| 9 | Cumulative Installed Capacity Growth | $C_{n} = C_{0}(1+g)^{n}$ | $g$ = CAGR (fraction) |
| 10 | Solar Constant (top of atmosphere) | $G_{sc} = 1367\ \text{W/m}^{2}$ | Used in all solar derivations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** For Module 1 problems, the four most-tested formulas are #1 (Per Capita Energy), #4 (Energy Demand Projection), #5 (Capacity Factor), and #6 (CO₂ Avoided). Memorize these first.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above metrics are not just academic — they form the **decision-making backbone** of every renewable-energy project report submitted to **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, Government of India)** and used by **NTPC Vidyut Vyapar Nigam (NVVN)** for tender evaluations. A solar project proposal must justify its installation using **LCOE**, **CF**, and **EPBT** calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption and Its Significance

**Given:**
- Total annual electricity consumption of a country: $E_{total}$ (in kWh)
- Total population: $P$ (number of persons)

**To Derive:** The expression for per capita consumption and interpret it as an indicator of standard of living.

**Step 1 — Definition**
Per capita energy consumption is the average electrical energy available to each individual in a given country, assuming perfectly equal distribution.

$$E_{pc} = \frac{E_{total}}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Unit Analysis**
$E_{total}$ is in kWh/year, and $P$ is dimensionless (people). Therefore, $E_{pc}$ has units of **kWh/person/year**.

**Step 3 — Real-World Benchmark Numbers (for interpretation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{pc}(\text{USA}) &\approx 12{,}000\ \text{kWh/person/year} \\
E_{pc}(\text{EU}) &\approx 6{,}500\ \text{kWh/person/year} \\
E_{pc}(\text{China}) &\approx 5{,}500\ \text{kWh/person/year} \\
E_{pc}(\text{India}) &\approx 1{,}300\ \text{kWh/person/year} \\
E_{pc}(\text{Sub-Saharan Africa avg.}) &\approx 500\ \text{kWh/person/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Why This Matters (Engineering Interpretation)**
A low $E_{pc}$ indicates either **low industrial development**, **inadequate grid infrastructure**, or **energy poverty**. For India to reach the global middle-income benchmark (~3,500 kWh/person/year), its total generation must **nearly triple** by 2035 — a target that **cannot be met by fossil fuels alone** due to climate commitments, hence the central role of non-conventional energy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Future Energy Demand Projection (Exponential Growth Model)

**Given:**
- Present energy demand: $E_{0}$
- Annual growth rate: $r$ (as a decimal, e.g., 0.05 for 5%)
- Forecast horizon: $n$ years

**Step 1 — Assumption**
Energy demand grows at a *compound annual growth rate* $r$ (commonly 4–6% for developing economies).

**Step 2 — Recurrence Relation**
The energy demand at the end of year 1 is:
$$E_{1} = E_{0} + r \cdot E_{0} = E_{0}(1+r)$$

The demand at the end of year 2:
$$E_{2} = E_{1}(1+r) = E_{0}(1+r)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Generalization by Mathematical Induction**
After $n$ years:

$$E_{n} = E_{0}(1+r)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**
Suppose India's current electricity demand is $E_{0} = 1{,}600$ TWh/year, growing at $r = 5\% = 0.05$. Find the demand after 10 years.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{10} &= 1{,}600 \times (1 + 0.05)^{10} \\
&= 1{,}600 \times (1.05)^{10} \\
&= 1{,}600 \times 1.62889 \\
&\approx 2{,}606.23\ \text{TWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**
The energy demand **grows by ~63% in a single decade**. This mathematically justifies the **urgent deployment** of non-conventional energy to bridge the supply–demand gap sustainably.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annual CO₂ Emission Avoided by a Renewable Plant

**Given:**
- Annual generation of a renewable plant: $E_{gen}$ (kWh/year)
- Emission factor of displaced fossil fuel: $EF$ (kg CO₂/kWh)

**Step 1 — Definition**
The displaced fossil-fuel generation (assumed to be the marginal grid source, typically coal in India) would have emitted:

$$\Delta CO_{2} = E_{gen} \times EF$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Tonnes (standard reporting unit)**
Since 1 tonne = 1,000 kg:

$$\Delta CO_{2}\ (\text{in tonnes}) = \frac{E_{gen} \times EF}{1000}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example**
A 1 MW solar PV plant with a Capacity Factor of $CF = 0.20$ generates annually:
$E_{gen} = 1{,}000 \times 8760 \times 0.20 = 1{,}752{,}000\ \text{kWh/year} = 1.752\ \text{GWh/year}$

Displacing coal ($EF \approx 0.95$ kg CO₂/kWh):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta CO_{2} &= \frac{1{,}752{,}000 \times 0.95}{1000} \\
&= 1{,}664.4\ \text{tonnes of CO}_{2}\text{ avoided/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight**
This is exactly how India claims its **"45% non-fossil installed capacity by 2030"** target at COP climate summits — through verifiable, formula-driven avoidance accounting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Diagram — Energy Sources on Earth

The following Mermaid diagram provides the official KTU Module 1 classification tree for energy sources. Memorize its hierarchy for 2-mark direct questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Energy Sources"] --> nodeB["Conventional Resources"]
    nodeA["Energy Sources"] --> nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"]
    nodeB["Conventional Resources"] --> nodeD["Fossil Fuels"]
    nodeB["Conventional Resources"] --> nodeE["Nuclear Fission"]
    nodeB["Conventional Resources"] --> nodeF["Large Hydro"]
    nodeD["Fossil Fuels"] --> nodeG["Coal"]
    nodeD["Fossil Fuels"] --> nodeH["Petroleum"]
    nodeD["Fossil Fuels"] --> nodeI["Natural Gas"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeJ["Solar Energy"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeK["Wind Energy"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeL["Biomass and Biofuels"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeM["Geothermal Energy"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeN["Ocean Energy"]
    nodeC["Non-Conventional Resources"] --> nodeO["Hydrogen and Fuel Cells"]
    nodeJ["Solar Energy"] --> nodeJ1["Photovoltaic"]
    nodeJ["Solar Energy"] --> nodeJ2["Solar Thermal"]
    nodeK["Wind Energy"] --> nodeK1["Onshore"]
    nodeK["Wind Energy"] --> nodeK2["Offshore"]
    nodeN["Ocean Energy"] --> nodeN1["Tidal"]
    nodeN["Ocean Energy"] --> nodeN2["Wave"]
    nodeN["Ocean Energy"] --> nodeN3["OTEC"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Five Pillars of Importance (Engineering Decision Map)

This diagram maps the **five-fold justification** for adopting non-conventional energy. It is the single most important visual for a 7-mark importance question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pillarA["PILLAR 1: RESOURCE DEPLETION"] --> output["NEED FOR NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY"]
    pillarB["PILLAR 2: ENVIRONMENTAL POLLUTION"] --> output
    pillarC["PILLAR 3: ENERGY SECURITY"] --> output
    pillarD["PILLAR 4: RURAL ELECTRIFICATION"] --> output
    pillarE["PILLAR 5: ECONOMIC GROWTH AND JOBS"] --> output
    output --> benefitA["Reduced CO2 Emissions"]
    output --> benefitB["Sustainable Energy Supply"]
    output --> benefitC["Lower LCOE in the Long Term"]
    output --> benefitD["Decentralized Power Access"]
```

### 4.3 Solar Radiation Geometry — Module 1 Visual Aid

Although geometry is covered in detail later in the module, the following reference block is essential background for understanding the *why* behind solar as a non-conventional source.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Sun (Surface Temp approx 5778 K)"] -->|Emits EM Radiation| nodeS2["Extraterrestrial Radiation"]
    nodeS2["Extraterrestrial Radiation"] -->|"Solar Constant 1367 W per m2"| nodeS3["Top of Atmosphere"]
    nodeS3["Top of Atmosphere"] -->|"Attenuation by Atmosphere"| nodeS4["Earth Surface"]
    nodeS4["Earth Surface"] -->|"Direct plus Diffuse plus Reflected"| nodeS5["Available for PV and Thermal Capture"]
    nodeS5["Available for PV and Thermal Capture"] --> nodeS6["Converted to Electricity or Heat"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define non-conventional energy. List any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Non-conventional energy refers to **renewable, inexhaustible, and ecologically sustainable** energy sources that are continuously replenished by natural processes and produce little-to-no greenhouse-gas emissions during operation. Unlike conventional fossil-based fuels, these sources are not depleted by consumption.

**Four examples:**
1. Solar Energy (Photovoltaic / Thermal)
2. Wind Energy
3. Biomass and Biofuels
4. Geothermal / Tidal / Wave Energy

*(3 marks awarded: 1 for definition, 2 for correct listing.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**State any three differences between conventional and non-conventional energy sources.**

**Model Answer (tabular form expected):**

| S.No. | Conventional Energy | Non-Conventional Energy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Finite / exhaustible (e.g., coal, oil) | Renewable / inexhaustible |
| 2 | High CO₂ / pollutant emissions | Low-to-zero emissions (clean) |
| 3 | Centralized generation only | Both centralized and decentralized |
| 4 | High running cost (fuel cost) | Low running cost (fuel is free) |
| 5 | Causes global warming & acid rain | Environmentally sustainable |

*(3 marks: 1 per correct difference, well-tabulated.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**A. (a) Define non-conventional energy. Explain in detail the various renewable energy sources available in India. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Non-conventional energy sources are those energy resources which are **renewable**, **naturally replenished**, **non-polluting**, and **locally available**. They are also called *alternative* or *green* energy sources.

**Classification and Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Solar Energy —** Harnessed using photovoltaic (PV) panels or solar thermal collectors. India receives ~300 sunny days/year with an average insolation of **4–7 kWh/m²/day**, making it one of the most solar-rich countries. Applications: rooftop PV, solar water heaters, solar farms.

2. **Wind Energy —** Kinetic energy of moving air is converted via turbines. India ranks **4th globally** in installed wind capacity (~47 GW as of 2024). High-potential zones: Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan.

3. **Biomass Energy —** Organic residues (crop stubble, wood, animal waste) are burnt directly or converted into biogas/biofuels. India produces ~500 million tonnes of agricultural residue annually.

4. **Geothermal Energy —** Heat from earth's interior (Puga Valley in Ladakh is India's most promising site). Used for direct heating and electricity generation.

5. **Ocean Energy —** Tidal, wave, and OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion). Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay are prime tidal sites.

6. **Hydrogen and Fuel Cells —** "Green hydrogen" produced via electrolysis powered by renewables. A major focus of India's **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023).**

---

**A. (b) Discuss the importance of non-conventional energy sources in the Indian context. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

India's importance argument rests on the **five-pillar framework**:

**Pillar 1 — Resource Depletion (1.5 marks):** India imports **>85% of crude oil** and **~50% of natural gas**, leading to a forex drain of billions of dollars annually. Domestic coal reserves are projected to last ~50 years at current extraction rates.

**Pillar 2 — Environmental Pressure (1.5 marks):** India is the **third-largest CO₂ emitter** globally (~3.4 Gt/year). The commitment under the **Paris Agreement** to reduce emissions intensity by 45% by 2030 (over 2005 levels) requires aggressive renewable deployment.

**Pillar 3 — Energy Access (1.5 marks):** ~33,000+ Indian villages still face unreliable grid supply. Decentralized solar mini-grids (e.g., **PM-KUSUM scheme**) and rooftop PV are vital for rural electrification.

**Pillar 4 — Economic & Industrial Growth (1 mark):** Renewable sector already employs ~3,00,000 people in India. LCOE of solar has fallen to ~₹2.5/kWh — making it the **cheapest new electricity source** in the country.

**Pillar 5 — Strategic Energy Security (1.5 marks):** Renewables reduce dependency on Middle Eastern oil and volatile global fuel markets. India's target: **500 GW of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030**.

**Conclusion:** Non-conventional energy is no longer an environmental luxury but an **economic and strategic necessity** for India.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**B. (a) With a neat block diagram, classify the various energy sources on Earth. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (with diagram):**

Energy sources can be broadly classified into **two categories**:

**1. Conventional Energy Sources (Finite / Stock Resources):**
- **Fossil fuels** — Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas
- **Nuclear fission** — U-235, Th-232
- **Large Hydroelectric** (>25 MW capacity)

**2. Non-Conventional Energy Sources (Renewable / Flow Resources):**
- **Solar** — PV & Thermal
- **Wind** — Onshore & Offshore
- **Biomass / Biofuels**
- **Geothermal**
- **Ocean** — Tidal, Wave, OTEC
- **Hydrogen & Fuel Cells**

**Block Diagram (3 marks for neat labeled diagram; rest for explanation):**

```
              EARTH'S ENERGY SOURCES
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |                             |
  CONVENTIONAL                  NON-CONVENTIONAL
 (Finite, Polluting)         (Renewable, Clean)
        |                             |
   +----+----+              +---------+---------+
   |    |    |              |    |    |    |    |
 Fossil Nuclear        Solar Wind Biomass Geo Ocean H2
```

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| Availability | Limited | Unlimited |
| Cost | High (fuel) | Low (free fuel) |
| Pollution | High | Negligible |
| Example | Coal, Oil | Solar, Wind |

---

**B. (b) Calculate the per capita energy consumption of a state whose total annual electricity consumption is 75,000 GWh and population is 60 million. Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E_{total} = 75{,}000\ \text{GWh/year} = 75{,}000 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kWh/year}$
- $P = 60\ \text{million} = 6 \times 10^{7}$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$E_{pc} = \frac{E_{total}}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{pc} &= \frac{75{,}000 \times 10^{6}}{6 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= \frac{75 \times 10^{9}}{6 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= \frac{75}{6} \times 10^{2} \\
&= 12.5 \times 100 \\
&= 1{,}250\ \text{kWh/person/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (3 marks):**
The state's per capita consumption (1,250 kWh/year) is **comparable to the Indian national average** (~1,300 kWh) and **far below** the developed-world benchmark (~6,500–12,000 kWh). This indicates:
- The state is still in a **developing-economy category**.
- There is a **significant scope for renewable energy deployment** to support industrial growth without increasing fossil-fuel imports.
- Decentralized solar and wind projects can bridge the gap sustainably.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing "renewable" with "non-conventional":** Large hydro (>25 MW) is technically **renewable but classified as conventional** by India's Ministry of Power. Students who wrongly classify it under "non-conventional" lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Writing $E_{pc} = 1{,}250$ **without "kWh/person/year"** costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Listing examples without classifying:** A 3-mark "list four examples" question demands *names + one-line description* each. Just listing "Solar, Wind" without context gets only 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting India's statistics:** Always support importance arguments with **Indian numbers** (e.g., "India imports 85% of crude oil"), not generic global claims — this is the difference between 5 and 7 marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in $(1+r)^n$:** Use the correct exponent. A common slip is using $n=5$ instead of $n=10$ in 10-year projections.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Definition & Importance: 60-Second Revision Sheet**
>
> - **Definition:** Non-conventional energy = renewable, inexhaustible, low-emission energy sources.
> - **Examples:** Solar, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal/Wave/OTEC, Hydrogen, Fuel Cells.
> - **Conventional vs Non-Conventional:** Stock vs Flow; Finite vs Inexhaustible; Polluting vs Clean.
> - **Important units:** 1 TWh = $10^9$ kWh; 1 GW = $10^6$ kW; Solar Constant = 1367 W/m².
> - **Must-memorize formulas:** Per Capita Energy, Capacity Factor, CO₂ Avoided, Energy Demand Projection, LCOE.
> - **Indian context data points:** 85% oil imports; 420 ppm atmospheric CO₂; 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030; solar LCOE = ₹2.5/kWh.
> - **Five Pillars of Importance:** Depletion, Environment, Energy Security, Rural Electrification, Economic Growth.
> - **Examiner's favorites:** Definitions (3-mark), 5-pillar importance (7-mark), Per Capita numerical (7-mark), Classification diagram (7-mark).
> - **Subtle classification rule:** Large Hydro = Conventional; Small/Mini/Micro Hydro = Non-Conventional.
> - **Climate commitment:** India's NDC = 45% reduction in emissions intensity by 2030 (vs 2005).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global and National Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global and National Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global and National Energy Scenario** refers to the systematic study of the worldwide and country-wise availability, production, distribution, consumption, and reserves of conventional (fossil fuel-based) and non-conventional (renewable) energy resources, along with their socio-economic, environmental, and geopolitical implications in the present and future time horizons.

In KTU 2024 scheme context, this topic establishes the **engineering rationale** for transitioning from fossil fuels to renewable energy systems. It forms the foundational justification for studying every subsequent module in Non-Conventional Energy Systems.

### Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Standard)

| Term | Exact KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Energy extracted directly from nature (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium). |
| **Secondary Energy** | Energy derived from primary sources after conversion (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen). |
| **Commercial Energy** | Energy bought and sold in the market (coal, oil, gas, electricity). |
| **Non-Commercial Energy** | Energy collected for local use without formal trade (firewood, dung, agricultural waste). |
| **R/P Ratio** | **Reserves-to-Production ratio** = years of supply left at current extraction rate. |
| **Per Capita Energy Consumption** | $\frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES)}}{\text{Population}}$ — a metric of standard of living. |
| **Energy Security** | Uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices. |
| **Energy Mix** | The percentage share of different energy sources in total consumption. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition Builder)

> [!NOTE]
> **🍱 The "Plate of Food" Analogy for Energy**
>
> Imagine the world's energy supply as a giant **plate of rice** shared among 8 billion people (global population).
> - The **white rice** = fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas). It cooks fast, tastes familiar, but the pot is *almost empty*. Every spoonful reduces future meals.
> - The **vegetable curry** = renewable energy (solar, wind, hydro). It is *unlimited*, takes longer to harvest, but the kitchen garden keeps producing.
> - The **dessert** = nuclear/hydrogen energy — small portion today, but potentially transformative tomorrow.
>
> The **"Global Energy Scenario"** is simply checking: *How much rice is left? How fast are we eating? And how quickly are we growing vegetables?*
> The **"National Energy Scenario"** asks the same question *for one country (India)* — like asking how much of the plate belongs to India's 1.4 billion people.

**Intuitive takeaway**: A country with **low per-capita consumption but high population** (like India) is an *energy-hungry emerging market*; a country with **high per-capita consumption but low reserves** (like Japan) is an *energy-import-dependent developed nation*. This single distinction drives all energy policy.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is the "Gateway" to Non-Conventional Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Highlight:** Every KTU question paper on Non-Conventional Energy Systems begins (either directly or indirectly) by justifying *why renewables are needed*. This module provides that **numerical and statistical justification**. Memorize at least 3 global + 3 India-specific data points for full marks.

The world currently faces a **threefold energy trilemma**:
1. **Energy Security** — Will reserves last?
2. **Energy Equity** — Can the poor access power?
3. **Environmental Sustainability** — Can emissions be cut?

This trilemma is the unspoken backbone of every renewable energy system (solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, ocean, hydrogen) you will study in the later modules of OECHT837.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Resources (Board-Favorite Diagram Topic)

Energy resources are classified along **three principal axes**, each appearing repeatedly in KTU examinations.

### A. Based on Regeneration Rate (Most Important for KTU)

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. **Non-Renewable (Conventional / Fossil Fuels)**
   - Formed over **millions of years** from decomposed organic matter under high temperature and pressure.
   - *Finite stock* — once burnt, they cannot be recreated within human timescales.
   - Examples: **Coal, Lignite, Peat, Petroleum (Crude Oil), Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium)**.

2. **Renewable (Non-Conventional)**
   - Replenished **naturally and continuously** at a rate faster than (or equal to) human consumption.
   - *Flow resources* — solar radiation, wind, tides, geothermal heat — flow perpetually.
   - Examples: **Solar, Wind, Hydro (<25 MW), Biomass, Biogas, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC, Hydrogen**.

3. **Continuously Recycling**
   - Bridge category — slow replenishment on geological timescale but faster than fossil fuels.
   - Example: **Wood from sustainably managed forests**.

### B. Based on Commercial Use

- **Commercial Energy** — Electricity, coal, oil, gas. Sold on the open market. Tracked in national statistics.
- **Non-Commercial Energy** — Firewood, cow-dung cakes, agricultural residue. Used predominantly in rural households. **Often invisible in official statistics but critical in India's rural energy mix** — a favorite KTU Part-A question.

### C. Based on Primary/Secondary Form

- **Primary** — Found in raw form in nature (crude oil in the ground, sunlight falling).
- **Secondary** — Converted from primary (refined diesel from crude oil, electricity from coal).

---

## 2.2 Global Energy Scenario — Key Facts (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Numbers (Use with units in bold):**

| Metric | Approximate Global Value (2023-2024 baseline) |
|---|---|
| Total World Primary Energy Supply | **~606 EJ (Exajoules) ≈ 14,000 Mtoe** |
| Share of Fossil Fuels in Global Mix | **≈ 81%** (Coal 27%, Oil 31%, Gas 23%) |
| Share of Renewables (incl. hydro) | **≈ 14%** |
| Share of Nuclear | **≈ 4%** |
| Largest Energy Consumer | **China (~26%)**, followed by USA (~16%), India (~7%) |
| Per Capita Consumption (World avg.) | **~75 GJ/person/year** |
| Per Capita (USA) | **~270 GJ/person/year** |
| Per Capita (India) | **~25 GJ/person/year** |

**Engineering "Why"**: The 81% dependency on fossil fuels = the primary motivator for the global push toward renewables (Paris Agreement, COP-28 pledges, India's Panchamrit targets).

---

## 2.3 R/P Ratio — The "Years Left" Indicator

**Definition (KTU exact wording):**
> "R/P Ratio is the number of years the remaining reserves would last if production were to continue at the current rate."

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\text{R/P Ratio (years)} = \frac{\text{Proven Reserves at End of Year}}{\text{Annual Production in Same Year}}
$$

**Interpretation for a KTU student:**
- **Coal R/P (world) ≈ 130 years**
- **Oil R/P (world) ≈ 50 years**
- **Gas R/P (world) ≈ 50 years**
- **Uranium R/P (world) ≈ 90 years**

> [!NOTE]
> R/P does **NOT** predict the future accurately because: (1) consumption rates *grow*, not stay constant; (2) *new reserves are discovered*; (3) *technology improves extraction* (e.g., fracking for gas). It is a *static snapshot*, useful only as a relative indicator of scarcity.

---

## 2.4 India's National Energy Scenario — KTU-Favorite Statistics

### A. India's Energy Profile at a Glance

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Primary Energy Supply | **~36 EJ (≈ 880 Mtoe)** |
| Population | **~1.43 billion (largest, but lowest per-capita among major economies)** |
| Per Capita Consumption | **~25 GJ/person/year** (1/10th of USA) |
| Coal Share in Electricity Generation | **~50% of installed thermal capacity** |
| Total Installed Electricity Capacity (2024) | **~430 GW** |
| Renewable Installed Capacity | **~190 GW (≈ 43% of total)** |
| Solar Installed | **~85 GW (world's 4th largest)** |
| Wind Installed | **~47 GW (world's 4th largest)** |
| Crude Oil Import Dependency | **≈ 85%** |
| Natural Gas Import Dependency | **≈ 50%** |

### B. India's Energy Resources at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this list — appears in almost every KTU Module-1 question:**

1. **Coal** — 4th largest global reserves (~10% of world), mainly Gondwana fields (Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, West Bengal).
2. **Oil & Gas** — 0.5% of world reserves; major fields: Mumbai High, Assam (Digboi, Naharkatiya), Gujarat (Ankleshwar, Cambay basin), KG Basin (Krishna-Godavari).
3. **Hydro** — 145 GW estimated potential; ~46 GW installed; major sites in Himalayan and Western Ghats regions.
4. **Solar** — 5-7 kWh/m²/day insolation across most of India; ~300 GW potential just from rooftop.
5. **Wind** — 60 GW estimated potential at 80m hub height; Muppandal (Tamil Nadu) is one of the largest wind farms in Asia.
6. **Biomass** — 500 million tonnes/year agricultural residue; 18 GW bagasse cogeneration potential.
7. **Geothermal** — 10 GW estimated potential; Puga valley (Ladakh), Manikaran (Himachal) are known sites.
8. **Tidal** — Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Cambay have ~9 GW potential.
9. **Thorium** — World's largest thorium reserves (Kerala's monazite sands, Odisha, Tamil Nadu beaches); ~25% of global reserves.

---

## 2.5 India's Renewable Energy Targets (Panchamrit & Net-Zero)

At **COP-26 (Glasgow, 2021)**, PM Modi announced the **Panchamrit** (five nectar elements) pledge:

1. **500 GW** of non-fossil installed capacity by **2030**.
2. **50%** of installed capacity from non-fossil sources by **2030**.
3. **45%** reduction in emissions intensity (vs 2005) by **2030**.
4. **1 billion tonnes** cumulative carbon reduction by **2030**.
5. **Net-Zero** by **2070**.

**Engineering utility**: Every single renewable module (2-5) of your syllabus is directly contributing to achieving at least one of these targets.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Reserves-to-Production ratio (years left) | years |
| 2 | $\text{Per Capita Energy} = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{Population}}$ | Energy per person per year | GJ/person/year |
| 3 | $\text{Energy Demand Growth} = \left(1+\dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n \cdot D_0$ | Compounded future demand | EJ or GW |
| 4 | $\text{Load Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}}$ | Plant utilization metric | % (0-100) |
| 5 | $\text{Capacity Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times 8760}$ | Renewables reliability | % |
| 6 | $\text{CO}_2 \text{ Emission} = \text{Fuel} \times \text{Emission Factor}$ | Carbon footprint | kg CO₂ / unit fuel |
| 7 | $\text{Energy Returned on Invested (EROI)} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Delivered}}{\text{Energy Invested}}$ | Quality of energy source | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{CAGR of Demand} = \left(\dfrac{D_n}{D_0}\right)^{1/n}-1$ | Annual growth rate | % |
| 9 | $\text{Doubling Time} = \dfrac{70}{\text{Growth Rate \%}}$ (Rule of 70) | Years for demand to double | years |
| 10 | $\text{Carbon Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ Emissions}}{\text{Primary Energy}}$ | Energy-system cleanliness | kg CO₂ / GJ |

**Where:**
- $R$ = Proven reserves, $P$ = Annual production
- $D_0$ = Present demand, $r$ = growth rate %, $n$ = number of years
- $D_n$ = Demand after $n$ years

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of this topic |
|---|---|
| **Power Plant Sizing** | Future demand forecasts drive installed capacity planning. |
| **Grid Integration** | Renewables' 14-43% share mandates grid balancing studies. |
| **Policy Engineering** | RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) mandates are derived from the energy-mix targets. |
| **Project Finance** | LCOE calculations depend on capacity factors (formula #5). |
| **Climate Engineering** | Carbon-intensity targets (formula #10) drive technology selection. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Demand Forecasting using Compound Growth Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most frequently asked calculation in KTU 2024 Module-1 ESE.** Know this derivation cold.

### Problem Setup

The energy demand of a region is $D_0$ EJ in year 0. It grows at a constant annual rate of $r\%$. Find the demand $D_n$ after $n$ years and the doubling time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Every Step Explicit)

**Step 1 — Write the demand after 1 year.**

$$
D_1 = D_0 + D_0 \cdot \frac{r}{100} = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)
$$

**Logic:** Annual demand increases by $r\%$ of the previous year's value. So we *add* a fraction $r/100$ of $D_0$.

**Step 2 — Demand after 2 years.**

$$
D_2 = D_1 + D_1 \cdot \frac{r}{100} = D_1 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right) = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^2
$$

**Logic:** Same fractional growth applies to the *new* value $D_1$, compounding.

**Step 3 — Inductive step to year $n$.**

Assume demand after $(n-1)$ years is $D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^{n-1}$.

Multiplying by the growth factor for the $n^{th}$ year:

$$
D_n = D_{n-1} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right) = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^{n-1} \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right) = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

**Final Compound Growth Formula:**

$$
\boxed{D_n = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n}
$$

**Step 4 — Doubling time derivation.**

For doubling: $D_n = 2 D_0$.

$$
2 D_0 = D_0 \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

Divide both sides by $D_0$:

$$
2 = \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln 2 = n \cdot \ln \left(1 + \frac{r}{100}\right)
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)}
$$

For small $r$ (in %), use the approximation $\ln(1 + x) \approx x$ (when $x$ is small):

$$
n \approx \frac{0.6931}{\dfrac{r}{100}} = \frac{69.31}{r} \approx \frac{70}{r}
$$

**Final Doubling Time (Rule of 70):**

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{double}} \approx \frac{70}{r \%} \text{ years}}
$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **[Example]** India's primary energy demand is **36 EJ** in 2024 and grows at **4% per year**. Calculate:
> (a) Demand in 2030 (6 years later).
> (b) Doubling time.
> (c) Per capita energy in 2024 if population is 1.43 billion.

#### Part (a) — Future demand in 2030

Given: $D_0 = 36$ EJ, $r = 4\%$, $n = 6$ years.

Apply the formula:

$$
D_6 = 36 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{4}{100}\right)^6
$$

Compute the bracket:

$$
\left(1.04\right)^6
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
(1.04)^2 = 1.0816
$$

$$
(1.04)^4 = (1.0816)^2 = 1.16986
$$

$$
(1.04)^6 = (1.04)^4 \cdot (1.04)^2 = 1.16986 \cdot 1.0816 = 1.2653
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_6 = 36 \cdot 1.2653 = 45.55 \text{ EJ}
$$

**Answer:** Energy demand in 2030 ≈ **45.55 EJ**.

#### Part (b) — Doubling time at 4% growth

$$
T_{\text{double}} = \frac{70}{4} = 17.5 \text{ years}
$$

**Answer:** At 4% growth, demand doubles every **17.5 years** — extremely fast for any infrastructure to keep pace with.

#### Part (c) — Per capita energy in 2024

$$
\text{Per Capita} = \frac{36 \text{ EJ}}{1.43 \times 10^9 \text{ persons}} = \frac{36 \times 10^{18} \text{ J}}{1.43 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
= 25.17 \times 10^9 \text{ J/person} = 25.17 \text{ GJ/person}
$$

**Answer:** Per capita energy ≈ **25.17 GJ/person/year**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: R/P Ratio with Production Growth Adjustment

The basic R/P ratio assumes *constant production*. In reality, production typically grows at rate $g\%$. The *adjusted* "years left" formula is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Reserves remaining in year $t$.**

If production grows at $g\%$, production in year $t$ is:

$$
P(t) = P_0 \left(1 + \frac{g}{100}\right)^t
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative extraction over $N$ years (geometric series).**

$$
R = \sum_{t=0}^{N-1} P_0 \left(1 + \frac{g}{100}\right)^t = P_0 \cdot \frac{\left(1 + \dfrac{g}{100}\right)^N - 1}{\dfrac{g}{100}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$ (the true lifetime).**

$$
\frac{R \cdot g}{100 \cdot P_0} = \left(1 + \frac{g}{100}\right)^N - 1
$$

$$
\left(1 + \frac{g}{100}\right)^N = 1 + \frac{R g}{100 P_0}
$$

$$
N = \frac{\ln \left(1 + \dfrac{R g}{100 P_0}\right)}{\ln \left(1 + \dfrac{g}{100}\right)}
$$

**Adjusted Lifetime Formula:**

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{\ln \left(1 + \dfrac{R \cdot g}{100 \cdot P_0}\right)}{\ln \left(1 + \dfrac{g}{100}\right)}}
$$

**Special Case Check:** If $g \to 0$, Taylor expansion gives $\ln(1+x) \approx x$, and:

$$
N \to \frac{\dfrac{R \cdot g}{100 \cdot P_0}}{\dfrac{g}{100}} = \frac{R}{P_0}
$$

which recovers the standard R/P ratio. ✓ (Consistency check passed.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Renewable vs Non-Renewable Trade-Off

This matrix is the most-likely 7-mark descriptive question in KTU Module-1.

| Comparison Parameter | Conventional (Fossil/Nuclear) | Non-Conventional (Renewables) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, etc. |
| **Availability** | Finite, R/P < 200 years | Practically infinite on human timescale |
| **Initial Cost (CAPEX)** | **High** | **Higher** (PV panels, turbines) |
| **Operating Cost (OPEX)** | Moderate-High (fuel + maintenance) | **Very low** (sunlight/wind is free) |
| **Carbon Emissions** | **High** (~820 gCO₂/kWh for coal) | **Near-zero** operational emissions |
| **Land Footprint** | Mining scars, ash ponds | Large for solar/wind farms |
| **Maturity / Reliability** | Mature technology, dispatchable | Improving; variable/seasonal for solar/wind |
| **Efficiency (Thermodynamic)** | 35-45% (thermal plants) | 15-25% (PV); 30-45% (wind CF) |
| **Time to Build** | 4-7 years (thermal); 10+ (nuclear) | 6-18 months (PV/wind) |
| **Environmental Cost** | Acid rain, smog, ash, tailings | Visual/noise (wind), e-waste (PV at EoL) |
| **Energy Security** | Imports required (India 85% oil) | Indigenous, decentralized |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Renewables trade off higher CAPEX and intermittency for *zero fuel cost* and *near-zero emissions*. This is the fundamental economic reason behind the global shift.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Practice — Carbon Emission Calculation

**Problem:** A thermal power plant burns **1000 tonnes of coal per day**. The emission factor of Indian coal is approximately **1.8 kg CO₂ per kg of coal**.
(a) Calculate daily CO₂ emission.
(b) Annual CO₂ emission.
(c) CO₂ emission per kWh if the plant generates **2.5 GWh per day**.

### Part (a) — Daily emission

Daily coal = $1000 \text{ tonnes} = 10^6 \text{ kg}$.

$$
E_{\text{day}} = 10^6 \text{ kg} \times 1.8 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kg coal}
$$

$$
E_{\text{day}} = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 1800 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{day}
$$

### Part (b) — Annual emission

$$
E_{\text{year}} = 1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 657 \times 10^6 \text{ kg CO}_2
$$

$$
E_{\text{year}} = 657{,}000 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year} \approx 0.657 \text{ million tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

### Part (c) — Emission intensity per kWh

Daily generation = $2.5 \text{ GWh} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}$.

$$
\text{Intensity} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^6 \text{ kg CO}_2}{2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}} = 0.72 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each unit of electricity from this plant produces **0.72 kg CO₂** — about half the world average (~0.82 kg CO₂/kWh) but still 50-100 times more than solar PV (~0.04 kg CO₂/kWh lifecycle).
