# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose marks on three issues in Module 1: (1) **forgetting the unit conversion** for H₀ (J vs Wh), (2) **mixing up signs** for δ and φ in Southern vs Northern Hemisphere, and (3) **skipping the eccentricity correction** when given a specific day. Always show units and write intermediate steps.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between conventional and non-conventional energy sources. List any four non-conventional energy sources.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| Nature | Finite, exhaustible | Infinite, replenishable |
| Pollution | High CO₂, SO₂, NOx | Negligible/zero emissions |
| Examples | Coal, oil, gas, nuclear (Uranium) | Solar, wind, biomass, tidal, geothermal |
| Running cost | High (fuel cost) | Low (no fuel cost) |
| Initial cost | Moderate | High (declining rapidly) |
| Carbon footprint | 0.8–1.0 kg CO₂/kWh | ~0.04 kg CO₂/kWh (manufacturing only) |

**Four Non-Conventional Sources:**
1. **Solar Energy** (PV + Thermal)
2. **Wind Energy**
3. **Biomass / Biogas**
4. **Tidal / Wave Energy**

(Marks distribution: 1 mark each for two distinctions × 2 + 0.5 each for four examples × 4 = 2 + 2 = ... typically 1.5 for comparison table and 1.5 for list.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define Solar Constant. What is its standard value? Why does the extraterrestrial radiation received on Earth vary throughout the year even though the solar constant is constant?

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The **Solar Constant** is the rate at which solar energy is received *per unit area* on a surface held *perpendicular* to the Sun's rays at the *mean Earth-Sun distance*, *outside* the Earth's atmosphere.

$$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 \quad (= 1.361 \text{ kW/m}^2)$$

**Reason for variation in extraterrestrial radiation:**

Although the *solar constant* (defined at the mean distance) is invariant, the *actual* extraterrestrial radiation at the top of the atmosphere varies throughout the year because of the **eccentricity of Earth's orbit**. Earth's orbit is elliptical with eccentricity e ≈ 0.0167. Hence the Earth-Sun distance varies:

$$r = r_0 \left(1 - e\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{365}\right)\right)$$

By the *inverse-square law*, the flux varies as $1/r^2$:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2 = G_{sc} \cdot \left[1 + 0.033 \cos\left(\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\right]$$

The maximum variation is about **±3.3%** — Earth is closest to the Sun around January 3 (perihelion) and farthest around July 4 (aphelion).

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Q. (a) Define the following terms with neat diagrams: Solar declination, Hour angle, Solar altitude, Zenith angle, and Azimuth angle. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Solar Declination (δ):**
The angular position of the Sun north or south of the equatorial plane. Varies between −23.45° (winter solstice) and +23.45° (summer solstice) over the year.

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)$$

**2. Hour Angle (ω):**
The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation. Measured in degrees, at 15° per hour.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \cdot (t_{solar} - 12)$$

Negative in morning, positive in afternoon, zero at solar noon.

**3. Solar Altitude (α):**
The angle of the Sun *above the horizon*.

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

**4. Zenith Angle (θ\_z):**
The angle between the Sun's rays and the *vertical* (zenith direction).

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$$

**5. Azimuth Angle (γ\_s):**
The compass bearing of the Sun's projection onto the horizontal plane, measured from *true north* (or true south in some conventions).

$$\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

---

#### **(b)** A flat-plate solar collector is to be installed at Coimbatore (latitude 11° N). On **February 14**, calculate: **[7 Marks]**
1. Solar declination
2. Hour angle at 11:00 AM solar time
3. Solar altitude
4. Solar zenith angle

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Day Number n:**
Jan = 31 days + Feb 14 = **45**.

**Step 2 — Declination (δ):**

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + 45)\right) = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \cdot 329}{365}\right)$$

$$= 23.45 \cdot \sin(324.49^\circ) = 23.45 \cdot (-0.5826) \approx -13.66^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Hour Angle (ω):**

$$\omega = 15 \cdot (11 - 12) = -15^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Solar Altitude (α):**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin(11^\circ)\sin(-13.66^\circ) + \cos(11^\circ)\cos(-13.66^\circ)\cos(-15^\circ)$$

Compute each term:
- $\sin(11^\circ) \approx 0.1908$
- $\sin(-13.66^\circ) \approx -0.2362$
- $\cos(11^\circ) \approx 0.9816$
- $\cos(-13.66^\circ) \approx 0.9717$
- $\cos(-15^\circ) = 0.9659$

$$\sin\alpha = (0.1908)(-0.2362) + (0.9816)(0.9717)(0.9659)$$

$$= -0.0451 + 0.9214 = 0.8763$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8763) \approx 61.18^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Zenith Angle (θ\_z):**

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - 61.18^\circ = 28.82^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Air Mass (Bonus check):**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(28.82^\circ)} = \frac{1}{0.8757} \approx 1.142$$

### ✅ Final Answers:
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Declination δ | **−13.66°** |
| Hour Angle ω | **−15°** |
| Altitude α | **61.18°** |
| Zenith Angle θ\_z | **28.82°** |

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

#### **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Q. (a) Derive the expression for the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface. State clearly all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The Sun emits radiation as a blackbody at ~5778 K.
2. The Earth-Sun distance is constant (mean value) for a given day.
3. The atmosphere is absent (extraterrestrial).
4. Solar rays are parallel (Sun is effectively at infinity).
5. The surface is perfectly horizontal (parallel to ground).
6. The day length is symmetric about solar noon.

**Derivation:** *(Already shown exhaustively in Section 3.4 — repeat here in summary form for exam write-up)*

Start with the instantaneous extraterrestrial flux on a horizontal surface:

$$I_{0h}(\omega) = G_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega + \sin\phi\sin\delta\right]$$

Integrate over the daylight period (from $-\omega_s$ to $+\omega_s$):

$$H_0 = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} I_{0h}(\omega) \cdot \frac{86400}{2\pi} d\omega$$

$$H_0 = \frac{86400}{2\pi} \cdot G_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta \cdot 2\sin\omega_s + \sin\phi\sin\delta \cdot 2\omega_s\right]$$

$$\boxed{H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} \cdot G_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi\omega_s}{360}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right] \quad \text{J/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

where:
- $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²
- $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$
- $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$
- $n$ = day of year, $\phi$ = latitude, $\delta$ = declination

---

#### **(b)** Calculate the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at Delhi (φ = 28.6° N) on **June 21**. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Day Number n = 172** (Summer Solstice).

**Step 2 — Declination:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right) = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{360 \cdot 456}{365}\right)$$

$$= 23.45 \cdot \sin(449.75^\circ) = 23.45 \cdot \sin(89.75^\circ) \approx 23.45 \cdot 0.99998 \approx 23.45^\circ$$

(Summer Solstice — Sun is at maximum northward declination.)

**Step 3 — Sunset Hour Angle:**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan(28.6^\circ)\tan(23.45^\circ)$$

$$\tan(28.6^\circ) \approx 0.5445, \quad \tan(23.45^\circ) \approx 0.4332$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -(0.5445)(0.4332) = -0.2359$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.2359) \approx 103.65^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Day Length:**

$$N = \frac{2 \cdot 103.65}{15} \approx 13.82 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Eccentricity Correction:**

$$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360 \cdot 172}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(169.64^\circ) = 1 + 0.033 \cdot (-0.9840) \approx 0.9675$$

**Step 6 — Plug into H₀ formula:**

$$H_0 = \frac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} \cdot 1367 \cdot 0.9675 \cdot \left[\cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(23.45^\circ)\sin(103.65^\circ) + \frac{2\pi \cdot 103.65}{360}\sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(23.45^\circ)\right]$$

Compute each term:
- $\cos(28.6^\circ) \approx 0.8779$
- $\cos(23.45^\circ) \approx 0.9174$
- $\sin(103.65^\circ) \approx 0.9718$
- $\sin(28.6^\circ) \approx 0.4784$
- $\sin(23.45^\circ) \approx 0.3977$
- $2\pi \cdot 103.65 / 360 = 1.8096$ rad

**Term 1 (Cosine part):**
$$0.8779 \cdot 0.9174 \cdot 0.9718 = 0.7825$$

**Term 2 (Sine part):**
$$1.8096 \cdot 0.4784 \cdot 0.3977 = 0.3444$$

**Sum of terms:**
$$0.7825 + 0.3444 = 1.1269$$

**Prefactor:**
$$\frac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} = 27501.6 \text{ s}$$

**Final calculation:**
$$H_0 = 27501.6 \cdot 1367 \cdot 0.9675 \cdot 1.1269$$

$$= 27501.6 \cdot 1367 \cdot 1.0902 \approx 27501.6 \cdot 1490.3$$

$$\approx 4.099 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

Convert to kWh/m²/day:
$$H_0 = 4.099 \times 10^7 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \approx 11.39 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$$

### ✅ Final Answer:
$$\boxed{H_0 \approx 40.99 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} = 11.39 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

(Reasonable — Delhi summer receives ~11 kWh/m²/day of extraterrestrial radiation. Actual measured terrestrial would be ~6–7 kWh/m²/day after atmospheric losses.)

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 High-Yield Revision Points:**

- [x] **Renewable Energy vs Non-Renewable:** Renewables are *replenishable*; non-renewables are *finite*.
- [x] **G\_sc = 1367 W/m²** — memorize this value precisely.
- [x] **Axial tilt = 23.45°** — defines the maximum declination.
- [x] **India's Renewable Capacity ≈ 190+ GW** (Solar leads at ~82 GW).
- [x] **Solar angles:** φ (latitude), δ (declination, ±23.45°), ω (hour angle, 15°/hr), θ\_z (zenith), α (altitude = 90° − θ\_z), γ\_s (azimuth).
- [x] **Declination formula:** $\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin(360(284+n)/365)$
- [x] **Sunset hour angle:** $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi\tan\delta$
- [x] **Day length:** $N = 2\omega_s / 15$ hours
- [x] **Air Mass:** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (for θ\_z < 90°)
- [x] **Eccentricity factor:** $E_0 = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$
- [x] **Daily Extraterrestrial H₀** is the *integrated* form of I₀ from −ω\_s to +ω\_s.
- [x] **Clearness Index K\_T = H/H₀** — measures atmospheric transparency.
- [x] **Solar radiation types:** Direct (beam) + Diffuse (sky) + Reflected (ground) = Global (terrestrial).
- [x] **India's solar target:** 100 GW (achieved/scaling); 500 GW renewable by 2030.
- [x] **JNNSM, ISA, PM-KUSUM, SAUBHAGYA** — key initiatives.
- [x] **7 Pillars of Need:** Depletion, Pollution, Global warming, Energy security, Rural electrification, SDGs, Decentralization.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mantra:** "**G 1367, Delta 2345, Omega 15, Phi latitude**" — memorize the four cardinal constants and you have 80% of the formulas covered.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement and Estimation Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Measurement and Estimation Techniques # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Measurement and Estimation Techniques (KTU 2024 — Non-Conventional Energy Systems):** > These are the standardized scientific methodologies, instrumentation protocols, and analytical procedures used to **quantify the availability, intensity, quality, and spatial–temporal distribution** of non-conventional (renewable) energy resources such as **solar radiation, wind speed/direction, biomass availability, small hydro potential, and tidal energy**, with the ultimate objective of assessing the **techno-economic feasibility** of harvesting these resources for power generation. In KTU 2024 terminology, this domain is often termed **"Energy Resource Assessment"** and forms the foundational block (Module 1) of the Non-Conventional Energy Systems course because every downstream design decision — turbine sizing, PV array rating, biomass plant capacity, micro-hydro selection — depends on accurate resource data. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like going fishing before stocking a freezer.** > Before you decide how big your freezer should be (system size) and how much fish you can realistically store per year (energy output), you must go out and *measure* — over many days and seasons — how many fish are available in the lake (resource availability). Then you *estimate* — using math models — how many you can catch on a typical day. > > **Measurement = direct reading from instruments (e.g., anemometer, pyranometer).** > **Estimation = indirect mathematical prediction (e.g., Weibull extrapolation, Angstrom's formula).** Just as a meteorologist needs decades of weather data to forecast climate, a renewable energy engineer needs **multi-year, site-specific measured and estimated data** to design a reliable plant. ## 1.3 Why This Module Comes First in KTU The KTU 2024 Scheme positions this as Module 1 because: - It establishes the **input data** for all later design modules. - It introduces the **physical, electrical, and metrological instruments** engineers must specify when procuring a renewable energy plant. - It develops the **statistical and probabilistic thinking** (Weibull, Angstrom, Beta distribution) essential for board exam numericals. ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following constants and standard metrics are **universally used** in KTU board exam numericals and **must be memorized**: > > - **Air density at sea level (ρ) = 1.225 kg/m³** > - **Gravitational acceleration (g) = 9.81 m/s²** > - **Water density (ρ_w) = 1000 kg/m³** > - **Solar constant (I_sc) = 1367 W/m²** (also written as 1361 W/m² by WMO) > - **Clearness index (K_T) range = 0.25 to 0.75** for most of India > - **Power Law exponent for wind profile (α) ≈ 1/7** (open terrain) per **IEC 61400-12** > - **Standard anemometer height = 10 m** (WMO standard) > - **Standard pyranometer height = 1 m to 2 m** above ground ## 1.5 Classification of Non-Conventional Energy Resource Measurement | Resource Type | Primary Measured Parameter | Key Instrument | Typical Measurement Duration | |---------------|---------------------------|----------------|------------------------------| | Solar | Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI), Diffuse, Direct Normal | Pyranometer, Pyrheliometer | 1 yr minimum (per MNRE) | | Wind | Wind speed (m/s), direction (°) | Cup/vane anemometer | 1 yr minimum (per IEC) | | Biomass | Surplus yield (tonnes/ha/yr), Moisture content | Field sampling, oven drying | Seasonal surveys | | Small Hydro | Head (m), Flow rate (m³/s) | Pressure transducer, current meter | 12 months continuous | | Tidal/Wave | Wave height (H_s), Period (T), Tidal range | Wave rider buoy, tide gauge | 1–3 years | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Solar Radiation Measurement — Operational Logic ### 2.1.1 Pyranometer (Global Horizontal Irradiance) **Operational concept:** - A thermopile-based transducer converts incident broadband solar radiation (wavelength **0.3 μm to 3 μm**) into a small DC voltage. - The voltage is linearly proportional to irradiance in **W/m²**. - A **black-and-white thermopile** alternates exposure to create a measurable temperature gradient. **The 'Why' behind the design:** The white surface reflects solar energy; the black surface absorbs it. The temperature difference (ΔT) between the two is proportional to irradiance. ### 2.1.2 Pyrheliometer (Direct Normal Irradiance — DNI) - Mounted on a **solar tracker** to keep the line of sight normal to the sun. - Has a **5.7° acceptance angle** (per ISO 9060). - Used to derive the **direct beam component** which is critical for concentrated solar power (CSP). ### 2.1.3 Sunshine Recorder (Campbell-Stokes) - A glass sphere focuses sunlight onto a **graduated paper card**. - Duration of burn marks = **sunshine hours (S)**. - Used for **Angstrom's empirical estimation**. ### 2.1.4 Diffuse Radiation Measurement - A **shadow ring** or **tracking disk** blocks the direct sun from the pyranometer, allowing only diffuse sky radiation to be recorded. ## 2.2 Wind Energy Measurement — Operational Logic ### 2.2.1 Cup Anemometer - Three conical cups rotate about a vertical shaft at a speed proportional to wind speed. - **Calibration constant** converts rotation frequency (Hz) to wind speed (m/s). - Accuracy class: **IEC 61400-12-1 Class A: ± 1% at 10 m/s**. ### 2.2.2 Wind Vane - Measures **wind direction** in degrees (0° = North, 90° = East). - Combined with cup anemometer → **propeller-type combined sensor** (used in most modern met masts). ### 2.2.3 Wind Rose Diagram - Polar histogram showing the **frequency of wind direction** and the **average wind speed per direction sector**. - Typically divided into **12 sectors of 30° each**. ### 2.2.4 SODAR (Sonic Detection and Ranging) - Remote sensing device using **acoustic pulses** to measure wind profile up to 200 m. - Used where installation of tall met masts is impractical. ### 2.2.5 LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) - Optical remote sensing, can measure wind up to **300 m** with high accuracy. - Preferred for **offshore wind assessment**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers ~80% of KTU board exam numericals on Module 1.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Wind power in a stream of area A | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}$ | W | Air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³ (sea level) | | 2 | Wind power density | $P / A = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v^{3}$ | W/m² | Independent of swept area | | 3 | Betz limit (max extractable) | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | — | Universal constant | | 4 | Wind speed power law (vertical profile) | $v(z) = v_{ref} \cdot (z / z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | m/s | $\alpha$ = 1/7 (open terrain, IEC) | | 5 | Angstrom empirical formula | $H / H_{o} = a + b \cdot (S / S_{o})$ | — | a + b < 1; a, b empirical | | 6 | Extraterrestrial radiation (daily) | $H_{o} = \tfrac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_{o} \cdot (\omega_{s \cdot \sin\varphi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\varphi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \sin\omega_s})$ | MJ/m²/day | $\omega_s$ = sunrise hour angle | | 7 | Mean wind power (Weibull) | $\bar{P} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A c^{3} \Gamma(1 + 0.1604 K_T + ...$ (piecewise) | — | Valid for $0.22 \le K_T \le 0.80$ | | 15 | Solar declination | $\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin(360 \cdot (284 + n) / 365)$ | degrees | n = day of year | | 16 | Sunrise hour angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi \tan\delta)$ | degrees | $\varphi$ = latitude | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These measurement and estimation techniques drive every major decision in the renewable energy industry: - **Wind farm developers** spend **$ 100,000 to $ 500,000** per site for a 12-month met mast campaign (per AWEA standards). - **MNRE (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy, India)** mandates at least **1 year of measured solar data** before approving any solar park > 50 MW. - **MNES guidelines** for biomass power mandate a **3-season crop-yield survey** (Kharif, Rabi, Zaid) before sizing a 1–10 MW plant. - **PGCIL (India)** requires wind resource maps (from NIWE) at **5 km × 5 km resolution** before grid-connecting wind projects. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Pattern:** Derivations form a **mandatory part (b) of 14-mark questions**. You are expected to write every algebraic step explicitly. --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Wind Power Available in a Free Stream **Starting concept:** A wind turbine extracts kinetic energy from a moving air mass. To find the *available* power, we must compute the kinetic energy flux passing through the rotor swept area per unit time. **Step 1 — Mass flow rate through area A at wind speed v:** The mass of air passing per second through cross-section A is: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$ where ρ is the air density (kg/m³) and A is the rotor swept area (m²). **Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass of air:** The kinetic energy of 1 kg of air moving at velocity v is: $$KE_{per \, kg} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply to obtain available power (no turbine yet):** Available power P is the kinetic energy flux: $$P = \dot{m} \times KE_{per \, kg} = (\rho A v) \cdot (\tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of power density (independent of A):** $$\frac{P}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$$ **Step 5 — Apply sea-level values to find a benchmark:** For ρ = **1.225 kg/m³** and v = **10 m/s**: $$P/A = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 10^{3} = 612.5 \quad W/m^{2}$$ **Conclusion:** Available power density scales with the **cube of wind speed** — this is why a small increase in wind speed dramatically boosts energy yield. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Betz Limit (Maximum Extractable Power Fraction) **Step 1 — Set up a control volume around the rotor:** Three sections: upstream (∞), rotor (R), downstream (D). Velocity reduces from v∞ to v_R (at rotor) to v_D. **Step 2 — Conservation of mass:** $\rho A_\infty v_\infty = \rho A_R v_R = \rho A_D v_D = \dot{m}$ Assume incompressible flow and use the same stream tube area A_R = A. **Step 3 — Power extracted by rotor (from momentum + energy):** The force on the rotor is the rate of change of momentum: $$F = \dot{m} (v_\infty - v_D) = \rho A v_R (v_\infty - v_D)$$ **Step 4 — Power extracted:** $$P = F \cdot v_R = \rho A v_R^{2} (v_\infty - v_D)$$ **Step 5 — Express v_R in terms of v∞ and v_D using energy conservation:** $$v_R = \tfrac{v_\infty + v_D}{2}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back:** $$P = \rho A \left(\tfrac{v_\infty + v_D}{2}\right)^{2} (v_\infty - v_D)$$ **Step 7 — Introduce induction factor a, where v_R = v∞(1 − a):** Then v_D = v∞(1 − 2a). Substitute: $$P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_\infty^{3} \cdot 4 a (1 - a)^{2}$$ **Step 8 — Define power coefficient:** $$C_p = \frac{P}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_\infty^{3}} = 4 a (1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 9 — Maximize by differentiating w.r.t. a and setting to zero:** $$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4[(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ Non-trivial solution: **a = 1/3**. **Step 10 — Substitute a = 1/3 to get Betz limit:** $$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ $$\boxed{C_{p,Betz} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad \text{(or 59.3\%)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** A common Part A question is *"Why can't a wind turbine extract 100% of the wind energy?"* The answer is the Betz limit — extracting 100% would require v_D = 0, which violates continuity. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Angstrom's Empirical Formula (for Solar Radiation Estimation) **Step 1 — Define clearness index:** $$K_T = \frac{H}{H_o}$$ where H = monthly mean daily global radiation on horizontal surface (measured), and H_o = monthly mean daily extraterrestrial radiation (computed from astronomical parameters). **Step 2 — Define relative sunshine fraction:** $$\sigma = \frac{S}{S_o}$$ where S = actual monthly mean daily sunshine hours (from sunshine recorder), S_o = maximum possible monthly mean daily sunshine hours (= N/2, where N = day length in hours). **Step 3 — Linear regression hypothesis:** Based on observations from many stations globally, Angstrom proposed a linear relationship: $$K_T = a + b \cdot \sigma$$ Substituting back: $$\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b \cdot \frac{S}{S_o}$$ $$\boxed{H = H_o \left[ a + b \cdot \tfrac{S}{S_o} \right]}$$ **Step 4 — Determination of constants a and b:** - They are **empirical, location-specific**. - For typical Indian stations, $a \approx 0.25$ and $b \approx 0.50$ (so $a + b \approx 0.75$). - Determined by linear regression on multi-year measured data of H and S. **Step 5 — Compute H_o (extraterrestrial radiation):** $$H_o = \tfrac{24 \cdot 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot (\omega_s \sin\varphi \sin\delta + \cos\varphi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s)$$ where: - $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi \tan\delta)$ - $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\tfrac{360(284+n)}{365}\right]$ - $E_o = 1 + 0.033 \cos\left[\tfrac{360 n}{365}\right]$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Weibull Mean Wind Speed and Power **Step 1 — Weibull probability density function (PDF):** $$f(v) = \tfrac{k}{c} \left(\tfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\tfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right]$$ for v ≥ 0; k = shape factor (typically 1.5 to 3.0), c = scale factor (m/s). **Step 2 — Definition of mean:** $$\bar{v} = \int_{0}^{\infty} v \, f(v) \, dv$$ **Step 3 — Use standard integral identity:** $$\int_{0}^{\infty} v^{n} \cdot \tfrac{k}{c} \left(\tfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^{k}} dv = c^{n} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{n}{k}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply with n = 1:** $$\bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{k}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Mean power (using $P \propto v^{3}$, so n = 3):** $$\overline{P} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A \cdot \overline{v^{3}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A c^{3} \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{3}{k}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Common KTU trap:** Do NOT write $\bar{v}^{3} = \overline{v^{3}}$. The mean of the cube is **not** the cube of the mean. Use the Weibull formulation. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Vertical Wind Profile (Power Law) **Step 1 — Empirical observation:** Wind speed increases with height due to reduced surface drag. **Step 2 — Reference measurement at hub height and reference height:** $$v(z) = v(z_{ref}) \cdot \left(\tfrac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Typical α values:** | Terrain | α (power-law exponent) | |---------|-------------------------| | Open water, flat coast | 0.10 | | Open grassland | 0.15 | | Wooded countryside | 0.20 | | Urban areas | 0.25 – 0.40 | **Step 4 — Example:** If v at 10 m = 6 m/s, find v at 80 m for α = 1/7: $$v(80) = 6 \cdot (80/10)^{1/7} = 6 \cdot 8^{0.143} = 6 \cdot 1.345 \approx 8.07 \text{ m/s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always show the power law formula and the substituted value of α. Do not leave α as a generic symbol. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A site has air density 1.15 kg/m³. Wind speed measurements show v = 8 m/s at 20 m height. Compute the wind power density at hub height of 80 m using α = 1/7. Take ρ at hub = 1.225 kg/m³. (KTU model — Part B sub-question) **Step 1 — Extrapolate wind speed to 80 m:** $$v_{80} = 8 \cdot \left(\tfrac{80}{20}\right)^{1/7} = 8 \cdot (4)^{0.143}$$ **Step 2 — Compute (4)^0.143:** $$(4)^{0.143} = e^{0.143 \cdot \ln 4} = e^{0.143 \cdot 1.386} = e^{0.1983} \approx 1.219$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$v_{80} = 8 \times 1.219 = 9.75 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute power density:** $$P/A = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (9.75)^{3}$$ $$(9.75)^{3} = 9.75 \times 9.75 \times 9.75 = 95.06 \times 9.75 \approx 926.8$$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$P/A = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 926.8 \approx 567.7 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{P/A \approx 568 \text{ W/m}^{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Betz-limited extractable power density:** $$P_{extract,\,max}/A = 0.593 \times 568 \approx 337 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagrams are mandatory in KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) answers.** All diagrams below are rendered using Mermaid — a clean, ASCII-safe subset has been used to ensure compilation. Do not submit diagrams without proper headings — examiners deduct marks for unlabeled figures. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the following terms with respect to non-conventional energy measurement:** **(a)** Pyranometer, **(b)** Pyrheliometer, **(c)** Sunshine recorder. **Model Answer:** **(a) Pyranometer:** A thermoelectric instrument that measures the **total (global) solar radiation** received on a horizontal surface from the entire hemisphere (sky + reflected ground). It works on the principle of a thermopile where a black surface absorbs radiation and the resulting temperature difference produces an EMF proportional to irradiance in **W/m²**. The instrument has a glass dome to protect against convection and transmits radiation in the wavelength band **0.3 μm to 3 μm**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for working principle, 1 mark for range/units)* **(b) Pyrheliometer:** An instrument mounted on a **solar tracker** that measures **direct normal irradiance (DNI)** only — the radiation coming directly from the sun's disk within a narrow acceptance angle of about **5.7°**. Used to determine the direct beam component of solar radiation, which is essential for designing concentrating solar collectors. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tracker role, 1 mark for 5.7°)* **(c) Sunshine Recorder (Campbell–Stokes):** A glass sphere focuses direct sunlight onto a specially calibrated paper card, where it burns a trace. The length of the burn trace is directly proportional to the **duration of bright sunshine hours (S)** in a day. This instrument is used in conjunction with **Angstrom's empirical formula** to estimate global solar radiation. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for paper card burn mechanism, 1 mark for Angstrom link)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Define wind power density. What is its significance in wind energy assessment?** **Model Answer:** **Wind power density** is defined as the **available wind power per unit area of the rotor swept plane** and is given by: $$\frac{P}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3} \quad (W/m^{2})$$ **Significance:** 1. It is a **site-independent metric** — it removes the effect of rotor size, enabling fair comparison between potential wind farm sites. *(1 mark)* 2. It quantifies the **wind resource strength** without requiring detailed turbine selection. *(1 mark)* 3. It is the **fundamental input to AEO (Annual Energy Output)** estimation when multiplied by rotor swept area, turbine efficiency, and capacity factor. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and **must offer internal choice**. The student attempts **ONE full question (a + b)**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]` **(a) Explain with the help of a block diagram the working of a wind resource measurement system. List the various sensors used and their function.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A wind resource measurement system is designed to capture multi-year wind data at a potential wind farm site following the standards of **IEC 61400-12-1** and **WMO guidelines**. The system consists of the following sub-systems: **1. Met Mast (Measurement Mast):** A lattice or tubular mast of **80 m to 120 m** height. Modern IEC Class A masts may be **120 m** for large multi-MW turbines. *(1 mark)* **2. Sensors and their locations:** - **Cup Anemometers:** Mounted at heights **10 m, 30 m, 50 m, 80 m** (and beyond) to capture vertical wind profile. Accuracy ± 1% at 10 m/s. *(1 mark)* - **Wind Vanes:** Mounted at **10 m and 80 m** to capture direction profile. *(0.5 mark)* - **Temperature Sensor:** To compute air density variation with temperature for energy corrections. *(0.5 mark)* - **Barometric Pressure Sensor:** For density correction. *(0.5 mark)* - **Relative Humidity Sensor:** For precise air density computation using: $$\rho = \frac{P}{R_{specific} T} \left(1 - \tfrac{0.378 \, e}{P}\right)$$ *(0.5 mark)* **3. Data Logger:** Stores **10-minute average values** of wind speed, direction, temperature, and pressure. Sampling rate 1 Hz. *(1 mark)* **4. Power Supply:** Solar PV with battery backup or grid; must operate continuously for 12+ months. *(0.5 mark)* **5. Validation and Processing (post-data):** Per IEC 61400-12-1, data is validated, missing values flagged, and **Weibull parameters (k, c)** are computed. *(1 mark)* **6. Final Deliverables:** Wind rose, diurnal pattern, vertical shear (α), and **AEO (Annual Energy Output)**. *(1 mark)* **Block diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes. *(The student should redraw a simplified version during the exam.)* --- **(b) Derive the expression for wind power available in a free stream of air moving with velocity v. Hence state and prove the Betz limit.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a free stream of air with uniform velocity v∞, density ρ, flowing through a wind turbine rotor swept area A. By the principle of conservation of mass, the mass flow rate is: $$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad \text{(at any stream tube section)}$$ Kinetic energy per unit mass of the air is $\tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$. Hence, the **available wind power** (kinetic energy flux) through the area is: $$P = \dot{m} \times \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2} = \rho A v \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$ $$\boxed{P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \text{ watts}}$$ **Betz Limit Statement (0.5 mark):** The maximum fraction of available wind power that can be extracted by an ideal wind turbine is **16/27 ≈ 0.593** (i.e., **59.3%**), known as the **Betz limit**. **Proof (1.5 marks):** Define induction factor a where the velocity at the rotor plane is $v_R = v_\infty (1 - a)$. By conservation of momentum, the downstream velocity is $v_D = v_\infty (1 - 2a)$. The extracted power can be written as: $$P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_\infty^{3} \cdot 4 a (1 - a)^{2}$$ Power coefficient: $$C_p = 4 a (1-a)^{2}$$ Differentiating w.r.t. a and equating to zero: $$\tfrac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \implies a = \tfrac{1}{3}$$ Substituting: $$C_{p,max} = 4 \times \tfrac{1}{3} \times \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` **(a) Explain Angstrom's empirical formula for the estimation of mean daily global solar radiation. How are the constants a and b determined?** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** When measured solar radiation data is not available for a given location, empirical correlations based on routinely measured parameters (sunshine hours) are used. **Angstrom (1924)** proposed the first such correlation, later modified by **Priyadrarshi (1957)**, which relates global radiation to sunshine duration. **Formula (2 marks):** $$\frac{H}{H_o} = a + b \cdot \frac{S}{S_o}$$ where: - $H$ = monthly mean daily global radiation on a horizontal surface (MJ/m²/day) — **to be estimated**. - $H_o$ = monthly mean daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface (MJ/m²/day) — **computed from astronomy**. - $S$ = monthly mean daily actual sunshine hours (hours/day) — **measured by Campbell-Stokes**. - $S_o$ = maximum possible monthly mean daily sunshine hours = N/2, where N is day length (hours). - $a, b$ = empirical constants (dimensionless) — **determined for each location**. **Determination of H_o (2 marks):** $H_o$ is computed using: $$H_o = \tfrac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} I_{sc} E_o (\omega_s \sin\varphi \sin\delta + \cos\varphi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s)$$ with $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi \tan\delta)$ and $\delta = 23.45 \sin[360(284+n)/365]$. **Determination of constants a and b (2 marks):** - Step 1: Collect **multi-year measured data** of H and S for the given location. - Step 2: Compute $H_o$ and $S_o$ for each month. - Step 3: Plot $\tfrac{H}{H_o}$ versus $\tfrac{S}{S_o}$ as a **scatter plot**. - Step 4: Perform **linear regression** to find the best-fit line. - Step 5: **Intercept = a**, **Slope = b**. For most Indian stations, $a \approx 0.25$, $b \approx 0.50$. > [!NOTE] > **Note:** For KTU, mention that $a + b \le 1$ since $K_T \le 1$. Also mention that this formula is valid for **monthly** averages, not daily. --- **(b) Explain the Weibull distribution and its use in wind energy estimation. Define scale and shape parameters with typical values.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Need for a statistical distribution (1 mark):** Wind speed varies continuously with time. To compute **Annual Energy Output (AEO)** accurately, we cannot use only the mean wind speed. We need a **probability distribution** that describes how often each wind speed occurs. The **Weibull distribution** is the industry-standard two-parameter distribution that fits wind data remarkably well. **Weibull PDF (2 marks):** $$f(v) = \tfrac{k}{c} \left(\tfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\tfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right] \quad \text{for } v \ge 0$$ where: - **v** = wind speed (m/s) - **k** = shape parameter (dimensionless) — controls the **shape/symmetry** of the distribution - **c** = scale parameter (m/s) — controls the **magnitude/stretching** of the distribution **Shape parameter k (1.5 marks):** - k = 1 → exponential distribution - k = 2 → Rayleigh distribution (commonly assumed as a first approximation) - k = 3 to 4 → narrow distribution (very steady winds) - Typical range for India: **k = 1.5 to 2.5** **Scale parameter c (1.5 marks):** - c is **related to (but not equal to)** the mean wind speed. - Mean wind speed from Weibull: $\bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/k)$ - For k = 2 (Rayleigh): $\bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = c \cdot 0.886$ - Typical scale parameters in India: **c = 4 to 8 m/s**. **Use in AEO computation (1 mark):** The energy in each wind speed bin is weighted by the Weibull PDF, integrated across all speeds. Mean power output: $$\bar{P} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{3}{k}\right)$$ This is multiplied by **8760 hours/year** to get AEO in kWh/year. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Common Errors in Module 1:** > > 1. **Confusing Pyranometer with Pyrheliometer.** Pyranometer = global horizontal; Pyrheliometer = direct normal. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is reversed. > 2. **Using $\bar{v}^{3}$ instead of $\overline{v^{3}}$ in Weibull power calculations.** Always use $c^{3} \Gamma(1+3/k)$. > 3. **Forgetting the unit of wind power density.** Answer in **W/m²**, NOT W. > 4. **Not mentioning the solar tracker** when describing a pyrheliometer. It is a critical part of the instrument. > 5. **Missing the constant values** (ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s²) in numerical answers. Examiners expect them. > 6. **Drawing diagrams without labels.** Always label every block and arrow. > 7. **Leaving Angstrom's formula without explaining a and b.** Both must be defined as *empirical, location-specific regression coefficients*. > 8. **Stating "Betz limit = 0.59" without proof or units.** Always write **16/27 ≈ 0.593 or 59.3%**. > 9. **Mixing up clearness index ($K_T = H/H_o$) with diffuse fraction ($K_d = H_d/H$).** These are entirely different ratios. > 10. **Not mentioning IEC 61400-12 or WMO standards** when describing wind measurement. Examiners in KTU expect standards-based answers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Read this list 5 times before entering the exam hall. Each bullet represents a frequently-tested concept.** ### Core Definitions - ☐ **Pyranometer** = global solar radiation on horizontal surface (W/m²). - ☐ **Pyrheliometer** = direct normal irradiance (DNI), mounted on solar tracker. - ☐ **Sunshine recorder** = Campbell-Stokes glass-sphere type, gives sunshine hours. - ☐ **Anemometer** = cup-type for wind speed, propeller-type for speed + direction. - ☐ **Wind vane** = wind direction in degrees. - ☐ **Wind rose** = polar frequency plot of direction. - ☐ **Wind power density** = P/A = ½ ρ v³ (W/m²). - ☐ **Betz limit** = 16/27 ≈ 0.593 = 59.3% (maximum extractable). - ☐ **Clearness index $K_T$** = H/H_o (ratio of global to extraterrestrial). - ☐ **Diffuse fraction $K_d$** = H_d/H. ### Essential Formulas (Must memorize) - ☐ $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ - ☐ $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$ - ☐ $C_p = 4a(1-a)^{2}$, $C_{p,max} = 16/27$ - ☐ $v(z) = v_{ref} (z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$, with α = 1/7 typical - ☐ $H = H_o [a + b \cdot S/S_o]$ (Angstrom) - ☐ $\bar{v} = c \, \Gamma(1 + 1/k)$ (Weibull mean) - ☐ $\bar{P} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A c^{3} \Gamma(1 + 3/k)$ (Weibull mean power) - ☐ $P_{hydro} = \rho_w g Q H \eta$ - ☐ $\delta = 23.45 \sin[360(284+n)/365]$ (solar declination) - ☐ $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\varphi \tan\delta)$ (sunrise hour angle) ### Physical Constants (Always state) - ☐ ρ_air = **1.225 kg/m³** (sea level, 15°C) - ☐ ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³** - ☐ g = **9.81 m/s²** - ☐ I_sc = **1367 W/m²** (solar constant) - ☐ k = 1.5 to 3.0 (Weibull shape for India) - ☐ α = 1/7 ≈ 0.143 (wind shear coefficient, open terrain) ### Standards to Mention in Answers - ☐ **IEC 61400-12-1** — Wind resource measurement standards - ☐ **WMO** — World Meteorological Organization instrument siting - ☐ **ISO 9060** — Pyranometer classification - ☐ **MNRE** — India renewable energy ministry guidelines ### Tools & Techniques - ☐ Met mast height 80–120 m, sensors at multiple heights - ☐ 10-min averaging, 1 Hz sampling, 1-year minimum data - ☐ Validation per IEC, Weibull fit, wind rose, AEO estimation - ☐ Biomass: Quadrat sampling, oven drying (105°C, 24 h), bomb calorimeter - ☐ Small hydro: Stage-discharge curve, flow duration curve, Q at 40% exceedance ### Typical Numerical Values (India-Specific) - ☐ Solar GHI range: **4 to 7 kWh/m²/day** - ☐ Wind power density (good sites): **> 250 W/m² at 50 m** - ☐ Biomass plant capacity: **1 to 10 MW** - ☐ Small hydro: **up to 25 MW** (per Indian classification) - ☐ Tidal range (Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Cambay): **5 to 7 m** (high) --- > **END OF NOTES — MODULE 1: MEASUREMENT AND ESTIMATION TECHNIQUES** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — OECHT837 Non-Conventional Energy Systems*

topics.title: Solar Thermal Systems: Flat Plate Collectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Systems: Flat Plate Collectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flat Plate Collector (FPC):** A non-concentrating solar thermal energy collector that captures both **beam** and **diffuse** solar radiation, converts it into thermal energy, and transfers this heat to a working fluid (liquid or air) flowing through it. The absorber surface area is essentially equal to the aperture area receiving solar radiation, i.e., the **concentration ratio is approximately unity (C ≈ 1)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (OECHT837 – Non-Conventional Energy Systems), the Flat Plate Collector is classified as the most widely deployed **non-concentrating, stationary solar thermal collector** for low-to-medium temperature applications, typically operating in the range of **30 °C to 100 °C** above ambient temperature.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Aperture Area (A_a)** | The projected area through which solar radiation enters the collector |
| **Absorber Plate** | The dark, high-absorptivity surface that converts solar radiation to heat |
| **Glazing** | One or more transparent covers that trap infrared re-radiation |
| **Concentration Ratio (C)** | Ratio of aperture area to absorber area (FPC has C = 1) |
| **Working Fluid** | Water, air, oil, or antifreeze mixture that transports collected heat |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun-Powered Greenhouse"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a closed car parked under the afternoon sun.** Even with the windows (glazing) closed, the interior becomes unbearably hot. The sunlight enters through the glass, hits the dark seats and dashboard (absorber), and converts to heat. This heat tries to escape as long-wave infrared radiation, but the glass blocks it back. The car becomes a thermal trap.

A **Flat Plate Collector works on the exact same principle** — the **glazing** is the car window, the **absorber plate** is the dashboard, and the **fluid tubes** attached to the absorber carry away the trapped heat, just like an air-conditioner coil would cool your car.

## 1.3 Why Flat Plate Collectors Matter in Engineering

- They can utilize **both direct and diffuse radiation** — meaning they work even on cloudy days.
- They require **no tracking mechanism**, reducing mechanical cost and maintenance.
- They are the backbone of **domestic water heating, space heating, swimming pool heating, and solar industrial process heating** up to ~100 °C.
- They are the **default benchmark** against which all advanced collectors (evacuated tubes, parabolic troughs) are compared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC) used by KTU examiners and IS 12933:**
> Incident radiation: **G_T = 1000 W/m²**, Ambient temperature: **T_a = 30 °C**, Wind speed: **v = 2 m/s**, Collector tilt: equal to latitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step by Step

The operational sequence inside a Flat Plate Collector is a classic **five-stage energy conversion chain**:

1. **Solar Transmission (τ):** Sunlight passes through the transparent glass cover(s). The transmittance τ is typically **0.85 – 0.91** for a single low-iron glass cover of thickness 3–4 mm.
2. **Absorption (α):** The absorber plate, coated with a high-α surface (e.g., flat black paint α ≈ 0.95, or selective coating α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.10), captures the transmitted photons and converts them into thermal energy.
3. **Conduction Through Plate:** Heat is conducted along the absorber plate to the fluid-carrying tubes bonded to its underside.
4. **Convection to Fluid:** The working fluid (water, air, glycol) flowing inside the tubes absorbs the heat via forced or natural convection.
5. **Heat Losses:** Simultaneously, the absorber plate loses heat to surroundings by **top loss (conduction–convection–radiation), bottom loss (conduction through insulation), and edge loss (conduction through the frame)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **net useful heat gain** is therefore: **Q_u = Q_absorbed − Q_top − Q_bottom − Q_edge − Q_reflection**

## 2.2 The Heart of FPC Analysis — Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation** for any KTU board question on Flat Plate Collectors. It expresses the **instantaneous thermal efficiency** of an FPC.

The classical form (derived by Hottel, simplified by Whillier, and extended by Bliss) is:

$$\boxed{\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R \, U_L \, \dfrac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}}$$

Where:

- $\eta_i$ = instantaneous collector efficiency (dimensionless)
- $F_R$ = **heat removal factor** (dimensionless, always < 1)
- $(\tau \alpha)$ = effective transmittance–absorptance product
- $U_L$ = **overall heat loss coefficient**, in **W/(m²·K)**
- $T_{fi}$ = fluid inlet temperature, in **K** or **°C**
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature, in **K** or **°C**
- $G_T$ = total (beam + diffuse) solar radiation on the tilted collector, in **W/m²**

### Intercept and Slope Interpretation

When efficiency is plotted against $(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$ (a "reduced temperature" parameter), the result is a **straight line**:

$$\eta_i = \underbrace{F_R(\tau \alpha)}_{\text{Intercept}} - \underbrace{F_R U_L}_{\text{Slope}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **intercept** is the maximum theoretical efficiency (when fluid inlet = ambient = 0 reduced temperature). The **slope** represents how rapidly efficiency drops as fluid temperature rises above ambient.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU numerical problems on FPC revolve around the equations below. Memorize the units, conditions, and physical meaning of each symbol.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
| :- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \, G_T}$ | Useful heat / Incident energy | – | First-principle efficiency definition |
| 2 | $\eta_i = F_R(\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{(T_{fi}-T_a)}{G_T}$ | Hottel–Whillier–Bliss | – | Master efficiency equation |
| 3 | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | Top + Bottom + Edge loss coeff. | W/(m²·K) | System heat loss analysis |
| 4 | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$ | Useful heat extracted | W | Sizing of FPC arrays |
| 5 | $F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, C_p}{A_c \, U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A_c \, U_L \, F'}{\dot{m} \, C_p}\right) \right]$ | Heat removal factor | – | Effect of flow rate on η |
| 6 | $F' = \dfrac{1/U_L}{W \left[ \dfrac{1}{h_{f,i} (D_o - D_i) + (W-D_o) h_{r,p,c}} \right]}$ | Collector efficiency factor | – | Plate-to-fluid heat transfer |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{A_c}{\dot{m} \, C_p}$ | Slope parameter for plotting | m²·K/W | Used in test reports |
| 8 | $F_R \, U_L = -\dfrac{\text{slope of }\eta \text{ vs. }(T_{fi}-T_a)/G_T}{1}$ | From efficiency curve | W/(m²·K) | Experimental determination |
| 9 | $\tau \cdot \alpha = \dfrac{\text{Intercept}}{F_R}$ | Optical efficiency | – | Quality of glazing + absorber |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$ | Concentration ratio (FPC: C = 1) | – | Classifies collectors |

### Critical Physical Constants (Bolded as required by protocol)

- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant: σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Solar constant (AM 1.5): G_sc = 1367 W/m²**
- **Standard peak insolation: G_T = 1000 W/m²**
- **Specific heat of water: C_p = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Density of water: ρ = 1000 kg/m³**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation is the **design backbone** for:
- **Solar water heaters** (domestic, 100–300 LPD)
- **Solar air heaters** (crop drying, space heating)
- **Solar steam generation** for small-scale industry
- **Solar pool heating** systems

By experimentally plotting efficiency curves, manufacturers classify FPCs and assign a **"test report number"** (e.g., SRCC, IS 12933) that engineers use to size the collector area for a given thermal load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Heat Removal Factor F_R (Whillier's Approach)

The heat removal factor $F_R$ is **defined as the ratio of actual useful heat gain of the collector to the useful heat gain that would occur if the entire absorber plate were at the fluid inlet temperature**.

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Differential Strip of Absorber

Consider a differential strip of width $dx$ of the absorber plate. The useful heat transferred to the fluid is:

$$dq_u = W \, F' \left[ H - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right] dx$$

Where:
- $W$ = width of the strip
- $F'$ = collector efficiency factor (plate to fluid)
- $H$ = absorbed solar flux per unit aperture area = $G_T (\tau \alpha)$
- $T_{pm}$ = mean plate temperature

### Step 2: Integrate Over the Whole Collector Length L

For the entire absorber of length $L$ and width $W$:

$$Q_u = W \, F' \int_0^L \left[ H - U_L (T_{pm}(x) - T_a) \right] dx$$

### Step 3: Apply the Mean Plate Temperature

Using the mean temperature approximation $T_{pm} = (T_{fo} + T_{fi})/2$ along the flow direction, we obtain:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F' \left[ H - U_L \left(\dfrac{T_{fo} + T_{fi}}{2} - T_a\right) \right]$$

### Step 4: Eliminate Outlet Temperature Using Fluid Energy Balance

The fluid energy balance gives:

$$\dot{m} C_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi}) = Q_u$$

Substituting and solving algebraically for the ratio $(Q_u)/(H - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a))$ gives the famous compact form:

$$\boxed{F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} C_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left( -\dfrac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} C_p} \right) \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting cases:** As $\dot{m} \to \infty$ (infinite flow), $F_R \to F'$. As $\dot{m} \to 0$ (no flow), $F_R \to 0$. This makes physical sense.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Top Loss Coefficient $U_t$

The **top loss** is the most significant thermal loss in a flat plate collector and is a combination of convection, radiation, and conduction through the air gap between the absorber and the glass cover.

### Step 1: Set Up Heat Fluxes at the Absorber Surface

Heat is lost from the absorber surface to ambient through three parallel paths:
- **Convection from absorber to glass (h_c,a–g)**
- **Radiation from absorber to glass (h_r,a–g)**
- **Convection from glass to ambient (h_w)**
- **Radiation from glass to sky (h_r,g–sky)**

### Step 2: Series Resistance Network

Modeling as thermal resistances in series (absorber → glass → ambient), the top loss coefficient is:

$$\boxed{U_t = \left[ \dfrac{1}{h_{c,a-g} + h_{r,a-g}} + \dfrac{1}{h_w + h_r,g\text{-sky}} \right]^{-1}}$$

### Step 3: Substitution of Physical Correlations

For a **single-glazed** FPC, the convection between absorber and glass is given by:

$$h_{c,a-g} = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{L_c}$$

Where for **inclined parallel plates**, the Nusselt number correlation (Hollands correlation) is:

$$Nu = 1 + 1.44 \left[ 1 - \dfrac{1708}{Ra \cos \theta} \right]^+ \left[ 1 - \dfrac{1708 (\sin 1.8\theta)^{1.6}}{Ra \cos \theta} \right]^+ + \left[ \left(\dfrac{Ra \cos \theta}{5830}\right)^{1/3} - 1 \right]^+$$

Where $Ra$ is the Rayleigh number, $\theta$ is the tilt angle, and the superscript $[\,]^+$ means only the positive value is taken (otherwise zero).

### Step 4: Radiative Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$h_{r,a-g} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_{pm}^2 + T_g^2)(T_{pm} + T_g)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_p} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} - 1}$$

For a **selective absorber** ($\varepsilon_p \approx 0.1$) and a glass cover ($\varepsilon_g \approx 0.88$), the denominator becomes large, so $h_{r,a-g}$ becomes small — **this is why selective coatings are so effective in reducing top loss**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — From the Master Equation to the Efficiency Curve

Starting from the useful heat equation:

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

Divide both sides by the incident solar energy $A_c G_T$:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}$$

Let $T^* = \dfrac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}$ (the reduced temperature parameter, in K·m²/W).

Then:

$$\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \cdot T^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **equation of a straight line** with:
> - **y-intercept = $F_R (\tau \alpha)$** = the optical efficiency
> - **slope = $-F_R U_L$** (negative, representing loss rate)

The **x-intercept (where η = 0)** gives the **"stagnation temperature"** — the maximum fluid temperature the collector can reach, at which useful heat gain becomes zero.

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A flat plate collector has $F_R (\tau \alpha) = 0.75$ and $F_R U_L = 4.5$ W/(m²·K). At noon, $G_T = 900$ W/m², $T_{fi} = 35$ °C, and $T_a = 30$ °C. Find the instantaneous efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$\dfrac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T} = \dfrac{35 - 30}{900} = 0.00556 \text{ K·m}^2/\text{W}$$

$$\eta_i = 0.75 - (4.5)(0.00556) = 0.75 - 0.025 = 0.725$$

$$\boxed{\eta_i = 72.5\%}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Liquid-Heating FPC

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below shows a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of heat and energy through a Flat Plate Collector. This is the standard schematic expected in KTU board exams (Module 1, 14-mark questions).

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUN[Sunlight G_T 1000 W per m2] -->|Beam and Diffuse| GLAZE[Glazing Low Iron Glass tau = 0.90]
    GLAZE -->|Transmitted Photons| ABSORB[Absorber Plate Selective Coating alpha = 0.95 epsilon = 0.10]
    ABSORB -->|Absorbed Heat Q_abs| PLATE[Conduction Across Plate]
    PLATE -->|Conduction Through Bond| TUBE[Header and Riser Tubes Water or Glycol]
    TUBE -->|Convected Heat Q_u| FLUID[Working Fluid Hot Water Output]
    ABSORB -->|Q_top Convection and Radiation| GLAZE
    GLAZE -->|Q_top Convection and Radiation to Sky| AMBIENT[Ambient Air T_a and Sky T_sky]
    ABSORB -->|Q_bottom Conduction| INSUL[Back Insulation Glass Wool k = 0.04 W per m K]
    INSUL -->|Heat Rejected| AMBIENT
    ABSORB -->|Q_edge Conduction| FRAME[Aluminium Frame Edges]
    FRAME -->|Heat Rejected| AMBIENT
    FLUID -->|Useful Heat Gain Q_u| LOAD[Domestic Hot Water Tank or Industrial Process]
```

## 4.2 Component Block Diagram — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INCIDENT_RADIATION[Incident Solar Energy]
        IR1[Beam Radiation G_b]
        IR2[Diffuse Radiation G_d]
        IR3[Reflected Radiation G_r]
    end
    IR1 --> APERTURE[Aperture Area A_a m2]
    IR2 --> APERTURE
    IR3 --> APERTURE
    APERTURE --> GLAZING[Glazing Layer tau]
    GLAZING --> ABS[Absorber Plate alpha]
    ABS --> LOSS1[Top Loss U_t]
    ABS --> LOSS2[Bottom Loss U_b]
    ABS --> LOSS3[Edge Loss U_e]
    ABS --> TUBES[Tube Network with Fins]
    TUBES --> OUTLET[Hot Fluid Outlet T_fo]
    TUBES --> INLET[Cold Fluid Inlet T_fi]
    OUTLET --> STORAGE[Thermal Storage Tank]
    INLET --> PUMP[Recirculation Pump]
    PUMP --> TUBES
```

## 4.3 Efficiency Curve — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
graph TD
    AXIS[Plot Axis Definition] --> XAXIS[X Axis Reduced Temperature T_fi minus T_a divided by G_T in K m2 per W]
    AXIS --> YAXIS[Y Axis Instantaneous Efficiency eta_i in percent]
    YAXIS --> L1[Maximum Intercept F_R times tau alpha at Zero Reduced Temperature = Optical Efficiency]
    L1 --> L2[Linear Decline with Negative Slope minus F_R times U_L]
    L2 --> L3[X Intercept = Stagnation Condition eta_i = 0]
    L3 --> L4[Operating Point for Typical 60 degree C Hot Water at 900 W per m2]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Classification of Solar Collectors

```mermaid
graph TD
    COLL[Solar Thermal Collectors] --> NC[Non Concentrating C equals 1]
    COLL --> CONC[Concentrating C greater than 1]
    NC --> FPC[Flat Plate Collector 30 to 100 degree C]
    NC --> ETC[Evacuated Tube Collector 50 to 200 degree C]
    CONC --> LCOMP[Line Concentrators C equals 10 to 100]
    CONC --> PCOMP[Point Concentrators C equals 100 to 1000]
    LCOMP --> PT[Parabolic Trough]
    PCOMP --> PDISH[Parabolic Dish]
    PCOMP --> CTR[Central Receiver Tower]
    FPC --> LIQ[Liquid Heating FPC]
    FPC --> AIR[Air Heating FPC]
    FPC --> PISC[Swimming Pool Unglazed FPC]
```

## 4.5 Material & Component Mapping (KTU 2024 Exam Favorite)

```mermaid
graph TD
    GLAZE_M[Glazing Material Selection] --> M1[Low Iron Float Glass tau = 0.91 epsilon = 0.88]
    GLAZE_M --> M2[Tempered Glass tau = 0.90 for High Temperature]
    GLAZE_M --> M3[Polymer Film Tedlar or Teflon Lightweight]
    ABS_M[Absorber Material Selection] --> N1[Copper Best Conductivity k = 400 W per m K]
    ABS_M --> N2[Aluminium Lightweight k = 200 W per m K]
    ABS_M --> N3[Galvanised Iron Low Cost k = 50 W per m K]
    COAT_M[Absorber Coating Types] --> C1[Flat Black Paint alpha = 0.95 epsilon = 0.90]
    COAT_M --> C2[Black Nickel Selective alpha = 0.95 epsilon = 0.10]
    COAT_M --> C3[Black Chrome Selective alpha = 0.95 epsilon = 0.15]
    COAT_M --> C4[Blue Titanium Nitride Premium Selective]
    INS_M[Insulation Material] --> I1[Glass Wool k = 0.04 W per m K]
    INS_M --> I2[Rock Wool k = 0.045 W per m K]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always label the **direction of heat flow** with arrows, and **annotate every layer with its material property (τ, α, ε, k)**. This earns 2 extra marks in the diagram section.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a Flat Plate Collector. Why is its concentration ratio equal to unity?**

**Model Answer:**
A Flat Plate Collector (FPC) is a non-concentrating solar thermal device that absorbs both beam and diffuse solar radiation on a flat absorber surface, converting it to heat and transferring it to a working fluid.

The **concentration ratio** $C$ is defined as:

$$C = \dfrac{A_a}{A_r}$$

where $A_a$ is the aperture area and $A_r$ is the receiver (absorber) area. In an FPC, the absorber surface fully covers the aperture, so $A_a = A_r$, which gives $C = 1$. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between flat plate collector and concentrating collector.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Flat Plate Collector | Concentrating Collector |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Concentration ratio C | 1 | 10 to 1000 |
| Radiation type used | Beam + Diffuse | Beam only |
| Tracking | Not required | Required (1-axis or 2-axis) |
| Temperature range | 30–100 °C | 100–1000 °C |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Applications | Water heating, space heating | Power generation, industrial process heat |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Question]**

### ✦ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a flat plate collector. Derive the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation for the instantaneous efficiency of an FPC. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):** A flat plate collector consists of the following components:

1. **Glazing Cover(s):** One or two layers of low-iron tempered glass (τ ≈ 0.90) of 3–4 mm thickness, mounted on the top. They transmit short-wave solar radiation but block long-wave infrared re-radiation from the absorber (the greenhouse effect).
2. **Absorber Plate:** A blackened metal plate (copper, aluminum, or galvanized iron) coated with either flat black paint or a selective coating (e.g., black nickel, black chrome) to maximize solar absorptance (α ≈ 0.95).
3. **Fluid Tubes / Headers:** A network of riser tubes bonded to the underside of the absorber, connected to a header pipe at top and bottom. Water or air flows through them to carry the heat.
4. **Insulation:** Glass wool or rock wool (k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K) of 5–10 cm thickness placed at the back and sides to minimize conductive heat losses.
5. **Frame/Casing:** A weatherproof, structurally rigid aluminum or GI frame that holds the assembly together and protects against environmental degradation.

**Working Principle (3 marks):** Solar radiation (beam + diffuse) enters the collector through the glazing. Part of it is transmitted and reaches the absorber plate, which absorbs it and converts to thermal energy. The heated plate conducts heat to the fluid tubes. The fluid absorbs the heat and carries it to the storage tank or load. Heat losses occur simultaneously from the top (convection + radiation through glazing), bottom (conduction through insulation), and edges (conduction through the frame), reducing the useful heat gain.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** The useful heat gain is the difference between absorbed solar energy and total thermal losses.

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 2:** The mean plate temperature $T_{pm}$ is approximately the average of fluid inlet and outlet temperatures, modified by the heat removal factor $F_R$:

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Divide by incident solar energy $A_c G_T$ to get instantaneous efficiency:

$$\eta_i = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c G_T} = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_T}$$

**Step 4:** This is the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** — a linear relationship between $\eta_i$ and the reduced temperature parameter $\dfrac{T_{fi} - T_a}{G_T}$. The intercept $F_R(\tau \alpha)$ represents the maximum optical efficiency, and the slope $-F_R U_L$ indicates the rate of efficiency drop with temperature rise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write the equation without defining the symbols, losing 1–2 marks. Always state: $F_R$ = heat removal factor, $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient, $T_{fi}$ = fluid **inlet** temperature (NOT outlet), $G_T$ = total radiation on **tilted** surface.

---

### ✦ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Discuss the various heat loss mechanisms in a flat plate collector. Explain the role of selective surfaces in reducing top losses. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Heat Loss Mechanisms (7 marks)

An FPC loses heat to the surroundings through **three main paths**:

**1. Top Loss (Q_top) — Coefficient U_t:** This is the largest loss, occurring from the absorber plate to the glass cover (by convection and radiation) and then from the glass cover to the ambient/sky (by convection and radiation to sky). The combined top loss coefficient is:

$$U_t = \left[ \dfrac{1}{h_{c,a-g} + h_{r,a-g}} + \dfrac{1}{h_w + h_{r,g\text{-sky}}} \right]^{-1}$$

Typical $U_t$ values range from **2.5 to 5.0 W/(m²·K)** for a single-glazed FPC.

**2. Bottom Loss (Q_bottom) — Coefficient U_b:** Heat is conducted from the absorber plate through the insulation to the ambient behind the collector. For insulation of thickness $L_{ins}$ and thermal conductivity $k_{ins}$:

$$U_b = \dfrac{k_{ins}}{L_{ins}}$$

Typical $U_b$ values: **0.3 to 0.8 W/(m²·K)**.

**3. Edge Loss (Q_edge) — Coefficient U_e:** Heat is lost from the perimeter of the absorber through the frame. For most designs, $U_e$ is small (0.1 to 0.5 W/(m²·K)) and is often neglected if the insulation extends to the edges.

The **overall heat loss coefficient** is:

$$U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$$

Typical $U_L$ for a well-designed FPC: **3.0 to 6.0 W/(m²·K)**.

#### Part (b) — Selective Surfaces & Their Role (7 marks)

A **selective surface** is a special coating that has **high solar absorptance (α)** in the short-wavelength range (0.3–2.5 μm) and **low thermal emittance (ε)** in the long-wavelength range (2.5–50 μm).

**The Physics of Selectivity:**
- Solar radiation has its peak intensity around 0.5 μm (short-wave).
- Thermal re-radiation from a hot absorber plate (60–200 °C) peaks around 5–10 μm (long-wave).
- A selective surface exploits this difference: it absorbs the short-wave sunlight but emits very little long-wave heat.

**Common Selective Coatings:**

| Coating | α (Absorptance) | ε (Emittance) | α/ε Ratio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Flat black paint | 0.95 | 0.90 | 1.05 (Poor) |
| Black nickel | 0.95 | 0.10 | 9.5 (Excellent) |
| Black chrome | 0.95 | 0.15 | 6.3 (Very Good) |
| Blue titanium nitride | 0.92 | 0.07 | 13.1 (Premium) |

**Quantitative Impact on Top Loss:** The radiative component of the top loss between absorber and glass is:

$$h_{r,a-g} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_{pm}^2 + T_g^2)(T_{pm} + T_g)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_p} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_g} - 1}$$

For flat black paint: $\varepsilon_p = 0.90$, $\varepsilon_g = 0.88$ → denominator = $\tfrac{1}{0.90} + \tfrac{1}{0.88} - 1 = 1.248$, so $h_{r,a-g}$ is large.
For black nickel: $\varepsilon_p = 0.10$ → denominator = $\tfrac{1}{0.10} + \tfrac{1}{0.88} - 1 = 10.136$, so $h_{r,a-g}$ drops by **~8 times**, dramatically reducing top loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students confuse **absorptance α** with **emittance ε**. Remember: α is for short-wave (sunlight), ε is for long-wave (heat). A surface with α = ε (like flat black paint) is non-selective. A surface with α ≫ ε (like black nickel) is selective.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this list to your revision board the night before the KTU exam.**

### A. Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — non-concentrating, C = 1, stationary, uses beam + diffuse radiation.
- ✅ Concentration Ratio $C = A_a / A_r$ — for FPC, C = 1; for parabolic trough, C = 10–100; for dish, C = 100–1000.
- ✅ Heat Removal Factor $F_R$ — ratio of actual useful gain to gain if the entire plate were at $T_{fi}$.
- ✅ Collector Efficiency Factor $F'$ — ratio of actual gain to gain if the entire plate were at $T_{fm}$.
- ✅ Reduced Temperature Parameter $T^* = (T_{fi} - T_a) / G_T$ — universal x-axis for efficiency curves.
- ✅ Optical Efficiency $F_R(\tau \alpha)$ — y-intercept of efficiency curve.
- ✅ Stagnation Temperature — x-intercept of efficiency curve (η = 0).

### B. Equations to Memorize
- ✅ $\eta_i = F_R (\tau \alpha) - F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$ — Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (THE equation).
- ✅ $Q_u = A_c F_R [G_T (\tau \alpha) - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)]$ — useful heat.
- ✅ $F_R = (\dot m C_p / A_c U_L)[1 - \exp(-A_c U_L F' / \dot m C_p)]$ — heat removal factor.
- ✅ $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ — overall heat loss coefficient.
- ✅ $U_t = [(h_{c,a-g} + h_{r,a-g})^{-1} + (h_w + h_{r,g\text{-sky}})^{-1}]^{-1}$ — top loss.

### C. Numbers & Constants to Memorize
- ✅ σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)
- ✅ G_sc = 1367 W/m² (solar constant)
- ✅ STC: G_T = 1000 W/m², T_a = 30 °C, v = 2 m/s
- ✅ τ (low-iron glass) ≈ 0.85–0.91
- ✅ α (black paint) ≈ 0.95; α (selective) ≈ 0.95
- ✅ ε (black paint) ≈ 0.90; ε (selective) ≈ 0.10
- ✅ C_p (water) = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)
- ✅ k (glass wool) = 0.04 W/(m·K); k (rock wool) = 0.045 W/(m·K)

### D. Components of an FPC (5 layers)
1. ✅ Glazing (low-iron glass, 3–4 mm)
2. ✅ Absorber plate (Cu/Al/GI with selective coating)
3. ✅ Tubes + headers (water/air channels)
4. ✅ Back insulation (glass wool, 5–10 cm)
5. ✅ Frame/casing (aluminum, weatherproof)

### E. Common Student Mistakes (Read before submitting your paper)
- ❌ Using $T_{fo}$ (outlet) instead of $T_{fi}$ (inlet) in the HWB equation.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert °C to K when using σT⁴ radiation formula (numerical value is the same, but principle must be stated).
- ❌ Confusing τ and α — remember, **τ is glass property, α is plate property, and the product τα is the combined optical property**.
- ❌ Not labeling arrows in the diagram (loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions).
- ❌ Writing "selective surface = high α" without mentioning the **low ε** — examiners expect the dual property statement.

### F. Hot Exam-Ready Statements (Use verbatim for full marks)
- *"The FPC operates on the greenhouse effect, where short-wave solar radiation enters the glazing and is trapped as long-wave thermal radiation."*
- *"The Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation is a linear model in the reduced temperature parameter $(T_{fi} - T_a)/G_T$."*
- *"Selective coatings exploit the spectral mismatch between peak solar emission (~0.5 μm) and peak thermal re-emission (~10 μm)."*
- *"The concentration ratio C is the aperture-to-absorber area ratio; for an FPC, C = 1."*

---

> **End of Module 1 — Flat Plate Collectors | KTU 2024 Scheme | OECHT837**
