topics.title: Concentrating Collectors: Parabolic Trough, Dish, and Solar Tower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concentrating Collectors: Parabolic Trough, Dish, and Solar Tower

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Concentrating Solar Collector (CSC)** is a specialized solar thermal device that employs optical elements (reflectors or refractors) to concentrate direct beam solar radiation onto a small absorber area, thereby elevating the working fluid temperature significantly beyond what is achievable with flat plate collectors. The concentration ratio $C$ is defined as the ratio of the aperture area $A_a$ (the area intercepting solar radiation) to the absorber/receiver area $A_r$:

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Concentrating collectors utilize **only the direct (beam) component** of solar radiation. They cannot utilize diffuse radiation, which is why they are unsuitable for cloudy or overcast climates and require accurate sun-tracking mechanisms.

## 1.2 Classification of Concentrating Collectors

Concentrating collectors are broadly classified based on their focusing geometry:

| Type | Geometry | Tracking | Typical Concentration Ratio |
|------|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| **Line-focusing (2-D)** | Parabolic Trough, Linear Fresnel | Single-axis | 10 – 100 |
| **Point-focusing (3-D)** | Parabolic Dish, Solar Tower | Two-axis | 100 – 10,000 |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Magnifying Glass"

Imagine using a magnifying glass to focus sunlight onto a single point on a piece of paper — the paper ignites because all the light energy spread over the lens area is squeezed into a tiny spot. A concentrating collector works on the exact same principle:

- The **parabolic mirror** acts as the magnifying lens.
- The **receiver/absorber tube** is the "paper" where concentrated heat is absorbed.
- The **sun's position** must be tracked (just as you tilt the magnifying glass to keep the focused spot stationary) — this is why these systems have **sun-tracking systems**.

The deeper the curvature (shorter focal length), the higher the temperature achievable, but the smaller the area of sunlight it can capture at any instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams**
> - **Flat plate collector** → absorbs both **beam + diffuse** radiation → low temperature (up to ~100 °C) → **no tracking required**.
> - **Concentrating collector** → absorbs **only beam** radiation → high temperature (200 °C – 2000 °C) → **tracking mandatory**.

## 1.4 Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio

The maximum achievable concentration ratio is governed by the angular size of the sun ($\theta_s \approx 0.265° \approx 4.65 \times 10^{-3}$ rad):

$$C_{max,\,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_s} \approx 215$$

$$C_{max,\,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s} \approx 46{,}200$$

These values are **theoretical upper limits**; practical systems achieve much lower values due to optical imperfections, tracking errors, and geometric aberrations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Principle of Concentration

The fundamental physics of concentration is based on **conservation of etendue (optical invariant)**. A perfect optical system cannot increase the angular spread of radiation beyond the source (the sun). Hence, to compress a large angular input into a small angular output, the area must shrink proportionally.

### 2.1.1 Why Tracking is Mandatory

If a non-tracking concentrator is used, the image of the sun shifts on the receiver as the sun moves, causing the concentrated spot to spread and dilute. A tolerance of approximately $\pm 0.5°$ to $\pm 1°$ is generally permitted, beyond which concentration efficiency drops sharply.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Concentrating System

1. **Sun's direct beam radiation** $I_b$ falls on the **reflector aperture** of area $A_a$.
2. The **reflector** (parabolic mirror) redirects all incident rays toward its **focal point/focal line**.
3. A **receiver/absorber** placed at the focal region intercepts the concentrated flux.
4. The **heat transfer fluid (HTF)** flowing through the receiver absorbs the concentrated thermal energy.
5. The heated fluid is used either directly (process heat) or to generate **superheated steam** for a Rankine/Brayton/Stirling cycle.
6. The **tracking mechanism** rotates the collector continuously to keep the sun's image on the receiver.

## 2.3 Loss Mechanisms in Concentrating Collectors

| Loss Component | Cause | Typical Magnitude |
|----------------|-------|-------------------|
| **Geometric / Optical** | Mirror reflectivity $\rho$ (typically 0.85 – 0.95) | 5–15% |
| **Cosine loss** | Off-normal incidence angle | 1–3% |
| **Tracking error** | Imperfect sun-tracking | 1–3% |
| **Blocking & Shadowing** | Receiver/support obstructs incoming rays | 2–5% |
| **Receiver thermal losses** | Convection + Radiation from hot absorber | 5–30% |
| **Interception factor** | Some rays miss the receiver | 1–5% |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they form the core of Module 1 numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = A_a / A_r$ | Geometric concentration ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{max,2D} = 1 / \sin(\theta_s)$ | Theoretical max (line focus) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_{max,3D} = 1 / \sin^2(\theta_s)$ | Theoretical max (point focus) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot K$ | Optical efficiency product | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{thermal} = \eta_{opt} - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{I_b A_a}$ | Instantaneous thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q_u = I_b A_a \eta_{opt} - A_r U_L (T_r - T_a)$ | Useful heat gain equation | W |
| 7 | $Q_{loss} = A_r \left[ h_c (T_r - T_a) + \varepsilon \sigma (T_r^4 - T_{sky}^4) \right]$ | Receiver heat loss | W |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{r}{4 f_d}$ for trough; $f = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2}{4 z}$ for dish | Parabola equation relating focal length & rim angle | m |
| 9 | $\eta_{receiver} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{absorbed}}$ | Receiver thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{avg} = \dfrac{C_{max}}{\cos \theta_i}$ (for off-axis) | Effective concentration | dimensionless |

**Symbol Legend:**
- $\rho$ = mirror reflectivity, $\tau$ = glass cover transmissivity, $\alpha$ = absorber absorptivity
- $\gamma$ = intercept factor, $K$ = cleanliness factor
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $\theta_s$ = half angular width of sun (≈ 4.65 mrad)
- $\theta_i$ = angle of incidence on aperture
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = **Stefan–Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Concentrating Collectors

- **Industrial process heat** (food, textile, chemical industries)
- **Solar thermal power plants** (SEGS, Ivanpah, Gemasolar)
- **Desalination** (Multi-Effect Distillation coupled with PTC)
- **Hydrogen production** via high-temperature thermochemical cycles
- **Solar fuels** — solar-to-chemical energy conversion at temperatures > 1000 °C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Connection**
> India's first commercial Concentrated Solar Power plant — the **62.5 MW capacity Gujarat Solar One (Reliance Power)** at Dhanera, Rajasthan — uses **parabolic trough** technology. The **Gemasolar plant in Spain** uses a **solar tower with molten salt storage** and supplied electricity 24 hours a day for 36 consecutive days in 2013.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Concentration Ratio for a 2-D (Line-Focus) Collector

**Conceptual Setup:** Consider a parabolic trough of aperture width $W$ and a linear receiver tube of diameter $d$ placed along its focal line. The sun subtends a half-angle $\theta_s$ at the Earth.

**Step 1 — Area relationships:**
For a line-focusing collector, the concentration is purely two-dimensional. The aperture "width" intercepting sunlight is $W$, while the receiver width is $d$.

**Step 2 — Angular invariance principle:**
The radiation entering the aperture has an angular spread of $\pm \theta_s$ (cone). For a perfect concentrator, all of this radiation must exit through the receiver, but the exit solid angle is limited by total internal reflection / acceptance angle $\theta_a$ of the receiver:

$$W \cdot \sin(\theta_s) = d \cdot \sin(\theta_a)$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging for concentration ratio:**

$$C = \frac{W}{d} = \frac{\sin(\theta_a)}{\sin(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum condition:**
The maximum concentration occurs when the acceptance angle is at its absolute physical limit of $\theta_a = 90°$ (i.e., $\sin \theta_a = 1$), because the receiver cannot accept radiation beyond the half-space.

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin(0.265°)} = \frac{1}{0.004625} \approx 215$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A line-focusing collector can concentrate beam radiation at most by a factor of ~215. Beyond this, the receiver would have to "see" a region larger than the entire sky hemisphere, which is physically impossible.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Concentration Ratio for a 3-D (Point-Focus) Collector

**Step 1 — Apply the same etendue principle in 2D (aperture plane).**
For a point-focus collector, both the aperture and the receiver are 2-D areas. The concentration is the ratio of areas.

**Step 2 — Etendue conservation in two dimensions:**

$$A_a \cdot \sin^2(\theta_s) = A_r \cdot \sin^2(\theta_a)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for concentration ratio:**

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{\sin^2(\theta_a)}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply maximum acceptance limit** $\theta_a = 90°$:

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$C_{max,3D} = \frac{1}{(0.004625)^2} = \frac{1}{2.139 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 46{,}747$$

This is the absolute theoretical upper limit; practical dish/tower systems achieve $C \approx 500$ – $3000$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Useful Heat Gain of a Parabolic Trough Collector

**Starting equation (energy balance on the receiver):**

$$Q_u = Q_{absorbed} - Q_{loss}$$

**Step 1 — Absorbed solar energy:**

$$Q_{absorbed} = I_b \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt}$$

where $\eta_{opt} = (\rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha) \cdot \gamma$ combines mirror reflectivity, glass transmissivity, absorber absorptivity, and intercept factor.

**Step 2 — Heat loss from the receiver (simplified linear model):**

$$Q_{loss} = A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A_a = C \cdot A_r$:**

$$Q_u = I_b \cdot C \cdot A_r \cdot \eta_{opt} - A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a)$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $I_b A_a$ to get efficiency:**

$$\eta_{thermal} = \eta_{opt} - \frac{U_L (T_r - T_a)}{C \cdot I_b}$$

**Step 5 — Key insight:** For a **fixed fluid temperature**, increasing $C$ improves efficiency because heat loss is normalized over a larger aperture area. This is the fundamental thermodynamic reason for **high-concentration systems**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Three Concentrating Collectors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative table is one of the most frequently asked KTU questions in Module 1.**

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Focus type** | Line focus (2-D) | Point focus (3-D) | Point focus (3-D) |
| **Receiver** | Linear tube | Cavity at focal point | Central tower-top receiver |
| **Tracking** | Single-axis (N-S or E-W) | Two-axis (azimuth + altitude) | Field of heliostats (two-axis each) |
| **Concentration ratio** | 10 – 100 | 500 – 3000 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Working temperature** | 150 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1000 °C |
| **Heat engine cycle** | Rankine (steam) | Stirling / Brayton | Rankine / Brayton |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 30 – 40% (collector); 15–25% (system) | 30 – 40% (system) | 30 – 35% (system) |
| **Capacity range** | 1 – 100 MW | 5 – 50 kW (modular) | 1 – 100 MW |
| **Thermal storage** | Possible (oil/salt) | Difficult | Easy (molten salt) |
| **Land use** | Moderate | High per kW | High (heliostat field) |
| **Representative plants** | SEGS (California), Gujarat Solar One | SES Stirling dishes (Arizona) | Ivanpah (392 MW), Gemasolar |
| **Main disadvantage** | Receivers lose 30%+ as wind/IR losses | Modular, no easy storage | High heliostat cost |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Module 1, 14-mark standard):**
> A parabolic trough collector has an aperture width of 3.5 m, length 50 m, mirror reflectivity 0.88, glass-tube transmissivity 0.96, absorber absorptivity 0.94, and intercept factor 0.95. The beam radiation is 850 W/m², ambient temperature 30 °C, fluid temperature 250 °C, and overall heat loss coefficient 8 W/m²K. Calculate: (a) Optical efficiency, (b) Useful heat gain, (c) Thermal efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(a) Optical efficiency:**

$$\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma = 0.88 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.95$$

$$\eta_{opt} = 0.7545 \approx 75.45\%$$

**(b) Useful heat gain:**

$$A_a = W \times L = 3.5 \times 50 = 175 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{absorbed} = I_b \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt} = 850 \times 175 \times 0.7545$$

$$Q_{absorbed} = 112{,}232 \text{ W} \approx 112.2 \text{ kW}$$

For receiver area (assuming receiver tube diameter d = 0.05 m):

$$A_r = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 50 = 7.854 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{loss} = A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a) = 7.854 \times 8 \times (250 - 30)$$

$$Q_{loss} = 7.854 \times 8 \times 220 = 13{,}823 \text{ W} \approx 13.82 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_u = 112.2 - 13.82 = 98.4 \text{ kW}$$

**(c) Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{thermal} = \frac{Q_u}{I_b A_a} = \frac{98{,}400}{850 \times 175} = 0.6614 \approx 66.1\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Concentrating Solar Power (CSP) Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sun Beam Radiation] --> B[Mirror Reflector Array]
    B --> C[Concentrated Solar Flux]
    C --> D[Receiver at Focal Point]
    D --> E[Heat Transfer Fluid HTF]
    E --> F[Thermal Energy Storage TES]
    F --> G[Heat Exchanger Steam Generator]
    G --> H[Turbine Rankine Brayton]
    H --> I[Electrical Generator]
    I --> J[Power Grid Output]
    D --> K[Heat Losses Convection Radiation]
    B --> L[Solar Tracker Control]
    L --> B
    E -.Cold HTF return.-> B
```

## 4.2 Parabolic Trough — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun1[Direct Solar Beam] --> Ref1[Parabolic Reflector Curved Mirror]
    Ref1 --> Tube1[Receiver Tube at Focal Line]
    Tube1 --> Fluid1[Heat Transfer Oil Mineral Molten Salt]
    Fluid1 --> HX1[Heat Exchanger]
    HX1 --> Steam1[Superheated Steam]
    Steam1 --> Turbine1[Steam Turbine]
    Turbine1 --> Gen1[Generator]
    Gen1 --> Grid1[Electricity to Grid]
    Tube1 --> Loss1[Thermal Losses Glass Envelope Vacuum]
    Tracker1[Single Axis Tracking Motor] -.Rotates.-> Ref1
```

## 4.3 Parabolic Dish — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun2[Direct Solar Beam] --> Dish2[Parabolic Dish Reflector]
    Dish2 --> Cavity2[Cavity Receiver at Focal Point]
    Cavity2 --> He2[He or H2 Working Gas]
    He2 --> Stirling2[Stirling Engine External Combustion]
    Stirling2 --> Gen2[Generator]
    Gen2 --> Output2[Electricity 5 to 50 kW]
    Cavity2 --> RadLoss2[Radiation Convection Losses 500 to 1500 C]
    Track2[Two Axis Azimuth Altitude Tracker] -.Orients.-> Dish2
```

## 4.4 Solar Tower — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Sun3[Direct Solar Beam] --> Helio[Heliostat Field Hundreds of Mirrors]
    Helio --> Tower3[Tower Top Central Receiver]
    Tower3 --> Salt3[Hot Molten Salt up to 565 C]
    Salt3 --> Storage3[Hot Salt Storage Tank]
    Storage3 --> SteamGen3[Steam Generator Heat Exchanger]
    SteamGen3 --> Turbine3[Steam Turbine]
    Turbine3 --> Gen3[Generator]
    Gen3 --> Grid3[Electricity to Grid]
    Salt3 -.Cold Salt Return Loop.-> Tower3
    Control3[Master Heliostat Controller] -.Aims.-> Helio
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Tracking & Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line Focus Trough] -->|Single Axis Rotation| A1[Focal Line Receiver]
    B[Point Focus Dish] -->|Two Axis Rotation| B1[Focal Point Cavity]
    C[Point Focus Tower] -->|Heliostat Field Two Axis| C1[Tower Top Receiver]
    A1 --> P1[Concentration C 10 to 100]
    B1 --> P2[Concentration C 500 to 3000]
    C1 --> P3[Concentration C 300 to 1500]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "concentration ratio" for a solar concentrating collector. What is the maximum theoretical concentration ratio for a 2-D (line-focusing) and a 3-D (point-focusing) collector, given the half-angular width of the sun $\theta_s = 0.265°$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **concentration ratio (C)** is defined as the ratio of the effective aperture area of the collector to the absorber/receiver area:

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates the degree to which the solar flux is intensified at the receiver. The maximum theoretical concentration ratio, derived from the principle of conservation of etendue, is:

- **For 2-D (line-focus) collector:**
$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)} = \frac{1}{\sin(0.265°)} = \frac{1}{0.004625} \approx 215$$

- **For 3-D (point-focus) collector:**
$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)} = \frac{1}{(0.004625)^2} \approx 46{,}200$$

These values represent ideal optical limits; practical systems achieve much lower ratios due to optical aberrations, tracking inaccuracies, and receiver geometry.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare parabolic trough, parabolic dish, and solar tower systems on the basis of: (i) type of focusing, (ii) tracking mechanism, (iii) achievable concentration ratio, and (iv) typical working temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Solar Tower |
|---------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| Focusing | Line focus (2-D) | Point focus (3-D) | Point focus (3-D) |
| Tracking | Single-axis | Two-axis | Heliostat field, two-axis each |
| Concentration ratio | 10 – 100 | 500 – 3000 | 300 – 1500 |
| Working temperature | 150 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C | 500 – 1000 °C |

The trough uses a linear receiver tube; the dish uses a cavity receiver at the focal point; the tower uses hundreds of sun-tracking mirrors (heliostats) that reflect sunlight onto a single receiver atop a tall tower.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a **parabolic trough concentrating collector**. Discuss the optical principle that limits its maximum concentration ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic trough collector has an aperture of 2.5 m, length 40 m, and receiver tube diameter of 0.05 m. The beam radiation is 900 W/m², optical efficiency is 75%, ambient temperature is 30 °C, and the fluid temperature is 220 °C. The overall heat loss coefficient is 7 W/m²K. Calculate the **useful heat gain** and the **thermal efficiency** of the system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Construction:**
A parabolic trough consists of a long, curved reflector (parabolic in cross-section) that focuses parallel sun rays onto a linear focal line. A blackened absorber tube (typically copper or steel) is placed along this focal line and is enclosed in a glass envelope to reduce convective losses. The trough is supported on a single-axis tracking structure (rotating about either a N-S or E-W axis) that follows the sun throughout the day.

**Working principle:**
The parabolic cross-section has the geometric property that **all rays parallel to its axis reflect to a single focal line**. Sunlight striking the curved mirror is therefore concentrated onto the receiver tube, where a heat transfer fluid (synthetic oil, water, or molten salt) absorbs the heat. The heated fluid is pumped to a heat exchanger, where it generates steam for power generation.

**Limitation on maximum concentration ratio:**
By the etendue conservation principle, for a 2-D (line) focus, the maximum concentration ratio is:

$$C_{max,2D} = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)} = \frac{1}{\sin(0.265°)} \approx 215$$

This limit is a **fundamental geometric-optical limit** and cannot be exceeded by any line-focusing collector, regardless of mirror quality.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- Aperture width $W = 2.5$ m, Length $L = 40$ m → $A_a = 100$ m²
- Receiver tube diameter $d = 0.05$ m
- Beam radiation $I_b = 900$ W/m²
- Optical efficiency $\eta_{opt} = 0.75$
- Ambient $T_a = 30°C$, fluid $T_f = 220°C$ (≈ receiver $T_r$)
- $U_L = 7$ W/m²·K

**Step 1 — Receiver area:**

$$A_r = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 40 = 6.283 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Absorbed heat:**

$$Q_{absorbed} = I_b \cdot A_a \cdot \eta_{opt} = 900 \times 100 \times 0.75 = 67{,}500 \text{ W} = 67.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Heat loss:**

$$Q_{loss} = A_r \cdot U_L \cdot (T_r - T_a) = 6.283 \times 7 \times (220 - 30)$$

$$Q_{loss} = 6.283 \times 7 \times 190 = 8{,}357 \text{ W} \approx 8.36 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Useful heat gain:**

$$Q_u = 67.5 - 8.36 = 59.14 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_u}{I_b \cdot A_a} = \frac{59{,}140}{900 \times 100} = 0.6571 \approx 65.71\%$$

---

### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Describe the construction, working, and advantages of a **solar tower (central receiver) power plant**. Explain why it is preferred for large-scale power generation with thermal storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic dish has a diameter of 10 m and a cavity receiver of diameter 0.3 m. The solar beam radiation is 950 W/m², mirror reflectivity is 0.92, absorptivity of the receiver surface is 0.95, and intercept factor is 0.90. Calculate: (i) the **geometric concentration ratio**, (ii) the **fraction of incident solar power absorbed by the receiver**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**Construction:**
A solar tower power plant consists of three main components:
1. **Heliostat field:** hundreds to thousands of large, flat, two-axis tracked mirrors (heliostats) distributed over several square kilometers.
2. **Central receiver:** mounted on top of a tall tower (50 – 200 m high), it absorbs the concentrated radiation.
3. **Power block:** heat exchanger, thermal storage, turbine, and generator.

**Working principle:**
Each heliostat independently tracks the sun and reflects its beam toward the top of the tower. The combined image of all heliostats is focused on the central receiver, which may be a cavity-type or external-type. A working fluid (molten salt, water/steam, or air) circulates through the receiver, absorbs the heat, and transports it for power generation.

**Advantages for large-scale power with storage:**
- **High concentration ratio** (300 – 1500) → higher temperatures → higher Carnot efficiency.
- **Easy integration of thermal storage** (e.g., molten salt tanks) → enables **24-hour power generation**, a unique advantage over PV.
- **Single, large receiver** simplifies maintenance compared to thousands of small receiver tubes.
- **Scalability** — power output scales with heliostat field area, not mirror count.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- Dish diameter $D = 10$ m → Aperture area $A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = 78.54$ m²
- Receiver diameter $d = 0.3$ m → Receiver area $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = 0.0707$ m²
- Beam radiation $I_b = 950$ W/m²
- $\rho = 0.92$, $\alpha = 0.95$, $\gamma = 0.90$

**(i) Geometric concentration ratio:**

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{78.54}{0.0707} = 1111$$

**(ii) Absorbed solar power:**

$$Q_{incident} = I_b \cdot A_a = 950 \times 78.54 = 74{,}613 \text{ W} = 74.61 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{opt} = \rho \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma = 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.7866$$

$$Q_{absorbed} = Q_{incident} \times \eta_{opt} = 74{,}613 \times 0.7866 = 58{,}696 \text{ W} \approx 58.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Fraction of incident solar power absorbed:**

$$f_{abs} = \frac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{incident}} = \eta_{opt} = 0.7866 \approx 78.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing aperture area with mirror area:** The aperture area is the projected area perpendicular to the sun's rays, not the actual curved mirror area. For a low-roughness dish, both are nearly equal; but for deep-dish designs, the mirror area is larger.
> 2. **Mixing up 2-D and 3-D concentration limits:** Students often quote $C_{max,2D} = 215$ for a dish (which is 3-D) and lose marks. Always use $\sin^2(\theta_s)$ for point-focus systems.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply optical factors:** A frequent error is computing $\eta_{opt} = \rho \alpha$ and forgetting $\gamma$ (intercept factor). The full product is $\rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot K$.
> 4. **Using total solar radiation (I_T) instead of beam radiation (I_b):** Concentrators **only use the direct beam component**. Using $I_T$ will give physically impossible answers and full marks will be cut.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency in $U_L$ calculation:** Ensure $U_L$ is in W/m²·K (not W/m·K), as it is multiplied by area, not length.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**

- ✅ Concentration ratio $C = A_a / A_r$ is **dimensionless** and a **key performance indicator** of all concentrating collectors.
- ✅ **Line-focus** collectors (parabolic trough) have $C_{max} \approx 215$; **point-focus** (dish, tower) have $C_{max} \approx 46{,}200$.
- ✅ Concentrators use **only the direct (beam) component** of solar radiation — not diffuse — so they require **clear-sky conditions** and accurate **sun tracking**.
- ✅ **Parabolic trough** → single-axis tracking → linear receiver tube → 150–400 °C → best for industrial process heat and steam generation.
- ✅ **Parabolic dish** → two-axis tracking → cavity receiver at focal point → 500–1500 °C → paired with **Stirling or Brayton** engine for modular 5–50 kW power.
- ✅ **Solar tower** → heliostat field (two-axis each) → central tower-top receiver → 500–1000 °C → best suited for **large-scale MW power** with **molten salt thermal storage**.
- ✅ Optical efficiency $\eta_{opt} = \rho \tau \alpha \gamma K$ — all factors are **< 1**, hence $\eta_{opt} < 1$.
- ✅ Heat loss from the receiver scales with $A_r$, absorbed heat scales with $A_a$ — **higher $C$ is always better** for thermal efficiency at a fixed fluid temperature.
- ✅ Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴** must be used for all radiation heat loss calculations.
- ✅ The sun's half-angle $\theta_s = 0.265° = 4.65 \times 10^{-3}$ rad — memorize this number!
- ✅ Solar tower plants with thermal storage (e.g., Gemasolar) can produce electricity **24/7** — a major advantage over photovoltaic systems.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems, the sequence is always: compute $\eta_{opt}$ → compute $Q_{absorbed}$ → compute $Q_{loss}$ → compute $Q_u$ → compute $\eta_{thermal}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Solar Water Heaters, Solar Dryers, Solar Ponds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications: Solar Water Heaters, Solar Dryers, Solar Ponds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Solar Water Heaters (SWH)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A Solar Water Heater is a device that converts incident solar radiation into useful thermal energy to raise the temperature of water for domestic, commercial, or industrial use. It typically consists of a **solar collector** (which absorbs solar energy and transfers it to a working fluid) coupled with a **storage tank** (which retains the heated fluid for use during periods of low or no solar radiation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SWH is one of the most commercially mature solar thermal technologies worldwide. The two principal collector designs assessed in this module are the **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** and the **Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a parked black car on a sunny afternoon. The metal body absorbs sunlight and the cabin becomes very hot — this is the **greenhouse / absorber effect**. A Solar Water Heater works on the exact same principle, but in a controlled, engineered manner. Sunlight passes through a transparent cover (like a car window), gets trapped and absorbed by a blackened metal plate (like the car body), and the resulting heat is transferred to water flowing through pipes attached to that plate. A well-insulated tank then stores this hot water for use at night or on cloudy days, just like a thermos flask keeps tea hot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The thermal performance of an SWH is universally evaluated using its **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation**, the **collector efficiency factor** $F_R$, and the **overall heat loss coefficient** $U_L$. A typical domestic FPC system delivers water at **60 °C to 80 °C**.

### 1.2 Solar Dryers

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A Solar Dryer is an enclosed structure that uses solar radiation to remove moisture from agricultural produce, food grains, fish, wood, or other materials by heating the air inside the enclosure and passing it over the product. Unlike open sun-drying, a solar dryer protects the product from rain, dust, insects, and birds, while significantly accelerating the drying rate through controlled temperature and airflow.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine drying clothes on a clothesline. They dry, but slowly, and if it suddenly rains, they get soaked again. Now imagine placing those clothes inside a small transparent tent with a small fan blowing warm air through it. The drying is much faster and protected. That is exactly what a solar dryer is — a **"sun-trapped tent"** where the product sits on perforated trays while hot, moisture-laden air is vented out. A cabinet dryer is like a small wardrobe with mesh shelves, while a tunnel dryer is like a long greenhouse tunnel with trolleys of produce rolling through it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Drying efficiency is expressed in terms of **moisture content (% wet basis or dry basis)**, **drying rate (kg water removed / hr)**, and the **pickup efficiency** — the fraction of solar energy actually used to evaporate moisture. Typical pickup efficiency values range from **25 % to 45 %**.

### 1.3 Solar Ponds

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A Solar Pond is a large, shallow body of saline water in which a **vertical salt concentration gradient** is artificially established. This gradient suppresses natural convection (warmer, less saline water rising to the top), enabling the lower layers to reach very high temperatures (**70 °C to 90 °C**) suitable for low-grade thermal applications such as space heating, desalination, and electricity generation via an Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Normally in a lake, sunlight warms only the top few centimetres because warm water rises to the surface (it is less dense) and cold water sinks — this is **natural convection**. A solar pond is like a **"stratified cocktail"** that refuses to mix. The salty bottom layer is very dense; the fresh top layer is less dense. So when the sun heats the bottom, the salty water **cannot** rise — it stays put and keeps getting hotter, just like how a weighted blanket traps body heat. Engineers then pump out this hot brine and run it through a heat exchanger to produce hot water or generate electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants:**
> - **Solar constant (Gsc):** $1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant ($\sigma$):** $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Typical salinity gradient (top to bottom):** 3 % → 25 % (by weight of NaCl)
> - **Bottom layer temperature (steady state):** **up to 100 °C** in large ponds

---

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot for Quick Reference

| Application | Primary Energy Output | Typical Operating Range | Main Energy Conversion |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Solar Water Heater (FPC) | Hot water | 60 – 80 °C | Solar → Thermal (single fluid) |
| Solar Water Heater (ETC) | Hot water | 80 – 100 °C | Solar → Thermal (evacuated) |
| Solar Dryer | Dehydrated product | 40 – 70 °C air | Solar → Thermal + Mass transfer |
| Solar Pond | Hot brine / power | 70 – 100 °C | Solar → Thermal (stored) |

> [!NOTE]
> All three systems fundamentally exploit the same physical chain: **incident solar radiation $\rightarrow$ absorption by a dark surface $\rightarrow$ conversion to thermal energy $\rightarrow$ transfer to a working medium (water, air, or brine).**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Solar Water Heater — Operating Principle and Thermal Model

A typical **thermosyphon-type Flat Plate Collector (FPC)** consists of the following layers stacked from top to bottom:

1. **Transparent glass cover (glazing):** Single or double pane; admits shortwave solar radiation and traps longwave re-radiation, behaving as a **blackbody cavity effect** (greenhouse effect).
2. **Air gap:** 2.5 – 5 cm thick; provides additional resistance to convection and conduction losses.
3. **Absorber plate:** Blackened (selective-coated) copper or aluminium sheet, with water tubes soldered/bonded to it.
4. **Insulation:** Rock wool / glass wool, 5 – 10 cm thick at the back and sides, to suppress bottom and edge losses.
5. **Casing:** Aluminium or galvanised iron enclosure.

**Operational sequence (Why and How):**

- **Why does water circulate without a pump in a thermosyphon?** Because hot water becomes less dense and rises. The heated water in the collector tubes rises to the top of the storage tank; cooler water from the bottom of the tank descends into the collector. This creates a natural closed loop called a **thermosyphon**.
- **How is heat transferred from sun to water?** Through three sequential resistances: (a) solar radiation penetrates the glazing; (b) absorbed by the selective surface and conducted through the absorber plate to the tubes; (c) convected from the tube inner wall to the water.

**The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (HWB) Equation — Cornerstone of KTU Problems:**

The instantaneous useful heat gain per unit collector area is given by:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

where:
- $A_c$ = effective collector aperture area (m²)
- $F_R$ = heat removal factor (dimensionless, typically 0.7 – 0.9 for FPC)
- $S$ = absorbed radiation per unit area = $I_T(\tau\alpha)$ (W/m²)
- $I_T$ = total (beam + diffuse) solar radiation on tilted surface
- $(\tau\alpha)$ = transmittance–absorptance product
- $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²K)
- $T_{in}$ = fluid temperature at collector inlet (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature (°C or K)

**Collector thermal efficiency** is defined as:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

This is a straight line when $\eta_c$ is plotted against $\frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$, with slope $-F_R U_L$ and intercept $F_R (\tau\alpha)$.

### 2.2 Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)

Each tube is a double-walled borosilicate glass tube with a vacuum between the two walls ($\approx 10^{-4}$ Pa). The vacuum eliminates convective and conductive losses, allowing **operating temperatures up to 100 – 150 °C** without significant loss in efficiency. Heat is transferred to water typically via a **heat pipe** (containing a small amount of alcohol) inserted into each tube.

### 2.3 Solar Dryer — Theory of Operation

**The drying process** has three distinct phases, and the KTU examiner expects you to know them:

1. **Initial adjustment period:** Surface moisture evaporates, product temperature approaches wet-bulb temperature.
2. **Constant rate period (CRP):** Internal moisture migrates to the surface by capillary action as fast as it evaporates. Drying rate is governed by **external conditions** (air velocity, temperature, humidity) — *not* by product properties.
3. **Falling rate period (FRP):** Surface moisture is gone; evaporation now occurs *inside* the product. Drying rate declines steadily and is governed by **internal moisture diffusion**.

**Moisture content** is defined two ways (both are examinable):

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \quad (\text{wet basis, %})$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \quad (\text{dry basis, %})$$

where $W_w$ = weight of wet sample, $W_d$ = weight of bone-dry sample.

The relation between the two:

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$$

**Drying rate** is defined as:

$$\text{Drying rate} = -\frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{\text{mass of water evaporated}}{\text{time} \times \text{dry mass of solid}}$$

Units: kg water / (hr · kg dry solid) or simply kg/hr for a system-level rate.

**Heat balance on a solar dryer:**

$$Q_{solar} = Q_{evap} + Q_{loss} + Q_{product}$$

$$\eta_{dryer} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{solar}}$$

where:
- $Q_{evap} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$ = latent heat needed to evaporate moisture (kJ/hr)
- $m_w$ = mass flow rate of water evaporated (kg/hr)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation of water $\approx 2400$ kJ/kg at 60 °C

### 2.4 Solar Pond — Theory of Operation

A **salt-gradient (non-convective) solar pond** has three distinct zones:

1. **Surface zone (upper convecting zone, UCZ):** ~5 – 30 cm; near-fresh water; close to ambient temperature. Small convection allowed.
2. **Non-convecting zone (NCZ):** ~50 – 100 cm; salt concentration increases linearly with depth. This gradient *suppresses* convection — a Rayleigh number criterion applies ($Ra < Ra_{crit} \approx 1000$).
3. **Heat storage zone (lower convecting zone, LCZ):** ~1 – 2 m; concentrated brine (saturated NaCl or MgCl₂); collects and stores solar energy as sensible heat. Temperatures of **70 – 100 °C** are routinely achieved.

**Stability criterion for the gradient:**

$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} < 0 \quad \text{(density must not increase upward)}$$

For a linear salinity gradient, the buoyancy frequency $N$ is:

$$N = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\rho_0} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{dz}}$$

For solar pond stability, the **Rayleigh number** must be sub-critical:

$$Ra = \frac{g \beta H^4}{\nu \alpha} \cdot \Delta T < Ra_{crit}$$

where $\beta = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{d\rho}{dT}$ is the thermal expansion coefficient.

**Thermal efficiency of a solar pond** (analogous to a flat plate collector):

$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{Q_{extracted}}{I_T \cdot A_{pond}}$$

The annual thermal storage efficiency of a well-designed pond is in the range **15 % – 25 %**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Application |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$ | Useful heat gain | W | SWH thermal analysis |
| 2 | $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \dfrac{(T_{in}-T_a)}{I_T}$ | Collector efficiency | dimensionless | FPC performance |
| 3 | $S = I_T (\tau\alpha)$ | Absorbed radiation | W/m² | Glazing + absorber effect |
| 4 | $Q_{evap} = m_w h_{fg}$ | Latent heat for moisture removal | kJ/hr | Solar dryer sizing |
| 5 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}\times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % | Drying calculations |
| 6 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}\times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % | Drying calculations |
| 7 | $N = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\rho_0}\dfrac{d\rho}{dz}}$ | Buoyancy frequency | rad/s | Solar pond stability |
| 8 | $Ra = \dfrac{g \beta H^4 \Delta T}{\nu \alpha}$ | Rayleigh number | dimensionless | Pond convection check |
| 9 | $\eta_{pond} = \dfrac{Q_{extracted}}{I_T A_{pond}}$ | Pond thermal efficiency | dimensionless | Pond performance |
| 10 | $h_{fg} \approx 2400$ kJ/kg at 60 °C | Latent heat of water | kJ/kg | Standard reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Notice that equations 1, 4, and 9 all have the same underlying structure — *useful output energy divided by input solar energy.* Whether you are computing **collector efficiency, dryer pickup efficiency, or pond efficiency**, the same template applies. Master this and you can answer a wide class of numerical questions.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SWH:** Widely used in hospitals, hostels, hostels, hostels, and hostels (Kerala hostels mandate solar water heaters), and industrial process heating.
- **Solar Dryers:** Used by FPOs (Farmer Producer Organisations) in Kerala for drying cardamom, pepper, turmeric, cashew, copra, and fish. The MCRC and other state agencies promote these.
- **Solar Ponds:** Used in Israel (Dead Sea brines), Rajasthan (Sambhar Lake, BITS Pilani pond), and research ponds at IIT campuses in India for experimental low-temperature power generation and desalination.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Useful Heat Gain in a Flat Plate Collector (Detailed)

**Step 1 — Define the absorbed solar radiation per unit area.**
The solar flux reaching the tilted collector surface is $I_T$ (W/m²). The glazing transmits a fraction $\tau$ of this shortwave radiation, and the absorber absorbs a fraction $\alpha$. The product is called the **transmittance-absorptance product** $(\tau\alpha)$.

$$S = I_T (\tau\alpha) \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the rate of thermal loss per unit area to the surroundings.**
Heat is lost from the absorber plate to the ambient by (a) top convection, (b) top radiation, and (c) bottom + edge conduction. Combining all gives a single overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$ (W/m²K). The driving temperature difference is the plate temperature above ambient.

$$Q_{loss} = A_c \, U_L (T_{pm} - T_a)$$

where $T_{pm}$ is the mean absorber plate temperature.

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state energy balance on the absorber plate.**
At steady state, absorbed radiation = heat lost to ambient + heat transferred to fluid:

$$A_c \, S = A_c \, U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) + Q_u$$

Solving for useful heat:

$$Q_u = A_c \left[ S - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the heat removal factor $F_R$ to relate mean plate temperature to inlet fluid temperature.**
In a real collector, fluid enters at $T_{in}$ and exits at $T_{out}$. The mean plate temperature is higher than the mean fluid temperature. The ratio of the *actual* useful heat gain to the *hypothetical* useful heat gain (if the entire absorber were at $T_{in}$) is defined as $F_R$:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{out} - T_{in})}{A_c \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]}$$

Solving for useful heat gain:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

This is the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation**.

**Step 5 — Derive the thermal efficiency expression.**
Dividing both sides by $A_c \, I_T$:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_T} = \frac{F_R S}{I_T} - \frac{F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

Since $S = I_T (\tau\alpha)$:

$$\eta_c = F_R (\tau\alpha) - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I_T}$$

This linear form is the basis of all KTU FPC numerical problems. **The slope is $-F_R U_L$ and the intercept is $F_R (\tau\alpha)$.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Useful Heat and Storage Time in a Solar Pond (Detailed)

**Step 1 — Set up the energy balance on the heat storage zone (LCZ).**
Let $A$ be the surface area of the pond, $h$ the depth of the LCZ, $\rho$ the brine density, $c_p$ the specific heat of brine, and $T_b$ the bottom temperature. The thermal storage capacity of the LCZ is:

$$Q_{stored} = \rho \, c_p \, A \, h \, (T_b - T_{top})$$

where $T_{top}$ is the temperature at the top of the LCZ.

**Step 2 — Compute the daily solar energy input to the LCZ.**
The fraction of incident solar radiation that penetrates through the UCZ and NCZ to reach the LCZ is called the **transmission factor** $T_r$ (typically 0.4 – 0.6 due to absorption and scattering in the upper zones). Daily mean insolation is $\bar{H}$ (W/m²) over sunshine hours $t_s$ (hr). Daily solar energy into the LCZ is:

$$Q_{in,LCZ} = A \, T_r \, \bar{H} \, t_s$$

**Step 3 — Energy lost by the LCZ to upper zones and ground.**
Heat flows upward by conduction through the NCZ (rate $\dot{Q}_{up}$) and downward by conduction to the soil beneath (rate $\dot{Q}_{ground}$). For a well-insulated pond floor, $\dot{Q}_{ground}$ can be neglected for a first-order model.

$$\dot{Q}_{up} = k_{NCZ} \, A \, \frac{T_b - T_{top}}{\Delta z_{NCZ}}$$

**Step 4 — Daily net useful heat extracted.**
The useful heat extracted per day equals the net heat stored minus the conductive loss, both integrated over the sunshine period:

$$Q_{useful,day} = Q_{in,LCZ} - \dot{Q}_{up} \cdot t_s$$

**Step 5 — Annual thermal efficiency of the pond.**
Total useful heat extracted over one year is $Q_{annual}$ (J/yr). Total annual insolation on the pond is $H_{annual} \cdot A$ (J/yr).

$$\eta_{pond} = \frac{Q_{annual}}{H_{annual} \cdot A}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drying Time and Energy in a Solar Dryer (Detailed)

**Step 1 — Initial and final moisture contents.**
Let $M_{wbi}$ and $M_{wbf}$ be the initial and final moisture contents on a wet basis. If $W_i$ and $W_f$ are the initial and final masses of the wet product:

$$M_{wbi} = \frac{W_i - W_d}{W_i} \times 100, \quad M_{wbf} = \frac{W_f - W_d}{W_f} \times 100$$

where $W_d$ = mass of dry solid (assumed constant).

**Step 2 — Mass of water to be removed.**
From the wet-basis definition, the mass of water initially is $W_i - W_d$, and finally is $W_f - W_d$. The mass to be removed is:

$$m_w = (W_i - W_d) - (W_f - W_d) = W_i - W_f$$

**Step 3 — Energy required for evaporation.**
The latent heat required is:

$$Q_{evap} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$$

**Step 4 — Time required at constant drying rate (CRP).**
If the constant drying rate is $R$ (kg water / hr per kg dry solid) and the dry mass is $W_d$:

$$t_{CRP} = \frac{m_w}{R \cdot W_d} = \frac{W_i - W_f}{R \cdot W_d}$$

**Step 5 — Total energy input from solar radiation.**
Average daily solar insolation is $H$ (kJ/m²/day) on the collector (or aperture) area $A$:

$$Q_{solar,day} = H \cdot A$$

**Step 6 — Thermal efficiency of the dryer.**
$$\eta_{dryer} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{solar,day}} = \frac{m_w \, h_{fg}}{H \cdot A}$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A flat plate solar water heater has a collector area of **3 m²** and an overall heat loss coefficient of **6.0 W/m²K**. The transmittance-absorptance product is **0.80** and the heat removal factor is **0.75**. During a particular hour, the average solar radiation on the tilted surface is **800 W/m²**, the ambient temperature is **28 °C**, and the water enters the collector at **45 °C**. Calculate:
(a) the useful heat gain per hour
(b) the instantaneous collector efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Compute the absorbed solar flux:

$$S = I_T (\tau\alpha) = 800 \times 0.80 = 640 \;\text{W/m}^2$$

Apply the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ S - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

Substituting:

$$Q_u = 3 \times 0.75 \times \left[ 640 - 6.0 \times (45 - 28) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.25 \times \left[ 640 - 6.0 \times 17 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.25 \times \left[ 640 - 102 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.25 \times 538 = 1210.5 \;\text{W}$$

Heat gain per hour:

$$Q_{u,hr} = 1210.5 \times 3600 = 4,357,800 \;\text{J} = 4357.8 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Step (b):** Collector efficiency:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} = \frac{1210.5}{3 \times 800} = \frac{1210.5}{2400} = 0.5044$$

$$\eta_c = 50.44 \;\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Check:** The absorber plate temperature must be positive for the model to hold. The temperature at which $Q_u = 0$ is called the **stagnation temperature**: $T_{stag} = T_a + S/U_L = 28 + 640/6.0 = 28 + 106.7 = 134.7 \;°C$. Since $T_{in} = 45 \;°C \ll T_{stag}$, the model is valid. A typical domestic FPC operates at $\eta_c$ between 40 % and 65 %.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU High-Yield View

| Parameter | Solar Water Heater (FPC) | Solar Dryer | Solar Pond |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Working fluid | Water | Hot air + moisture vapour | Saline brine |
| Energy storage | Sensible (in water tank) | Latent (in dried product) | Sensible (in LCZ) |
| Storage duration | 12 – 24 hours | Days – weeks (post-dried product) | Seasonal (months) |
| Typical efficiency | 40 – 65 % (instantaneous) | 25 – 45 % (pickup) | 15 – 25 % (annual) |
| Maximum temperature | 60 – 80 °C | 40 – 70 °C air | 70 – 100 °C brine |
| Key loss mechanism | Top + bottom conduction + convection | Vapour leakage, air infiltration | Upward conduction through NCZ |
| Governing equation | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss | $Q_{evap} = m_w h_{fg}$ | $\eta_{pond} = Q_{extracted}/(I_T A)$ |
| Common application | Domestic hot water | Agricultural produce | Power (ORC), desalination |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Solar Water Heater (FPC + Thermosyphon Tank)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: Incident Solar Radiation IT] --> B[Glazing: Transparent Glass Cover]
    B --> C[Air Gap 2.5 to 5 cm]
    C --> D[Absorber Plate: Blackened Copper with Tubes]
    D --> E[Insulation: Rock Wool 5 to 10 cm]
    E --> F[Aluminium Casing]
    D -->|Hot Water Rises| G[Storage Tank: Insulated]
    G -->|Cold Water Returns| D
    G --> H[Hot Water Outlet to User]
    I[Ambient Air Temperature Ta] --> D
    J[Inlet Water Tin] --> D
    D --> K[Useful Heat Gain Qu and Collector Efficiency Eta]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solar radiation enters through the glazing.
- The absorber plate converts light to heat.
- Hot water rises naturally to the storage tank (thermosyphon effect).
- Cooler water returns from the tank bottom to the collector inlet.
- Useful heat $Q_u$ is delivered to the user via the outlet.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Indirect-Type Solar Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: Incident Solar Radiation] --> B[Transparent Solar Air Heater Collector]
    B -->|Heated Air via Fan| C[Drying Chamber with Perforated Trays]
    D[Moist Product Loaded on Trays] --> C
    C -->|Moisture Absorption| E[Hot Humid Air]
    E --> F[Chimney Exhaust with Damper]
    C -->|Dried Product| G[Output: Dehydrated Product]
    H[Ambient Inlet Air] --> B
    I[Moisture Loss: Mass of Water Evaporated] --> C
    J[Dryer Efficiency: Evap Heat divided by Solar Heat] --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Ambient air enters the solar air heater collector, where it is warmed by the absorber.
- A fan (or natural draft) drives the heated air into the drying chamber.
- The hot air picks up moisture from the product on the perforated trays.
- Moisture-laden air is exhausted through a chimney.
- Drying efficiency is the ratio of latent heat carried by evaporated water to solar input.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Salt-Gradient Solar Pond

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: Incident Solar Radiation] --> B[Upper Convecting Zone UCZ: Fresh Water]
    B --> C[Non Convecting Zone NCZ: Salt Gradient 3 percent to 20 percent]
    C --> D[Heat Storage Zone LCZ: Saturated Brine]
    D -->|Hot Brine Pumped Up| E[External Heat Exchanger]
    E -->|Useful Hot Water or ORC Working Fluid| F[End Use: Heating or Power]
    E -->|Cooled Brine Returned| D
    G[Soil Bed below Pond: Minimal Heat Loss] --> D
    H[Stability Check: Buoyancy Frequency and Rayleigh Number] --> C
    I[Thermal Efficiency: Qextracted over IT times A] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Sunlight penetrates the UCZ with low absorption.
- The NCZ has a stable salinity gradient that blocks natural convection.
- The LCZ stores solar energy as sensible heat in dense, hot brine.
- Hot brine is pumped to an external heat exchanger, giving up its energy.
- Cooled brine is returned to the LCZ to repeat the cycle.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Energy Flow Chain (Cross-Cutting View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solar Resource IT] --> B[Collection Stage: Collector, Dryer, or Pond]
    B --> C[Absorption Stage: Selective Surface or Saline Water]
    C --> D[Transfer Stage: Water Tubes, Air, or Brine]
    D --> E[Storage Stage: Tank, Product, or LCZ]
    E --> F[End Use: Hot Water, Dried Produce, or Electricity]
    F --> G[Losses: Convection, Radiation, Conduction, Vapour Loss]
    G -->|Feedback| B
```

This single chain **unifies all three applications** at a system level. KTU questions on "compare the three" can be answered using this single conceptual scaffold.
