## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "transmittance-absorptance product" $(\tau\alpha)$ as applied to a solar flat plate collector. State its typical range for a single-glazed FPC with a black chrome selective absorber.**

**Model Answer:**

The transmittance-absorptance product $(\tau\alpha)$ is the combined fraction of incident solar radiation that is actually absorbed by the absorber plate after accounting for losses due to transmission through the glass cover and reflection from the absorber surface.

$$\text{Solar absorbed per unit area} \; S = I_T (\tau\alpha)$$

For a single-glazed flat plate collector with a black chrome selective absorber, the typical value of $(\tau\alpha)$ lies in the range **0.75 to 0.85**. The corresponding overall heat loss coefficient $U_L$ is around **4.5 – 6.0 W/m²K**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $\tau$ and $\alpha$ separately and forget that they are **multiplied together** for a single-glazed system. For a double-glazed collector, $(\tau\alpha)$ further reduces to roughly 0.6 – 0.7. **Do not confuse $(\tau\alpha)$ with $\tau + \alpha$.**

---

#### **Question 2. (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is a "salt-gradient solar pond"? Why is a salinity gradient essential for its operation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **salt-gradient (or non-convective) solar pond** is a large saline water body in which the salt concentration is made to increase with depth, creating a stable vertical density gradient.

The salinity gradient is **essential** because, without it, the surface of the pond would warm up by absorbing solar radiation, become less dense, rise to the top (natural convection), and dump the heat back to the atmosphere. This would prevent any storage of thermal energy.

By establishing a **stable density gradient** (dense salty water at the bottom, lighter fresh water at the top), the warmer, less-saline upper layers cannot sink, and the cooler, denser bottom layers cannot rise. Convection is **suppressed**, and the lower layers progressively heat up to **70 – 100 °C**, acting as a long-term thermal energy store.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common student error is to say "salinity prevents evaporation". Salinity does **not** prevent evaporation — it only suppresses **convection**. Markers specifically look for the word "convection" or "density gradient."

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar flat plate collector has an area of **2.5 m²** and an overall heat loss coefficient of **5.5 W/m²K**. The heat removal factor $F_R$ is **0.80** and the transmittance-absorptance product $(\tau\alpha)$ is **0.78**. During peak sunshine, the average solar intensity on the tilted surface is **900 W/m²**, ambient temperature is **30 °C**, and water enters at **50 °C**. Calculate the **useful heat gain per hour** and the **instantaneous collector efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

**Construction:** (Diagrammatic description — refer to Section 4.1.)

A flat plate collector consists of:
- A **transparent glass cover** (single or double-glazed) on top, which reduces convection and radiation losses from the absorber.
- An **air gap** of 2.5 – 5 cm between the cover and absorber.
- A **blackened absorber plate** (copper or aluminium) with water tubes (risers) bonded to it; selective coatings like black chrome are used to enhance absorption and reduce emissivity.
- A **bottom insulation** layer of rock wool (5 – 10 cm).
- A **galvanised iron or aluminium casing** to house the assembly.

**Working Principle:**
1. **Solar radiation** ($I_T$) falls on the glass cover, which transmits a fraction $\tau$ of it.
2. The transmitted radiation is absorbed by the black absorber plate (absorptance $\alpha$).
3. Heat is conducted through the plate to the riser tubes and then convected to the water flowing inside.
4. The hot water rises by **thermosyphon** to an insulated storage tank placed above the collector.
5. Cold water from the bottom of the tank descends into the collector, maintaining a natural circulation.
6. Net useful heat gain is governed by the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ I_T (\tau\alpha) - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
$A_c = 2.5 \; \text{m}^2$, $U_L = 5.5 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $F_R = 0.80$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.78$, $I_T = 900 \; \text{W/m}^2$, $T_a = 30 \;°\text{C}$, $T_{in} = 50 \;°\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Compute absorbed solar flux per unit area:**

$$S = I_T (\tau\alpha) = 900 \times 0.78 = 702 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature-difference driving loss:**

$$\Delta T = T_{in} - T_a = 50 - 30 = 20 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:**

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.80 \times \left[ 702 - 5.5 \times 20 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.0 \times \left[ 702 - 110 \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.0 \times 592 = 1184 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to heat gain per hour:**

$$Q_{u,hr} = 1184 \times 3600 = 4,262,400 \; \text{J} = 4262.4 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Compute collector efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c \, I_T} = \frac{1184}{2.5 \times 900} = \frac{1184}{2250} = 0.5262$$

$$\eta_c = 52.62 \;\%$$

**Result:** Useful heat gain = **4262.4 kJ/hr**; Efficiency = **52.6 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> 1. **Do not forget to convert W to kJ/hr:** multiply by 3.6.
> 2. **Do not mix up $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$** — the HWB equation uses $T_{in}$ (collector inlet), not the tank outlet.
> 3. **Always show units** in every step. KTU markers commonly deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **three zones** of a salt-gradient solar pond with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the **Rayleigh number** in maintaining pond stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar dryer is used to dry **200 kg** of wet cardamom from an initial moisture content of **80 % (wet basis)** to a final moisture content of **10 % (wet basis)**. The dryer operates for **6 hours per day** in the constant-rate period. If the latent heat of vaporisation is **2400 kJ/kg** and the average daily solar insolation on the collector of area 8 m² is **18,000 kJ/m²/day**, calculate the **drying efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three Zones of a Salt-Gradient Solar Pond:**

Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.3. The three zones are:

1. **Upper Convecting Zone (UCZ):** Top 5 – 30 cm. Near-fresh water. Convection is allowed here so the surface temperature equilibrates with ambient.
2. **Non-Convecting Zone (NCZ):** 50 – 100 cm. Salt concentration increases linearly with depth (e.g., 3 % at top, 20 % at bottom). This stable gradient blocks natural convection and acts as a transparent "insulator."
3. **Lower Convecting Zone / Heat Storage Zone (LCZ):** 1 – 2 m deep. Saturated brine that collects and stores solar energy as sensible heat, reaching 70 – 100 °C.

**Role of the Rayleigh Number:**
The **Rayleigh number** $Ra$ is the dimensionless ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous and thermal diffusion forces in a fluid layer. Convection is initiated when $Ra$ exceeds a critical value $Ra_{crit} \approx 1000$ for a horizontal fluid layer.

$$Ra = \frac{g \beta H^4 \Delta T}{\nu \alpha}$$

For solar pond stability, the salinity-induced density gradient must be strong enough that the effective Rayleigh number of the NCZ remains **below** this critical value. The salt gradient introduces a **stabilising buoyancy term** that opposes the destabilising thermal gradient, ensuring $Ra_{eff} < Ra_{crit}$. This suppression of convection is precisely what allows the lower layers to retain their heat.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_i = 200 \; \text{kg}$ (initial wet mass)
- $M_{wbi} = 80 \;\%$
- $M_{wbf} = 10 \;\%$
- $h_{fg} = 2400 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $H = 18,000 \; \text{kJ/m}^2/\text{day}$
- $A = 8 \; \text{m}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the dry mass of the product:**

$$M_{wbi} = \frac{W_i - W_d}{W_i} \times 100 \implies 80 = \frac{200 - W_d}{200} \times 100$$

$$W_i - W_d = 0.80 \times 200 = 160 \; \text{kg}$$

$$W_d = 200 - 160 = 40 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the final mass of the wet product:**

$$M_{wbf} = \frac{W_f - W_d}{W_f} \times 100 \implies 10 = \frac{W_f - 40}{W_f} \times 100$$

$$0.10 \, W_f = W_f - 40 \implies 0.90 \, W_f = 40 \implies W_f = \frac{40}{0.90} = 44.44 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of water removed:**

$$m_w = W_i - W_f = 200 - 44.44 = 155.56 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the latent heat required for evaporation:**

$$Q_{evap} = m_w \cdot h_{fg} = 155.56 \times 2400 = 373,344 \; \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total solar energy input:**

$$Q_{solar} = H \cdot A = 18,000 \times 8 = 144,000 \; \text{kJ/day}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the drying efficiency:**

$$\eta_{dryer} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{solar}} = \frac{373,344}{144,000} = 2.59$$

Wait — this exceeds 100 %, which is **physically impossible**. This indicates that the **given data is unrealistic** for a single solar day (200 kg wet cardamom contains too much water to be dried in one day with this collector area). The student is expected to **identify this anomaly** and comment on it.

**Realistic interpretation:** The drying process would actually take **multiple days**, with the calculation extended as:

$$N_{days} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{solar}} = 2.59 \text{ days} \approx 3 \text{ days}$$

The actual **instantaneous daily efficiency** if drying is spread over 3 days:

$$\eta_{day} = \frac{373,344 / 3}{144,000} = \frac{124,448}{144,000} = 0.864 = 86.4 \;\%$$

This is still very high; in practice, the realistic pickup efficiency is **25 – 45 %**. The remaining energy is lost as hot exhaust air, convection from the dryer walls, and re-radiation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):**
> 1. **Most students blindly compute $\eta = Q_{evap}/Q_{solar}$** without checking the magnitude. A value $>100\%$ is a red flag. KTU examiners love this type of "sanity check" question; **comment on physical plausibility** for full marks.
> 2. **Wet basis vs Dry basis confusion** is a classic blunder. Always write the formula down before substituting.
> 3. **Do not confuse the latent heat of vaporisation at 100 °C (2257 kJ/kg) with that at 60 °C (2400 kJ/kg).** Use the value given in the problem.

---

### KTU Examiner's General Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones Across This Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up $T_{in}$ and $T_{out}$** in the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation — full 1 mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Writing $(\tau\alpha)$ as $\tau + \alpha$** — this is a fundamental misconception. Markers flag this immediately.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert** W to kJ or hours to seconds. KTU numericals require SI consistency.
> 4. **Saying solar pond "stores heat by evaporation"** — it stores heat by **sensible heating** of brine. Evaporation is a *loss* mechanism.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in long-answer questions** — a neat labelled diagram of an FPC or solar pond is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> 6. **Confusing the three drying periods** (initial, CRP, FRP) — examiners specifically test the boundaries between them.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Bullets:**

- The three applications share a common chain: **Solar $\to$ Absorber $\to$ Working Fluid $\to$ End Use**.
- **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** is the master equation for FPC: $Q_u = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)]$.
- **Collector efficiency** is a linear function of $(T_{in} - T_a)/I_T$ with intercept $F_R(\tau\alpha)$ and slope $-F_R U_L$.
- **Thermosyphon effect** is the natural circulation driver in passive SWH — no pump required if the tank is above the collector.
- **ETC** outperforms FPC at high temperatures (above 80 °C) because the vacuum eliminates convection losses; FPC is more economical for low-temperature domestic use.
- **Solar dryer efficiency** is based on latent heat of evaporation of water removed: $Q_{evap} = m_w h_{fg}$.
- **Wet-basis and dry-basis moisture** formulas are different; always state which one you are using in the solution.
- **Three drying periods:** initial adjustment, constant rate period (CRP), falling rate period (FRP). CRP is governed by *air conditions*; FRP is governed by *internal moisture diffusion*.
- **Salt-gradient solar pond** has three zones: UCZ, NCZ, LCZ.
- **Salinity gradient suppresses convection**, not evaporation — the bottom layer stores heat as sensible heat.
- **Rayleigh number stability criterion:** $Ra < Ra_{crit} \approx 1000$ for the NCZ.
- **Typical efficiencies:** FPC 40–65 %, ETC 50–70 %, solar dryer 25–45 %, solar pond 15–25 % (annual).
- **Standard reference temperatures:** stagnation temperature $T_{stag} = T_a + S/U_L$; wet-bulb depression governs CRP drying rate.
- **Kerala-specific applications:** SWH mandatory for hostels and hospitals; solar dryers for cardamom, pepper, turmeric, copra, cashew; solar ponds are largely research-stage in India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic Notes — Module 1, OECHT837**
> This concludes the KTU 2024 Scheme study notes for "Applications: Solar Water Heaters, Solar Dryers, Solar Ponds." All key formulas, derivations, diagrams, and KTU-pattern questions have been covered. Practise at least three numericals on the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation and two numericals on moisture content for assured Part B performance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Photovoltaic Systems: PV Cell Principles and Materials (Silicon, Thin-Film, Organic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Photovoltaic Systems: PV Cell Principles and Materials (Silicon, Thin-Film, Organic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Cell
A **Solar Photovoltaic Cell** is a solid-state semiconductor device that converts incident solar radiation (photons) directly into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**, discovered by Edmond Becquerel in **1839**. The cell is essentially a large-area P-N junction diode, fabricated from semiconductor materials whose bandgap energy ($E_g$) lies in the range of **1.0 eV to 1.7 eV** for optimal solar spectrum matching.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A PV cell is fundamentally a current source whose output is dependent on incident solar irradiance ($G$, in W/m²). The KTU module demands mastery over both the *physical operating principle* and the *engineering classification* of cell materials.

### 1.2 The Photovoltaic Effect
The **photovoltaic effect** is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. It is a three-step quantum process:
1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed by a valence electron in the semiconductor.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** The electron is excited from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving behind a positively charged "hole."
3. **Charge Separation & Collection:** The built-in electric field of the P-N junction sweeps the electron to the N-side and the hole to the P-side, producing a usable photocurrent at the external load.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Photon Bucket" System**
> Imagine a **two-story water tank**. Photons are like raindrops falling from the sky. The semiconductor's valence band is the *lower tank* (full of "water" = electrons), and the conduction band is the *upper tank* (empty). The bandgap is the **height of the wall** between the two tanks. A photon with enough energy (a big raindrop) can splash an electron over the wall into the upper tank. The P-N junction's electric field acts like a **sloped floor** that drains electrons downhill to the N-side, while holes drift the other way, and the external circuit collects this flow as electricity.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Planck's Constant ($h$):** $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **Electron Charge ($q$):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- **Boltzmann Constant ($k_B$):** $1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Solar Constant (AM1.5G, $G_{STC}$):** $1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ (Standard Test Conditions)
- **Cell Temperature ($T_{STC}$):** $25^\circ\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
- **Bandgap of Crystalline Silicon:** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$ at 300 K

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — The Diode Equation Foundation:** A PV cell is electrically modeled as a **current source in parallel with a diode**. This is the cornerstone of all PV engineering analysis. Every I-V curve, efficiency calculation, and fill-factor problem in KTU exams stems from this single equivalent circuit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The P-N Junction as the Heart of a PV Cell
A PV cell is fabricated by creating a junction between a P-type semiconductor (doped with Boron, creating excess holes) and an N-type semiconductor (doped with Phosphorus, creating excess electrons). At the junction, a **depletion region** forms with a built-in electric field ($E_{bi}$).

**Why it matters:** Without this built-in field, photo-generated electron-hole pairs would simply **recombine** within microseconds, producing heat instead of electricity. The junction field is the "engine" that separates charges.

### 2.2 The Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit Model
The KTU-recommended lumped-parameter model comprises:
- A photo-generated current source $I_L$
- A diode (representing the P-N junction) with saturation current $I_0$
- A series resistance $R_s$ (ohmic losses in contacts and bulk)
- A shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (leakage current paths)

**Output Current Equation:**

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $I$ = Terminal current (A)
- $V$ = Terminal voltage (V)
- $I_L$ = Photogenerated current (A) ≈ proportional to irradiance $G$
- $I_0$ = Diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)
- $R_s$ = Series resistance ($\Omega$)
- $R_{sh}$ = Shunt resistance ($\Omega$)

### 2.3 Open-Circuit Voltage, Short-Circuit Current & Maximum Power Point
At **open-circuit** ($I = 0$):

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$$

At **short-circuit** ($V = 0$), the current is maximum:

$$I_{sc} \approx I_L$$

At the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, the cell delivers $(V_m \cdot I_m)$. The **Fill Factor (FF)** quantifies the "squareness" of the I-V curve:

$$FF = \frac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$$

### 2.4 Conversion Efficiency
The **Power Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)** is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

where $A$ is the cell area and $G$ is the incident irradiance.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $E$ in eV, $\lambda$ in $\mu$m | Use $E(\text{eV}) = \frac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}$ for quick calculation |
| 2 | Bandgap Cut-off Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | $\lambda_c$ in $\mu$m | Photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ are NOT absorbed |
| 3 | Single-Diode I-V Equation | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{q(V+IR_s)}{nk_BT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | All in SI units | Ideality factor $n \in \vert 1, 2 \vert$ |
| 4 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = \frac{nk_BT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}+1\right)$ | $V_{oc}$ in V | Thermal voltage $V_T = \frac{k_BT}{q} \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 5 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} \approx I_L \approx k \cdot G$ | $I_{sc}$ in A, $G$ in W/m² | Linearly proportional to irradiance |
| 6 | Fill Factor (Empirical) | $FF \approx \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc}+0.72)}{v_{oc}+1}$ | $v_{oc} = \frac{V_{oc}}{nV_T}$ | Used for ideal cells (no $R_s$, infinite $R_{sh}$) |
| 7 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G A}$ | $\eta$ dimensionless | STC: $G = 1000$ W/m², $T = 25^\circ$C |
| 8 | Spectral Response | $SR(\lambda) = \frac{J_{sc}(\lambda)}{q\Phi(\lambda)}$ | SR in A/W | $\Phi$ = photon flux (photons/s/m²) |
| 9 | Quantum Efficiency | $QE(\lambda) = \frac{\text{electrons collected}}{\text{photons incident}}$ | QE in percent | $QE \leq 100\%$ always |
| 10 | Energy Bandgap vs Temperature | $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \frac{\alpha T^2}{T+\beta}$ | $E_g$ in eV, $T$ in K | Varshni Equation; Si: $E_g(0)=1.17$, $\alpha=4.73\times10^{-4}$, $\beta=636$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The single-diode model is the universal language of PV system design. It is used in MATLAB/Simulink models, PVSyst software, and every commercial PV datasheet. In KTU Part B 14-mark problems, you will be expected to *derive* $V_{oc}$ from first principles, *plot* the I-V curve, and *compute* $\eta$ at given irradiance/temperature.

### 2.6 Classification of PV Cell Materials
KTU 2024 syllabus classifies PV materials into three generations:

**Generation I — Crystalline Silicon (Wafer-Based):**
- **Monocrystalline Silicon (mono-Si):** Single-crystal ingots grown by Czochralski process. Highest efficiency (~24-26% lab, ~20-22% commercial).
- **Polycrystalline (Multicrystalline) Silicon (poly-Si / mc-Si):** Cast ingots with multiple crystal grains. Lower efficiency (~18-20% commercial) but cheaper.

**Generation II — Thin-Film Technologies:**
- **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si):** Non-crystalline thin film, used in calculators and flexible panels.
- **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe):** Single-junction thin film; First Solar's mainstream technology.
- **Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS):** Highest-efficiency thin-film technology.
- **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs):** Used primarily in space and concentrated PV.

**Generation III — Emerging / Organic Technologies:**
- **Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs):** Photoelectrochemical, mimics photosynthesis.
- **Organic Photovoltaics (OPVs):** Carbon-based polymer or small-molecule cells.
- **Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs):** Hybrid organic-inorganic, fastest-growing efficiency record.
- **Quantum Dot Solar Cells (QDSCs):** Uses semiconductor nanocrystals for tunable bandgap.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)

**Step 1 — Start with the single-diode I-V equation.**

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the open-circuit boundary condition.**
At open circuit, no current flows through the external terminals. So $I = 0$. Also assume an ideal cell (negligible $R_s$ and infinite $R_{sh}$) for the first derivation. Thus $IR_s = 0$ and the shunt term vanishes.

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] - 0
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term.**

$$
I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] = I_L
$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $I_0$.**

$$
\exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 = \frac{I_L}{I_0}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$
\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} = \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$.**

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

This is the KTU-standard expression. Since $I_L \gg I_0$ in practical cells (typically by 8-10 orders of magnitude), the "+1" inside the logarithm is often dropped for engineering estimates.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Power Condition ($\frac{dP}{dV} = 0$)

**Step 1 — The terminal power delivered to the load is:**

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the single-diode equation (ideal case, $R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$):**

$$
P = V \left[ I_L - I_0 \left( e^{qV/nk_BT} - 1 \right) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $P$ with respect to $V$ and set equal to zero for MPP.**

$$
\frac{dP}{dV} = I_L - I_0 \left( e^{qV_m/nk_BT} - 1 \right) - V_m \cdot I_0 \cdot \frac{q}{n k_B T} e^{qV_m/nk_BT} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify. This transcendental equation must be solved numerically (Newton-Raphson) in practice, but its solution is the MPP voltage $V_m$.**

**Step 5 — The Fill Factor is then:**

$$
FF = \frac{V_m I_m}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A silicon PV cell has the following parameters at STC:
- $I_L = 3.2 \text{ A}$, $I_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ A}$, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300 \text{ K}$
- Cell area $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$
- Irradiance $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$

**Compute:** (a) $V_{oc}$, (b) $I_{sc}$, (c) $\eta$ assuming $FF = 0.75$.

**Solution (a) — $V_{oc}$:**

$$
V_T = \frac{nk_BT}{q} = 1.3 \times 0.02585 \text{ V} = 0.0336 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.0336 \times \ln\!\left( \frac{3.2}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}} + 1 \right) = 0.0336 \times \ln(3.2 \times 10^{9})
$$

$$
\ln(3.2 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(3.2) + 9 \ln(10) = 1.163 + 20.723 = 21.886
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.0336 \times 21.886 = 0.735 \text{ V}
$$

**Solution (b) — $I_{sc}$:** For an ideal cell, $I_{sc} = I_L = 3.2 \text{ A}$.

**Solution (c) — Efficiency:**

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 0.735 \times 3.2 \times 0.75 = 1.764 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{1.764}{1000 \times 0.01} = \frac{1.764}{10} = 0.1764 = 17.64\%
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of PV Materials

| Property | Mono-Si | Poly-Si | Amorphous Si (a-Si) | CdTe | CIGS | GaAs | Perovskite (PSC) | Organic (OPV) |
|----------|---------|---------|---------------------|------|------|------|------------------|---------------|
| **Crystal Structure** | Single crystal | Multi-grain | Amorphous | Polycrystalline | Polycrystalline | Single crystal | Hybrid | Amorphous/Polymer |
| **Bandgap (eV)** | 1.12 | 1.12 | 1.7 | 1.45 | 1.0-1.7 (tunable) | 1.42 | 1.2-2.3 (tunable) | 1.4-2.0 |
| **Lab Efficiency Record** | 26.7% | 23.3% | 14.0% | 22.1% | 23.4% | 32.9% (conc.) | 26.7% | 18.2% |
| **Commercial Module Eff.** | 20-22% | 17-19% | 6-9% | 18-19% | 14-17% | 28-30% (MJ) | 16-20% (pilot) | 8-12% (pilot) |
| **Temperature Coeff. (per °C)** | -0.40% | -0.40% | -0.20% | -0.25% | -0.36% | -0.23% | -0.30% | -0.10% |
| **Manufacturing Cost** | High | Medium | Low | Lowest | Medium | Very High | Low (solution) | Very Low |
| **Degradation Rate** | 0.5%/yr | 0.7%/yr | 1-2%/yr (initial) | 0.5%/yr | 0.5%/yr | Negligible | 1-5%/yr (T80) | 5-15%/yr |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid | Rigid | Flexible | Rigid | Flexible | Rigid | Flexible | Flexible |
| **Toxicity / Eco** | Safe | Safe | Safe | Cadmium (toxic) | Indium (rare) | Arsenic (toxic) | Lead (toxic) | Safe |
| **Typical Application** | Rooftop, utility | Rooftop | Consumer electronics | Utility farms | BIPV, rooftop | Space, CPV | Research, BIPV | Wearables, IoT |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PV Cell Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photon Source Sunlight] --> B[Anti-Reflection Coating ARC]
    B --> C[Top Contact Grid]
    C --> D[N-Type Emitter Layer]
    D --> E[Depletion Region Built-in Field]
    E --> F[P-Type Base Layer]
    F --> G[Back Surface Field BSF]
    G --> H[Back Contact Metal]
    E --> I[Photo-Generated Electron-Hole Pairs]
    I --> J{Charge Separation by Junction Field}
    J --> K[Electrons Drift to N-Side]
    J --> L[Holes Drift to P-Side]
    K --> M[External Circuit Load]
    L --> M
    M --> N[DC Electrical Output]
```

### 4.2 PV Cell Layered Cross-Section (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    layerA[Incident Sunlight Photons] --> layerB[Anti-Reflection Coating SiNx or TiO2]
    layerB --> layerC[Top Metallic Grid Silver Fingers]
    layerC --> layerD[N-Type Silicon Emitter Phosphorus Doped 0.2 to 0.5 micrometer]
    layerD --> layerE[P-N Junction Depletion Region]
    layerE --> layerF[P-Type Silicon Base Boron Doped 200 to 300 micrometer]
    layerF --> layerG[Back Surface Field Al BSF Layer]
    layerG --> layerH[Aluminum Back Contact]
    layerH --> layerI[External Load Resistor]
    layerC --> layerI
```

### 4.3 Material Generation Classification (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[PV Cell Materials] --> rootB[Generation I Wafer Based Crystalline]
    rootA --> rootC[Generation II Thin Film]
    rootA --> rootD[Generation III Emerging and Organic]
    rootB --> matA1[Monocrystalline Silicon mono-Si]
    rootB --> matA2[Polycrystalline Silicon poly-Si]
    rootC --> matB1[Amorphous Silicon a-Si]
    rootC --> matB2[Cadmium Telluride CdTe]
    rootC --> matB3[Copper Indium Gallium Selenide CIGS]
    rootC --> matB4[Gallium Arsenide GaAs]
    rootD --> matC1[Dye Sensitized Solar Cells DSSC]
    rootD --> matC2[Organic Photovoltaics OPV]
    rootD --> matC3[Perovskite Solar Cells PSC]
    rootD --> matC4[Quantum Dot Solar Cells QDSC]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Photocurrent Source IL Proportional to G] ---|in parallel| dioA[Diode D with I0 and ideality n]
    srcA ---|in parallel| rshA[Shunt Resistance Rsh Losses]
    dioA --- rsA[Series Resistance Rs Ohmic Losses]
    rshA --- rsA
    rsA --- outA[Output Terminals V and I to Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The photocurrent source $I_L$ produces current when light strikes the cell. The diode represents the inherent P-N junction behavior, $R_{sh}$ accounts for manufacturing defects causing leakage, and $R_s$ represents the unavoidable resistance of the semiconductor bulk and metal contacts. The output terminals deliver DC power to the external load.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the photovoltaic effect. With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a solar PV cell.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The photovoltaic effect is the phenomenon of generating a voltage and current in a semiconductor material when it is exposed to light. A photon with energy greater than the bandgap energy ($h\nu \geq E_g$) excites an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an electron-hole pair.

**Construction (1 Mark):** A PV cell consists of a thin N-type emitter layer (~0.2-0.5 $\mu$m) on top of a thicker P-type base (~200-300 $\mu$m). An anti-reflection coating (SiNx) is deposited on the front, with metallic grid fingers for current collection, and a back contact (Al) at the rear.

**Working (1 Mark):** Light generates electron-hole pairs in the depletion region. The built-in electric field of the P-N junction separates the charges—electrons drift to the N-side, holes to the P-side. This charge separation produces a photovoltage; when an external load is connected, photocurrent flows.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain the three generations of solar PV cell materials with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Generation I — Crystalline Silicon Wafer-Based (1 Mark):** These are first-generation cells made from crystalline silicon wafers. Examples: Monocrystalline Silicon (single-crystal, highest efficiency ~26.7% lab) and Polycrystalline Silicon (multi-grain, lower cost).

**Generation II — Thin-Film Technologies (1 Mark):** These cells are fabricated by depositing thin semiconductor layers (~1-5 $\mu$m) on glass, metal, or polymer substrates. Examples: Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) and Copper Indium Gallium Selenide (CIGS).

**Generation III — Organic and Emerging Materials (1 Mark):** These use organic polymers, dyes, or perovskite structures as the absorber. Examples: Dye-Sensitized Solar Cells (DSSCs), Organic Photovoltaics (OPVs), and Perovskite Solar Cells (PSCs).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the equivalent circuit of a solar PV cell. Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ in terms of the photocurrent $I_L$ and reverse saturation current $I_0$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A silicon PV cell has $I_L = 3.5$ A, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.2$, and operates at 300 K. Calculate (i) the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$, (ii) the maximum possible efficiency if the fill factor is 0.78 and the cell area is 125 cm² under standard irradiance of 1000 W/m². (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Diagram (2 Marks):** The single-diode equivalent circuit comprises a photocurrent source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode (representing the P-N junction), a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$, all connected in series with a series resistance $R_s$. The output terminals V and I feed the load.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Starting with the single-diode equation:

$$
I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

Apply open-circuit condition $I = 0$, ideal cell $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$:

$$
0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right]
$$

$$
I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) - 1 \right] = I_L
$$

$$
\exp\!\left( \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k_B T} \right) = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1
$$

$$
V_{oc} = \frac{n k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Open-Circuit Voltage (3 Marks):**

Thermal voltage:

$$
V_T = \frac{k_B T}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}
$$

$$
nV_T = 1.2 \times 0.02585 = 0.03102 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.03102 \times \ln\!\left( \frac{3.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} + 1 \right) = 0.03102 \times \ln(2.33 \times 10^{9})
$$

$$
\ln(2.33 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.33) + 9 \ln(10) = 0.847 + 20.723 = 21.570
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 0.03102 \times 21.570 = 0.669 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Efficiency (4 Marks):**

Assuming $I_{sc} = I_L = 3.5$ A, $A = 125 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0125 \text{ m}^2$, $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$:

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 0.669 \times 3.5 \times 0.78 = 1.826 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{1.826}{1000 \times 0.0125} = \frac{1.826}{12.5} = 0.1461 = 14.61\%
$$

---

**Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the three main categories of solar PV cell materials: monocrystalline silicon, thin-film (CdTe/CIGS), and organic (perovskite/OPV). Discuss their efficiency, cost, stability, and typical applications. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the construction of a silicon solar cell with a neat cross-sectional diagram. Describe the role of the anti-reflection coating, top contact grid, and back surface field. How does the choice of bandgap energy affect the choice of semiconductor material for PV applications? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Monocrystalline Silicon (2 Marks):** Single-crystal silicon grown by Czochralski process. Highest commercial efficiency (20-22%, lab record 26.7%). High manufacturing cost due to energy-intensive crystal growth. Excellent long-term stability (degradation <0.5%/yr). Used in residential rooftop and utility-scale installations.

**Thin-Film — CdTe/CIGS (3 Marks):** Deposited as 1-5 $\mu$m layers on glass. CdTe has commercial efficiency of 18-19% and the lowest manufacturing cost per watt globally (First Solar). CIGS achieves 22-23% lab efficiency. Both offer better temperature coefficients than c-Si. Applications: utility-scale farms (CdTe), building-integrated PV (CIGS). Disadvantages: CdTe contains toxic cadmium; CIGS uses scarce indium.

**Organic — Perovskite/OPV (2 Marks):** Perovskite cells (e.g., CH₃NH₃PbI₃) have rapidly evolved from 3.8% in 2009 to 26.7% lab efficiency. OPV uses conducting polymers with 18.2% lab record. Both can be solution-processed at very low cost on flexible substrates. Major challenges: poor long-term stability (perovskite degrades in moisture), toxicity (Pb in perovskite), and roll-to-roll manufacturing scale-up.

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Construction (3 Marks):** A silicon solar cell from top to bottom: (1) Anti-reflection coating (ARC, ~70-80 nm SiNx), (2) Front metallic grid (silver paste, ~100 $\mu$m wide fingers), (3) N-type emitter (P-doped, ~0.2-0.5 $\mu$m), (4) P-type base (B-doped, ~200-300 $\mu$m), (5) Back Surface Field (BSF, p⁺ Al-alloyed layer), (6) Aluminum back contact.

**Role of Layers (2 Marks):**
- **ARC:** Reduces reflection losses from 35% to <5% by destructive interference, increasing photon absorption.
- **Top Grid:** Collects photogenerated current from the emitter; trade-off between shading and series resistance.
- **BSF:** Creates a high-low junction at the back, repelling minority carriers and reducing rear-surface recombination.

**Bandgap Selection (2 Marks):** The semiconductor bandgap must be optimized to balance two competing losses: (a) photons with $h\nu < E_g$ are not absorbed, (b) excess photon energy $(h\nu - E_g)$ is lost as heat. The Shockley-Queisser limit gives optimal $E_g \approx 1.34$ eV for a single-junction cell under AM1.5G spectrum. Si (1.12 eV), GaAs (1.42 eV), and CdTe (1.45 eV) are all close to this optimum. Amorphous Si (1.7 eV) absorbs blue light better but wastes red photons.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the "+1" in $V_{oc}$:** Many students drop the "+1" inside the logarithm. While $I_L/I_0$ is large enough that this is a small error (~0.01%), KTU examiners *expect* the full expression for full marks. Always write: $\ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$.
> - **Unit Mistakes:** $V_T$ at 300 K = 25.85 mV (NOT 25.85 V). Forgetting the millivolt-to-volt conversion is the #1 source of off-by-1000 errors.
> - **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_{sc}$:** In an ideal cell ($R_s=0$, $R_{sh}=\infty$), $I_{sc} = I_L$. In a real cell, $I_{sc} < I_L$ due to shunt losses.
> - **Stating $I_{sc}$ without the area:** In commercial datasheets, $I_{sc}$ is often given in A or mA per cm². Always state the cell/module area when computing $\eta$.
> - **No diagram = loss of 2 marks:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a "neat diagram" for full marks on PV cell questions. Always include the cross-sectional sketch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **PV cell** = solid-state semiconductor device converting light → electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**.
- The PV cell is electrically equivalent to a **current source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode**, plus $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$.
- The fundamental governing equation is the **single-diode model**: $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(q(V+IR_s)/nk_BT) - 1] - (V+IR_s)/R_{sh}$.
- **Open-circuit voltage**: $V_{oc} = (nk_BT/q)\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$.
- **Short-circuit current**: $I_{sc} \approx I_L \propto G$ (linearly proportional to irradiance).
- **Fill Factor (FF)** = $V_m I_m / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$; for a good silicon cell, FF ≈ 0.7-0.85.
- **Efficiency** $\eta = (V_{oc} I_{sc} FF)/(G \cdot A)$; under STC ($G=1000$ W/m², $T=25^\circ$C).
- **Photovoltaic effect** requires photon energy $E \geq E_g$; cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g(\text{eV})$.
- **Shockley-Queisser optimum** bandgap for single-junction cell ≈ **1.34 eV** under AM1.5G.
- **Three generations of materials**: (I) Crystalline Si (mono, poly), (II) Thin-film (a-Si, CdTe, CIGS, GaAs), (III) Organic/Emerging (DSSC, OPV, Perovskite, QDSC).
- **Standard test conditions (STC)**: $G = 1000$ W/m², AM 1.5G spectrum, $T_{cell} = 25^\circ$C.
- **Temperature dependence**: $V_{oc}$ decreases with temperature (~-2 mV/°C for Si); $I_{sc}$ slightly increases.
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K**: $V_T = k_BT/q \approx 25.85$ mV.
- **Czochralski process** = method for growing mono-Si single crystals.
- **Anti-reflection coating (ARC)** is typically SiNx deposited by PECVD.
- **Bandgap vs. temperature** is given by the Varshni equation: $E_g(T) = E_g(0) - \alpha T^2/(T+\beta)$.
- **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is found by solving $dP/dV = 0$.
- **Series resistance $R_s$** reduces FF; **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** reduces $V_{oc}$.
- **Top contact grid** trade-off: more coverage = less shadow loss but more series resistance.
- **Back Surface Field (BSF)** reduces rear-surface recombination velocity.
- **Perovskite cells** are the fastest-advancing technology; record efficiency rose from 3.8% (2009) to 26.7% (2024).
- **Organic PV (OPV)** cells are flexible, cheap, and printable but suffer from low efficiency and short lifespan.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PV Modules and Arrays, Solar Farms, Off-Grid Systems, and Solar Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PV Modules and Arrays, Solar Farms, Off-Grid Systems, and Solar Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photovoltaic (PV) Module

**Formal Definition:**
A **Photovoltaic (PV) Module** is an encapsulated, weather-protected assembly of interconnected solar cells (typically 36, 60, or 72 crystalline silicon cells) that converts incident solar radiation directly into direct current (DC) electricity via the **photovoltaic effect** discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839. Each module is the smallest commercially available, field-replaceable unit of a solar PV system, rated under Standard Test Conditions (STC) of **1000 W/m²** irradiance, **AM 1.5G** spectrum, and **25 °C** cell temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A PV module is *not* a solar cell. A single cell produces only ~0.5–0.6 V; modules stack cells in series to deliver usable voltages (e.g., 36 cells ≈ 18 V for battery charging).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Battery Brick":**
Think of a single solar cell as a tiny AA battery that only works in sunlight. A PV module is like a factory-pack of 60 such "batteries" wired together inside a tough, waterproof, glass-covered brick. You can pick it up, mount it on a roof, and it will produce electricity for 25+ years. One brick isn't enough to power a house — but string several bricks together, and you have a power plant.

---

## 1.2 PV Array

**Formal Definition:**
A **PV Array** is the complete power-generating unit of a solar PV system, formed by electrically interconnecting multiple PV modules in **series strings** (to raise voltage) and **parallel strings** (to raise current) to meet a specified voltage, current, and power rating.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Solar Battery Pack":**
If a PV module is a brick, the PV array is the **entire wall of bricks** — arranged in neat rows and columns — wired up so that the wall delivers enough voltage and current to run an entire factory. Engineers decide how many bricks in series (height of wall = voltage) and how many bricks in parallel (width of wall = current).

---

## 1.3 Solar Farm (Utility-Scale PV Plant)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Solar Farm** is a large-scale, ground-mounted photovoltaic power installation ranging from **1 MW** (small solar farm) to over **1 GW** (utility-scale solar park), typically spread over **2–5 acres per MW**, and connected to the medium/high-voltage grid through step-up transformers and inverters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** The terms *Solar Farm*, *Solar Power Plant*, and *PV Power Station* are used interchangeably in KTU exam questions. They all refer to a grid-connected, utility-scale installation.

---

## 1.4 Off-Grid (Standalone) PV System

**Formal Definition:**
An **Off-Grid PV System** (also called a *Standalone* or *Isolated* system) is a self-contained solar installation **not connected to the utility grid**, incorporating a **battery bank** for energy storage, a **charge controller** to protect batteries, and optionally a **standalone inverter** for AC loads.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Solar Island":**
Imagine a remote mountain village with no power lines. An off-grid system is the village's *own* mini power utility — solar panels collect sunlight, batteries store energy for nighttime, and the lights stay on. It is fully independent, like an island surrounded by the ocean of un-electrified countryside.

---

## 1.5 Solar Power Plant (Grid-Connected)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Grid-Connected Solar Power Plant** is a PV installation tied to the public electricity grid via a **grid-tie inverter** that synchronizes with grid frequency (**50 Hz** in India) and feeds power through bidirectional metering (net metering or gross metering).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Solar Employee":**
A grid-connected solar plant is like an employee who works only when the sun is out, sending extra energy into the company's "common pool" (grid). At night, when the employee "sleeps," the company pays the employee back from the same pool via net metering.

---

## 1.6 Standard Rating Conditions — The "Benchmarks"

| Parameter | Standard Value | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Irradiance (G) | **1000 W/m²** | Peak sun condition |
| Spectrum | **AM 1.5G** | Mid-latitude sun |
| Cell Temp (T) | **25 °C** | Reference temperature |
| Wind Speed | **1 m/s** | Reference |

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU exam values for module/array ratings (Wp, Vmp, Imp, Voc, Isc) are quoted at STC unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a PV Module — Layer by Layer

A typical crystalline-silicon PV module consists of the following layered structure (from top to bottom):

- **Tempered Low-Iron Glass Cover** — Allows >91% transmission, protects from hail/impact.
- **Encapsulant (EVA — Ethylene Vinyl Acetate)** — Bonds cells, prevents moisture ingress.
- **Solar Cells** (c-Si or multi-c-Si) — Active semiconductor layer.
- **Rear Encapsulant (EVA)** — Symmetric protection.
- **Backsheet (TPT/TPE)** — Weather-resistant polymer insulation, typically white.
- **Aluminium Frame** — Structural rigidity for mounting.
- **Junction Box (IP67)** — Houses bypass diodes and output cables (MC4 connectors).

## 2.2 Electrical Configuration — Why Series and Parallel?

PV cells (typically **0.5 V Voc**) are connected in **series** within a module to raise voltage. The classic configurations are:

| Cells in Series (Ns) | Voc (V) | Typical Vmp (V) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **36 cells** | ~21 V | ~17–18 V | 12 V battery charging |
| **60 cells** | ~37 V | ~30–32 V | Grid-tie residential |
| **72 cells** | ~44 V | ~36–38 V | Commercial / string inverters |

**Why not all series?**
- Higher DC voltage → higher inverter efficiency
- BUT: shading on one cell affects the entire string → **bypass diodes** mitigate.

**Why not all parallel?**
- Higher current → thicker cables needed
- Mismatch current loss is amplified.

**Best Practice:** Combine *Ns* (modules in series → string) with *Np* (strings in parallel → array).

## 2.3 Module-to-Array Sizing — Logic Steps

1. **Fix DC bus voltage** required by inverter (e.g., 600 V, 1000 V, 1500 V).
2. **Compute modules per string** = (Vdc,inverter min) / (Vmp,module)
3. **Verify Voc cold limit** = Ns × Voc,module < Vdc,inverter,max
4. **Compute strings in parallel** = (Required DC power) / (String power)
5. **Verify Isc protection** = Np × Isc,module < Fuse / Inverter limit

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they form the core of every Module 1 numerical in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{module} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ | Module output power at MPP | W |
| 2 | $P_{array} = N_s \times N_p \times P_{module}$ | Total array DC power | W |
| 3 | $V_{oc,string} = N_s \times V_{oc,cell}$ | String open-circuit voltage | V |
| 4 | $I_{sc,string} = I_{sc,cell}$ (series) | String short-circuit current | A |
| 5 | $I_{mp,array} = N_p \times I_{mp,module}$ | Array max-power current | A |
| 6 | $V_{mp,array} = N_s \times V_{mp,module}$ | Array max-power voltage | V |
| 7 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor (typ. 0.6–0.8) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{module} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \times A}$ | Module conversion efficiency | % |
| 9 | $E_{annual} = P_{array} \times PSH \times 365$ | Annual energy yield | kWh/year |
| 10 | $BOS = \dfrac{E_{L} \times A_{d}}{V_{bus} \times \eta_{batt} \times DOD_{max}}$ | Battery bank sizing (Ah) | Ah |
| 11 | $C_{panel} = \dfrac{E_{L,day}}{PSH \times \eta_{sys}}$ | PV array sizing for off-grid | kWp |
| 12 | $PR = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{ideal,STC}}$ | Performance Ratio (0.7–0.85) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $C_{T} = C_{STC} \times [1 + \alpha(T-25)]$ | Temperature-corrected power | W |
| 14 | $A_{land} \approx 5–7 \, \text{acres/MW}$ | Land requirement for solar farm | acre/MW |

> **Notation:** $V_{mp}$ = voltage at max power, $I_{mp}$ = current at max power, $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage, $I_{sc}$ = short-circuit current, $G$ = irradiance, $A$ = module area, $PSH$ = Peak Sun Hours (typically 4–6 h/day in India), $DOD$ = Depth of Discharge, $E_L$ = daily load energy, $A_d$ = autonomy days.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

- **Residential Rooftop (1–10 kWp):** Off-grid or grid-tied; powers a home's daily load (~10–30 kWh/day).
- **Commercial & Industrial (10 kWp–1 MWp):** Reduces peak demand charges; net-metered.
- **Utility Solar Farm (>1 MWp):** Feeds base/intermediate load; bid into PPAs at ₹2.5–4/kWh.
- **Space PV (Satellites):** Use high-efficiency multi-junction cells (η > 30%).
- **Pumped-Hydro + Solar:** Off-grid solar drives pumps; batteries optional.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fill Factor and Conversion Efficiency of a PV Module

The I–V characteristic of a solar cell is governed by the **single-diode model**:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right) - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}
$$

For an ideal cell ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right)
$$

### Step 1 — Open-Circuit Condition (I = 0)

At open circuit, the current is zero and the terminal voltage equals $V_{oc}$:

$$
0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}} - 1 \right)
$$

Solving for $V_{oc}$:

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$:

$$
V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Short-Circuit Condition (V = 0)

At short circuit, the terminal voltage is zero, and the terminal current equals $I_{sc}$:

$$
I_{sc} = I_{ph} - I_0 \left( e^{0} - 1 \right) = I_{ph}
$$

So, **the short-circuit current equals the photo-generated current**.

### Step 3 — Maximum Power Point (MPP)

The output power is $P = V \cdot I$. The MPP satisfies:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I + V \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

Differentiating the ideal diode equation:

$$
\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{q I_0}{nkT} e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}}
$$

Substituting and simplifying at the MPP voltage $V_{mp}$:

$$
I_{mp} = \dfrac{q V_{mp}}{nkT} \cdot I_0 \cdot e^{\dfrac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}}
$$

### Step 4 — Define Fill Factor (FF)

The Fill Factor is the "squareness" of the I–V curve:

$$
FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}
$$

For a typical c-Si cell: $V_{mp} \approx 0.85 V_{oc}$ and $I_{mp} \approx 0.90 I_{sc}$, so:

$$
FF \approx 0.85 \times 0.90 \approx 0.765
$$

Empirical approximation (Green, 1981):

$$
FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}
$$

where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$.

### Step 5 — Module Efficiency

$$
\eta_{module} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \times A_{module}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} I_{sc} FF}{G \times A_{module}}
$$

> **Note:** A standard 60-cell c-Si module with $A \approx 1.65 \text{ m}^2$ and $P_{max} = 330 \text{ W}$ gives:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{330}{1000 \times 1.65} = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Array Sizing for a 10 kWp System

**Given:**
- Inverter MPPT range: **200 V – 800 V**
- Module specs: $V_{mp} = 32 \text{ V}$, $V_{oc} = 38 \text{ V}$, $I_{mp} = 9.5 \text{ A}$, $P_{max} = 305 \text{ W}$
- Required array power: **10 kWp**

**Step 1 — Modules per string (lower voltage limit):**

$$
N_s \ge \dfrac{V_{dc,min}}{V_{mp,module}} = \dfrac{200}{32} = 6.25
$$

Round up: $N_s = 7$ modules per string → $V_{mp,string} = 7 \times 32 = 224 \text{ V}$ ✓

**Step 2 — Verify upper voltage limit (cold Voc):**

Worst case (module at 0 °C): $V_{oc,0°C} \approx V_{oc,STC} \times \left(1 - 0.003 \times 25\right) = 38 \times 0.925 = 35.15 \text{ V}$

$$
V_{oc,string,min} = 7 \times 35.15 = 246 \text{ V} < 800 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Total modules needed:**

$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{305} = 32.79 \approx 33 \text{ modules}
$$

**Step 4 — Strings in parallel:**

$$
N_p = \dfrac{33}{7} = 4.71 \approx 5 \text{ strings}
$$

But 5 × 7 = 35 modules gives $P = 35 \times 305 = 10{,}675 \text{ Wp}$ ≈ **10.7 kWp** ✓

**Step 5 — Verify current limit:**

$$
I_{sc,array} = 5 \times 9.5 = 47.5 \text{ A} < 60 \text{ A inverter limit} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Off-Grid Battery Bank Sizing

**Given:**
- Daily load: $E_L = 5 \text{ kWh/day}$
- Autonomy: $A_d = 3 \text{ days}$
- Battery: Lead-acid, $V_{bus} = 48 \text{ V}$, $\eta_{batt} = 0.80$, $DOD = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Total Wh to be stored:**

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{E_L \times A_d}{DOD \times \eta_{batt}} = \dfrac{5000 \times 3}{0.70 \times 0.80} = 26{,}786 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Ampere-hours:**

$$
C_{batt} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{V_{bus}} = \dfrac{26{,}786}{48} = 558 \text{ Ah}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of 12 V, 150 Ah batteries:**

Batteries per string: $48/12 = 4$ in series.
Strings: $558/150 = 3.72 \approx 4$ parallel.
Total: $4 \times 4 = 16$ batteries.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | Key Formula | Typical Twist |
|---|---|---|
| 36-cell vs 72-cell module | $V_{oc,total} = N_s \times V_{oc,cell}$ | Identify suitable for 12 V / 24 V / 48 V battery |
| Array I–V with bypass diode | $V_{string,shaded} = (N_s - 2) \times V_{mp}$ | Two cells shaded → one bypass diode skips them |
| Performance Ratio | $PR = E_{actual}/(P_{STC} \times PSH)$ | Indian PR ≈ 0.75–0.85 |
| Annual Energy Yield | $E = P_{peak} \times PSH \times 365 \times PR$ | For 1 MWp in Kerala: ~1.4 GWh/year |
| Mismatch loss | $\eta_{mm} \approx 2\%$ typical | Tolerances in $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$ |
| Inverter sizing | $P_{inv} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0 \times P_{DC,array}$ | DC/AC ratio |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PV Module — Internal Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAYER_TOP["TOP LAYER - Sunlight Entry"]
        G[Tempered Low Iron Glass 3.2 mm]
        E1[EVA Encapsulant Layer 1]
    end

    subgraph LAYER_ACTIVE["ACTIVE LAYER - Energy Conversion"]
        C1[Solar Cell 1]
        C2[Solar Cell 2]
        C3[Solar Cell 3]
        C4[Solar Cell N-th]
    end

    subgraph LAYER_BOTTOM["BOTTOM LAYER - Protection"]
        E2[EVA Encapsulant Layer 2]
        BS[Backsheet TPT or TPE]
        AF[Aluminium Frame]
        JB[Junction Box IP67 with Bypass Diodes]
    end

    SUN([Sunlight Photons]) --> G
    G --> E1
    E1 --> C1
    E1 --> C2
    E1 --> C3
    E1 --> C4
    C1 --> E2
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E2
    C4 --> E2
    E2 --> BS
    BS --> AF
    AF --> JB

    JB --> OUT([DC Output via MC4 Connectors])
```

## 4.2 PV Array — Series × Parallel Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STR1["STRING 1 - Series Ns modules"]
        M1A[Module 1A]
        M1B[Module 1B]
        M1C[Module 1C]
    end

    subgraph STR2["STRING 2 - Series Ns modules"]
        M2A[Module 2A]
        M2B[Module 2B]
        M2C[Module 2C]
    end

    subgraph STR3["STRING 3 - Series Ns modules"]
        M3A[Module 3A]
        M3B[Module 3B]
        M3C[Module 3C]
    end

    M1A --- M1B --- M1C
    M2A --- M2B --- M2C
    M3A --- M3B --- M3C

    M1C --> JBOX1[String Junction Box 1]
    M2C --> JBOX2[String Junction Box 2]
    M3C --> JBOX3[String Junction Box 3]

    JBOX1 --> CB[DC Combiner Box with Fuses and SPD]
    JBOX2 --> CB
    JBOX3 --> CB

    CB --> INV[Grid Tie Inverter MPPT]

    INV --> ISLAND[AC Isolator]
    ISLAND --> METER[Bidirectional Net Meter]
    METER --> GRID[Utility Grid 11 kV or 415 V]
```

## 4.3 Off-Grid Standalone PV System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PV[PV Array 1 to 10 kWp]
    CC[Charge Controller MPPT or PWM]
    BATT[Battery Bank Lead Acid or Lithium 48 V]
    INV[Standalone Inverter Sine Wave]
    LOAD_AC[AC Loads 230 V 50 Hz]
    LOAD_DC[DC Loads 12 V or 24 V]
    BD[Bypass Diode Protection]

    PV --> BD
    BD --> CC
    CC --> BATT
    BATT --> INV
    INV --> LOAD_AC
    BATT --> LOAD_DC

    PV -.->|Surplus| REG[Voltage Regulator for DC]
    REG --> LOAD_DC

    DG([Optional DG Backup]) -.->|AC| INV

    style PV fill:#FFD700
    style BATT fill:#87CEEB
    style INV fill:#90EE90
    style LOAD_AC fill:#FFA07A
    style LOAD_DC fill:#FFA07A
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        subgraph BLOCK_A["Block A - 250 kWp"]
            A1[PV Array Block A]
            A2[String Inverters 50 kW x 5]

    D2 --> MCC

    MCC --> STEP[Step Up Transformer 0.8 kV to 11 kV or 33 kV]
```
