# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a PV module. Explain the role of bypass diodes in a PV module with a neat sketch.*

**Model Answer:**

A PV module is an encapsulated assembly of series-connected solar cells (typically 36, 60, or 72) that generates DC electricity from sunlight via the photovoltaic effect, rated at STC (1000 W/m², AM 1.5G, 25 °C).

**Role of Bypass Diodes:**
- When one or more cells in a series string are partially shaded, the shaded cell becomes reverse-biased and dissipates power as heat, creating a "hot spot" that can permanently damage the module.
- A **bypass diode** is connected in anti-parallel with a group of cells (typically 18–24 cells per diode). When shading occurs, the diode becomes forward-biased and **bypasses the shaded group**, allowing current to flow through the un-shaded cells.
- This protects the module from thermal damage and minimizes the loss of output due to partial shading.
- Typically, a 60-cell module has **3 bypass diodes**, each protecting 20 cells.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish between a PV module, a PV array, and a solar farm. State the standard test conditions (STC).*

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **PV Module** | Encapsulated unit of series-connected solar cells | ~100–400 Wp, ~1.6 m² |
| **PV Array** | Interconnection of multiple modules in series and parallel | ~1 kWp – 1 MWp |
| **Solar Farm** | Large ground-mounted grid-connected PV plant | >1 MWp, >5 acres |

**Standard Test Conditions (STC):**
1. Irradiance $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$
2. Spectrum = AM 1.5G
3. Cell temperature $T = 25°\text{C}$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** *With the help of a block diagram, explain the components and working of a grid-connected solar PV power plant. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A grid-connected solar PV power plant converts sunlight into AC electricity and feeds it into the utility grid. The major components are:

1. **PV Array (1–500 MWp)** — Series-parallel connected modules mounted on fixed-tilt or single-axis trackers. Generates DC electricity at ~600–1500 V DC.
2. **String Inverters / Central Inverters** — Convert DC to AC (3-phase, 50 Hz, 415 V / 11 kV). Use MPPT algorithm (Perturb & Observe) to extract maximum power.
3. **DC Combiner Box** — Combines multiple strings with DC fuses, surge protection, and disconnect switches.
4. **Step-up Transformer** — Boosts low-voltage AC to medium voltage (11 kV / 33 kV) for grid interconnection.
5. **HV Switchgear & Protection** — Circuit breakers, relays, and isolators for grid faults (over/under voltage, frequency, anti-islanding).
6. **Net Meter / Bi-directional Meter** — Records energy exported and imported.
7. **SCADA System** — Real-time monitoring of generation, irradiance, temperature, and inverter health.
8. **Earthing & Lightning Protection** — Equipotential bonding, chemical earthing, and surge arresters.

**Working:** Sunlight → DC array → Inverter MPPT → 3-φ AC → Step-up transformer → Grid. The plant operates only when grid frequency and voltage are within specified limits (49.5–50.5 Hz; 0.9–1.1 pu V).

**(b)** *A 1 MWp solar farm uses 330 Wp modules with the following specifications: $V_{mp} = 33.2 \text{ V}$, $I_{mp} = 9.95 \text{ A}$, $V_{oc} = 40.8 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 10.6 \text{ A}$. The inverter MPPT range is 600 V – 1000 V, with a maximum input current of 1100 A. Design a suitable series-parallel configuration. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Modules per string (lower MPPT limit):**

$$
N_s \ge \dfrac{V_{DC,min}}{V_{mp}} = \dfrac{600}{33.2} = 18.07 \implies N_s = 19
$$

**Step 2 — Verify upper voltage limit (cold Voc):**
At 0 °C, $V_{oc,0} = 40.8 \times [1 - 0.003 \times 25] = 40.8 \times 0.925 = 37.74 \text{ V}$

$$
V_{oc,string} = 19 \times 37.74 = 717.06 \text{ V} < 1000 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Total modules:**

$$
N_{total} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{330} = 3030.3 \approx 3030
$$

**Step 4 — Strings in parallel:**

$$
N_p = \dfrac{3030}{19} = 159.47 \implies N_p = 160
$$

Total modules = $19 \times 160 = 3040$ → Array power = $3040 \times 330 = 1{,}003{,}200 \text{ Wp} \approx 1.003 \text{ MWp}$

**Step 5 — Current check:**

$$
I_{sc,total} = 160 \times 10.6 = 1696 \text{ A}
$$

This exceeds 1100 A → Use **central inverters** with parallel MPPT inputs, or split into **2 sub-arrays** of 80 strings each, $I_{sc} = 848 \text{ A} < 1100 \text{ A}$ ✓

**Final Design:** 19 modules/string × 80 strings/sub-array × 2 sub-arrays, each fed to a 500 kW central inverter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** *Explain the working of an off-grid (standalone) solar PV system with a block diagram. List the components and state the role of the charge controller. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

An off-grid solar PV system is a self-contained electricity generator that operates independently of the utility grid, typically used in remote areas, islands, telecom towers, and rural electrification.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 above)

**Components & Their Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **PV Array** | Converts sunlight to DC electricity |
| **Charge Controller (MPPT/PWM)** | Regulates battery charging, prevents overcharge/deep discharge |
| **Battery Bank** | Stores energy for night/ cloudy days (Lead-acid / Li-ion) |
| **Standalone Inverter** | Converts DC → 230 V AC, 50 Hz sine wave |
| **DC Loads** | LED lights, fans, mobile charging |
| **AC Loads** | TV, refrigerator, water pump |
| **Backup (optional)** | Diesel generator for critical loads |

**Role of Charge Controller:**
- **MPPT type** extracts maximum power by tracking the panel's operating point; efficiency 93–98%.
- **PWM type** switches battery connection on/off to limit voltage; efficiency 75–80%.
- Prevents **overcharging** (above 14.4 V for 12 V battery) — extends battery life.
- Prevents **deep discharge** (below 10.5 V) — protects battery from permanent damage.
- Provides **load disconnect** and **reverse polarity** protection.

**(b)** *Design an off-grid solar PV system for a rural home with the following daily loads: 3 × 9 W LED bulbs (5 h), 1 × 60 W fan (8 h), 1 × 75 W TV (4 h), 1 × 200 W refrigerator (24 h, runs 8 h compressor on). Use 48 V battery bus, 0.7 DOD, 0.85 battery efficiency, 5 PSH, 0.75 system derate. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily energy load:**

$$
E_{LED} = 3 \times 9 \times 5 = 135 \text{ Wh}
$$
$$
E_{fan} = 1 \times 60 \times 8 = 480 \text{ Wh}
$$
$$
E_{TV} = 1 \times 75 \times 4 = 300 \text{ Wh}
$$
$$
E_{fridge} = 1 \times 200 \times 8 = 1600 \text{ Wh}
$$
$$
E_{L,total} = 135 + 480 + 300 + 1600 = 2515 \text{ Wh/day} \approx 2.52 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 2 — PV array sizing:**

$$
P_{PV} = \dfrac{E_L}{PSH \times \eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{2515}{5 \times 0.75} = 670.7 \text{ Wp}
$$

Round up → **PV array ≈ 700 Wp** (e.g., 3 × 250 Wp modules in parallel).

**Step 3 — Battery bank sizing (assume 2 autonomy days):**

$$
C_{batt} = \dfrac{E_L \times A_d}{V_{bus} \times DOD \times \eta_{batt}} = \dfrac{2515 \times 2}{48 \times 0.7 \times 0.85} = 184.2 \text{ Ah}
$$

Round up → **200 Ah battery bank** at 48 V (4 × 12 V, 200 Ah batteries in series).

**Step 4 — Inverter sizing:**
Peak load = $3\times 9 + 60 + 75 + 200 = 362 \text{ W}$. With 25% safety margin:
$$
P_{inv} = 1.25 \times 362 \approx 453 \text{ W} \rightarrow \text{Use } 1 \text{ kVA inverter}
$$

**Final Design:**
- **PV Array:** 3 × 250 Wp modules in parallel
- **Battery Bank:** 4 × 12 V 200 Ah in series (48 V, 200 Ah)
- **Inverter:** 1 kVA, 48 V DC → 230 V AC, pure sine wave
- **Charge Controller:** 48 V, 30 A MPPT type

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PV Cell vs Module vs Array** — A single 0.5 V unit is a *cell*, not a module. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the cold-Voc check** — String $V_{oc}$ at 0 °C can exceed inverter's max DC voltage, destroying the equipment. Always show this step.
> 3. **Mismatched units** — Use Wp (peak watts) for PV ratings, not average W. Use PSH (not hours of daylight).
> 4. **No diagram = 1–2 marks lost** — KTU examiners explicitly check for neat block diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. **Bypass diode = 3 per 60-cell module** — Don't write "one bypass diode per cell" — that's wrong and costs a mark.
> 6. **Battery Ah vs Wh confusion** — Divide by system voltage (48 V), don't multiply.
> 7. **No mention of STC** — When stating module ratings, you must cite STC conditions to earn full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

### Core Definitions
- ☑ **PV Cell** = Single semiconductor p-n junction device (~0.5 V).
- ☑ **PV Module** = Encapsulated series-connected cells (36, 60, or 72).
- ☑ **PV Array** = Series-parallel combination of modules.
- ☑ **Solar Farm** = Utility-scale (≥1 MW) ground-mounted grid-tied plant.
- ☑ **Off-Grid System** = Standalone + battery storage, no utility connection.
- ☑ **Solar Power Plant** = Grid-connected PV installation.

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- ☑ **STC:** 1000 W/m², AM 1.5G, 25 °C.
- ☑ **Voc,cell ≈ 0.6 V**, **Vmp,cell ≈ 0.48 V**.
- ☑ **Fill Factor** for c-Si ≈ 0.70–0.80.
- ☑ **Module efficiency** (c-Si) ≈ 15–22%.
- ☑ **Temperature coefficient** of power ≈ −0.4 %/°C.
- ☑ **Land:** ~5–7 acres per MW (India).
- ☑ **PSH** in Kerala ≈ 4.5–5.5 h/day.
- ☑ **PR** in India ≈ 0.75–0.85.
- ☑ **DOD**: Lead-acid = 0.5–0.7; Li-ion = 0.8–0.9.
- ☑ **Inverter efficiency** ≈ 95–98%.

### Key Formula Triggers
- ☑ String voltage add up (series), string current stays the same.
- ☑ Array power = Ns × Np × Pmodule.
- ☑ Battery Ah = (E_L × A_d) / (V_bus × DOD × η_batt).
- ☑ Annual Energy = P_peak × PSH × 365 × PR.

### Component Knowledge
- ☑ Bypass diodes protect against hot-spot damage from shading.
- ☑ Blocking diodes prevent reverse current flow from battery at night.
- ☑ MPPT charge controller gives 20–30% more energy than PWM.
- ☑ String inverters (residential) vs Central inverters (utility-scale).
- ☑ Net metering for grid-tied systems (export-import billing).
- ☑ Anti-islanding protection in grid-tied inverters — must trip within 2 s.

### Diagram-Ready List
- ☑ PV Module layered structure (Glass → EVA → Cells → EVA → Backsheet).
- ☑ Array series-parallel block diagram.
- ☑ Off-grid block diagram (PV → MPPT → Battery → Inverter → Load).
- ☑ Grid-tied power plant (PV → Inverter → Transformer → Grid).

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In Part B, always start with a labelled block diagram, then define each component, then do a numerical design (3 sections of 2–3 marks each = clean 7 marks). Use SI units, cite STC, and show the cold-Voc check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conversion Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Conversion Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Energy Conversion System?

An **Energy Conversion System (ECS)** in the context of non-conventional (renewable) energy refers to the engineered arrangement of devices, machines, and processes that capture ambient or naturally replenished energy forms—such as solar radiation, wind, biomass, tides, waves, ocean thermal gradients, and geothermal heat—and transform them into useful end-use energy carriers, primarily **electricity**, **thermal energy (heat)**, or **mechanical work**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> *Energy Conversion Systems* encompasses the study of how various non-conventional energy sources are converted into usable forms. The conversion pathways are governed by the physical nature of the source (radiant, kinetic, thermal, chemical, gravitational) and the end-use requirement (electrical, thermal, mechanical).

### 1.2 Classification of Energy Conversion Pathways

| Source Form | Primary Conversion Device | Output Form | Conversion Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Radiation (Photons) | Photovoltaic (PV) Cell | DC Electricity | Photovoltaic Effect |
| Solar Radiation (Heat) | Flat Plate / Concentrating Collector | Thermal Energy | Greenhouse Absorption |
| Wind (Kinetic) | Wind Turbine (HAWT / VAWT) | Mechanical / Electrical | Aerodynamic Lift/Drag |
| Biomass (Chemical) | Gasifier, Biogas Digester, Combustor | Fuel / Heat / Electricity | Thermochemical / Biochemical |
| Tides (Gravitational) | Tidal Barrage / Tidal Stream | Mechanical / Electrical | Head Difference / Kinetic |
| Waves (Kinetic/Potential) | Oscillating Water Column, Pelamis | Mechanical / Electrical | Wave Motion Capture |
| Ocean Thermal | OTEC Plant (Closed/Open Cycle) | Electricity | Rankine / Kalina Cycle |
| Geothermal | Dry Steam / Flash / Binary Plant | Electricity / Heat | Steam Turbine Cycle |
| Fuel Cell (Chemical) | PEMFC, SOFC, AFC | DC Electricity | Electrochemical Oxidation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Adapter" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Universal Power Adapter**
> Think of non-conventional energy sources as foreign plug types (different sockets from different countries), and your household appliances as the load (lights, fans, laptops). An **Energy Conversion System is the universal adapter** that:
> 1. **Accepts** the raw "foreign" energy form (e.g., sunlight, wind gusts),
> 2. **Translates** it through a physical/chemical process into a usable form (electricity at 230 V, 50 Hz AC),
> 3. **Delivers** it safely and efficiently to the load.
>
> Just as a poor-quality adapter wastes energy as heat, an inefficient conversion system wastes renewable potential as entropy—making **conversion efficiency** the single most important performance metric.

## 1.4 Why "Non-Conventional" Conversion is Special

Unlike fossil-fuel systems where the chemical energy is **concentrated** (e.g., coal ~24 MJ/kg), renewable sources are typically **diffuse**:
- **Solar flux**: ~1000 W/m² at peak (S.I. value)
- **Wind power density**: ~500 W/m² in good sites
- This **low energy density** mandates large collector areas or rotor diameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constant:**
> - Solar Constant (AM 1.5, outside atmosphere) = **1367 W/m²** (often rounded to **1361 W/m²** per recent satellite data)
> - Standard Test Conditions for PV: Irradiance = **1000 W/m²**, Cell Temperature = **25°C (298.15 K)**, Air Mass = **1.5**
> - Air Density (sea level, 15°C) = **1.225 kg/m³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Conversion

### Operational Principle
When photons with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy) strike a semiconductor junction (typically silicon), electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the built-in electric field of the p-n junction, producing DC current.

### Key Equations

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Photon Energy | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of incident photon |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Maximum voltage at zero current |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Current when terminals shorted |
| Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | "Squareness" of I-V curve |
| Cell Efficiency | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Electrical out / Solar in |
| Maximum Power | $P_{mp} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Operating point of optimum |

where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $q$ = Electron charge = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

## 2.2 Wind Energy Conversion

### Aerodynamic Foundation
The kinetic energy of a moving air mass of mass $m$ at velocity $v$ is:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

The **power in the wind** sweeping a rotor of swept area $A$ is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

This $v^{3}$ dependency is the most critical parameter—doubling wind speed **8×** the available power.

### Power Coefficient and Betz Limit

The fraction extracted by the turbine rotor is the **power coefficient** $C_p$:

$$C_{p} = \frac{P_{rotor}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{rotor}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}}$$

**Betz Limit (1919):** No turbine can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy.

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

### Tip-Speed Ratio

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 v}$$

where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ = rotor radius (m), $n$ = rpm.

## 2.3 Biomass Conversion Systems

Three principal conversion routes exist, selected by moisture content and end-use:

| Route | Moisture Suitability | End Products | Efficiency Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Combustion** | <50% moisture | Heat, Steam, Power | 20–40% |
| **Thermochemical** (Pyrolysis, Gasification) | <30% moisture | Bio-char, Bio-oil, Syngas | 30–60% |
| **Biochemical** (Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation) | >80% moisture | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), Ethanol | 30–50% |

**Combustion Energy Released:**

$$Q = m \cdot CV \cdot \eta_{comb}$$

where $CV$ = calorific value (MJ/kg), $m$ = mass flow, $\eta_{comb}$ = combustion efficiency.

**Biogas Yield (Buswell Equation):**

$$C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2 O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4$$

## 2.4 Tidal Energy Conversion

Tidal range creates potential energy; tidal streams create kinetic energy.

**Tidal Barrage Power (Potential):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^{2} \cdot \frac{dR}{dt}$$

where $R$ = tidal range (m), $A$ = basin area (m²).

**Tidal Stream (Kinetic, analogous to wind):**

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \cdot C_{p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Seawater density $\rho \approx$ **1025 kg/m³** (≈836× air density), making tidal streams powerful even at low velocities.

## 2.5 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)

Utilizes temperature difference $\Delta T$ between warm surface ($\geq$ 25°C) and cold deep ($\leq$ 5°C) waters. Minimum $\Delta T \geq$ **20°C** for viable operation.

**Carnot (Theoretical) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{warm}} = \frac{T_{warm} - T_{cold}}{T_{warm}}$$

**Real Rankine Cycle Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{actual} = \eta_{carnot} \cdot \eta_{rel} \cdot \eta_{mech}$$

Typical real efficiency: **3–6%** (low, but energy is free and continuous).

## 2.6 Geothermal Conversion

Three plant types based on resource temperature:
- **Dry Steam** (T > 200°C): Direct steam to turbine, $\eta \approx$ 10–15%
- **Flash Steam** (150–200°C): High-pressure liquid flashed to steam
- **Binary Cycle** (70–150°C): Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) using low-boiling fluids (e.g., isobutane, R-134a)

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Primary Application Domain |
|---|---|
| PV Arrays | Rooftop solar, solar farms, satellite power |
| Wind Turbines | Utility-scale wind farms, offshore wind |
| Biomass Gasifier | Rural electrification, industrial process heat |
| Tidal Barrage | Coastal baseload (e.g., La Rance, France) |
| OTEC | Tropical island states (Kerala coast potential) |
| Geothermal Binary | Low-enthalpy reservoirs (Puga Valley, Ladakh) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power in Wind and Betz Limit

We model air as an incompressible fluid flowing through an ideal disk (actuator) of radius $R$ and area $A = \pi R^2$.

### Step 1: Define Control Volume
Upstream velocity = $v$, far-downstream (wake) velocity = $v_w$, disk velocity = $v_d$.

### Step 2: Mass Continuity
Mass flow rate is constant across the disk:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_d = \rho A_1 v = \rho A_2 v_w$$

### Step 3: Apply Momentum Equation
The thrust force on the disk equals the rate of momentum change:

$$F = \dot{m}(v - v_w) = \rho A v_d (v - v_w)$$

### Step 4: Apply Bernoulli's Equation
Across the disk (no friction, no work done by disk on fluid separately, but disk does extract energy):

$$F = \rho A (v^{2} - v_w^{2}) / 2$$

Wait — the disk extracts energy, so the **pressure drop** across the disk provides the force:

$$F = (P_{up}^{+} - P_{down}^{+}) A = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v^{2} - v_w^{2}) A$$

### Step 5: Equate Step 3 and Step 4

$$\rho A v_d (v - v_w) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A(v^{2} - v_w^{2})$$

$$v_d = \frac{v + v_w}{2}$$

### Step 6: Power Extracted by Disk
Power = Force × disk velocity:

$$P = F \cdot v_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v^{2} - v_w^{2}) \cdot \frac{v + v_w}{2}$$

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} \cdot \frac{(1 + v_w/v)(1 - (v_w/v)^{2})}{2}$$

Let $x = v_w/v$ (wake velocity ratio, $0 < x < 1$):

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3} \cdot \frac{(1+x)(1-x^{2})}{2}$$

### Step 7: Maximize with Respect to x

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{d}{dx}\left[(1+x)(1-x^{2})\right] = 0$$

Expand: $(1+x)(1-x^{2}) = 1 + x - x^{2} - x^{3}$

Differentiate:

$$\frac{d}{dx}(1 + x - x^{2} - x^{3}) = 1 - 2x - 3x^{2} = 0$$

Solve quadratic $3x^{2} + 2x - 1 = 0$:

$$x = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}}{6} = \frac{-2 \pm 4}{6}$$

Take positive root: $x = \frac{1}{3}$

### Step 8: Compute Maximum Power Coefficient

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{(1 + 1/3)(1 - 1/9)}{2} = \frac{(4/3)(8/9)}{2} = \frac{32}{54} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 = 59.26\%}$$

This is the **Betz Limit** — proven by Albert Betz in 1919.

## 3.2 Derivation: Photovoltaic Fill Factor (Approximate)

For an ideal diode, the I-V characteristic is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

### Step 1: Identify Open-Circuit Voltage
At $I = 0$, $V = V_{oc}$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$V_{oc} \approx \frac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

(assuming $I_L \gg I_0$)

### Step 2: Normalized Voltage Variable
Define $v = \dfrac{qV}{kT}$ and $i = \dfrac{I}{I_L}$, $i_0 = \dfrac{I_0}{I_L} \ll 1$:

$$i = 1 - i_0 \left(e^{v} - 1\right) \approx 1 - i_0 e^{v}$$

### Step 3: Maximum Power Condition
$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ where $P = VI$. In normalized form:

$$\frac{d}{dv}(vi) = 0 \implies i + v\frac{di}{dv} = 0$$

Since $\dfrac{di}{dv} = -i_0 e^{v} = -(1-i)$:

$$i - v(1 - i) = 0 \implies i = \frac{v}{1+v}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $v_{mp}$
Substitute into diode equation:

$$\frac{v_{mp}}{1+v_{mp}} = 1 - i_0 e^{v_{mp}}$$

Solving iteratively (Newton-Raphson) gives $v_{mp} \approx v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 1)$

### Step 5: Fill Factor Expression (Green's Approximation)

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

For typical silicon cells with $V_{oc} \approx 0.6$ V, $FF_0 \approx$ **0.83**.

## 3.3 Derivation: OTEC Thermal Efficiency with Real Working Fluid

For an OTEC plant using ammonia as working fluid in a closed Rankine cycle:

### Step 1: Net Heat Input
$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_{wf}(h_1 - h_4)$$

where $h_1$ = turbine inlet enthalpy, $h_4$ = pump inlet (condenser exit) enthalpy.

### Step 2: Net Work Output
$$W_{net} = \dot{m}_{wf}\left[(h_1 - h_2) - (h_3 - h_4)\right]$$

where states 1→2 = turbine expansion, 3→4 = pump compression.

### Step 3: Cycle Thermal Efficiency
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) - (h_3 - h_4)}{h_1 - h_4}$$

### Step 4: Numerical Example (Typical OTEC)
Let warm surface = 28°C (301 K), cold deep = 5°C (278 K).

**Carnot efficiency:**

$$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{278}{301} = 0.0764 = 7.64\%$$

**Actual Rankine efficiency** (assuming 60% of Carnot with practical turbine and pumps):

$$\eta_{actual} \approx 0.60 \times 7.64\% \approx 4.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This low efficiency is offset by **24/7 availability** — unlike solar/wind, OTEC operates continuously (baseload), making the **capacity factor** a critical metric (~90%).

## 3.4 Derivation: Tidal Stream Power Density

Analogous to wind, but with seawater density $\rho_w = 1025$ kg/m³.

### Step 1: Kinetic Power
$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_w A v^{3} C_p$$

### Step 2: Specific Power per Unit Area
$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_w v^{3} C_p$$

### Step 3: Numerical Comparison with Wind
For $v = 2$ m/s tidal current and $v = 10$ m/s wind:

$$\text{Tidal: } \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2}(1025)(2)^{3}(0.4) = 1640 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

$$\text{Wind: } \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(10)^{3}(0.4) = 245 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

**Tidal at 2 m/s ≈ 6.7× the power density of wind at 10 m/s** — a striking result.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Conversion Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **overall energy conversion architecture** showing how every non-conventional source feeds into a unified end-use distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Solar Radiation] --> convA1[PV Cell Array]
    srcA --> convA2[Solar Thermal Collector]
    convA1 --> inv[Power Conditioning Inverter MPPT]
    convA2 --> th1[Thermal Storage Tank]
    inv --> grid[AC Busbar Grid]
    th1 --> hvac[Space Heating Absorption Chiller]

    srcB[Wind Kinetic Energy] --> convB1[HAWT VAWT Rotor]
    convB1 --> gb[Gearbox Step Up]
    gb --> genB[Doubly Fed Induction Generator]
    genB --> grid

    srcC[Biomass Feedstock] --> convC1[Gasifier]
    srcC --> convC2[Anaerobic Digester]
    convC1 --> ic[Internal Combustion Engine]
    convC2 --> bg[Biogas Storage]
    ic --> genC[Genset]
    bg --> ic
    genC --> grid

    srcD[Tidal Wave Ocean] --> convD1[Tidal Barrage]
    srcD --> convD2[OTEC Plant]
    convD1 --> turbinD[Kaplan Turbine]
    convD2 --> rankine[Closed Rankine Cycle Ammonia]
    turbinD --> genD[Synch Generator]
    rankine --> genD
    genD --> grid

    srce[Geothermal Reservoir] --> convE1[Binary ORC Plant]
    convE1 --> genE[Organic Fluid Vapor Turbine]
    genE --> grid

    grid --> load[Industrial Commercial Residential Load]
```

## 4.2 Detailed PV Cell Conversion Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    photonA[Incident Photons h nu] --> junctionA[p n Junction Si]
    junctionA --> eh[Electron Hole Pair Generation]
    eh --> field[Built in Electric Field V bi]
    field --> sep[Charge Separation]
    sep --> termA[Front Contact Ag Fingers]
    sep --> termB[Back Contact Al Layer]
    termA --> ext[External Circuit DC Load]
    termB --> ext
    ext --> inv[DC AC Inverter MPPT]
    inv --> ac[230V 50Hz AC Output]
```

## 4.3 Wind Turbine Energy Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    windA[Wind Stream Velocity v] --> bladeA[Blade Aerodynamic Profile]
    bladeA --> liftA[Lift Force Generates Torque]
    liftA --> hubA[Rotor Hub Low Speed Shaft]
    hubA --> gearA[Gearbox Step Up Ratio 1 50]
    gearA --> hsa[High Speed Shaft]
    hsa --> genA[Generator DFIG or PMSG]
    genA --> pcuA[Power Converter AC DC AC]
    pcuA --> transfA[Step Up Transformer]
    transfA --> gridA[Grid Integration Point of Common Coupling]
```

## 4.4 Biomass Multi-Path Conversion

```mermaid

    sort -->|MC above 80 percent| pathC[Biochemical Anaerobic Digestion Fermentation]
    pathA --> prodA[Heat Steam Ash]
    pathA --> prodA2[Steam Turbine Electricity]
    pathB --> prodB[Syngas H2 CO CH4]
    pathB --> prodB2[Bio oil via Pyrolysis]
    pathC --> prodC[Biogas 60 percent CH4 40 percent CO2]
    prodB --> engineA[IC Engine or Gas Turbine]
    prodC --> engineA
    engineA --> genset[Electrical Generator]
    prodA2 --> genset
    genset --> distrib[Distribution Network]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **"Energy Conversion System"** as applied to non-conventional energy sources. List any four non-conventional sources with their corresponding primary conversion devices.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
An Energy Conversion System (ECS) is an engineered assembly of devices that captures naturally replenished energy forms (solar, wind, biomass, tidal, geothermal, ocean thermal, wave) and transforms them into useful end-use energy carriers such as electricity, thermal energy, or mechanical work, governed by the physical/chemical principle applicable to each source.

**Four Sources and Conversion Devices (1.5 Marks):**

| Source | Conversion Device |
|---|---|
| Solar Radiation | Photovoltaic (PV) Cell |
| Wind (Kinetic) | Wind Turbine (HAWT/VAWT) |
| Biomass (Chemical) | Gasifier / Biogas Digester |
| Tidal (Gravitational/Kinetic) | Tidal Barrage / Tidal Stream Turbine |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the **Betz Limit** for wind energy conversion. What is its significance in turbine design?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):**
The Betz Limit, derived by Albert Betz in 1919, states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3%) of the kinetic energy** of the incident wind. Mathematically:

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

**Derivation Basis (1 Mark):**
It emerges from applying conservation of mass, momentum, and energy to an ideal actuator disk model in an incompressible flow.

**Significance in Design (1 Mark):**
- Establishes the **upper theoretical ceiling** for turbine aerodynamic efficiency.
- Real turbines operate at $C_p \approx 0.35–0.45$ due to rotational losses, tip losses, and wake turbulence.
- Guides blade designers to optimize **tip-speed ratio** and **pitch angle** to approach (but never exceed) the Betz ceiling.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) energy conversion system**. Derive the expression for **fill factor** and **cell efficiency**. Assume Standard Test Conditions (STC). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PV module has the following specifications at STC: Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = 45$ V, Short-circuit current $I_{sc} = 8$ A, and Fill Factor $FF = 0.75$. The module area is $1.6$ m². Compute: **(i)** Maximum power output, **(ii)** Cell efficiency, **(iii)** Power output when two such modules are connected in series. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Working of PV System (3.5 Marks) + Derivation (3.5 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2):**
PV array → DC Combiner → MPPT Charge Controller → Battery Bank (optional) → DC-AC Inverter → AC Load/Grid.

**Working Principle:**
1. Photons strike the p-n junction of a silicon cell.
2. If photon energy $E_{ph} = h\nu \geq E_g$ (silicon bandgap = 1.12 eV), electron-hole pairs are generated.
3. The built-in electric field at the junction separates the charges — electrons to n-side, holes to p-side.
4. Connecting an external circuit allows current to flow from n-side through load to p-side.

**Derivation — Fill Factor and Cell Efficiency:**

From the I-V characteristic of a PV cell:

$$I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$$

At maximum power point: $\dfrac{d(VI)}{dV} = 0$

This yields the condition (as derived in Section 3.2):

$$V_{mp} = V_{oc} - \frac{kT}{q}\ln\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{kT} + 1\right)$$

**Fill Factor:**

$$\boxed{FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}}$$

**Cell Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{P_{mp}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

where $G$ = solar irradiance (W/m²), $A$ = cell area (m²).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{oc} = 45$ V, $I_{sc} = 8$ A, $FF = 0.75$, $A = 1.6$ m², $G = 1000$ W/m² (STC).

**(i) Maximum Power Output (2 Marks):**

$$P_{mp} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 45 \times 8 \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{P_{mp} = 270 \text{ W}}$$

**(ii) Cell Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 1.6 = 1600 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{cell} = \frac{P_{mp}}{P_{in}} = \frac{270}{1600} = 0.16875$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{cell} = 16.88\%}$$

**(iii) Series Connection Output (3 Marks):**

When two modules are connected in **series**:
- Voltages add: $V_{oc,total} = 45 + 45 = 90$ V
- Current remains same: $I_{sc,total} = 8$ A
- Fill Factor unchanged (approximately): $FF = 0.75$

$$P_{series} = V_{oc,total} \cdot I_{sc,total} \cdot FF = 90 \times 8 \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{P_{series} = 540 \text{ W}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Derive the **Betz Limit** for maximum power extraction by a wind turbine from kinetic wind energy, starting from the actuator disk model. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m operating at a wind speed of 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. The turbine operates at a power coefficient of 0.40 (below Betz limit). Calculate: **(i)** Swept area, **(ii)** Available wind power, **(iii)** Rotor mechanical power output, **(iv)** Annual energy generation assuming 30% capacity factor (8760 hours/year). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Derivation of Betz Limit (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Steady, incompressible, homogeneous, axial flow.
2. No friction losses (ideal fluid).
3. Uniform velocity across the rotor disk (actuator disk model).
4. Infinite number of blades; no rotational wake.
5. Pressure drop across disk occurs entirely due to energy extraction.

**Derivation Steps (6 Marks) — Refer Section 3.1 for full derivation:**

Define control volume with: $v$ = upstream velocity, $v_w$ = wake velocity, $v_d$ = disk velocity.

**Mass continuity:**

$$\rho A v_d = \rho A_1 v = \rho A_2 v_w$$

**Momentum equation (force on disk):**

$$F = \rho A v_d (v - v_w)$$

**Energy equation (force from pressure drop via Bernoulli):**

$$F = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v^2 - v_w^2)$$

**Power extracted:**

$$P = F \cdot v_d = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 \cdot \frac{(1+x)(1-x^2)}{2}$$

where $x = v_w/v$.

**Maximizing** by setting $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = 0$:

$$1 - 2x - 3x^2 = 0 \implies x = \frac{1}{3}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 = 59.3\%}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 80$ m, $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.40$, $CF = 0.30$.

**(i) Swept Area (1.5 Marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 1600\pi$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(ii) Available Wind Power (1.5 Marks):**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(1728)$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Rotor Mechanical Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.32$$

$$\boxed{P_{rotor} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iv) Annual Energy Generation (2 Marks):**

$$E_{annual} = P_{rotor} \times CF \times 8760 = 2.13 \times 0.30 \times 8760$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 5597.6 \text{ MWh/year} \approx 5.60 \text{ GWh/year}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit Errors (–1 to –2 Marks):** Students often forget to convert mm² to m² when calculating swept area, leading to absurd answers like "5000 km²". Always check dimensional homogeneity before writing the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing $C_p$ vs $\eta$:** $C_p$ is the **aerodynamic** power coefficient (Betz-limited). $\eta$ is the **overall** system efficiency (which includes gearbox, generator, transformer losses — typically 0.85–0.95 of $C_p \times P_{wind}$).
> 3. **STC vs Real Conditions:** PV efficiency computed at STC (1000 W/m², 25°C) drops significantly with temperature rise (–0.4 to –0.5% per °C above 25°C). Examiners expect STC assumptions to be **stated explicitly**.
> 4. **Wind Power Cube Law:** Forgetting the $v^3$ dependency is the most common error. A 10% increase in wind speed yields **33%** more power, not 10%.
> 5. **Series vs Parallel in PV:** Series connection increases voltage; parallel increases current. Mis-stating this in Part B loses full marks on the connection question.
> 6. **OTEC Carnot Calculation:** Students frequently forget to use **absolute temperatures in Kelvin**, not Celsius. Using 28 – 5 = 23°C as the temperature difference yields a nonsensical result.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

### Core Conceptual Pillars
- ☑ An **Energy Conversion System** transforms ambient renewable flux into usable energy forms.
- ☑ Every ECS has three core components: **Capture → Convert → Deliver**.
- ☑ The **Betz Limit (16/27 ≈ 59.3%)** is the absolute maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy extractable by an ideal turbine.
- ☑ Wind power depends on the **cube of velocity** $P \propto v^3$.
- ☑ PV conversion is governed by the **photovoltaic effect** in p-n semiconductor junctions.

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☑ Wind Power: $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$
- ☑ Rotor Power: $P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind}$, where $C_p \leq 16/27$
- ☑ PV Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$
- ☑ OTEC Carnot: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{warm}}$ (use Kelvin)
- ☑ Photon Energy: $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$, Bandgap condition: $E_{ph} \geq E_g$

### Numerical Constants (Frequently Tested)
- ☑ Solar Constant (AM 1.5) ≈ **1367 W/m²**
- ☑ Air Density at Sea Level = **1.225 kg/m³**
- ☑ Seawater Density ≈ **1025 kg/m³**
- ☑ Silicon Bandgap = **1.12 eV**
- ☑ Planck's Constant $h$ = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**

### System-Specific Highlights
- ☑ **PV** — Low maintenance, modular, but intermittent (CF ≈ 20%).
- ☑ **Wind** — High CF offshore (40–50%); needs gearbox + power electronics.
- ☑ **Biomass** — Only renewable with **dispatchable** baseload capability (CF ≈ 80%).
- ☑ **Tidal** — Highly predictable; twice-daily cycle; high seawater density gives high power density.
- ☑ **OTEC** — Low efficiency (~3–6%) but 24/7 availability; requires $\Delta T \geq 20°C$.
- ☑ **Geothermal** — Highest CF (~90%); location-locked.

### KTU-Specific Exam Strategy
- ☑ Always **state assumptions** explicitly (STC for PV, steady incompressible flow for wind, etc.).
- ☑ Always show the **formula substitution step** before computing.
- ☑ **Box the final numerical answer** with units.
- ☑ For 14-mark questions, expect **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** with internal logical flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Extraction Principles: Hot Water and Dry Steam Systems; Wind Energy: Wind Characteristics and Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Extraction Principles: Hot Water and Dry Steam Systems; Wind Energy: Wind Characteristics and Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Geothermal Energy – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Geothermal Energy** is the thermal energy generated and stored within the Earth's crust, originating from the residual heat of planetary formation (~4.5 billion years old) and the continuous radioactive decay of isotopes such as Uranium-238, Thorium-232, and Potassium-40. It is harnessed as electricity when the subsurface heat is brought to the surface via water or steam.

The standard reference temperature of the Earth's interior increases with depth, called the **Geothermal Gradient**, with an average global value of approximately **25–30 °C per km of depth**. In tectonically active regions (e.g., the "Pacific Ring of Fire" which includes Iceland, Japan, and the western USA), this gradient can exceed **50–80 °C/km**, making commercial extraction viable.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy – The Earth's Pressure Cooker

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "Earth's Natural Pressure Cooker"**
> Imagine a giant pressure cooker buried under your backyard. Deep inside, the Earth contains immense stores of heat. In some pockets, rainwater has seeped down through fractured rocks and is trapped against an impermeable layer. The immense pressure from above (hundreds of atmospheres) and the high temperatures (200–350 °C) keep this water in a superheated liquid or vapor state. When we drill a well into this "cooker," the pressurized fluid naturally rushes to the surface — just like opening a pressure cooker valve. The difference? We capture the released steam/water to spin a turbine and generate electricity!

### 1.3 Classification of Geothermal Resources (KTU High-Yield)

| Resource Class | Description | Typical Reservoir | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Steam (Vapor-Dominated)** | Natural steam directly available | > **180 °C** | Larderello (Italy), The Geysers (USA) |
| **Hot Water (Liquid-Dominated)** | Hot pressurized water with dissolved gases | **150 – 250 °C** | Wairakei (New Zealand) |
| **Flash Steam Reservoir** | Hot water flashing to steam on depressurization | **>180 °C** | Cerro Prieto (Mexico) |
| **Binary Cycle (Low-Enthalpy)** | Hot water heats a secondary working fluid | **100 – 170 °C** | Chena Hot Springs (Alaska) |
| **Hot Dry Rock (HDR / EGS)** | Engineered reservoir, no natural fluid | **>200 °C** | Soultz-sous-Forêts (France) |
| **Geo-pressured** | High-pressure brine with dissolved methane | **150 – 200 °C** | Gulf of Mexico (USA) |

### 1.4 Wind Energy – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Wind Energy** is the kinetic energy of large-scale atmospheric air movement (caused primarily by differential solar heating of the Earth's surface) that can be converted into useful electrical or mechanical work using wind turbines. It is classified as an **indirect form of solar energy** because the sun's uneven heating generates pressure gradients that drive wind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "Sun's Slow Hand on the Atmosphere"**
> Picture the atmosphere as a vast, slow-moving river of air. The sun heats the equator more than the poles, much like placing one end of a swimming pool near a heater. Warm air rises at the equator and cool air sinks at the poles — this constant circulation is **wind**. A wind turbine simply dips a paddle into this air-river and extracts a fraction of the moving fluid's kinetic energy, just as a watermill extracts energy from a flowing stream!

### 1.5 Wind Characteristics – The Six "Vitals"

A wind energy engineer must quantify the wind resource using six fundamental parameters:

1. **Wind Speed (v):** Measured in **m/s**, typically at hub height (commonly **10 m, 30 m, 50 m, 80 m, or 100 m**).
2. **Wind Direction:** Expressed in degrees from true north (0°–360°), recorded by a **Wind Vane**.
3. **Air Density (ρ):** Function of temperature and pressure; standard value = **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15 °C.
4. **Wind Shear:** The change in wind speed with height; governed by the **Power Law** or **Logarithmic Law**.
5. **Turbulence Intensity:** Ratio of standard deviation of wind speed to mean wind speed; ideally < **0.16** for utility-scale turbines.
6. **Wind Power Density (WPD):** Power per unit swept area, in **W/m²**; the most useful single metric for site evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geothermal Energy Extraction – System Architectures

#### 2.1.1 Dry Steam Power Plant (Vapor-Dominated)

This is the **simplest and oldest** geothermal plant architecture, first demonstrated at **Larderello, Italy in 1904** and now famously operational at **The Geysers, California** (the world's largest dry steam field, ~1,500 MW installed).

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Production Well:** Drilled 1–3 km deep; natural high-pressure steam (5–35 bar) flows up the wellbore due to reservoir overpressure.
- **Step 2 — Steam-Centric Separator:** Unlike flash plants, the fluid is already vapor; only entrained rock particles and condensate are removed via a **centrifugal separator** (cyclone-type).
- **Step 3 — Turbine:** Dry saturated steam expands through a multi-stage impulse/reaction turbine.
- **Step 4 — Condenser:** Spent steam is condensed using an air-cooled or water-cooled surface condenser at ~0.05 bar absolute.
- **Step 5 — Injection Well:** The condensed water (and any non-condensable gases like H₂S, CO₂ after scrubbing) is reinjected into the deep reservoir to maintain pressure (sustainability).
- **Why This Matters:** Dry steam plants have the **highest conversion efficiency (~10–20%)** among geothermal types because there is no two-phase flashing loss.

#### 2.1.2 Hot Water / Flash Steam Power Plant (Liquid-Dominated)

This is the **most common commercial configuration** in modern geothermal projects. The reservoir fluid is **pressurized hot water** at 180–250 °C.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Production Well:** Hot water rises; some "flashing" occurs up the wellbore due to pressure drop.
- **Step 2 — Steam Separator (Flash Tank):** A **throttling valve** (orifice) reduces the pressure to about **0.4–0.7 bar (40–70 kPa absolute)**. The corresponding saturation temperature is **~75–90 °C**, so a fraction of the water **flashes into steam** instantly.
- **Step 3 — Steam to Turbine:** The flashed steam drives a low-pressure turbine.
- **Step 4 — Brine Disposal:** The remaining hot brine is either injected back (best practice) or cascaded to a **double-flash** unit for additional energy recovery.
- **Step 5 — Condensate Return:** The turbine exhaust is condensed and the condensate is also injected.
- **Why This Matters:** Flash plants offer the best balance of cost and energy yield for **medium-to-high enthalpy** reservoirs.

#### 2.1.3 Binary Cycle Power Plant (Low-Enthalpy / Hot Water Type)

For reservoirs with temperatures **below ~170 °C**, flashing is impractical. The binary cycle uses a **secondary working fluid** (e.g., isobutane, isopentane, R-134a, R-245fa) that has a low boiling point and high vapor pressure.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- **Step 1 — Heat Exchanger (Primary):** Geothermal brine (typically 120–170 °C) flows through shell-and-tube heat exchangers.
- **Step 2 — Vaporization:** The secondary fluid (boiling point ~30–50 °C at low pressure) absorbs heat and vaporizes without ever contacting the brine (closed loop).
- **Step 3 — Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC):** The organic vapor drives a turbine, often of axial-flow type designed for low-pressure, high-volume flow.
- **Step 4 — Condenser:** The vapor is condensed (air-cooled preferred to avoid water consumption).
- **Step 5 — Pump:** A liquid pump returns the working fluid to the heat exchanger at high pressure.
- **Why This Matters:** Binary plants produce **zero emissions**, can use 100% reinjection (zero fluid loss), and unlock vast low-temperature resources. They are now the **fastest-growing** geothermal technology.

### 2.2 Wind Energy – Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 The Physics of "Power in the Wind"

Consider a control volume of air with cross-sectional area **A** (m²) approaching a turbine. In one second, a mass of air equal to **ρAv** passes through the disk. The kinetic energy per unit time (power) is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

**Key Engineering Insights:**

- **Why v³?** Doubling wind speed increases available power by **8 times** — this cubic dependency is why even modest wind-speed improvements dramatically increase yield.
- **Why ½?** This is the kinetic energy per unit mass from classical Newtonian mechanics: **KE = ½mv²**.
- **Why ρ matters:** Cool, dense air carries more energy. Standard sea-level air density = **1.225 kg/m³**; at high altitudes or warm sites, ρ may be **1.0–1.1 kg/m³**.

#### 2.2.2 The Betz Limit — Theoretical Maximum Extraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Albert Betz (1919) proved** that no turbine can extract more than **16/27 ≈ 59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind stream. This is the **Betz Coefficient (C_p,max)** and is a direct consequence of momentum conservation.

The **Power Coefficient (C_p)** is defined as:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{turbine}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3}$$

For an *ideal* turbine, $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$. Real machines achieve $C_p = 0.35 - 0.45$.

#### 2.2.3 Wind Speed Distribution — Weibull Function

Wind speed is **not constant**; it varies stochastically. The probability of the wind speed *v* occurring is given by the **Weibull probability density function**:

$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right]$$

Where:
- **k** = shape parameter (typically **1.5 – 3.0**; k = 2 corresponds to the **Rayleigh distribution**)
- **c** = scale parameter (m/s), related to mean wind speed

The **cumulative distribution function (CDF)** is:

$$F(v) = 1 - \exp\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^k\right]$$

**Engineering Utility:** Knowing the Weibull parameters, the **mean wind power density** can be computed for an entire year, allowing accurate annual energy production (AEP) forecasting.

#### 2.2.4 Wind Measurement – The "MET Mast"

Wind resource assessment requires at least **one full year** of on-site measurement (the **IEC 61400-12-1** standard). The instrumentation suite is mounted on a **Meteorological Mast (MET mast)** of height 60–120 m, with the following sensors:

| Sensor | Quantity Measured | Units | Mounting Height(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cup Anemometer** | Wind speed | m/s | 2–4 levels (e.g., 40, 60, 80, 100 m) |
| **Wind Vane (Potentiometric/Ultrasonic)** | Wind direction | degrees | 2–3 levels |
| **Thermometer (Pt100 RTD)** | Air temperature | °C | 1–2 levels |
| **Barometer** | Atmospheric pressure | hPa | At base |
| **Hygrometer** | Relative humidity | % | At base |
| **Pyranometer** | Solar irradiance | W/m² | At top (for correlation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend:** **Remote Sensing Devices** such as **Sodar (Sound Detection and Ranging)** and **Lidar (Light Detection and Ranging)** are increasingly replacing tall MET masts. They measure wind speed up to **200 m** using backscattered acoustic/laser pulses.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam and form the analytical backbone of geothermal and wind energy design.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Key | Typical Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | ρ = density, A = swept area, v = wind speed | W | Foundation formula |
| 2 | $C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient | dimensionless | Performance metric |
| 3 | $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit | dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 4 | $P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Practical power | W | Real turbine output |
| 5 | $f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k}$ | Weibull PDF | s/m | Wind statistics |
| 6 | $F(v) = 1 - e^{-(v/c)^k}$ | Weibull CDF | dimensionless | Probability of v ≤ V |
| 7 | $\frac{v}{v_0} = \left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Power Law (wind shear) | α = shear exponent | Vertical extrapolation |
| 8 | $\frac{v_z}{v_{ref}} = \frac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_{ref}/z_0)}$ | Logarithmic Law | z₀ = roughness length | More accurate than power law |
| 9 | $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A c^3 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$ | Mean Weibull power | W | Annual power average |
| 10 | $\eta_{dry-steam} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Plant thermal efficiency | dimensionless | Geothermal efficiency |
| 11 | $Q_{flash} = h_{water}(T_1) - h_{water}(T_2)$ | Flashing enthalpy drop | kJ/kg | Flash plant analysis |
| 12 | $m_{steam} = m_{brine} \cdot x_{flash}$ | Steam quality post-flash | kg/s | Mass balance |
| 13 | $AEP = P_{rated} \cdot CF \cdot 8760$ | Annual Energy Production | kWh/yr | Energy yield |
| 14 | $CF = \frac{AEP}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Capacity Factor | dimensionless | Performance metric |
| 15 | $T_{depth} = T_{surface} + (\text{gradient}) \cdot d$ | Subsurface temperature | °C | Geothermal exploration |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Geothermal:** Direct dry steam (like at The Geysers) supplies ~**1,500 MW** of baseload power — unique because geothermal runs 24/7/365 with capacity factors of **90–95%**, dwarfing solar (~25%) and wind (~35–45%).
- **Wind Power:** The global installed wind capacity crossed **1 TW** in 2023, with Denmark generating **>50%** of its electricity from wind. The largest single turbine (Vestas V236-15.0 MW) has a rotor diameter of **236 m** and a swept area of **~43,477 m²**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind

> **Objective:** Derive $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ from first principles.

**Given:**
- Air density: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Cross-sectional (swept) area of turbine: $A$ (m²)
- Free-stream wind velocity: $v$ (m/s)

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate (Continuity Equation):**

In one second, a cylindrical column of air with length $v$ and cross-section $A$ passes through the turbine disk.

$$V_{column} = A \cdot v \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

Multiplying by density:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad (\text{kg}/\text{s})$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy per Unit Mass:**

From Newtonian mechanics, the kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \quad (\text{J})$$

**Step 3 — Power = Energy per Unit Time:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{KE}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Mass Flow Rate:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad (\text{W})}$$

> **Sanity Check (Dimensional Analysis):** $[\text{kg/m}^3] \cdot [\text{m}^2] \cdot [\text{m/s}]^3 = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2 / \text{s}^3 = \text{W}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Betz Coefficient (16/27)

> **Objective:** Show that the maximum extractable fraction of wind power is **16/27 ≈ 0.5926**.

**Step 1 — Define the Control Volume (Stream Tube):**

Let the air far upstream have velocity $v_1$, density $\rho$, and cross-section $A_1$. At the rotor disk (section 2), velocity is $v_2$ and area is $A$. Far downstream (section 3), velocity drops to $v_3$ and area expands to $A_3$.

By **continuity**:
$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v_2 = \rho A_3 v_3$$

**Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v_2$$

**Step 3 — Thrust (Momentum) Equation:**

The force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_3) = \rho A v_2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

The power extracted by the rotor is force × velocity at the disk:

$$P = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A v_2^2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's Turbine Equation (Velocity Triangle):**

By the **angular momentum theorem**, the velocity at the disk is the arithmetic mean of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v_2 = \frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $v_2$ into Power Expression:**

$$P = \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}\right)^2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

**Step 6 — Simplify Using Difference of Squares:**

Let $u = v_1 - v_3$. Then $(v_1 + v_3)(v_1 - v_3) = v_1^2 - v_3^2$, and:

$$P = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1 + v_3)^2 (v_1 - v_3) = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_3^2)(v_1 + v_3)$$

**Step 7 — Express Extracted Power vs. Available Power:**

$$C_p = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\frac{1}{4}\rho A (v_1^2 - v_3^2)(v_1 + v_3)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{v_3^2}{v_1^2}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v_3}{v_1}\right)$$

Let $x = v_3 / v_1$ (induction factor ratio). Then:

$$C_p = \frac{1}{2}(1 - x^2)(1 + x) = \frac{1}{2}(1 + x - x^2 - x^3)$$

**Step 8 — Maximize $C_p$ with Respect to $x$:**

$$\frac{dC_p}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 2x - 3x^2) = 0$$

Solve the quadratic:

$$3x^2 + 2x - 1 = 0$$

$$x = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 12}}{6} = \frac{-2 \pm 4}{6}$$

The physically meaningful (positive) root is:

$$x = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 9 — Compute Maximum $C_p$:**

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2\right]\left(1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{32}{54} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** When $v_3 = v_1/3$, the wake velocity is exactly one-third of free-stream, and the extracted power peaks at 16/27. If the rotor tried to extract more, the air would completely stagnate, choking the flow.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mean Wind Power Density from Weibull Distribution

> **Objective:** Derive the formula for the average power density in the wind.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Power Density:**

The power density (power per unit area) is:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3$$

**Step 2 — Mean Power Density = Expectation of $v^3$:**

$$\left\langle \frac{P}{A} \right\rangle = \frac{1}{2} \rho \langle v^3 \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \rho \int_0^\infty v^3 f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Weibull PDF:**

$$\langle v^3 \rangle = \int_0^\infty v^3 \cdot \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k} dv$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u = (v/c)^k$ so $v = c \cdot u^{1/k}$ and $dv = \frac{c}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$:**

After substitution, this integral reduces to the **Gamma function** form:

$$\langle v^n \rangle = c^n \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{n}{k}\right)$$

For $n = 3$:

$$\langle v^3 \rangle = c^3 \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final Mean Power Density:**

$$\boxed{\left\langle \frac{P}{A} \right\rangle = \frac{1}{2} \rho c^3 \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)}$$

> **Numerical Example:** For $c = 7$ m/s, $k = 2$ (Rayleigh), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\Gamma(2.5) = 1.329$:
> $\langle P/A \rangle = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 343 \times 1.329 \approx 279 \, \text{W/m}^2$ — a Class 3 wind regime.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Flashed Steam Mass in a Geothermal Flash Plant

> **Objective:** Derive the steam mass fraction in a single-flash geothermal plant.

**Step 1 — Energy Balance on the Throttle Valve:**

Across the throttling valve, **enthalpy is conserved** (isenthalpic, no work, no heat loss):

$$h_{brine}(T_1, P_1) = h_{brine}(T_2, P_2) \quad (\text{no})$$

Actually, the throttling is isenthalpic but the fluid then separates:

$$h_1 = h_{brine}(T_1, P_1)$$

After the throttle, we have a **two-phase mixture** at lower pressure $P_2$ with quality $x$ (mass fraction of vapor):

$$h_1 = (1 - x) h_f(T_2) + x h_g(T_2)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Quality $x$:**

$$x_{flash} = \frac{h_1 - h_f(T_2)}{h_g(T_2) - h_f(T_2)} = \frac{h_1 - h_f(T_2)}{h_{fg}(T_2)}$$

**Step 3 — Steam Mass Flow Rate:**

$$m_{steam} = x_{flash} \cdot m_{brine}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration:**

Suppose brine enters at $T_1 = 240$ °C, $P_1 = 35$ bar. From steam tables:
- $h_1 \approx 1037$ kJ/kg
- After throttling to $P_2 = 0.5$ bar, $T_{sat} \approx 81.3$ °C
- $h_f \approx 341$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} \approx 2305$ kJ/kg

$$x_{flash} = \frac{1037 - 341}{2305} = \frac{696}{2305} \approx 0.302$$

So for every **1 kg of brine**, **0.302 kg of steam** is produced — a 30% conversion efficiency per pass.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Capacity Factor for a Wind Farm

**Step 1 — Energy Output Definition:**

$$AEP = \sum_{i=1}^{8760} P_i \cdot \Delta t = \int_0^{8760} P(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Capacity Factor Definition:**

$$CF = \frac{AEP}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760} = \frac{\text{Energy actually produced}}{\text{Energy if turbine ran at rated power 24/7}}$$

**Step 3 — Typical Values:**
- Onshore wind: $CF \approx 0.25 - 0.40$
- Offshore wind: $CF \approx 0.40 - 0.55$
- Geothermal: $CF \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ (one of its key advantages!)

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. At a hub-height wind speed of 12 m/s, air density is 1.18 kg/m³, and the power coefficient is 0.40. Calculate: (a) the power available in the wind, (b) the actual power extracted, (c) the percentage of Betz limit achieved.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Swept area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

**Part (b) — Available power:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.18 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 = 5.126 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \approx 5.13 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (c) — Actual extracted power:**

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.126 \text{ MW} = 2.05 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (d) — Betz limit achievement:**

$$\frac{C_p}{C_{p,max}} = \frac{0.40}{0.5926} = 0.675 \text{ or } 67.5\% \text{ of Betz limit}$$

> This is a **typical, well-designed turbine** in the modern industry.
