## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a Dry Steam and a Flash Steam geothermal power plant. List any three advantages of the Binary Cycle plant over the Flash Steam plant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Dry Steam Plant | Flash Steam Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Reservoir Fluid** | Natural steam (vapor-dominated) | Hot pressurized water (liquid-dominated) |
| **Separator Required?** | Minimal (only for cleaning particulates) | Yes, to flash brine into steam |
| **Operating Temperature** | > 180 °C | 150 – 250 °C |
| **Example Location** | Larderello (Italy), The Geysers (USA) | Wairakei (NZ), Cerro Prieto (Mexico) |
| **Conversion Efficiency** | 10 – 20% (highest among geothermal) | 8 – 15% |

**Three Advantages of Binary Cycle over Flash Steam:**
1. Can utilize **low-enthalpy resources (< 170 °C)** that flash plants cannot exploit.
2. **Zero emissions** — secondary working fluid is fully contained in a closed loop; the geothermal brine never contacts the atmosphere.
3. **Higher reinjection ratio (100%)** ensures reservoir sustainability and prevents land subsidence.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Wind Power Density." What are its units? Why is it considered a better indicator of wind energy potential than mean wind speed alone?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition:** Wind Power Density (WPD) is the power available in the wind per unit area of the plane perpendicular to the wind direction. It is calculated as: $\text{WPD} = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3$, expressed in **W/m²**.
- **Why it is superior to mean wind speed alone:** Because the power in the wind varies as the **cube of wind speed**, two sites with identical mean wind speeds can have vastly different energy potentials if one site has more frequent high-speed gusts. WPD inherently captures the **cubic effect** and is the standard metric for wind resource classification (e.g., NREL uses WPD classes 1–7).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module internal choice; each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Dry Steam Geothermal Power Plant**. Discuss its major components and state any four advantages of dry steam systems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Betz limit** of a wind turbine. Show that the maximum extractable fraction of wind kinetic energy is **16/27**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A Dry Steam Geothermal Power Plant, also known as a **Vapor-Dominated** plant, is the simplest geothermal energy conversion technology. It is the oldest form of geothermal power generation, with the first commercial plant at **Larderello, Italy (1904)**. The world's largest dry steam field is **The Geysers in California (USA)** with an installed capacity of approximately **1,500 MW**.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Deep Reservoir Dry Steam 240C] -->|Production Well 1 to 3 km| S1[Centrifugal Separator]
    S1 -->|Filtered Dry Steam| TUR[Multi-Stage Steam Turbine]
    TUR -->|Low-Pressure Steam| CON[Condenser 0.05 bar]
    CON -->|Liquid Condensate| P1[Condensate Pump]
    P1 -->|Reinjection| R2[Reservoir via Injection Well]
    CON -->|Non-Condensable Gases H2S CO2| GAS[Gas Scrubbing System]
    TUR -->|Mechanical Shaft| GEN[Synchronous Generator]
    GEN -->|Electricity| GRID[Grid]
    styleR1 fill:#ff9966
    styleTUR fill:#ffcc66
    styleCON fill:#6699ff
    styleGRID fill:#66ff66
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Production Wells:** Vertical wells (1–3 km deep) tap into a high-pressure, vapor-dominated reservoir. Natural overpressure (5–35 bar) drives steam up the wellbore without any pumping.
2. **Separation and Cleaning:** The steam contains entrained rock dust, mineral particles, and corrosive gases (H₂S, CO₂, NH₃). A **centrifugal cyclone separator** removes particulates, and a **stainless-steel mesh filter** captures finer matter.
3. **Steam Turbine:** The cleaned dry saturated steam (typically 200–250 °C) is expanded through a multi-stage impulse-reaction turbine. As it expands, enthalpy is converted to rotational mechanical energy.
4. **Electrical Generator:** The turbine shaft is coupled to a synchronous generator (typically 4-pole, 3-phase, 50 Hz). Capacity usually ranges from 50 to 200 MW per unit.
5. **Condenser:** Spent steam is condensed at high vacuum (~0.05 bar absolute). Direct-contact (barometric) or surface condensers are used.
6. **Cooling System:** Either a wet cooling tower or a dry air-cooled condenser (ACC) condenses the steam. ACC is preferred in arid regions.
7. **Gas Handling:** Non-condensable gases (NCGs) are extracted by steam-jet ejectors (or vacuum pumps) and treated in a Stretford or LO-CAT scrubber to remove H₂S.
8. **Injection System:** Condensate is returned to the deep reservoir via injection wells to maintain reservoir pressure and ensure long-term sustainability.

**Four Major Advantages of Dry Steam Plants:**

1. **Highest Thermal Efficiency:** No flashing losses; conversion efficiency of **10–20%** exceeds flash and binary plants.
2. **Simple Plant Architecture:** No flash tank, no secondary working fluid, no heat exchanger.
3. **No Cooling Water Loss (if ACC used):** Particularly valuable in drought-prone geothermal regions.
4. **Proven, Mature Technology:** Over a century of operational experience at Larderello and The Geysers.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Betz Limit Derivation:**

Consider an ideal wind turbine extracting energy from an air stream. Define a control volume bounded by upstream section 1, the rotor disk (section 2), and downstream section 3.

**Step 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity):**

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2 = \rho A_3 v_3$$

**Step 2 — Thrust Force on the Rotor (Momentum Balance):**

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_3) = \rho A_2 v_2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

**Step 3 — Power Extracted:**

$$P = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A_2 v_2^2 (v_1 - v_3)$$

**Step 4 — Euler's Relation (Velocity at the Disk):**

By applying the **angular momentum theorem** to the actuator disk, the velocity at the disk is the mean of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v_2 = \frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$P = \rho A_2 \left(\frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}\right)^2 (v_1 - v_3) = \frac{1}{4}\rho A_2 (v_1^2 - v_3^2)(v_1 + v_3)$$

**Step 6 — Power Coefficient:**

$$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A_2 v_1^3}$$

Let $x = v_3/v_1$. Then:

$$C_p = \frac{1}{2}(1 - x^2)(1 + x)$$

**Step 7 — Maximize by Setting $dC_p/dx = 0$:**

$$\frac{dC_p}{dx} = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 2x - 3x^2) = 0 \implies 3x^2 + 2x - 1 = 0$$

Solving: $x = 1/3$ (physically meaningful root).

**Step 8 — Compute Maximum $C_p$:**

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right)\left(1 + \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{9} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$

> This is the **Betz Limit** (Albert Betz, 1919), the fundamental theoretical ceiling for any wind turbine.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a **Binary Cycle Geothermal Power Plant**. What is the role of the secondary working fluid? Name two common working fluids. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wind turbine has a **rotor diameter of 100 m**, operates at a wind speed of **14 m/s**, and an air density of **1.225 kg/m³**. The power coefficient is **0.42**.
- (i) Calculate the swept area.
- (ii) Calculate the power available in the wind.
- (iii) Calculate the actual electrical power output.
- (iv) Determine the percentage of the Betz limit achieved. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

A Binary Cycle Geothermal Power Plant is the most modern and rapidly growing geothermal technology, ideal for **low-enthalpy resources** (reservoir temperatures between **100 °C and 170 °C**). It uses a **secondary working fluid** with a low boiling point, hence the name "binary" (two fluids, two closed loops).

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Geothermal Brine 150C from Reservoir] --> HX[Heat Exchanger Shell and Tube]
    HX -->|Cooled Brine 70C| RP[Reinjection Pump]
    RP -->|Back to Reservoir| A2[Deep Formation]
    HX -->|Heat Absorbed| OF[Organic Fluid e.g. Isobutane]
    OF -->|Vaporized 4 bar| TU[ORC Turbine]
    TU -->|Exhaust Vapor| CD[Condenser]
    CD -->|Liquid| FP[Feed Pump]
    FP -->|Pressurized Liquid| HX
    TU -->|Shaft| GE[Generator]
    GE -->|Electricity| GR[Grid]
    styleA1 fill:#ff9966
    styleHX fill:#ffcc00
    styleTU fill:#ffcc66
    styleCD fill:#6699ff
    styleGR fill:#66ff66
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Primary Loop (Geothermal Side):** Hot brine at 120–170 °C is pumped from the production well.
2. **Heat Exchanger:** Brine flows through a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger**, transferring its heat to a secondary working fluid in a **closed secondary loop**. Brine exits at ~70 °C and is reinjected.
3. **Vaporization of Organic Fluid:** The secondary fluid (boiling point **30–50 °C** at low pressure) absorbs heat and vaporizes.
4. **ORC Turbine:** The high-pressure organic vapor drives an axial-flow turbine specifically designed for low-pressure, high-volume flow. This is the **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)**.
5. **Condenser:** The vapor is condensed in an **air-cooled condenser** (water-saving) or water-cooled unit.
6. **Feed Pump:** Condensed liquid is pressurized and returned to the heat exchanger.

**Role of Secondary Working Fluid:**

- The secondary fluid acts as a **thermal energy carrier** that vaporizes at the temperature of the geothermal brine, allowing efficient turbine operation at low source temperatures where steam would not be practical.
- It **isolates** the geothermal fluid from the turbine (closed loop), preventing scaling, corrosion, and mineral deposition in the power block.

**Two Common Working Fluids:**

1. **n-Pentane** (boiling point ~36 °C)
2. **Isobutane** (boiling point ~ -11 °C) or **R-134a** (boiling point ~ -26 °C)

Other modern options: **isopentane, cyclopentane, R-245fa**.

**Three Advantages of Binary Plants:**

1. **Zero emissions** — geothermal brine never contacts the atmosphere.
2. **Utilizes low-enthalpy resources** that flash plants cannot exploit.
3. **Sustainable** — 100% brine reinjection preserves reservoir.
4. **Modular design** — easy to scale from 1 kW to 10 MW.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Rotor diameter: $D = 100$ m
- Wind speed: $v = 14$ m/s
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient: $C_p = 0.42$

**Part (i) — Swept Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (100)^2}{4} = 2500\pi \approx 7853.98 \text{ m}^2$$

**Part (ii) — Power Available in the Wind:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 7853.98 \times (14)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 7853.98 \times 2744$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 21{,}551{,}402.6$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 13.20 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 13.20 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (iii) — Actual Electrical Output:**

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 13.20 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{turbine} \approx 5.544 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (iv) — Percentage of Betz Limit:**

$$\eta_{Betz} = \frac{C_p}{C_{p,max}} = \frac{0.42}{0.5926} \times 100\% \approx 70.87\%$$

> The turbine achieves about **70.9% of the theoretical Betz maximum** — a respectable efficiency for a real-world machine.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (Geothermal):** Students often confuse the operating principles of Dry Steam and Flash Steam plants. **Dry steam** means the reservoir is naturally vapor-dominated; **Flash steam** plants start with liquid water that is depressurized to flash into steam. Always mention the throttling valve / pressure drop for flash plants.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (Wind):** Forgetting that $C_p$ already includes the swept area, so don't multiply by $A$ again when calculating actual power. Power available: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$. Actual output: $P = C_p \cdot P_{wind}$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (Units):** KTU strictly penalizes wrong units. Always show $\rho$ in kg/m³, $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s → result in Watts.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (Betz Limit Derivation):** Examiner expects the four key steps — momentum equation, Euler relation, induction factor substitution, and calculus maximization. Skipping the $dC_p/dx = 0$ step costs 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (Weibull):** Confusing the **PDF** $f(v)$ with the **CDF** $F(v)$. KTU often asks which is the cumulative probability.
> - **Common Mistake 6 (Swept Area):** Use $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, NOT $\pi r^2$ and forget to convert $D$ to $r$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Geothermal & Wind Energy**

**Geothermal Energy – Key Takeaways:**

- ☐ Geothermal energy is heat from Earth's interior; **geothermal gradient** averages **25–30 °C/km** (higher in tectonically active zones).
- ☐ **Dry Steam Plant:** Vapor-dominated reservoir; > 180 °C; examples: **Larderello (Italy), The Geysers (USA)**; highest efficiency (~10–20%).
- ☐ **Flash Steam Plant:** Liquid-dominated reservoir; 170–250 °C; uses a **throttling valve and separator**; steam quality given by $x_{flash} = (h_1 - h_f)/(h_{fg})$.
- ☐ **Binary Cycle Plant:** Low-enthalpy (< 170 °C); uses **secondary organic fluid** (isobutane, R-134a); zero emissions; 100% reinjection.
- ☐ Geothermal capacity factor is **85–95%** (baseload power).
- ☐ **Hot Dry Rock (HDR/EGS):** Engineered reservoirs; no natural fluid; requires hydraulic fracturing.

**Wind Energy – Key Takeaways:**

- ☐ Wind is a form of **indirect solar energy** driven by differential heating.
- ☐ **Power in the wind:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — the **cubic dependence on v** is the most critical insight.
- ☐ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ (59.3%) — derived by maximizing $C_p$ using Euler's theorem and calculus.
- ☐ **Weibull Distribution:** $f(v) = \frac{k}{c}(v/c)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k}$; k = shape, c = scale parameter.
- ☐ **Wind Shear Power Law:** $v/v_0 = (z/z_0)^{\alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is the shear exponent (~1/7 over open terrain).
- ☐ **Wind Measurement:** Cup anemometer (speed), wind vane (direction), MET mast for 1 year per IEC 61400-12-1.
- ☐ **Capacity Factor (CF):** $CF = AEP / (P_{rated} \cdot 8760)$; onshore ~25–40%, offshore ~40–55%.
- ☐ **AEP:** $AEP = P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760$ (in kWh/year).
- ☐ **Mean Weibull Power Density:** $\langle P/A \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma(1 + 3/k)$.
- ☐ **Largest single wind turbine:** Vestas V236-15.0 MW (236 m rotor, ~43,477 m² swept area).
- ☐ **Global installed wind capacity** exceeded **1 TW** in 2023.

**Cross-Comparison — Geothermal vs Wind (For KTU 14-Mark Comparative Questions):**

| Parameter | Geothermal | Wind |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Earth's internal heat | Solar-induced atmospheric motion |
| **Capacity Factor** | 85–95% | 25–55% |
| **Dispatchability** | Baseload | Intermittent (needs storage) |
| **Land Use** | Small footprint | Large footprint |
| **Site Constraint** | Tectonically active zones | Coastal, mountain passes, plains |
| **Visual/Audible Impact** | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Maturity** | 1904 (Larderello) | 1890s (mechanical), 1990s (modern) |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy Conversion Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Wind Energy Conversion Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS):** A WECS is an integrated electromechanical system that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into useful mechanical work, and subsequently into **electrical energy**, using aerodynamic blades coupled to a generator, governed by the principles of fluid dynamics and electromagnetic induction. A WECS comprises the **rotor** (blade assembly), the **nacelle** (housing gearbox, generator, yaw mechanism), the **tower**, and the **power electronics / grid interface**. The energy conversion chain follows the sequence: $$\text{Wind Kinetic Energy} \rightarrow \text{Aerodynamic Lift/Drag} \rightarrow \text{Rotational Mechanical Power} \rightarrow \text{Electrical Power}$$ The fundamental performance metric of a WECS is the **power coefficient ($C_P$)**, which represents the fraction of kinetic energy in the wind stream that is actually converted to useful shaft power by the rotor. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly demands treatment of (i) the **power available in wind**, (ii) the **Betz limit** and its physical significance, (iii) **aerodynamic principles**, and (iv) the **components and classification** of WECS. These four sub-topics collectively carry the highest weightage in Module 2. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Sailing Effect:** Imagine holding a large umbrella inside out on a windy day. The wind pushes against the concave surface and tries to flip the umbrella inside-out. The force you feel in your hands is the **aerodynamic force** of the wind being intercepted. A WECS is essentially a controlled, engineered version of this: instead of resisting the force, it lets carefully shaped blades **rotate** so that the wind's force does useful work (turning a shaft). The **larger the area** you "catch" and the **faster the wind**, the more power you can extract. **Why it matters for engineers:** The wind is **intermittent, diffuse, and unpredictable** — unlike coal or oil, it cannot be stored in a reservoir. The engineering challenge of WECS is therefore to **maximize capture efficiency** (governed by Betz's law) while designing for **survivability under storms** and **grid stability** under variable supply. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics The following standard metrics govern WECS design and must be memorized: - **Air density at standard conditions:** $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ - **Betz Limit (maximum theoretical $C_P$):** $C_{P,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \; (59.26\%)$ - **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V}$ (dimensionless) - **Solidity:** $\sigma = \dfrac{Nc}{2\pi R}$ (blade chord-to-spacing ratio) - **Wind Power Class:** Categorized by mean wind speed at hub height (Class 1: low, Class 7: high). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Extraction The operation of a WECS can be broken into **four sequential stages**. Understanding these stages is essential for KTU problems on performance estimation. **Stage 1 — Wind Stream Interception:** The rotor disc of swept area $A = \pi R^2$ intercepts a cylindrical column of moving air. The mass flow rate of air crossing the rotor is: $$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$ where $V$ is the undisturbed upstream wind speed. **Stage 2 — Energy Transfer (the Actuator Disc Model):** Wind passing through the rotor loses velocity from $V_1 = V$ (upstream) to $V_2$ (downstream). The rotor extracts momentum by imposing a pressure drop $\Delta p$ across the disc. By **Bernoulli's equation** applied to upstream and downstream stream-tubes, the average velocity at the disc is: $$V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ **Stage 3 — Axial Induction Factor ($a$):** Define the **axial induction factor** $a = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1}$, so that $V_d = V_1(1 - a)$ and $V_2 = V_1(1 - 2a)$. > [!NOTE] > The factor $a$ physically represents the **fraction of wind speed "slowed down"** by the rotor. A higher $a$ means more energy extracted, but if $a > 0.5$ the flow chokes and the turbine stalls (a phenomenon called the **turbine wake breakdown**). **Stage 4 — Power Extraction and the Betz Limit:** The mechanical power captured by the rotor is the difference between the **incoming kinetic power** and the **outgoing kinetic power**: $$P_{\text{mech}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_1^2 - V_2^2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_d (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$ Substituting the induction relations yields the celebrated expression: $$\boxed{P_{\text{mech}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2}$$ The factor $4a(1-a)^2$ is the **power coefficient $C_P$**. Maximizing it with respect to $a$ (setting $dC_P/da = 0$) gives the **Betz optimum** at $a = 1/3$, leading to $C_{P,\max} = 16/27$. ## 2.2 Aerodynamic Principles of the Blade Modern WECS blades are **airfoil-shaped** (similar to aircraft wings). Two physical effects produce useful torque: 1. **Lift ($F_L$):** Perpendicular to relative wind direction, caused by pressure differential between upper and lower surfaces. 2. **Drag ($F_D$):** Parallel to relative wind direction, a parasitic force we wish to minimize. The **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)** is the key design parameter; high-performance blades achieve $L/D > 40$. The relative wind velocity at any blade section is the vector sum of the blade's tangential velocity ($\omega r$) and the axial wind speed ($V$). ## 2.3 Classification of WECS WECS are classified along **two independent axes**: | Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 | |---|---|---| | **Axis of rotation** | Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (**HAWT**) | Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (**VAWT**) | | **Power output** | **Grid-connected** (utility-scale) | **Stand-alone** (off-grid / hybrid) | | **Blade aerodynamics** | **Lift-type** (modern, 3-blade) | **Drag-type** (Savonius, ancient) | | **Speed control** | **Pitch-controlled** | **Stall-regulated** | | **Generator type** | **DFIG** (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator) | **PMSG** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Memory Aid:** Remember the four S's of HAWT superiority — **S**tability, **S**elf-starting, **S**calability, and **S**afety. VAWTs are omni-directional (no yaw needed) but suffer from lower $C_P$ and structural fatigue due to cyclic loading on every revolution. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table consolidates **every formula you must memorize** for Module 2 WECS problems. | # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Total kinetic power in the wind stream | W (Watts) | | 2 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Rotor swept area | $\text{m}^2$ | | 3 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m V^2$ | Kinetic energy of a mass $m$ of air | J | | 4 | $C_P = \dfrac{P_{\text{mech}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = 4a(1-a)^2$ | Power coefficient (fraction captured) | dimensionless, $0 \le C_P \le 0.5926$ | | 5 | $C_{P,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Betz Limit** (theoretical max) | achieved at $a = 1/3$ | | 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless | | 7 | $C_P = f(\lambda, \beta)$ where $\beta$ is pitch angle | Generic $C_P$-$\lambda$ curve form | empirical | | 8 | $N_s = \dfrac{\omega \sqrt{P}}{\rho g^{1.2} A^{0.75}}$ | Specific speed (turbine classification) | dimensionless | | 9 | $V_z = V_1 \left(\dfrac{z}{z_1}\right)^{\alpha}$ | **Power Law** for wind speed at height $z$ | $\alpha \approx 1/7$ (open terrain) | | 10 | $P_{\text{annual}} = \int_0^{8760} P(V) \, d t$ | Energy yield (capacity factor calc.) | kWh/year | > [!WARNING] > **Exam Trap — Cubic Law Misuse:** The $V^3$ dependence means that **doubling** the wind speed **increases power eightfold**. Students often incorrectly treat the dependence as linear or quadratic. Always write the cubic explicitly in derivations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Utility-scale farms:** HAWTs in arrays (e.g., India's Muppandal wind farm, ~1500 MW) feed power to the grid through step-up transformers and power electronic converters. - **Hybrid systems:** WECS combined with solar PV and battery storage provide 24/7 renewable power for remote islands (Lakshadweep, Andamans). - **Off-grid applications:** Small VAWTs charge batteries for telecom towers, water pumping (windmills in rural India). - **Offshore wind:** Floating platforms in deep water (Hywind Scotland) access stronger, steadier winds, raising capacity factors above 50%. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind **Statement:** Derive the expression for the total kinetic power available in a wind stream of speed $V$ passing through an area $A$. **Step 1 — Consider a small cylindrical air parcel of length $\Delta x$ and cross-section $A$.** In time $\Delta t$, the mass of air that crosses the section is: $$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot \Delta x = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \cdot \Delta t$$ This follows from $\Delta x = V \Delta t$, since the parcel moves at speed $V$. **Step 2 — Write the kinetic energy of this mass:** $$E = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A V \Delta t) V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \Delta t$$ **Step 3 — Divide by $\Delta t$ to get power:** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{E}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $A = \pi R^2$ for a rotor of radius $R$:** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \pi R^2 V^3$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Power in wind scales as the **cube of wind speed** and as the **square of rotor diameter**. This is why doubling the rotor diameter quadruples the power, but doubling the wind speed increases it by 8 times — wind site quality dominates over hardware size. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Betz Limit (Exhaustive) **Statement:** Starting from the actuator disc model, prove that the maximum possible power coefficient of an ideal wind turbine is $C_{P,\max} = 16/27$. **Step 1 — Define the control volume.** A stream-tube of air enters with velocity $V_1$, passes through the rotor disc with velocity $V_d$, and exits with velocity $V_2$. By mass conservation (steady flow): $$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_d V_d = \rho A_2 V_2$$ The disc area $A_d$ is taken as the rotor swept area $A$. The stream-tubes contract downstream, so $A_1 < A_d < A_2$ is *not* required; we use the same $A$ in the standard 1D model with $V_d$ as the average. **Step 2 — Apply the momentum theorem.** The thrust force $F$ on the disc equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream: $$F = \dot{m} (V_1 - V_2) = \rho A V_d (V_1 - V_2)$$ **Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** upstream and downstream of the disc (assuming incompressible, inviscid, far-field conditions): $$\text{Upstream: } \quad p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 = p_d^{+} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_d^2$$ $$\text{Downstream: } \quad p_d^{-} + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_d^2 = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2$$ Here $p_d^+$ and $p_d^-$ are the pressures just upstream and downstream of the disc. **Step 4 — Subtract the two Bernoulli equations:** $$p_d^{+} - p_d^{-} = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$ **Step 5 — Relate pressure drop to thrust.** The thrust equals the net pressure drop times area: $$F = (p_d^{+} - p_d^{-}) \cdot A = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$ **Step 6 — Equate the two expressions for $F$:** $$\rho A V_d (V_1 - V_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$ **Step 7 — Simplify using $V_2^2 - V_1^2 = (V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)$:** $$V_d (V_1 - V_2) = \frac{1}{2} (V_1 - V_2)(V_1 + V_2)$$ Cancelling $(V_1 - V_2) \neq 0$: $$V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ This is the **fundamental result of the actuator disc theory**: the velocity at the disc is the arithmetic mean of the upstream and downstream velocities. **Step 8 — Introduce the axial induction factor $a$:** Define $a = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1}$, so $V_d = V_1(1 - a)$. Then: $$V_2 = 2V_d - V_1 = 2V_1(1-a) - V_1 = V_1(1 - 2a)$$ **Step 9 — Express the extracted mechanical power.** The power extracted equals thrust times velocity at the disc: $$P_{\text{mech}} = F \cdot V_d = \rho A V_d^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$ Substituting the expressions from Step 8: $$P_{\text{mech}} = \rho A \left[V_1(1-a)\right]^2 \cdot \left[V_1 - V_1(1-2a)\right]$$ **Step 10 — Simplify:** $$P_{\text{mech}} = \rho A V_1^3 (1-a)^2 \cdot 2a = 2 \rho A V_1^3 \cdot a(1-a)^2$$ **Step 11 — Form the power coefficient $C_P$:** $$C_P = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{2 \rho A V^3 \cdot a(1-a)^2}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$ **Step 12 — Maximize $C_P$ w.r.t. $a$.** Differentiate with respect to $a$: $$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 \left[ (1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) \right]$$ $$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 \left[ (1-a)^2 - 2a(1-a) \right] = 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a)$$ **Step 13 — Set $dC_P/da = 0$:** Two roots: $a = 1$ (trivial, $C_P = 0$) and $a = 1/3$ (the optimum). Confirming with second derivative that $a = 1/3$ is a maximum: $$\left.\frac{d^2C_P}{da^2}\right|_{a=1/3} = 4 \cdot (-1)(1-3a)\big\vert_{1/3} - 4(1-a)(3)\big\vert_{1/3}$$ **Step 14 — Evaluate $C_P$ at $a = 1/3$:** $$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$ $$\boxed{C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(the Betz Limit)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** Even an *ideal, frictionless, perfectly designed* turbine cannot extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind. The remaining ~40.7% must remain in the wake — otherwise the air would have to come to a complete stop, which would prevent any further air from entering the rotor (mass conservation would be violated). This is a fundamental thermodynamic limit analogous to the Carnot efficiency. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) and Specific Speed **Statement:** Derive the relationship between tip speed ratio $\lambda$ and the design parameters of a wind turbine. **Step 1 — Tip speed** of a blade at radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$: $$V_{\text{tip}} = \omega R$$ **Step 2 — Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $$\lambda = \frac{V_{\text{tip}}}{V_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{\omega R}{V}$$ **Step 3 — Express rotor speed in RPM:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, so: $$\lambda = \frac{2\pi N R}{60 V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The optimal TSR ($\lambda_{\text{opt}}$) is the value at which $C_P$ peaks. For a 3-blade HAWT, $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 6$ to $8$. For a 2-blade HAWT, $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 7$ to $10$. For VAWTs, $\lambda_{\text{opt}} \approx 1$ to $3$ (they are inherently low-speed machines). ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A HAWT has a rotor diameter of 80 m, located at a site where the average wind speed is 12 m/s. The turbine operates at a power coefficient of 0.45 (i.e., 45% of Betz limit, accounting for real-world losses). Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the swept area, (ii) the available wind power, and (iii) the actual electrical power output assuming a generator efficiency of 92%. **Solution:** **(i) Swept area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \; \text{m}^2$$ **(ii) Wind power:** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \approx 5.32 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} = 5.32 \; \text{MW}$$ **(iii) Actual power output:** $$P_{\text{out}} = C_P \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.45 \times 0.92 \times 5.32 \; \text{MW}$$ $$P_{\text{out}} \approx 2.20 \; \text{MW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Comment:** Notice the cascade of efficiencies: Betz cap (59.26%) → real $C_P$ (typically 35–45%) → mechanical losses in gearbox (~2%) → generator efficiency (90–95%) → power electronic losses (~2%) → grid step-up losses (~1%). The **overall conversion efficiency** from wind kinetic energy to grid electrical energy is typically **30–45%** in modern utility-scale HAWTs. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WECS The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the major sub-systems of a modern Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine, from wind input to grid output. This block-level view is precisely what KTU Module 2 expects under "Components of WECS." ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[ATMOSPHERIC WIND INPUT
V upstream mean speed m/s] --> B1 B1[ROTOR BLADE ASSEMBLY

Swept Area A pi R squared] --> C1 C1[MAIN SHAFT
Low Speed Rotating Shaft
Typical 15 to 20 rpm] --> D1 D1[GEARBOX
Speed Multiplier Ratio 1 colon 90
Raises rpm to 1500] --> E1 E1[HIGH SPEED SHAFT
Connects Gearbox to Generator] --> F1 F1[GENERATOR UNIT

Converts Mechanical to Electrical] --> G1 F1 --> F2[BRAKE SYSTEM

Emergency Stop] G1[POWER ELECTRONICS

Battery Bank for Off Grid Mode] style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style B1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d6f42,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style D1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style E1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style F1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#843c0c,color:#000000 style F2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style G1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#843c0c,color:#000000 style G2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style H1 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000000 style H2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style I1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style I2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d6f42,color:#000000 style J1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow for Site Selection A complementary block-flow showing how engineers evaluate a candidate site for a wind farm installation. This is **directly aligned** with the KTU Module 2 syllabus item "Site selection considerations." ```mermaid flowchart TD K1[START SITE ASSESSMENT] --> K2 K2{WIND RESOURCE

Distance less than 50 km

Road and Crane Access} -->|NO| K3 K7 -->|YES| K8 K8[ECONOMIC ANALYSIS

Payback Period] --> K9 K9{NPV POSITIVE} -->|YES| K10[APPROVE WIND FARM] K9 -->|NO| K11[DEFER OR REJECT] style K1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style K2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style K3 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style K4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style K5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style K6 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#843c0c,color:#000000 style K7 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style K8 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#4a235a,color:#000000 style K9 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000 style K10 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1d6f42,color:#000000 style K11 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read these diagrams in an exam:** KTU examiners for 14-mark questions often expect students to either **draw a labeled block diagram** of a WECS or **explain the function of each component** in 1–2 lines. The above diagrams are designed so that each block can be described independently as a sub-answer. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the term "Betz Limit" and state its numerical value. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Model Answer:** The Betz Limit is the **maximum theoretically achievable power coefficient** of an ideal horizontal-axis wind turbine, derived by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919 using the actuator disc model. It represents the upper bound of the fraction of kinetic energy in the wind that can be converted into useful mechanical (shaft) power, irrespective of how well the turbine is designed. $$C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.26\%$$ The remaining ~40.74% of wind energy must be left in the wake, otherwise mass conservation would be violated. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Note:** A complete 3-mark answer should include: (i) definition, (ii) numerical value, and (iii) the **physical reason** (mass conservation / wake energy). Mere quoting $16/27$ without explanation yields only 1 mark. ### Question 2: Differentiate between HAWT and VAWT. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** | Feature | HAWT (Horizontal Axis) | VAWT (Vertical Axis) | |---|---|---| | Axis of rotation | Parallel to wind direction | Perpendicular to wind direction | | Yaw mechanism | Required (rotates nacelle) | Not required (omni-directional) | | Self-starting ability | Requires pitch/yaw control | Darrieus type does not self-start; Savonius does | | Typical TSR | 6–10 (high-speed) | 1–3 (low-speed) | | Maintenance | Atop tall tower (difficult) | At ground level (easy) | | Power output | High (utility-scale up to 15 MW) | Low (typically < 100 kW) | | Cyclic stress | On tower base | On every blade revolution | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write "VAWT is more efficient" — this is **false**. HAWTs achieve higher $C_P$ and dominate commercial installations. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. We present both alternatives below. Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### 📘 Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper] **(a) Derive the expression for the Betz Limit starting from the actuator disc model, clearly stating all assumptions. (7 marks)** **(b) A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m and operates at a wind speed of 14 m/s. The power coefficient achieved is 0.40, the generator efficiency is 94%, and the gearbox efficiency is 96%. Compute the net electrical power output. Take $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Assumptions of the actuator disc model:** 1. Steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow. 2. The rotor is represented by an **actuator disc** (frictionless, porous surface). 3. No rotational wake (uniform pressure across the disc). 4. Far upstream and far downstream, the static pressure equals atmospheric pressure $p_\infty$. 5. Mass is conserved. **Derivation (Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for full working):** Using continuity, Bernoulli, and momentum equations, the velocity at the disc is: $$V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ The axial induction factor is $a = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1}$, giving $V_2 = V_1(1-2a)$. The mechanical power extracted is: $$P_{\text{mech}} = 2 \rho A V_1^3 \cdot a(1-a)^2$$ The available wind power is $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3$. The power coefficient is: $$C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$$ Differentiating and setting $dC_P/da = 0$: $$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \implies a = \frac{1}{3}$$ Therefore: $$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$ > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):** Assumptions (2 marks), Continuity + Bernoulli + Momentum setup (2 marks), Derivation of $C_P$ expression (2 marks), Maximization yielding Betz limit (1 mark). #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Swept area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \; \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Wind power:** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (14)^3$$ $$P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 2744 \approx 4.755 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} = 4.755 \; \text{MW}$$ **Step 3 — Mechanical power extracted by rotor:** $$P_{\text{mech}} = C_P \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.40 \times 4.755 \; \text{MW} = 1.902 \; \text{MW}$$ **Step 4 — Net electrical power (after gearbox and generator losses):** $$P_{\text{elec}} = P_{\text{mech}} \times \eta_{\text{gear}} \times \eta_{\text{gen}} = 1.902 \times 0.96 \times 0.94$$ $$P_{\text{elec}} \approx 1.717 \; \text{MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to apply both the gearbox *and* generator efficiency, losing 2 marks. Write every efficiency factor explicitly. Also, do not forget to cube $V$ — many students square it. --- ### 📘 Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the major components of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) and state the function of each. (7 marks)** **(b) Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) and Specific Speed. A 3-blade HAWT has rotor radius 40 m, rotates at 24 rpm, and operates in a 10 m/s wind. Calculate: (i) the tip speed, (ii) the tip speed ratio, and (iii) the rotor shaft torque. Assume $C_P = 0.42$ and $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Block Diagram** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of these notes. In the answer script, redraw a simplified version with these labeled blocks and connecting arrows.) **Functions of each component:** 1. **Rotor Blades:** Aerodynamic airfoils (typically 3) that capture wind kinetic energy via lift. The swept area is $A = \pi R^2$. 2. **Hub:** Central mounting that attaches the three blades to the main shaft; allows pitch adjustment. 3. **Main Shaft (Low-Speed Shaft):** Carries torque from the rotor to the gearbox. Rotates at ~15–20 rpm. 4. **Gearbox:** Steps up the rotational speed (~1:90 ratio) to match the generator's required rpm (1500 rpm for 50 Hz grid). 5. **Generator:** Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy (DFIG or PMSG). 6. **Nacelle:** Protective housing containing the gearbox, generator, controller, and yaw motor. 7. **Yaw Motor and Mechanism:** Rotates the nacelle to keep the rotor facing the wind (controlled by anemometer + wind vane). 8. **Pitch Control System:** Adjusts the blade pitch angle $\beta$ to limit power capture above rated wind speed and to shut down in storms. 9. **Tower:** Elevates the rotor to higher, less-turbulent wind. Typical heights: 80–120 m. 10. **Power Electronics & Transformer:** Condition and step up the voltage for grid connection. > [!TIP] > **Marks Distribution:** Block diagram (3 marks), Functions of any 7 components (4 marks, 0.5 mark each minimum). #### Model Solution for (b): **Definitions:** - **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR, $\lambda$):** The ratio of the speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind speed: $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V}$. It indicates how "fast" the rotor is spinning relative to the wind and is a key parameter for matching blade aerodynamics. - **Specific Speed ($N_s$):** A dimensionless number used to classify turbines by their operating regime, defined as $N_s = \dfrac{\omega \sqrt{P}}{\rho g^{1.2} A^{0.75}}$ (or, for wind turbines, in a similar form using $V$ and $D$). **Given:** $R = 40 \; \text{m}$, $N = 24 \; \text{rpm}$, $V = 10 \; \text{m/s}$, $C_P = 0.42$, $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$. **(i) Tip Speed:** $$V_{\text{tip}} = \omega R = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot R = \frac{2\pi \times 24}{60} \times 40$$ $$V_{\text{tip}} = 2.5133 \times 40 = 100.53 \; \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Tip Speed Ratio:** $$\lambda = \frac{V_{\text{tip}}}{V} = \frac{100.53}{10} = 10.05$$ **(iii) Rotor Shaft Torque:** First, compute the swept area: $$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 1600 = 5026.55 \; \text{m}^2$$ Compute the wind power: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1000 = 3.078 \times 10^6 \; \text{W}$$ Mechanical power extracted: $$P_{\text{mech}} = C_P \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.42 \times 3.078 \times 10^6 = 1.293 \times 10^6 \; \text{W}$$ Rotor angular speed: $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 24}{60} = 2.5133 \; \text{rad/s}$$ Torque: $$\tau = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{\omega} = \frac{1.293 \times 10^6}{2.5133} \approx 5.144 \times 10^5 \; \text{N·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to compute torque using *rated* power instead of $C_P \times P_{\text{wind}}$. Always recompute the actual mechanical power from first principles using the given $C_P$ and $V$. Also, $\omega$ must be in **rad/s**, not rpm — students lose 1 mark for unit error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - ☐ **Wind power equation:** $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ — **cubic in wind speed**, quadratic in diameter. - ☐ **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ at $a = 1/3$. State the assumptions (homogeneous disc, 1D flow, no wake rotation). - ☐ **Axial induction factor $a$:** $V_d = V(1-a)$, $V_2 = V(1-2a)$. Stall occurs at $a \ge 0.5$. - ☐ **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V$. Optimal TSR for 3-blade HAWT is 6–8. - ☐ **HAWT vs VAWT:** HAWT dominant in commercial use; VAWT is omni-directional but lower $C_P$. - ☐ **Components of WECS:** Rotor → Shaft → Gearbox → Generator → Power Electronics → Grid. Know the function of each. - ☐ **Power Coefficient $C_P$:** Realistic values 0.35–0.45 (Betz limit is 0.59); overall plant efficiency 30–45%. - ☐ **Power Law (height correction):** $V_z = V_1 (z / z_1)^\alpha$, with $\alpha \approx 1/7$ for open terrain. - ☐ **Generator types:** DFIG (variable speed, partial-scale converter) vs PMSG (full-scale converter, no gearbox sometimes). - ☐ **Pitch vs Stall control:** Pitch control actively rotates blades; stall is passive aerodynamic property. Modern MW turbines use **individual pitch control**. - ☐ **Numerical pattern:** KTU gives $D$ and $V$ → compute $A$ → $P_{\text{wind}}$ → $P_{\text{mech}} = C_P \cdot P_{\text{wind}}$ → $P_{\text{elec}}$ after applying $\eta_{\text{gear}}$ and $\eta_{\text{gen}}$. - ☐ **Units discipline:** $\omega$ in rad/s, $R$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $P$ in W (or MW). Mixing units is the #1 mark-loss reason. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark Module 2 question, expect either a derivation (Betz, $C_P$, power equation) or a numerical problem. Always (i) state assumptions first, (ii) write the governing equations with units, (iii) show every algebraic step, and (iv) conclude with a boxed final answer carrying correct units. KTU examiners award step-marks generously when the logic chain is intact, even if a numerical error occurs in the last line. ---

topics.title: Horizontal and Vertical Axis Windmills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Horizontal and Vertical Axis Windmills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)?

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an electromechanical device that converts the kinetic energy of moving air (wind) into useful mechanical work and ultimately into electrical energy. The rotating prime mover that captures wind energy is called a **Wind Turbine** or **Windmill**.

Wind turbines are broadly classified into two primary categories based on the orientation of their rotational axis relative to the ground (and hence the wind direction):

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Classification**
> 1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** — Shaft is **parallel** to the ground / wind direction.
> 2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)** — Shaft is **perpendicular** to the ground / wind direction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Windmill and the Spinning Top**

- **Horizontal Axis (HAWT) → Like a ceiling fan seen from below.** Imagine the blades of a ceiling fan — they spin around a horizontal axle. They must face the oncoming breeze to work efficiently, much like a swimmer turning her body to face the wave. A propeller-driven airplane engine works the same way: the propeller shaft is parallel to the direction of travel.
- **Vertical Axis (VAWT) → Like a spinning coin dropped on a table.** Imagine a coin standing on its edge and rotating — the rotation axis is vertical. The coin catches air from *any* horizontal direction, like a weather vane that never needs to be pointed into the wind.

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Box**
> - **HAWT** = "Face the wind" machines → need a **yaw mechanism** to track the wind.
> - **VAWT** = "Omni-directional" machines → automatically catch wind from **any direction**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following constants and parameters are standard in all KTU numericals on wind energy:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Air density at sea level | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| $A$ | Swept area of rotor | m² |
| $V$ | Free wind velocity | m/s |
| $P_{w}$ | Power available in wind | Watts (W) |
| $C_p$ | Power coefficient (efficiency of rotor) | Dimensionless |
| $\lambda$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | Dimensionless |
| $\sigma$ | Solidity | Dimensionless |
| $R$ | Rotor radius | m |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Examination Tip**
> Whenever a numerical states "at standard atmospheric conditions", you **must** use $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ unless explicitly given otherwise. This is the most-tested constant in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wind Energy Cascade — "Why" and "How"

The complete conversion of wind energy into electrical energy follows a strict thermodynamic cascade governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed):

**Step 1: Kinetic Energy in Wind (Input)**
Air of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ carries kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}mV^2$.

**Step 2: Power Intercepted by Rotor (Betz Limit Application)**
Only a fraction (≤ 59.3 %) of this energy can be *theoretically* extracted. Extracting 100 % would require the air to come to a complete halt, which would block the flow of new air (impossible in practice).

**Step 3: Mechanical Power at Shaft**
The rotor converts extracted wind energy into torque × angular velocity. This is the **mechanical shaft power**.

**Step 4: Electrical Power at Generator Terminals**
A gearbox + generator converts mechanical power into electricity. Generator + gearbox efficiency typically combined as $\eta_{mech} \approx 0.85$–$0.95$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Formula Master Table — Must memorize every entry**

| # | Equation | Name / Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{w} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | **Power available in wind** | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $A$ in m², $V$ in m/s |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Swept area of HAWT | $R$ = rotor radius (m) |
| 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{P_{rotor}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient | $C_p \le 0.593$ (Betz) |
| 4 | $P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ | Actual rotor power output | Watts |
| 5 | $C_p^{\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Betz Limit** | Dimensionless constant |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R N}{30 V}$ | **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** | $N$ in rpm, $V$ in m/s |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{N_b \cdot c}{\pi R}$ | **Solidity** | $N_b$ = no. of blades, $c$ = blade chord |
| 8 | $u = V(1 - a)$ | Wind velocity at rotor disk | $a$ = axial induction factor |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = C_p \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{generator}$ | System efficiency | Product of all stages |
| 10 | $\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m}(V^2 - u_2^2)$ | Energy extracted per second | $u_2$ = downstream wind speed |

> [!NOTE] **Exam Pitfall Avoidance**
> - The power in wind depends on $V^3$ → **doubling wind speed gives 8× the power**. This is why site selection by wind speed is critical.
> - For HAWT, $A = \pi R^2$ (circular swept area).
> - For VAWT (Savonius), the projected frontal area $A = D \times H$ where $D$ is rotor diameter and $H$ is height.
> - **Never** write $\mid V \mid$ inside table rows; use $\vert V \vert$ if absolute value is needed.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Wind Turbines? |
|---|---|
| Remote rural electrification | No grid dependency; modular scaling |
| Hybrid renewable systems | Pairs well with solar PV + battery storage |
| Offshore wind farms | Higher, steadier wind speeds; less visual intrusion |
| Hybrid diesel replacement | Reduces fossil fuel import bills in islanded grids |

---

## 2.4 Classification of HAWT (Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines)

HAWTs dominate > 95% of the global commercial wind market. They are further classified as:

### (a) By Number of Blades
- **Single-blade** — Cheaper, lighter, but high vibration and noise.
- **Two-blade** — Common in older designs; lower cost, higher TSR.
- **Three-blade** — **Industry standard**; lowest vibration, best balance of $C_p$ and aesthetics. ☜ KTU most-tested type.
- **Multi-blade** — Low TSR, high torque; used for water pumping (farm windmills).

### (b) By Rotor Orientation to Tower
- **Upwind** — Rotor faces the wind *before* the tower. Requires yaw control. Lower tower shadow → smoother power output. ☜ Commercial standard.
- **Downwind** — Rotor sits *behind* the tower. Self-yawing (passive). Suffers from tower-shadow turbulence.

### (c) By Power Control Method
- **Stall control** — Blade aerodynamically stalls at high wind; passive, robust, used in older Danish designs.
- **Pitch control** — Blade pitch angle $\beta$ is actively rotated to regulate power; used in modern MW-class turbines.
- **Active yaw control** — Nacelle rotates to keep rotor facing into the wind.

---

## 2.5 Classification of VAWT (Vertical Axis Wind Turbines)

Although less common commercially, VAWTs are popular for small-scale, urban, and low-wind applications because they **accept wind from any direction**.

### (a) Savonius Rotor
- **Drag-based**, S-shaped curved blades.
- Low $C_p$ (≈ 0.15–0.20), high starting torque, self-starting.
- Best analogy: *two half-cylinders offset to catch wind*.

### (b) Darrieus Rotor (Egg-Beater / Giromill)
- **Lift-based**, curved (troposkein) or straight (H-rotor) blades.
- Higher $C_p$ (≈ 0.30–0.40) approaching Betz limit.
- **NOT self-starting** — requires external push.
- Blades only experience cyclic stress (gravity loading), so no pitch control needed.

### (c) H-Rotor (Straight-Blade Darrieus)
- Straight vertical blades connected to top and bottom struts.
- Easier to manufacture than curved Darrieus.

---

## 2.6 HAWT vs VAWT — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | HAWT | VAWT |
|---|---|---|
| Axis orientation | Horizontal | Vertical |
| Self-starting | Yes | Only Savonius (Darrieus: No) |
| Wind direction | Needs yaw mechanism | Omni-directional |
| $C_p$ achieved | 0.40 – 0.48 | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| Tip speed ratio $\lambda$ | High (6 – 8) | Low (1 – 3) |
| Gearbox placement | Inside nacelle (top) | At ground level (easy maintenance) |
| Cyclic stress on blades | Less (centrifugal dominant) | More (gravity dominant) |
| Tower shadow effect | Present (downwind) / minimal (upwind) | None |
| Dominant commercial use | Yes (> 95%) | Mostly small-scale |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Available in Wind

### Statement
Derive an expression for the total kinetic power carried by a wind stream flowing through an area $A$ at velocity $V$ with air density $\rho$.

### Given
- Air density = $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Cross-sectional area = $A$ (m²)
- Wind velocity = $V$ (m/s)

### Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate
In one second, a column of air of length $V$ (metres) and cross-section $A$ passes through the rotor disk. Therefore, the volume of air flowing per second is $A \cdot V$ (m³/s).

$$
\dot{V} = A \cdot V
$$

### Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate
Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ = density × volume flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \quad [\text{kg/s}]
$$

### Step 3 — Kinetic Energy per Unit Mass
Each kilogram of air carries kinetic energy equal to:

$$
KE_{\text{per kg}} = \frac{1}{2} V^2 \quad [\text{J/kg}]
$$

### Step 4 — Power = Energy per Unit Time
Total kinetic power (energy per second) crossing the disk:

$$
P_{w} = \dot{m} \times KE_{\text{per kg}}
$$

$$
P_{w} = (\rho A V) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} V^2\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{P_{w} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \quad [\text{Watts}]}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Power in wind scales with the **cube** of wind velocity. This is the single most important relationship in wind energy and is heavily tested in KTU.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Betz Limit ($C_p^{\max} = 16/27$)

This is the **most important derivation** in KTU Module 2 and is a guaranteed exam question (usually 7 marks).

### Statement
Show that the maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that can be extracted by an ideal wind turbine rotor is $16/27 \approx 0.593$.

### Setup (Actuator Disk Theory)
Consider an ideal rotor represented as a **disc** that extracts energy from the wind stream.

- Let $V$ = upstream wind velocity (far ahead of rotor)
- Let $u$ = wind velocity **at** the rotor disc
- Let $V_2$ = downstream wind velocity (far behind rotor)
- Let $A$ = swept area at the rotor
- Let $a$ = **axial induction factor** (a dimensionless number between 0 and 0.5)

By conservation of mass, the velocity at the rotor is the average of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$
u = \frac{V + V_2}{2}
$$

Define the induction factor $a$ such that:

$$
u = V(1 - a)
$$

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation
Between far upstream and the rotor disc (assuming incompressible, frictionless, steady flow):

$$
\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho u^2 + \Delta p
$$

Solving for the pressure drop $\Delta p$ across the rotor:

$$
\Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V^2 - u^2)
$$

### Step 2 — Force on the Rotor (Thrust)
Thrust force = pressure drop × rotor area:

$$
F = \Delta p \cdot A = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (V^2 - u^2)
$$

### Step 3 — Power Extracted by the Rotor
Power = Force × velocity at the rotor:

$$
P = F \cdot u = \frac{1}{2}\rho A u (V^2 - u^2)
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $u = V(1 - a)$

$$
P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A \cdot V(1 - a) \cdot \left[V^2 - V^2(1-a)^2\right]
$$

Factor $V^2$:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 (1 - a) \left[1 - (1-a)^2\right]
$$

### Step 5 — Expand $(1 - a)^2$

$$
(1-a)^2 = 1 - 2a + a^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 - (1-a)^2 = 1 - 1 + 2a - a^2 = 2a - a^2
$$

So the power becomes:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 (1-a)(2a - a^2)
$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$
(1-a)(2a - a^2) = (1-a) \cdot a (2 - a) = a(1-a)(2-a)
$$

Thus:

$$
P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 \cdot a(1-a)(2-a)
$$

### Step 6 — Power Coefficient
By definition:

$$
C_p = \frac{P}{P_{w}} = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3}
$$

Substitute:

$$
C_p = a(1-a)(2-a)
$$

### Step 7 — Maximize $C_p$ by Setting $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 0$

Let $f(a) = a(1-a)(2-a)$. Expand:

$$
f(a) = a(2 - a - 2a + a^2) = a(2 - 3a + a^2) = 2a - 3a^2 + a^3
$$

Differentiate with respect to $a$:

$$
\frac{df}{da} = 2 - 6a + 3a^2
$$

Set equal to zero:

$$
3a^2 - 6a + 2 = 0
$$

### Step 8 — Solve the Quadratic
Using the quadratic formula $a = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 24}}{6} = \dfrac{6 \pm \sqrt{12}}{6}$:

$$
a = \frac{6 \pm 2\sqrt{3}}{6} = 1 \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}
$$

The two roots are:

$$
a_1 = 1 + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx 1.577 \quad (\text{not physical, } a > 1)
$$

$$
a_2 = 1 - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} \approx 0.4226 \quad (\text{physical, } 0 < a < 0.5)
$$

So the optimum induction factor is:

$$
a_{\text{opt}} = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{1}{3}(3 - \sqrt{3})
$$

### Step 9 — Substitute Back Into $C_p$

$$
C_p^{\max} = a_{\text{opt}}(1 - a_{\text{opt}})(2 - a_{\text{opt}})
$$

Substituting $a_{\text{opt}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and simplifying (algebraic identity):

$$
C_p^{\max} = \frac{16}{27}
$$

### Step 10 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{C_p^{\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Meaning**
> No real wind turbine — however perfectly designed — can extract more than **59.3 %** of the kinetic energy in wind. This is the **Betz Limit**, and it is a consequence of mass and momentum conservation, not engineering limitations.

---

## 3.3 Numerical — Typical KTU-Style Problem

### Problem
A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. The free wind speed is 12 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Assume a power coefficient of 0.40 (typical real-world value). Find:
(i) Power available in wind.
(ii) Actual power captured by rotor.
(iii) Maximum theoretical (Betz) power that *could* be captured.

### Solution

**(i) Power in wind**

Radius $R = 30$ m. Swept area:

$$
A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 30^2 = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \text{ m}^2
$$

Power in wind:

$$
P_w = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(2827.4)(12)^3
$$

$$
P_w = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(2827.4)(1728) \approx 2{,}992{,}260 \text{ W} \approx 2.99 \text{ MW}
$$

**(ii) Actual power captured**

$$
P_{rotor} = C_p \times P_w = 0.40 \times 2.99 \text{ MW} = 1.197 \text{ MW}
$$

**(iii) Betz limit power**

$$
P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \times P_w = 0.5926 \times 2.99 \text{ MW} \approx 1.77 \text{ MW}
$$

> **Sanity Check:** The actual rotor power (1.197 MW) is less than the Betz limit (1.77 MW). ✓

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)

The figure below maps the complete functional architecture of a typical 3-blade upwind HAWT, showing how wind energy cascades from kinetic input to electrical output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Free Wind Stream V at upstream] --> B[Rotor Blades 3-blade upwind configuration]
    B --> C[Main Shaft High-speed rotation]
    C --> D[Gearbox Step-up ratio 1 to 50 to 100]
    D --> E[Generator Doubly-fed induction or PMSG]
    E --> F[Power Converter AC to DC to AC grid-tie]
    F --> G[Transformer Step-up voltage for transmission]
    G --> H[Grid or Local Load Electrical output]
    B --> I[Yaw Motor Nacelle rotation to face wind]
    I --> B
    C --> J[Pitch Control System Blade angle beta regulation]
    J --> B
    E --> K[SCADA System Monitoring and control feedback]
    K --> I
    K --> J
    K --> F
```

### Functional Block Reading
- The **Rotor Blades** capture wind kinetic energy → produce low-rpm torque.
- The **Gearbox** raises shaft speed to generator-compatible rpm (typically 1500–1800 rpm).
- The **Generator** converts mechanical → electrical.
- The **Power Converter** conditions the electrical output (variable frequency → grid frequency 50 Hz).
- The **Yaw Motor + Pitch Control** are the two feedback loops ensuring the rotor always faces wind at the optimum blade angle.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT, Darrieus H-Rotor)

The figure below maps the functional architecture of a straight-blade Darrieus VAWT, highlighting its key contrast with HAWT (no yaw mechanism; gearbox at ground level).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Omni-directional Wind Any horizontal direction] --> B[Vertical Blades Straight H-rotor configuration]
    B --> C[Vertical Shaft Low rpm direct drive]
    C --> D[Gearbox at Ground Easy maintenance access]
    D --> E[Generator Ground-mounted]
    E --> F[Power Electronics Inverter and grid interface]
    F --> G[Grid Connection Electrical output]
    B --> H[Struts Top and bottom structural support]
    H --> B
    C --> I[Cyclic Stress Gravity loading per revolution]
    I --> B
    F --> J[Self-Start Motor or Savonius booster]
    J --> B
```

### Functional Block Reading
- The **Vertical Shaft** rotates at low rpm (typically 40–80 rpm) → often allows **direct-drive** generators.
- **No yaw mechanism** is required — the rotor accepts wind from any horizontal direction.
- **Strut** is essential for connecting vertical blades; takes cyclic gravity loading.
- **Self-Start Motor** is required because Darrieus is not self-starting (a small Savonius rotor is often paired with it as a booster).

---

## 4.3 Comparison Schematic — Rotor Architectures Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HAWT_GROUP["HAWT CLASSIFICATION"]
        H1[Single Blade] --> H2[Two Blade]
        H2 --> H3[Three Blade industry standard]
        H3 --> H4[Multi Blade water pumping]
        H3A[Upwind yaw controlled] -.-> H3
        H3B[Downwind passive yaw] -.-> H3
    end
    subgraph VAWT_GROUP["VAWT CLASSIFICATION"]
        V1[Savonius drag type] --> V2[Darrieus lift type]
        V2 --> V3[H-Rotor straight blades]
        V2 --> V4[Curved Troposkein Blades]
        V2A[Self Starting via Savonius] -.-> V1
        V2B[Requires External Push] -.-> V2
    end
```

### Reading the Schematic
- The **solid arrows** show the primary taxonomy lineage (number of blades, blade shape).
- The **dotted arrows** show additional classification axes (upwind/downwind for HAWT; self-starting/non-self-starting for VAWT).
