# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind from First Principles

### Statement
Show that the power per unit area (specific power) of a wind stream of velocity $v$ and density $\rho$ is $P/A = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the kinetic energy of a single air parcel.**
Consider a column of air of unit cross-sectional area ($A = 1 \text{ m}^2$) and length equal to the distance the wind travels in one second, which is $v$ meters. The volume of this air parcel is:

$$V = A \times v = 1 \times v = v \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$

**Step 2: Compute the mass of the air parcel.**
Using the density $\rho$ (kg/m³):

$$m = \rho V = \rho v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

This is the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$ per unit area.

**Step 3: Apply the kinetic energy formula.**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Since this mass moves past the reference plane in one second, the kinetic energy per unit time equals the power per unit area:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho v) v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^3 \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

**Step 4: Extend to a rotor of swept area $A$.**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad [\text{W}]$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **cubic dependence** on $v$. A 10 m/s wind has $10^3 = 1000$ relative power units, while a 5 m/s wind has only $5^3 = 125$ units — an **8× difference** for a 2× speed change.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Complete Betz Limit Derivation (Actuator Disc Theory)

### Statement
Using momentum conservation and Bernoulli's equation, prove that the maximum power coefficient of a wind turbine is $C_p = 16/27$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Set up the control volume.**
Consider an idealized rotor as a porous **actuator disc** of area $A$ perpendicular to the wind.

| Location | Velocity | Pressure |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Far upstream (1) | $v_1$ | $p_1$ |
| At the disc (Rotor plane) | $v$ | $p_2 - p_1 = \Delta p$ |
| Far downstream in wake (2) | $v_2$ | $p_1$ (pressure recovers) |

**Step 2: Apply Continuity (mass conservation).**
The mass flow rate is constant through the stream tube:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A v = \rho A_2 v_2$$

For an idealized disc of area $A$, this simplifies to:

$$A v = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

**Step 3: Apply Momentum (Force on the disc).**
The net force $F$ on the disc equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Energy).**
Bernoulli along the streamline from upstream to the disc plane:

$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = p_{disc,front} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$$

From disc plane to downstream wake:

$$p_{disc,back} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Since the pressure jump across the disc equals the force per unit area:

$$F/A = p_{disc,front} - p_{disc,back}$$

Subtracting the two Bernoulli equations:

$$\frac{F}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 5: Equate the two force expressions.**

$$\rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$\rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A (v_1 + v_2)(v_1 - v_2)$$

Cancel $(v_1 - v_2)$ (assuming $v_1 \neq v_2$):

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

This confirms the disc velocity is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities.

**Step 6: Compute the power extracted by the disc.**
The power is the force times the velocity at the disc:

$$P = F \cdot v = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2) \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

**Step 7: Define the induction factor.**
Let $a = \dfrac{v_1 - v}{v_1}$ (axial induction factor). Then:

$$v = v_1(1-a)$$
$$v_1 - v_2 = 2(v_1 - v) = 2 a v_1$$
$$v_1 + v_2 = 2v - v_1 + v_1 = ... \Rightarrow v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

**Step 8: Express $C_p$ in terms of $a$.**

$$P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1(1-a)\left[v_1^2 - v_1^2(1-2a)^2\right] = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

$$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 9: Maximize $C_p$.**

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)(1-a-2a) = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Non-trivial root: $a = 1/3$.

**Step 10: Compute the maximum.**

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

**Q.E.D.** ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Wake Has v₂ = v₁/3:**
> At Betz optimum, $a = 1/3$, so $v = v_1(1 - 1/3) = (2/3)v_1$ and $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2/3) = (1/3)v_1$. The wind is reduced to one-third of its original speed in the far wake. This is the energetically ideal deceleration.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rotor Torque from Power Equation

### Statement
Derive an expression for the torque $T$ on the rotor shaft in terms of the thrust force $F_T$, the wind speed $v$, and the rotor radius $R$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Relate power to torque and angular velocity.**

$$P = T \omega$$

where $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ is the angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 2: Express the thrust force on the disc.**
The thrust force from momentum theory is:

$$F_T = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2) = 2 \rho A v_1^2 a (1-a)$$

**Step 3: Compute the torque from blade-element analysis.**
By combining the tangential momentum balance with blade-element theory (omitted here for brevity; full derivation in Manwell et al.), the torque is:

$$T = C_T \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R v^2$$

where $C_T$ is the **torque coefficient** (analogous to $C_p$ but for torque).

**Step 4: Relationship to TSR and Cp.**

$$C_p = \lambda C_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_T = \frac{C_p}{\lambda}$$

Hence:

$$T = \frac{C_p}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R v^2$$

**Step 5: Power check (sanity).**

$$P = T \omega = \frac{C_p}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R v^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda v}{R} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 \quad \checkmark$$

The result reproduces the standard power equation.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

### Problem
A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s, and the air density is 1.225 kg/m³. The power coefficient is 0.4. Calculate:
1. The power available in the wind.
2. The power captured by the rotor.
3. The rotor torque (assume TSR = 6).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow R = 40$ m
- $v = 12$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $C_p = 0.4$
- $\lambda = 6$

**Step 1: Compute the swept area.**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 40^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

**Step 2: Power available in the wind.**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \approx 5{,}320{,}938 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Power captured by the rotor.**

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.4 \times 5.32 \text{ MW} = 2.128 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4: Compute the rotational speed.**

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi D N}{60 v} \Rightarrow N = \frac{60 \lambda v}{\pi D} = \frac{60 \times 6 \times 12}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{4320}{251.33} \approx 17.19 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Compute the angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 17.19}{60} \approx 1.80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 6: Compute the rotor torque.**

$$T = \frac{P_{rotor}}{\omega} = \frac{2{,}128{,}000}{1.80} \approx 1{,}182{,}222 \text{ N·m} \approx 1.18 \text{ MN·m}$$

### Verification (Using Torque Coefficient Form)

$$T = \frac{C_p}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R v^2 = \frac{0.4}{6} \times 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 40 \times 144$$

$$T = 0.0667 \times 0.6125 \times 5026.55 \times 5760 \approx 1.18 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Specific Rated Capacity and Annual Energy Output

### Statement
Derive the formula for Annual Energy Output (AEO) of a wind farm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Rated power.**
The rated power of a single turbine:

$$P_{rated} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_r^3 C_{p,rated}$$

where $v_r$ is the rated wind speed.

**Step 2: Capacity factor.**
The **capacity factor** $CF$ is the ratio of actual annual output to the theoretical maximum:

$$CF = \frac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760 \text{ h}}$$

**Step 3: Solve for annual energy.**

$$E_{annual} = CF \times P_{rated} \times 8760 \quad [\text{kWh}]$$

**Step 4: Wind farm output.**
For $N_t$ turbines with array losses and availability factor:

$$E_{farm} = N_t \times E_{annual} \times \eta_{array} \times \eta_{availability}$$

where $\eta_{array} \approx 0.95$ and $\eta_{availability} \approx 0.95$.

## 3.6 The Wind Speed Distribution and Power Density Integration

### Statement
Show that the average wind power density is $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \overline{v^3}$, where $\overline{v^3}$ is the mean cube wind speed computed from the Weibull PDF.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define average power density.**

$$\overline{P} = \int_0^\infty \frac{1}{2}\rho v^3 \cdot f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Weibull PDF.**

$$\overline{P} = \frac{\rho}{2} \int_0^\infty v^3 \cdot \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k} dv$$

**Step 3: Substitute $u = (v/c)^k \Rightarrow v = c u^{1/k}$, $dv = (c/k) u^{1/k - 1} du$.**

$$\overline{P} = \frac{\rho k}{2c^k} \int_0^\infty v^{k+2} e^{-(v/c)^k} dv$$

After the substitution and recognizing the Gamma function:

$$\overline{P} = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$

This is the **average power density** at the site.

**Step 4: For Rayleigh (k=2).**

$$\Gamma(1 + 3/2) = \Gamma(2.5) = \frac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \approx 1.3293$$

$$\overline{P}_{Rayleigh} = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \times 1.3293 \approx 0.815 \rho c^3$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Key Equations at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Day Analytical Map**

| Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Coupling | Engineering Output |
|---------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Energy in air | $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Quadratic in $v$ | Kinetic energy |
| Power flux | $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Cubic in $v$ | Power (W) |
| Betz efficiency | $C_p = 16/27$ | Constant | Ceiling on $C_p$ |
| Tip Speed | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | Linear in $\omega, R$ | Aerodynamic matching |
| Torque | $T = P / \omega$ | Inverse in $\omega$ | Shaft loading |
| Site power | $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma(1+3/k)$ | Cubic in $c$ | Site assessment |
| Wind profile | $v(h) = v_{ref}(h/h_{ref})^\alpha$ | Power law | Hub-height extrapolation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WindSource["Wind Source\nKinetic Energy in Air Mass"] --> Aerodyn["Aerodynamic Stage\nRotor Blades\nLift and Drag Forces"]
    Aerodyn --> LowShaft["Low Speed Shaft\nMulti-ton Rotor\n10 to 20 rpm"]
    LowShaft --> Gearbox["Gearbox Stage\nStep-Up Transmission\nRatio 1 to 100"]
    Gearbox --> HighShaft["High Speed Shaft\nGenerator Input\n1500 to 1800 rpm"]
    HighShaft --> Generator["Generator Stage\nInduction or PMSG\nAC Output"]
    Generator --> PowerElect["Power Electronics\nRectifier and Inverter\nGrid Synchronization"]
    PowerElect --> Transformer["Step-Up Transformer\nLV to MV to HV"]
    Transformer --> Grid["Electrical Grid\nUtility Transmission Lines"]
    Aerodyn -. Yaw Control .-> YawMech["Yaw Drive Motor\nNacelle Orientation"]
    Aerodyn -. Pitch Control .-> PitchMech["Pitch Actuator\nBlade Angle Adjustment"]
    WindSource -. Anemometer Feedback .-> Controller["SCADA Controller\nSensors and Actuators"]
    Controller -. Control Signals .-> YawMech
    Controller -. Control Signals .-> PitchMech
    Controller -. Control Signals .-> PowerElect
    Controller -. Control Signals .-> Generator
```

## 4.2 Actuation Disc Model — Aerodynamic Streamtube Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Upstream["Upstream Region 1\nVelocity v1\nPressure p1\nUndisturbed Wind"]
    DiscActuator["Actuator Disc\nInfinitely Thin\nArea A\nVelocity v through Disc"]
    Downstream["Downstream Wake Region 2\nVelocity v2 less than v1\nPressure recovers to p1\nWake Region"]
    Upstream -->|Flow enters with v1| DiscActuator
    DiscActuator -->|Flow exits with v2| Downstream
    DiscActuator -. Pressure Drop delta p .-> DiscActuator
    Upstream -. Streamtube Expansion .-> Downstream
```

## 4.3 Wind Turbine Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WindTurbine["Wind Turbines Classification"]
    Axis["Based on Axis Orientation"]
    HAWT["HAWT\nHorizontal Axis Wind Turbine"]
    VAWT["VAWT\nVertical Axis Wind Turbine"]
    Upwind["Upwind Type\nRotor faces wind directly"]
    Downwind["Downwind Type\nRotor in tower shadow"]
    Dutch["Dutch Type\nHigh Solidity\nMany Blades"]
    Savonius["Savonius Type\nDrag-Based\nTwo Half Cylinders"]
    Darrieus["Darrieus Type\nLift-Based\nEggbeater Shape"]
    Modern["Modern Propeller HAWT\nLow Solidity\nTwo or Three Blades"]
    WindTurbine --> Axis
    Axis --> HAWT
    Axis --> VAWT
    HAWT --> Upwind
    HAWT --> Downwind
    HAWT --> Modern
    HAWT --> Dutch
    VAWT --> Savonius
    VAWT --> Darrieus
```

## 4.4 Power Curve — Three Operational Wind Speed Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RegionA["Region I\nv less than v cut-in\nNo Power Output\nTurbine Shutdown"]
    RegionB["Region II\nv cut-in to v rated\nPower Output P equals Cp times 0.5 rho A v cubed\nCubic Cube Law Region"]
    RegionC["Region III\nv rated to v cut-out\nConstant Rated Power\nPitch Control Active"]
    RegionD["Region IV\nv greater than v cut-out\nTurbine Shut Down\nStorm Protection Mode"]
    RegionA --> RegionB
    RegionB --> RegionC
    RegionC --> RegionD
```

## 4.5 Numerical Worked Example — Power Curve Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Inputs\nD equals 80 m\nv equals 12 m/s\nrho equals 1.225 kg per m cubed\nCp equals 0.4"]
    Step1["Step 1 Compute Area\nA equals pi R squared"]
    Step2["Step 2 Wind Power\nP wind equals 0.5 rho A v cubed"]
    Step3["Step 3 Rotor Power\nP rotor equals Cp times P wind"]
    Step4["Step 4 Rotational Speed\nN equals 60 lambda v over pi D"]
    Step5["Step 5 Torque\nT equals P rotor over omega"]
    Output["Final Outputs\nP wind equals 5.32 MW\nP rotor equals 2.128 MW\nN equals 17.19 rpm\nT equals 1.18 MN m"]
    Input --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step2 --> Step3
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Output
```

## 4.6 Energy Conversion Chain — Wind to Wire Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SolarOrigin["Solar Energy\nOrigin of Wind"]
    WindKin["Wind Kinetic Energy\n0.5 rho v squared"]
    MechShaft["Mechanical Shaft Work\nPower equals T times omega"]
    ElecGen["Electrical AC Power\nPower equals V times I times cos phi"]
    GridDist["Grid Distribution\nStep-up Transformer"]
    EndUser["End User\nIndustries and Homes"]
    SolarOrigin --> WindKin
    WindKin --> MechShaft
    MechShaft --> ElecGen
    ElecGen --> GridDist
    GridDist --> EndUser
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture** representations rather than physical engineering drawings. They map the flow of energy and the logical interaction between components, which is the appropriate level of abstraction for an undergraduate KTU engineering examination.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**"Define the term 'Power Coefficient' in the context of a wind energy conversion system. What is its theoretical maximum value and who proposed it?"**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **Power Coefficient ($C_p$)** is defined as the ratio of the actual mechanical power extracted by the wind turbine rotor to the total power available in the incident wind stream:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{rotor}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{rotor}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3}$$

The **theoretical maximum** value of the power coefficient is:

$$C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ or } 59.3\%$$

This is known as the **Betz Limit**, proposed by **Albert Betz** in 1919. It states that no wind turbine can extract more than 59.3% of the kinetic energy from the wind, regardless of design sophistication, because extracting all the energy would completely stop the airflow behind the rotor. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for value, 1 mark for attribution and explanation)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between HAWT and VAWT wind turbines. Mention two advantages of each."**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | HAWT (Horizontal Axis) | VAWT (Vertical Axis) |
|---------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Axis Orientation | Parallel to wind direction | Perpendicular to wind direction |
| Yaw Control | Required (nacelle rotates) | Not required (omnidirectional) |
| Examples | Modern propeller, Dutch windmill | Savonius, Darrieus |

**HAWT Advantages:**
1. Higher aerodynamic efficiency (achieves $C_p$ close to Betz limit, ~0.45–0.50).
2. Can be installed at higher hub heights, accessing stronger winds.

**VAWT Advantages:**
1. No yaw mechanism required — accepts wind from any direction.
2. Generator and gearbox can be placed at ground level (easier maintenance). *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for advantages)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the power available in a wind stream of velocity $v$ and density $\rho$ passing through an area $A$. What is meant by the **Betz Limit**? Derive its value using the actuator disc theory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wind turbine has the following specifications:
- Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m
- Hub height wind speed $v = 10$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³
- Power coefficient $C_p = 0.42$
- Generator efficiency $\eta_g = 0.92$
- Gearbox efficiency $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$

Calculate: (i) Power available in the wind, (ii) Power output at the generator terminals, (iii) Annual energy generation assuming capacity factor $CF = 0.30$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Define the kinetic energy of an air parcel of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \quad [\text{J}]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ through area $A$ at velocity $v$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the wind power as kinetic energy per unit time:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 \quad [\text{W}]$$

**Step 4 (0.5 marks):** Define the Betz Limit — it is the theoretical maximum value of $C_p$ achievable by any wind turbine.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Apply actuator disc theory. Define induction factor $a = (v_1 - v)/v_1$. Derive the velocity at the disc as the arithmetic mean: $v = (v_1 + v_2)/2$. Equate momentum-based force to Bernoulli-based force.

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Derive $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$. Maximize by $dC_p/da = 0$, yielding $a = 1/3$. Substitute to get $C_p = 16/27 \approx 0.593$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 60$ m $\Rightarrow R = 30$ m, $v = 10$ m/s, $\rho = 1.20$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.42$, $\eta_g = 0.92$, $\eta_{gb} = 0.95$, $CF = 0.30$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Swept area:

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 30^2 = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Power available in the wind:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^3 = 0.6 \times 2827.43 \times 1000$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 1{,}696{,}460 \text{ W} \approx 1.696 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Rotor shaft power (mechanical):

$$P_{rotor} = C_p \times P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 1.696 \text{ MW} \approx 0.7124 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Power output at generator terminals (after gearbox and generator losses):

$$P_{elec} = P_{rotor} \times \eta_{gb} \times \eta_g = 0.7124 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$

$$P_{elec} \approx 0.6225 \text{ MW} \approx 622.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Annual energy generation:

$$E_{annual} = P_{elec} \times CF \times 8760$$

$$E_{annual} = 622.5 \text{ kW} \times 0.30 \times 8760 \approx 1{,}635{,}510 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 1.636 \text{ GWh/year}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of the **gearbox**, **generator**, and **pitch control mechanism** in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a potential wind farm site, the mean wind speed is 7.5 m/s and the Weibull shape parameter is $k = 2.0$. Calculate: (i) the Weibull scale parameter $c$, (ii) the most probable wind speed, (iii) the average power density in the wind, and (iv) the energy captured per square meter of swept area per year. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer B

#### Part (a) — HAWT Description (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A modern HAWT consists of:
- **Rotor** with 2 or 3 blades made of fiberglass-reinforced polyester or wood epoxy.
- **Nacelle** housing the gearbox, generator, and control electronics.
- **Tower** (typically tubular steel) supporting the nacelle at hub heights of 60–120 m.
- **Yaw mechanism** to orient the nacelle into the wind.
- **Anemometer and wind vane** at the top for wind sensing.
- **Brake system** (aerodynamic and/or mechanical) for emergency shutdown.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Wind strikes the rotor blades, generating aerodynamic lift (Bernoulli's principle). The lift force produces a torque on the rotor hub, causing rotation. The low-speed shaft (rotor, ~15 rpm) drives the gearbox, which steps up the speed to ~1500 rpm for the generator. The generator produces AC electricity, which is conditioned by power electronics and fed into the grid.

**Role of Key Components (2 marks):**
- **Gearbox:** Steps up rotational speed (typically 1:80 ratio) for efficient generator operation.
- **Generator (usually DFIG or PMSG):** Converts mechanical rotation into electrical AC power.
- **Pitch Control:** Adjusts blade pitch angle to optimize $C_p$ at varying wind speeds and to feather blades during high winds for safety.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $\bar{v} = 7.5$ m/s, $k = 2.0$ (Rayleigh case), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For a Rayleigh distribution ($k = 2$):

$$\bar{v} = c \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{4}} = c \times \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

$$c = \frac{2\bar{v}}{\sqrt{\pi}} = \frac{2 \times 7.5}{1.7725} \approx 8.462 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Most probable wind speed (mode of Weibull):

$$v_{mode} = c \left(\frac{k-1}{k}\right)^{1/k} \quad \text{(for } k > 1\text{)}$$

$$v_{mode} = 8.462 \times \left(\frac{2-1}{2}\right)^{1/2} = 8.462 \times 0.7071 \approx 5.984 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Average power density:

$$\overline{P} = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma(2.5)$$

$$\Gamma(2.5) = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \approx 1.3293$$

$$\overline{P} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times (8.462)^3 \times 1.3293$$

$$\overline{P} = 0.6125 \times 605.92 \times 1.3293 \approx 493.4 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Annual energy per m²:

$$E_{annual/m^2} = \overline{P} \times 8760 = 493.4 \times 8760 \approx 4{,}322{,}184 \text{ Wh/m}^2 \approx 4.322 \text{ MWh/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert all lengths to meters, velocities to m/s, and densities to kg/m³ before substituting. Mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical errors.
> 2. **Cubic vs Quadratic:** Students often confuse $v^2$ (kinetic energy) with $v^3$ (power). Remember: energy has $v^2$; power has $v^3$.
> 3. **Betz Limit Direction:** Students sometimes write $C_p = 27/16$. The correct expression is $C_p = 16/27$. Double-check direction.
> 4. **Induction Factor:** The relationship $a = (v_1 - v)/v_1$ is defined at the disc plane, not at the wake. State this clearly in derivations.
> 5. **Weibull vs Rayleigh:** For Rayleigh, $k = 2$, and the formula simplifies dramatically. Do not use Weibull general formulas when Rayleigh special case is intended.
> 6. **Air Density:** Use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at standard sea-level conditions; if altitude or temperature is specified, recalculate using $\rho = p/(RT)$.
> 7. **Capacity Factor Definition:** $CF$ is dimensionless and lies between 0 and 1. Annual energy = $CF \times P_{rated} \times 8760$.
> 8. **Tip Speed Ratio:** Units must be consistent — $\omega$ in rad/s, $R$ in m, $v$ in m/s. Alternatively, use the rpm-based formula $\lambda = \pi D N / (60 v)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Equations (Must Memorize)
- [ ] Wind Power: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$
- [ ] Rotor Power: $P_{rotor} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$
- [ ] Betz Limit: $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$
- [ ] Induction Factor: $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$
- [ ] Tip Speed Ratio: $\lambda = \omega R / v = \pi D N / (60 v)$
- [ ] Swept Area: $A = \pi R^2 = \pi D^2/4$
- [ ] Wind Shear: $v(h) = v_{ref}(h/h_{ref})^\alpha$
- [ ] Capacity Factor: $CF = AEO / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$
- [ ] Rayleigh Mean: $\bar{v} = c\sqrt{\pi/4}$
- [ ] Avg Power Density: $\bar{P} = \frac{1}{2}\rho c^3 \Gamma(1+3/k)$

### Critical Concepts (Must Explain Clearly)
- [ ] The cube law of wind power and its implications.
- [ ] Why Betz Limit is 59.3% (actuator disc theory derivation steps).
- [ ] The physical meaning of $a = 1/3$ (wake velocity $v_2 = v_1/3$).
- [ ] Difference between HAWT and VAWT (axis, examples, advantages).
- [ ] Roles of gearbox, generator, pitch control, yaw control.
- [ ] Three operational regions: cut-in to rated (cubic), rated to cut-out (constant), beyond cut-out (shutdown).
- [ ] Solidity classification: high (>0.4), medium, low (<0.1).
- [ ] Weibull shape parameter $k$ and scale parameter $c$.
- [ ] Capacity factor vs. efficiency distinction.
- [ ] Wind shear exponent $\alpha$ and its effect on hub-height design.

### Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] Don't forget the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in $\frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$.
- [ ] Don't confuse $D$ with $R$ in the swept area formula.
- [ ] Don't apply Betz Limit as $C_p$ value in real turbines — real $C_p$ is 0.35–0.45 for modern HAWTs.
- [ ] Don't forget to multiply by efficiency chain in actual electrical output calculations.

### Site Selection Criteria (Frequently Tested)
- [ ] Mean wind speed > 5–6 m/s at hub height.
- [ ] Low turbulence intensity.
- [ ] Smooth, unobstructed terrain (open plains, ridges, off-shore).
- [ ] Proximity to grid substations and road access.
- [ ] Low population density (noise, aesthetic concerns).
- [ ] Wind direction consistency (low directional variability).

### Environmental and Economic Aspects (Quick Notes)
- [ ] Wind energy is clean, renewable, and inexhaustible.
- [ ] Capacity factor for on-shore wind: 25–45%; off-shore: 50–60%.
- [ ] Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) for wind is now competitive with fossil fuels.
- [ ] Main challenges: intermittency, storage, land use, avian impacts, noise.
- [ ] Curtailment, grid balancing, and hybrid systems (wind-solar) are modern solutions.

### KTU Exam Day Tips
- [ ] Always draw a neat labeled diagram of the wind turbine system — even for theory questions.
- [ ] State units clearly in every step of numerical problems.
- [ ] Use the Betz derivation as a **structured 6-step argument** — examiners award step-wise marks.
- [ ] Show $v^3$ explicitly when writing wind power — never just write "$v^2$".
- [ ] For Part B 14-mark questions, expect sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks** with the **7-mark** part being either a derivation or a numerical.

> **End of Module 2 — Wind Power Generation Notes**
> **Coverage:** Definitions → Theoretical Foundations → Derivations → Diagrams → Examination Bank
> **Aligned with:** KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (OECHT837), NEP 2020 Outcomes, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L1–L5.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Energy Resources: Tidal Energy, Wave Energy, and Ocean Thermal Energy
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Ocean Energy Resources: Tidal, Wave, and Ocean Thermal Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Takeaway**: The oceans cover **~71% of Earth's surface** and store immense renewable energy in three distinct forms — mechanical (tides & waves) and thermal (ocean temperature gradients). KTU examiners frequently test these as 14-mark analytical questions involving power calculations, cycle efficiencies, and device classifications.

## 1.1 What Are Ocean Energy Resources?

> **Formal KTU Definition**: *Ocean Energy* refers to the renewable energy harnessed from the ocean's natural motion and thermal gradients. It broadly encompasses **Tidal Energy** (gravitational kinetic energy of seawater), **Wave Energy** (wind-induced surface oscillation), **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion / OTEC** (latent thermal energy across depth), and to a lesser extent, Salinity Gradient and Marine Current energy.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition**:
Imagine the ocean as a giant, multi-layered battery:
- **Tides** are like a slow, predictable mechanical pendulum charged twice a day by the Moon and Sun's gravity.
- **Waves** are like rapid, chaotic vibrations on the surface — wind blowing across water shakes the top layer, packing enormous kinetic energy.
- **OTEC** works like a natural refrigerator: the warm surface water is the "hot reservoir" and the cold deep water (4 °C, ~1 km below) is the "cold reservoir" — together they form a heat engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for Ocean Energy**
> - Density of seawater: **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
> - Minimum ΔT for OTEC viability: **≈ 20 °C**
> - Global tidal energy potential: **> 2 TW** (theoretical)

## 1.2 Classification Map of Ocean Energy

| Resource | Energy Source | Energy Form | Conversion Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Energy | Gravitational pull of Moon & Sun | Mechanical (Potential + Kinetic) | Barrage / Stream turbines |
| Wave Energy | Wind friction on sea surface | Mechanical (Kinetic + Potential) | Oscillating bodies / Columns |
| OTEC | Solar heat stored in surface water | Thermal | Rankine / Claude cycle |
| Salinity Gradient | Fresh & salt water mixing | Chemical (osmosis) | Pressure retarded osmosis |

---

## 1.3 Tidal Energy — Intuitive Overview

Tides are the periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the combined **gravitational attraction** of the Moon (≈ 67% effect) and the Sun (≈ 33% effect), along with **centrifugal forces** from the Earth-Moon and Earth-Sun rotations.

**Two tides per lunar day** (~24 hours 50 minutes) — this is why tidal cycles are ~12 hours 25 minutes apart. Spring tides (maximum range) occur at full/new moon; neap tides (minimum range) occur at quarter moons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tidal Range (R)** = Difference in height between **High Water (HW)** and **Low Water (LW)** at a given location. Ranges above **5 m** are commercially viable. The **Bay of Fundy (Canada)** records the world's largest tidal range at **~16 m**.

### Working Principle (Tidal Barrage)
A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary with sluice gates and low-head bulb turbines. Water flows in during high tide (flood generation), gets trapped, and is released through turbines during low tide (ebb generation) — generating electricity in both directions.

**Real-World Benchmark**: The **La Rance Tidal Power Station (France, 1966)** is the world's first and largest operational tidal barrage — **240 MW capacity**, with a **barrage length of 750 m** and a tidal range of **~13.5 m**.

---

## 1.4 Wave Energy — Intuitive Overview

Wind blowing across the ocean surface transfers momentum to water, creating waves. Wave energy is concentrated near the surface and decays **exponentially with depth**.

A wave carries both **kinetic energy** (water particle motion) and **potential energy** (elevation above still water level). The total energy per unit surface area is:

$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \frac{\rho g H^2}{16} \quad \text{[J/m²]}$$

where **H** = wave height (m), **ρ** = water density, **g** = gravitational acceleration.

**Conceptual Analogy**: Picture pushing a child on a swing — your hand transfers small impulses, but the swing (wave) oscillates with stored energy. Waves are similar: continuous small wind impulses "pump" up large oscillating energy reservoirs that travel thousands of kilometres with little loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Global Wave Power Potential**: West coasts of Europe (UK, Portugal, Ireland), the Pacific Northwest (USA/Canada), and southern Australia are prime wave-energy hotspots, with average wave power densities of **40–70 kW/m of crest width**.

---

## 1.5 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) — Intuitive Overview

The ocean's surface absorbs **~70% of incoming solar radiation**, warming the upper ~100 m to **25–30 °C** in tropical regions. Below the **thermocline** (typically 800–1000 m depth), water remains at a near-constant **4–5 °C**.

This **ΔT ≈ 20–25 °C** is the thermal "head" that drives OTEC power plants — exactly like a low-temperature heat engine.

**Conceptual Analogy**: Think of OTEC as the ocean's own air-conditioner running in reverse. Just as an AC needs a hot and cold side to pump heat, OTEC needs warm surface water (boils a working fluid like ammonia) and cold deep water (re-condenses it) to spin a turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot Efficiency Bound for OTEC**: With **T_H = 300 K** (27 °C) and **T_C = 278 K** (5 °C), the maximum theoretical efficiency is:
> $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{278}{300} \approx 7.33\%$$
> Real OTEC plants achieve only **2–3%**, but the enormous heat flow makes even low-efficiency conversion commercially attractive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tidal Energy — Theory & Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Origin of Tides
The Moon's gravitational force on the Earth is **stronger on the side facing the Moon** and **weaker on the opposite side**, creating two bulges. Earth's rotation beneath these bulges produces **two high tides and two low tides per lunar day**.

- **Spring Tide**: Sun, Earth, Moon aligned → maximum tidal range (1.4× mean).
- **Neap Tide**: Sun–Earth–Moon at right angles → minimum tidal range (0.6× mean).
- **Mean tidal range** = arithmetic mean of spring and neap ranges.

### 2.1.2 Available Energy from a Tidal Basin

For a tidal basin of surface area **A** filled to a tidal range **R**:

**Potential Energy Stored:**
$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

**Derivation Logic**: Consider a small water slice at depth *y* below high-water mark with thickness *dy*. Its area is *A* and mass *dm = ρ·A·dy*. PE relative to low-water level is *dm·g·y*.

Total PE = ∫₀ᴿ ρ·g·A·y·dy = ½·ρ·g·A·R².

**Average Tidal Power (two cycles per day, period T = 12.42 h ≈ 44,712 s):**
$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_p}{T} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2T} \quad \text{[W]}$$

### 2.1.3 Power Extraction Using a Tidal Stream

For a tidal stream of cross-sectional area **A** moving with velocity **V**:

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \quad \text{[W]}$$

This is identical in form to wind power, but **ρ_water ≈ 800× ρ_air**, making tidal streams enormously more energy-dense than wind.

> [!TIP]
> **Betz Limit Analogy**: A tidal turbine can extract at most **59.3%** of the kinetic energy flux (analogous to wind Betz limit). Practical tidal turbines achieve **35–45% efficiency**.

### 2.1.4 Single-Basin vs Double-Basin Schemes
- **Single Basin (Single Effect)**: Generates power only during ebb tide (~10 h generation, then idle).
- **Single Basin (Double Effect)**: Generates during both flood and ebb (~14–16 h generation).
- **Double Basin**: Two interconnected basins — one always retains a head difference → **continuous 24-h generation**, but lower instantaneous power.

### 2.1.5 Advantages & Limitations of Tidal Energy

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages**: Predictable (centuries ahead), zero fuel cost, no GHG emissions, long plant life (~100 years), high energy density.
>
> **Limitations**: High capital cost (dam construction), limited suitable sites, environmental impact on estuarine ecosystems, intermittent (twice-daily cycle), silting of basins.

---

## 2.2 Wave Energy — Theory & Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Deep-Water Wave Theory (Airy / Linear Wave Theory)

In deep water (depth > ½ wavelength), water particles trace **circular orbits** that decay exponentially with depth. Key parameters:

- **Wave Height (H)**: Vertical distance trough-to-crest [m]
- **Wavelength (L)**: Distance between successive crests [m]
- **Wave Period (T)**: Time between successive crests at a fixed point [s]
- **Wave Amplitude (a)**: H/2 [m]

**Dispersion Relation (deep water):**
$$L = \frac{g T^2}{2\pi} \quad \text{or} \quad \omega^2 = g k$$

where **ω = 2π/T** is angular frequency and **k = 2π/L** is wavenumber.

**Wave celerity (phase speed):**
$$c = \frac{L}{T} = \frac{g T}{2\pi}$$

### 2.2.2 Wave Energy Density (Per Unit Horizontal Area)

Total wave energy per unit surface area (sum of kinetic and potential):

$$\boxed{E = \frac{\rho g H^2}{16} \quad \text{[J/m²]}}$$

**Power per unit crest width (Wave Power Flux):**
$$\boxed{P_w = E \cdot c_g = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} \quad \text{[W/m]}}$$

where **c_g = c/2** is the group velocity for deep-water waves (energy travels at half the phase speed).

### 2.2.3 Wave Energy Conversion Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation**: Be able to classify devices and explain at least one working principle in detail.

| Device Type | Working Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Absorber** | Buoy heaves up-down at one location | PowerBuoy (USA) |
| **Attenuator** | Long floating body aligned with wave direction | Pelamis (Scotland) |
| **Terminator** | Captures wave reflection perpendicular to direction | Wave Dragon (Denmark) |
| **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** | Compressed air drives Wells turbine | LIMPET (Islay, Scotland), 500 kW |
| **Overtopping Device** | Waves spill into reservoir above sea level | Wave Dragon |

**Oscillating Water Column (OWC) — Detailed Operation**:
1. Wave enters a partly submerged chamber from below.
2. As wave rises, air above water is compressed → expelled through a **Wells turbine** (rotates same direction regardless of airflow).
3. As wave retreats, air is sucked back in → Wells turbine still rotates in same direction.
4. Continuous bidirectional air motion → unidirectional turbine rotation.

### 2.2.4 Wave Energy Advantages & Limitations
- **Advantages**: Highest energy density among renewables (~30 kW/m avg), predictable with 24–48 h forecasts, low visual impact (submerged buoys).
- **Limitations**: Storm damage risk, salt corrosion, marine fouling, transmission losses to shore, immature technology.

---

## 2.3 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) — Theory & Cycles

### 2.3.1 OTEC Site Requirements

For an OTEC plant to be viable:
- **Minimum ΔT = 20 °C** between surface and deep water.
- **Depth of cold water pipe (CWP) ≤ 1000 m**.
- **Geographic location**: within **±20° latitude** of equator (tropical oceans).
- **Land-based, shelf-mounted, or floating** configurations.

### 2.3.2 The Three OTEC Cycles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic**: Expect a 14-mark question comparing these three cycles.

#### A. Closed Cycle OTEC (Anderson / Rankine Cycle)
- Working fluid: low-boiling-point liquid (ammonia NH₃, or R-134a).
- Warm surface water (25–30 °C) vaporizes ammonia in the **evaporator**.
- Vapor drives a turbine → **condenser** cooled by cold deep water (5 °C) re-liquefies vapor.
- Cycle repeats in a sealed loop.

**Net Power per unit mass flow of working fluid:**
$$w_{net} = \eta_{turbine} \cdot (h_1 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_3)$$

where *h₁ → h₂* is turbine expansion, *h₄ → h₃* is pump work.

#### B. Open Cycle OTEC (Claude Cycle)
- Warm seawater itself is the working fluid.
- Surface water is flash-evaporated in a near-vacuum chamber.
- Steam drives a low-pressure turbine.
- Exhaust steam is condensed by cold deep water → fresh water by-product (desalination).
- Pressure ≈ 3 kPa (≈ 0.03 atm) — enormous turbines needed.

#### C. Hybrid Cycle
- Combines features: closed Rankine cycle for power + flash evaporation for desalinated water.

### 2.3.3 Heat Exchanger Sizing — Critical Bottleneck

OTEC's biggest engineering challenge is the **massive heat exchangers** required because ΔT is small (≤ 25 °C).

Required heat transfer area:
$$A_{HX} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

where **U** = overall heat transfer coefficient (typically **200–500 W/m²K** for OTEC) and **ΔT_LMTD** = log-mean temperature difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb**: An OTEC plant requires ~**25–40 m² of heat exchanger per kW** of net output — much larger than conventional power plants. This makes OTEC capital-intensive but operationally clean.

---

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Available Energy in a Tidal Basin

**Problem Setup**: A tidal basin has surface area **A**, mean tidal range **R**, and water density **ρ**. Derive the average power potential.

**Step 1 — Slice the water column into thin horizontal slabs**.
Consider a thin slice at depth *y* below the highest water level, with thickness *dy*. The slice has:
- Volume: $dV = A \cdot dy$
- Mass: $dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dy$

**Step 2 — Compute PE of this slice relative to low-water level**.
When water drops from the high-tide level, this slice falls a vertical distance *y*.

$$dE_p = dm \cdot g \cdot y = \rho g A y \, dy$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from the top (y = 0) to the bottom (y = R)**.

$$E_p = \int_{0}^{R} \rho g A y \, dy = \rho g A \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^R = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert to average power using tidal period**.
The tidal period is **T = 12.42 h = 44,712 s** (semi-diurnal lunar tide).

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_p}{T} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check**: A basin with A = 1 km² = 10⁶ m², R = 5 m:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 \times 25}{2 \times 44712} \approx 2.81 \text{ MW}$$

(with practical turbine efficiency ≈ 30%, deliverable power ≈ **0.84 MW** — plausible).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wave Power Flux Per Unit Crest Width

**Problem Setup**: Derive power transported per metre of wave crest using linear (Airy) wave theory.

**Step 1 — Write expressions for KE and PE densities**.

Average kinetic energy density per unit area:
$$\bar{E}_k = \frac{1}{16} \rho g H^2$$

Average potential energy density per unit area:
$$\bar{E}_p = \frac{1}{16} \rho g H^2$$

Both equal! Total energy density:
$$\bar{E}_{total} = \bar{E}_k + \bar{E}_p = \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2$$

**Step 2 — Identify group velocity**.
For deep-water linear waves, energy propagates at the **group velocity** c_g, which is half the phase velocity:

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{g T}{2\pi} = \frac{g T}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Compute power per unit crest width**.
Power per unit crest width = energy density × group velocity:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \bar{E}_{total} \times c_g \\
&= \frac{1}{8} \rho g H^2 \times \frac{g T}{4\pi} \\
&= \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check using typical North Atlantic values**.
Take H = 3 m, T = 8 s, ρ = 1025 kg/m³:

$$P_w = \frac{1025 \times 9.81^2 \times 9 \times 8}{32\pi} \approx 71.8 \text{ kW/m}$$

This matches published North Atlantic averages of **60–80 kW/m** — formula validated.

> [!TIP]
> **Useful Simplified Form**: Combining g = 9.81 m/s² and ρ = 1025 kg/m³, the practical shortcut used by wave-energy engineers is:
> $$P_w \approx \frac{H^2 \cdot T}{1.0} \quad \text{[kW/m]}$$
> For H = 3 m, T = 8 s → ≈ 72 kW/m. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation: OTEC Carnot Efficiency & Practical Limits

**Step 1 — Write the Carnot expression**.
For a heat engine operating between T_H and T_C:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Step 2 — Apply typical OTEC reservoir temperatures**.
- Surface: T_H = 27 °C = **300 K**
- Deep: T_C = 5 °C = **278 K**

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{278}{300} = \frac{22}{300} \approx 7.33\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply practical efficiency factors**.
A real OTEC plant has:
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: η_t ≈ 85%
- Pump isentropic efficiency: η_p ≈ 80%
- Heat exchanger losses: η_HX ≈ 90%
- Parasitic loads (CWP pumping): subtract ~30% of gross output

Net plant efficiency:
$$\eta_{net} \approx \eta_{Carnot} \times \eta_t \times \eta_{HX} \approx 0.0733 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 \approx 5.6\%$$

After parasitic loads, **net electrical output ≈ 2.5–3% of thermal input**.

**Step 4 — Compute useful power per m³/s of warm water**.

Heat absorbed per second from warm water flow rate $\dot{m}_w$:
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot (T_H - T_{evap})$$

where c_p ≈ 4.0 kJ/(kg·K) for water, and T_evap ≈ T_C + 4 °C (small approach temperature).

If $\dot{m}_w$ = 1000 kg/s, ΔT_in = 22 K:
$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 1000 \times 4000 \times 22 = 88 \text{ MW (thermal)}$$

Net electrical output:
$$P_{net} = 0.025 \times 88 = 2.2 \text{ MW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Even with low efficiency, OTEC is attractive because the "fuel" (warm seawater) is free and unlimited. The plant's output is essentially **capital cost-limited**, not fuel cost-limited.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Tidal Stream Power

**Problem**: A tidal channel has cross-section A = 50 m² and mean flow velocity V = 2.5 m/s during peak tide. Compute available power and power at Betz limit.

**Solution**:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{available} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 50 \times (2.5)^3 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 50 \times 15.625 \\
&= 400{,}390 \approx 400 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

At Betz limit (η_B = 16/27 ≈ 0.593):
$$P_{Betz} = 0.593 \times 400 = 237 \text{ kW}$$

With realistic turbine efficiency of 40%:
$$P_{realistic} = 0.40 \times 400 = 160 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical: Wave Energy Capture

**Problem**: Estimate annual energy yield from a 100 m long attenuator (Pelamis-type) deployed where H = 2 m and T = 7 s, with 30% conversion efficiency and 80% capacity factor.

**Step 1**: Power per metre of crest:
$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 4 \times 7}{100.53} \approx 27.5 \text{ kW/m}$$

**Step 2**: Gross power for 100 m crest:
$$P_{gross} = 100 \times 27.5 = 2750 \text{ kW} = 2.75 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3**: After efficiency and capacity factor:
$$P_{avg} = 2.75 \times 0.30 \times 0.80 = 0.66 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4**: Annual energy:
$$E_{annual} = 0.66 \times 8760 = 5782 \text{ MWh/year}$$

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define tidal energy. List any two advantages and two disadvantages of tidal power plants.**

**Model Answer**:
Tidal energy is the renewable mechanical energy extracted from the periodic rise and fall of sea levels caused by the gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks)** + **Advantages & Disadvantages (1 mark)**.

**Advantages**:
1. Highly predictable — tides can be forecast centuries in advance.
2. Zero fuel cost; very long plant life (> 100 years); minimal GHG emissions.

**Disadvantages**:
1. Very high initial capital cost (civil works, barrage construction).
2. Limited suitable sites with sufficient tidal range; environmental disruption to estuarine ecosystems.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is OTEC? State the minimum temperature difference required for commercial viability.**

**Model Answer**:
OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion) is a renewable energy technology that converts the thermal energy stored in the ocean's warm surface water into electricity by exploiting the temperature difference with cold deep water.

**Minimum ΔT for commercial viability**: **20 °C** between surface (~25–28 °C) and deep (~4–6 °C) water layers.

> [!TIP]

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **tidal barrage power plant** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between single-basin single-effect, single-basin double-effect, and double-basin schemes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal basin has a surface area of 8 km² and a mean tidal range of 6 m. Assuming ρ = 1025 kg/m³ and tidal period T = 44,712 s, calculate:
   1. Total potential energy stored per tidal cycle.
   2. Average power potential.
   3. Effective power output if overall conversion efficiency is 30%. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Tidal Barrage Working Principle (7 marks)**:

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary. It contains:
- **Sluice gates** (for filling/emptying the basin without generating power),
- **Bulb turbines** (low-head Kaplan-type) embedded in the barrage,
- A **lock system** for navigation.

**Operating Sequence**:
1. **Flood Generation**: At low tide, sluice gates are opened. As tide rises, water flows into the basin through turbines, generating power.
2. **Hold Phase**: At high tide, gates and turbines are closed. Water is held in the basin.
3. **Ebb Generation**: As sea level falls, water is released from basin back to sea through turbines in reverse, generating power again.
4. **Wait Phase**: Basin refills at next high tide.

**Comparison of Schemes**:

| Scheme | Operating Mode | Generation Hours/Day | Power Output | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Basin Single-Effect | One direction only | ~10 h | Higher peak | Simple |
| Single-Basin Double-Effect | Both directions | ~14–16 h | Lower peak | Medium |
| Double Basin | Continuous | ~24 h | Lower instantaneous | Complex; 2 basins |

Single-effect uses the head during one phase only; double-effect exploits both flood and ebb tides; double-basin uses two interconnected basins to maintain a head difference continuously, trading power density for continuity.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**:

Given: A = 8 km² = 8 × 10⁶ m², R = 6 m, ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², T = 44,712 s, η = 0.30.

**Part (b.1) — Potential Energy**:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 10^6 \times 36$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_p &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 10^6 \times 36 \\
&= 1.448 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \\
&\approx 1448 \text{ GJ per tidal cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b.2) — Average Power**:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_p}{T} = \frac{1.448 \times 10^{12}}{44{,}712} = 32.4 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (b.3) — Effective Power**:

$$P_{eff} = \eta \times P_{avg} = 0.30 \times 32.4 = 9.72 \text{ MW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Using R = 6 m as the **full range** but forgetting R² in the formula. Many write $E_p = \rho g A R$ (linear in R) — wrong, it is **quadratic in R**.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Using T = 12 hours instead of T = 12.42 hours (44,712 s). The lunar semi-diurnal period is **12 h 25 min**, not 12 h.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Forgetting to convert km² to m² — dimensional errors cascade.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Writing "32.4 MW" without specifying *average* tidal power — must clarify peak vs average and apply efficiency after computing gross.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** plant using a closed Rankine cycle. State any three site requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wave-energy site has a mean wave height of 2.5 m and period of 8 s. Calculate:
   1. Total wave energy per unit surface area.
   2. Wave power per unit crest width.
   3. Annual energy yield from a 200 m long attenuator assuming 30% efficiency and 75% capacity factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Closed-Cycle OTEC Working (7 marks)**:

**Site Requirements** (any 3):
1. Minimum **ΔT = 20 °C** between warm surface water and cold deep water.
2. Location within **±20° latitude** of equator (tropical oceans).
3. Cold water pipe (CWP) depth ≤ **1000 m**.
4. Stable thermocline year-round.

**Working Principle (Closed Rankine Cycle)**:
1. **Warm Surface Water (27 °C)** is pumped through the **evaporator** heat exchanger.
2. Heat vaporizes the working fluid — typically **ammonia (NH₃)** with boiling point ≈ −33 °C at atmospheric pressure.
3. High-pressure ammonia vapor drives a **turbine** coupled to an electrical generator.
4. Low-pressure vapor enters the **condenser**, where **cold deep water (5 °C, pumped from ~1000 m)** removes heat and re-condenses ammonia to liquid.
5. Liquid ammonia is pressurized by the **feed pump** and returned to the evaporator — completing the closed loop.

**Key Output**: Fresh, desalinated water can be extracted as a by-product if a hybrid cycle is used.

**(b) Wave Energy Numerical (7 marks)**:

Given: H = 2.5 m, T = 8 s, ρ = 1025 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².

**Part (b.1) — Wave Energy per Unit Area**:

$$E = \frac{\rho g H^2}{16} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25}{16} = 3927 \text{ J/m²} \approx 3.93 \text{ kJ/m²}$$

**Part (b.2) — Wave Power per Unit Crest Width**:

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi} = \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 6.25 \times 8}{100.53}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 50}{100.53} \\
&= \frac{4{,}932{,}300}{100.53} \\
&\approx 49{,}060 \text{ W/m} \approx 49.1 \text{ kW/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b.3) — Annual Energy from 200 m Attenuator**:

Gross power for 200 m crest:
$$P_{gross} = 200 \times 49.1 = 9814 \text{ kW} = 9.81 \text{ MW}$$

Net average power (efficiency × capacity factor):
$$P_{net} = 9.81 \times 0.30 \times 0.75 = 2.21 \text{ MW}$$

Annual energy:
$$E_{annual} = 2.21 \times 8760 = 19{,}360 \text{ MWh/year} \approx 19.36 \text{ GWh/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Wave Energy Question**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Using $H^2$ in some places but $H$ in others — students confuse wave height H with amplitude a = H/2. Always use **H** (trough-to-crest) directly in $H^2$.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting the **group velocity factor** c/2; writing $P_w = E \cdot c$ instead of $P_w = E \cdot c_g$. For deep water, c_g = c/2 — easy to omit.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Not multiplying by **crest length** (200 m) when computing total power.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Confusing capacity factor with efficiency — they are multiplied, not added.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Master These for KTU Exams**

### **Tidal Energy Essentials**
- Tides caused by **Moon (67%) + Sun (33%)** gravitational pull; two cycles per lunar day.
- **Spring tides** at new/full moon (1.4× mean); **neap tides** at quarter moons (0.6× mean).
- **Tidal period** = 12 h 25 min = 44,712 s (not 12 h).
- Basin PE: $E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ — quadratic in R.
- Stream power: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$ — cubic in V (small V changes → big P changes).
- **Betz limit** for tidal turbines ≈ 59.3%.
- **La Rance** (France) — 240 MW, world's largest tidal barrage.

### **Wave Energy Essentials**
- Linear (Airy) wave theory valid for **H/L << 1** (small amplitude waves).
- Deep-water dispersion: $L = gT^2 / (2\pi)$.
- Energy density: $E = \rho g H^2 / 16$ (KE = PE, both = $\rho g H^2 / 16$).
- Power per unit crest: $P_w = \rho g^2 H^2 T / (32\pi)$.
- Practical shortcut: $P_w \approx H^2 T$ kW/m for typical seawater.
- **Group velocity** = c/2 in deep water — energy travels at half phase speed.
- Five device types: Point Absorber, Attenuator, Terminator, OWC, Overtopping.
- **OWC** uses **Wells turbine** (rotates same direction in both airflows).

### **OTEC Essentials**
- Viability threshold: **ΔT ≥ 20 °C**.
- **Tropical locations** within ±20° of equator; CWP depth ≤ 1000 m.
- **Carnot efficiency** with T_H = 300 K, T_C = 278 K → ~7.33% max.
- Real plant efficiency: **2–3%** after parasitic losses.
- **Closed cycle**: Rankine with NH₃ working fluid.
- **Open cycle (Claude)**: Seawater itself; produces freshwater by-product.
- **Hybrid**: Closed cycle + desalination.
- Largest heat exchangers required of any power plant technology.

### **Comparative Snapshot for Exam**

| Feature | Tidal | Wave | OTEC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best Predictability | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Power Density | High | Highest per m² | Low |
| Site Specificity | Very High | Moderate | Very High |
| Maturity | Commercial | Pilot | Pilot |
| 1-line Definition | Gravitational PE of seawater | Wind-induced surface oscillation | Thermal gradient between layers |

### **Formula Quick-Recall (Write in Margin During Exam)**
- Tidal basin PE: $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$
- Tidal avg power: $P = \tfrac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$ with **T = 44,712 s**
- Tidal stream: $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$
- Wave energy density: $E = \tfrac{\rho g H^2}{16}$
- Wave power flux: $P_w = \tfrac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$
- OTEC Carnot: $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU Valuation Desk**: For 14-mark questions, always (1) state the principle with a diagram, (2) write the relevant formula clearly, (3) substitute with proper units step-by-step, (4) highlight assumptions (e.g., "assuming deep water, linear wave theory"). Marks are lost mostly on missing units and unstated assumptions — not on the arithmetic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal Energy Conversion Systems: Tidal Barrages and Tidal Stream Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tidal Energy Conversion Systems: Tidal Barrages and Tidal Stream Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Tidal Energy?

Tidal energy is a form of **renewable hydropower** that converts the energy contained in the periodic rise and fall of sea levels (tides) into useful electrical power. The tides are generated by the **gravitational attraction of the Moon and Sun** on the Earth's oceans, combined with the Earth's rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> Tidal energy is the kinetic and potential energy of ocean waters harnessed by two principal technologies:
> 1. **Tidal Barrage Systems** — capture the *potential energy* difference between high and low tides.
> 2. **Tidal Stream Systems** — capture the *kinetic energy* of horizontal water currents.

## 1.2 Key Tidal Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **High Tide** | Maximum sea level reached during a tidal cycle |
| **Low Tide** | Minimum sea level reached during a tidal cycle |
| **Tidal Range (h)** | Vertical distance between successive high and low tides |
| **Tidal Period (T)** | **12 hours 25 minutes** (44714 seconds) for a semi-diurnal tide |
| **Spring Tide** | Maximum tidal range (Sun, Moon, Earth aligned) |
| **Neap Tide** | Minimum tidal range (Sun and Moon at right angles) |
| **Tidal Stream** | Horizontal flow of water during tide changes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underwater Dam" and the "Underwater Windmill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
>
> 🔹 **Tidal Barrage** = Imagine a **regular hydroelectric dam**, but instead of blocking a river, you block the *mouth of a bay or estuary*. When the tide comes in, the basin fills up. When the tide goes out, water trapped inside is released through **turbines** — exactly like opening the floodgates of a dam. The energy is **gravitational (potential) energy** of the elevated water column.
>
> 🔹 **Tidal Stream System** = Imagine a **wind turbine, but placed underwater in a fast-flowing tidal channel**. The strong horizontal currents (caused by tides squeezing through narrow passages) spin the blades. The energy is **kinetic energy** of moving water.

## 1.4 Why Tidal Energy Matters

- **Predictable** — unlike solar and wind, tides are astronomically predictable centuries in advance.
- **High Energy Density** — seawater density is **~1025 kg/m³**, almost **835 times** that of air (**1.225 kg/m³**), so tidal streams carry far more power per unit area than wind.
- **Non-Polluting** — Zero greenhouse gas emissions during operation.
- **Long Life** — Tidal plants have a lifespan of **75–100 years**.

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Density of seawater, **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**
> - Density of air, **ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Tidal period (semi-diurnal), **T = 12 h 25 min ≈ 44714 s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tidal Barrage Systems — Concept and Working

A **tidal barrage** is a long dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary or bay. It contains:
1. **Sluice Gates** — Low-head gates used to fill and empty the basin at minimum head loss.
2. **Bulb Turbines** — Reversible hydraulic machines that can generate power during both ebb and flood tides.
3. **Navigation Locks** — Allow ships to pass through the barrage.
4. **Embankment and Spillways** — Civil structures for safety.

### Working Principle (Ebb Generation — Most Common)

The complete operating cycle of a single-basin ebb-generation scheme occurs over **one full tidal cycle (~12 h 25 min)**:

| Phase | Duration | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **High Tide — Basin Filling** | ~2–3 hours | Sluice gates opened; basin fills with seawater at high level. |
| 2. **Holding (Waiting)** | ~2–3 hours | Sluice gates closed; basin water trapped at high level as sea recedes. |
| 3. **Ebb Generation** | ~3–4 hours | Head difference between basin and sea reaches a usable value; water released through turbines. |
| 4. **Low Tide — Equalization** | ~1–2 hours | Basin level equals sea level; generation stops. |
| 5. **Flooding Begins** | Rest | Sea level starts rising again; cycle repeats. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A typical tidal barrage generates power for only **4–5 hours per cycle (~7–8 hours standby)**, giving a **capacity factor of ~25–30%**.

## 2.2 Types of Tidal Barrage Schemes

### A. Single-Basin Single-Effect (Ebb Generation)
- Generates only during the **ebb tide** (high → low).
- Power is generated in one direction of flow.
- Simplest, lowest capital, but lowest output.

### B. Single-Basin Double-Effect
- Generates during **both** ebb and flood tides.
- Uses **reversible bulb turbines** that act as pumps and generators in both flow directions.
- **Doubles** the energy output compared to single-effect.

### C. Double-Basin Scheme
- Uses **two connected basins** at different levels.
- Provides **continuous, near-constant power output** — one basin generates while the other fills.
- Higher output and stability, but significantly higher capital cost.

## 2.3 Site Selection Criteria for Tidal Barrage

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Criteria:**
> 1. **High tidal range** (> 5 m economical; ideal > 7 m).
> 2. **Narrow, wide-mouthed estuary** (minimizes barrage length).
> 3. **Large basin area** to maximize stored volume.
> 4. **Rocky seabed** for dam foundation.
> 5. **Proximity to grid** and demand centers.
> 6. **Environmentally acceptable** (low ecological sensitivity).

## 2.4 Tidal Stream Systems — Concept and Working

Tidal stream systems extract energy from **horizontal water movement** as tides flood and ebb through channels, straits, and around headlands. They are essentially **underwater wind turbines**.

### Major Components

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Rotor Blades** | Capture kinetic energy of water flow (typically 2–3 blades) |
| **Nacelle** | Houses gearbox, generator, and control electronics |
| **Gearbox** | Steps up low rotor RPM to generator RPM |
| **Generator** | Converts mechanical energy to electricity (usually permanent magnet synchronous) |
| **Substructure (Pile/Mount)** | Anchors the turbine to the seabed (monopile, jacket, or gravity base) |
| **Yaw/Pitch System** | Aligns blades with current direction and controls power output |

### Types of Tidal Stream Turbines
1. **Horizontal Axis Turbine** — axis parallel to flow (most common, like a wind turbine).
2. **Vertical Axis Turbine** — axis perpendicular to flow (e.g., Gorlov, Darrieus).
3. **Oscillating Hydrofoil** — flap-like devices moving up/down in flow.
4. **Venturi/Tidgen-type** — uses converging channel to accelerate flow over a turbine.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula you must memorize** for KTU Part-B questions on Tidal Energy.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ | Energy available per tidal cycle from a basin | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho g A h^2}{2T}$ | Average tidal power per cycle (Barrage) | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 C_p$ | Power extracted by a tidal stream turbine | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $C_p^{max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Maximum (Betz) power coefficient for a turbine | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{ideal} = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho A V^3$ | Maximum extractable power (Betz limit) | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $P_{actual} = \eta \cdot P_{gross}$ | Actual electrical output (with overall efficiency η ≈ 0.30–0.40) | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $V = V_s + V_p$ | Spring tide velocity ≈ base velocity + phase velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $T_{semi} = 44714$ s | Semi-diurnal tidal period | seconds |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_{water}}{\rho_{air}} = \dfrac{1025}{1.225} \approx 837$ | Power density ratio water to air | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $h_{usable} = 0.68\, h_{max}$ | Effective (usable) head from full tidal range | m |

**Notation Key:**
- $\rho$ = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $A$ = basin area (m²) or rotor swept area (m²)
- $h$ = tidal range (m)
- $T$ = tidal period (s)
- $V$ = free-stream tidal current velocity (m/s)
- $C_p$ = power coefficient of the turbine
- $\eta$ = overall plant efficiency

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous Installations (Memorize for KTU Viva):**
> - **La Rance Tidal Barrage, France** (240 MW, operational since 1966) — world's first large-scale tidal barrage.
> - **Sihwa Lake Tidal Barrage, South Korea** (254 MW, 2011) — world's largest.
> - **SeaGen, Northern Ireland** (1.2 MW) — world's first commercial tidal stream generator.
> - **MeyGen, Scotland** — world's largest tidal stream array (6 MW, expandable to 86 MW).

## 2.7 Comparison: Tidal Barrage vs. Tidal Stream

| Feature | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy form harnessed** | Potential (head) | Kinetic (velocity) |
| **Civil structure** | Massive dam across estuary | Submerged turbine on seabed |
| **Visual impact** | High (large dam) | Low (mostly underwater) |
| **Ecological impact** | Significant (estuary ecosystem) | Moderate (localized) |
| **Capacity factor** | ~25–30% | ~40–60% |
| **Site requirement** | High tidal range + bay | Strong tidal current |
| **Construction cost** | Very high | Moderate to high |
| **Examples** | La Rance, Sihwa | SeaGen, MeyGen |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Power from a Tidal Barrage (Single Basin)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Logic Overview:** The power available from a tidal barrage is the rate at which potential energy is released as water flows from the high-tide basin level to the sea level.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
Consider a tidal basin of surface area $A$ (m²) with a tidal range $h$ (m) at mean tide. The volume of water in the basin varies between $0$ (at low tide) and $A \cdot h$ (at high tide).

**Step 2 — Express instantaneous potential energy.**
At an instant when the head between basin and sea is $h_i$ (m), the volume of water above sea level is $A \cdot h_i$. The potential energy of this elevated water is:

$$E_i = \rho \cdot g \cdot (\text{Volume}) \cdot (\text{Height above mean sea level})$$

**Step 3 — Consider incremental discharge.**
As the tide falls, an incremental volume $dV = A \cdot dh$ falls through a height $h$ to reach sea level. The energy released by this incremental volume is:

$$dE = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h \cdot dh$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the full tidal range.**
Total energy available per tidal cycle (from $h = 0$ at low tide to $h = h_{max}$ at high tide):

$$E_{cycle} = \int_{0}^{h} \rho \, g \, A \, h \, dh$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$E_{cycle} = \rho \, g \, A \left[ \dfrac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{h}$$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2 \quad \text{(Joules)}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute average power.**
Average power is the energy per cycle divided by the tidal period $T$:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{2T}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{2T} \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply practical correction factor.**
In practice, the full tidal range is not usable (turbines need a minimum head, and some water remains). The usable range is approximately **0.68 × h_max**:

$$P_{actual} = \dfrac{0.68^2 \cdot \rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{2T} = 0.231 \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{T}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A tidal barrage is proposed across an estuary with a basin area of **20 km²** and an average tidal range of **10 m**. Estimate:
> (a) The energy available per tidal cycle.
> (b) The average power output.
> Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T = 44714$ s.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Basin area, $A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Tidal range, $h = 10$ m
- Density of seawater, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Gravity, $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Tidal period, $T = 44714$ s

**Part (a): Energy per Cycle**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, g \, A \, h^2$$

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times (10)^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^7 \times 100$$

$$E = 1.005525 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.0055 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} \approx 10.055 \text{ GJ}}$$

**Part (b): Average Power**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, A \, h^2}{2T}$$

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{1.005525 \times 10^{13}}{44714}$$

$$P_{avg} = 2.249 \times 10^{8} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 224.9 \text{ MW}}$$

**Verification:** This is consistent with real-world large tidal plants (La Rance: 240 MW at ~8 m range over ~22 km²).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Power from a Tidal Stream Turbine (Betz Limit Approach)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation below mirrors the **wind turbine Betz-limit derivation**, but uses the **density of seawater**.

**Step 1 — Control volume setup.**
Consider a tidal stream turbine with rotor swept area $A$ placed in a free-stream current of velocity $V_1$. Just downstream of the rotor, the velocity reduces to $V_2$, and far downstream it recovers to $V_3$.

**Step 2 — Apply continuity.**
Mass flow rate through the rotor:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_3}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply momentum conservation.**
The thrust force on the rotor equals the rate of momentum change:

$$F = \dot{m} (V_1 - V_3) = \rho \, A \, \dfrac{V_1^2 - V_3^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Power extracted by the rotor.**
Power is force times velocity at the rotor (where velocity is the average of $V_1$ and $V_3$):

$$P = F \cdot \dfrac{V_1 + V_3}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4} \rho \, A \, (V_1 + V_3)(V_1^2 - V_3^2)$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of the induction factor.**
Let $V_3 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$ where $a$ is the axial induction factor. Then:

$$P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, A \, V_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

Define the power coefficient $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$.

**Step 6 — Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$.**

$$\dfrac{dC_p}{da} = 4 \left[ (1-a)^2 - 2a(1-a) \right] = 0$$

$$(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0 \implies a = \dfrac{1}{3}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute optimal $a$ back.**

$$C_p^{max} = 4 \times \dfrac{1}{3} \times \left( \dfrac{2}{3} \right)^2 = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

**Step 8 — Maximum extractable power.**

$$\boxed{P_{ideal} = \dfrac{16}{27} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \, A \, V^3 = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho \, A \, V^3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Betz is 16/27, half-of-half is 8/27, multiply by rho, A, V-cubed."*

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Tidal Stream Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A tidal stream turbine with rotor diameter **15 m** operates in a current of **2.5 m/s**. Density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³. Calculate:
> (a) The maximum theoretical power (Betz limit).
> (b) The actual electrical power output at an overall efficiency of 35%.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 15$ m $\Rightarrow$ Area $A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (15)^2/4 = 176.71$ m²
- Velocity $V = 2.5$ m/s
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³

**Part (a): Betz-limit power**

$$P_{ideal} = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho A V^3 = \dfrac{8}{27} \times 1025 \times 176.71 \times (2.5)^3$$

$$P_{ideal} = 0.2963 \times 1025 \times 176.71 \times 15.625$$

$$\boxed{P_{ideal} \approx 8.37 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \approx 837 \text{ kW}}$$

**Part (b): Actual electrical output**

$$P_{actual} = \eta \times P_{ideal} = 0.35 \times 837 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 293 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Engineering Analysis

| Criterion | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| Governing equation | $P = \rho g A h^2 / (2T)$ | $P = C_p \cdot \rho A V^3 / 2$ |
| Primary energy type | Potential | Kinetic |
| Head/velocity needed | Head $\geq 5$ m typical | Velocity $\geq 2$ m/s typical |
| Capacity factor | 25–30% | 40–60% |
| Construction time | 7–10 years | 2–4 years |
| Lifetime | 75–100 years | 25–30 years (rotor components) |
| Capital cost per kW | **High (\$3000–6000)** | **Moderate-High (\$2500–5000)** |
| LCOE (estimate) | \$0.10–0.20/kWh | \$0.15–0.30/kWh |
| Environmental impact | Major habitat change | Localized seabed disturbance |
| Grid integration challenge | Intermittent; needs storage | More predictable, still intermittent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Tidal Barrage Operating Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tide Rises - Sea Level Increases] --> B{High Tide Reached?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Open Sluice Gates - Basin Fills]
    C --> D[Close Sluice Gates - Holding Phase]
    D --> E{Sufficient Head Available?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Water Flows Through Bulb Turbines]
    F --> G[Generator Produces Electricity - Ebb Generation]
    G --> H{Basin Level equals Sea Level?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Generation Stops - Equalization]
    I --> J[Tide Begins to Rise Again - Cycle Repeats]
    E -- No --> D
    H -- No --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Tidal Stream System Components and Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tidal Current Flow] --> B[Rotor Blades Capture Kinetic Energy]
    B --> C[Shaft Rotation]
    C --> D[Gearbox Steps Up RPM]
    D --> E[Permanent Magnet Generator]
    E --> F[Power Conditioning Unit]
    F --> G[Subsea Cable to Shore]
    G --> H[Onshore Grid / Load]
    I[Yaw and Pitch Control System] --> B
    J[Substructure Anchored to Seabed] --> B
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Comparison of Tidal Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Tidal Energy Source] --> S{Conversion Method?}
    S -- Head Difference --> P1[Tidal Barrage System]
    S -- Flow Velocity --> P2[Tidal Stream System]
    P1 --> Q1[Single Basin - Single Effect]
    P1 --> Q2[Single Basin - Double Effect]
    P1 --> Q3[Double Basin Scheme]
    P2 --> R1[Horizontal Axis Turbine]
    P2 --> R2[Vertical Axis Turbine]
    P2 --> R3[Oscillating Hydrofoil]
    P2 --> R4[Venturi / Tidgen Type]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Power Flow in a Tidal Stream Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Current Input - Velocity V] --> B[Rotor - Converts KE to Mechanical Energy]
    B --> C[Gearbox - Steps Up RPM]
    C --> D[Generator - Mechanical to Electrical]
    D --> E[AC to AC Converter]
    E --> F[Step-up Transformer]
    F --> G[Subsea Transmission Cable]
    G --> H[Onshore Switchyard]
    H --> I[Utility Grid]
    B -. Control Signals .-> J[SCADA and Pitch Controller]
    J -. Feedback .-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When drawing block diagrams in the exam, always show **three essential elements** — *Energy source*, *Conversion chain*, and *Output/Delivery*. Marks are awarded for clear flow arrows and properly labeled blocks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between Tidal Barrage and Tidal Stream Energy Conversion Systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Energy form | Potential energy (head) | Kinetic energy (current) |
| 2. Civil structure | Dam across estuary | Submerged turbine on seabed |
| 3. Site criteria | High tidal range, narrow bay | Fast tidal current (>2 m/s) |
| 4. Visual impact | Significant | Minimal (underwater) |
| 5. Capacity factor | ~25–30% | ~40–60% |
| 6. Example | La Rance (France) | SeaGen (N. Ireland) |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**List the main components of a tidal barrage and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sluice Gates** — Allow water to fill/empty the basin at low head loss.
2. **Bulb Turbines** — Reversible machines generating electricity from head difference.
3. **Embankment / Dam Wall** — Holds back water and forms the basin.
4. **Navigation Locks** — Permit ship passage across the barrage.
5. **Spillways** — Handle overflow during extreme tides/storms.
6. **Powerhouse** — Houses generators, transformers, and switchgear.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Module 3 Internal Choice):** Both Question A and Question B cover Module 3. You must answer **one**. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a single-basin tidal barrage in ebb-generation mode**. Discuss its advantages and limitations.

**(b)** A tidal barrage has a basin area of **15 km²** and an average tidal range of **6 m**. Calculate:
   (i) The energy available per tidal cycle.
   (ii) The average power output.
   Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T = 44714$ s.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 Marks)**

A single-basin tidal barrage in **ebb-generation mode** operates in the following sequence within one tidal period (~12 h 25 min):

**Step 1 — Flooding / Filling Phase (2 marks)**
As the tide rises outside the barrage, sluice gates are opened. The basin fills to the level of the high tide. No power is generated during this phase.

**Step 2 — Holding / Waiting Phase (2 marks)**
After high tide, sluice gates are closed. The basin retains water at high-tide level while the sea level begins to recede with the outgoing tide. Power is not yet generated.

**Step 3 — Generation Phase (2 marks)**
