When the head difference between the basin and the sea exceeds the **minimum operating head** of the bulb turbine (typically 1–2 m), the turbines are opened. Water flows from basin → turbines → sea, generating electricity. Generation continues for 3–4 hours.

**Step 4 — Equalization (1 mark)**
Generation stops when basin and sea levels equalize at low tide. The cycle then repeats with the next flood tide.

**Advantages (Bonus 1 mark if asked):** Simple design, single turbine type, lowest cost.
**Limitations:** Generation only during ebb → low capacity factor (~25%), high civil cost.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $A = 15$ km² = $15 \times 10^6$ m²
- $h = 6$ m
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T = 44714$ s

**Step 1 — Energy per cycle (3 marks)**

$$E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (15 \times 10^6) \times 36$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 5.4 \times 10^8$$

$$E \approx 2.715 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \approx 2.715 \text{ TJ}$$

**Step 2 — Average power (3 marks)**

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E}{T} = \dfrac{2.715 \times 10^{12}}{44714}$$

$$P_{avg} \approx 6.07 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \approx 60.7 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Final answer (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.715 \text{ TJ/cycle}, \quad P_{avg} \approx 60.7 \text{ MW}}$$

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **components, working, and types of tidal stream energy conversion systems**. Discuss two advantages over tidal barrages.

**(b)** A horizontal-axis tidal stream turbine has a rotor diameter of **20 m** and operates at a free-stream velocity of **2.8 m/s**.
   (i) Calculate the **maximum theoretical power** (Betz limit).
   (ii) Find the **actual electrical output** if the overall efficiency is **38%**.
   Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Components, Working, Types (7 Marks)**

**Components (3 marks):**
- **Rotor blades** (2 or 3, typically lift-based, like an aircraft wing) — capture kinetic energy.
- **Nacelle** — houses the gearbox, generator, brakes, yaw mechanism.
- **Drive train** — gearbox steps up rotor RPM (typically 10–20 RPM) to generator RPM (~1500 RPM).
- **Generator** — usually permanent-magnet synchronous (PMSG) for high efficiency at variable speed.
- **Substructure** — monopile, jacket, or gravity base anchoring the device to the seabed.
- **Yaw/pitch control** — orients turbine to flow direction and limits power in high currents.

**Working (2 marks):**
Tidal currents flowing through the rotor cause the blades to rotate (lift force). The gearbox transmits torque to the generator. The generated AC is conditioned, stepped up, and sent via subsea cable to shore.

**Types (1 mark):**
1. Horizontal axis (most common)
2. Vertical axis (Gorlov helical)
3. Oscillating hydrofoil
4. Venturi-effect / Tidgen

**Advantages over barrages (1 mark):**
1. No large dam → minimal environmental disruption.
2. Modular, scalable; can be deployed in arrays.
3. Higher capacity factor; lower visual impact.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 20$ m $\Rightarrow A = \pi (20)^2/4 = 100\pi \approx 314.16$ m²
- $V = 2.8$ m/s
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $C_p^{max} = 16/27$, $\eta = 0.38$

**Step 1 — Betz-limit power (3 marks)**

$$P_{ideal} = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho A V^3 = \dfrac{8}{27} \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times (2.8)^3$$

$$P_{ideal} = 0.2963 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times 21.952$$

$$\boxed{P_{ideal} \approx 2.09 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \approx 2.09 \text{ MW}}$$

**Step 2 — Actual electrical output (3 marks)**

$$P_{actual} = \eta \times P_{ideal} = 0.38 \times 2.09 \text{ MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 0.794 \text{ MW} \approx 794 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Final answer (1 mark):** Power at Betz limit ≈ 2.09 MW; actual output ≈ 0.794 MW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to convert km² to m² loses **2 marks** instantly. Always write $1 \text{ km}^2 = 10^6 \text{ m}^2$.
> 2. **Tidal Period Trap:** Using $T = 12$ hours = 43200 s instead of 44714 s may cost a mark for "approximation."
> 3. **Betz Formula:** Many students incorrectly write $P = \rho A V^3 / 2$ without $C_p$. The correct Betz-limited form is $P = 8\rho A V^3/27$.
> 4. **Forgetting Efficiency:** Tidal stream power must always be multiplied by overall plant efficiency $\eta$ (mechanical + electrical + transmission).
> 5. **Confusing Barrage vs. Stream:** In a barrage, energy = potential; in a stream, energy = kinetic. Wrong formula in the wrong context = **0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 6. **No Diagram in ESE:** Always include a **neat, labeled block diagram**. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for diagrams in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Tidal Energy Conversion Systems**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Tidal energy comes from **gravitational pull of Moon (major) and Sun (minor)**.
- ☐ Two main technologies: **Tidal Barrage (potential)** and **Tidal Stream (kinetic)**.
- ☐ **Tidal period (semi-diurnal) = 12 h 25 min = 44714 s** — *MUST memorize*.
- ☐ Seawater density = **1025 kg/m³**; gives ~837× more power density than air.

**Tidal Barrage:**
- ☐ Components: Sluice gates, bulb turbines, locks, spillways, powerhouse.
- ☐ Single-basin **single-effect (ebb)**: power for ~4–5 h/cycle, capacity factor ~25–30%.
- ☐ Single-basin **double-effect**: uses reversible turbines; doubles output.
- ☐ **Double-basin**: continuous output, high cost.
- ☐ Site: high tidal range (>5 m), narrow bay, large area, rocky foundation.

**Tidal Stream:**
- ☐ Components: Rotor, gearbox, generator, substructure, control system.
- ☐ Types: Horizontal axis, vertical axis, oscillating hydrofoil, venturi.
- ☐ Power formula: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3 C_p$.
- ☐ Betz limit: $C_p^{max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$.

**Key Equations (Memorize for Numerical Solving):**
- ☐ $E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ — barrage energy per cycle.
- ☐ $P_{avg} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{2T}$ — barrage average power.
- ☐ $P_{ideal} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V^3$ — stream power at Betz limit.
- ☐ $P_{actual} = \eta \times P_{gross}$ — apply efficiency.

**Examples to Remember:**
- ☐ La Rance (France) — 240 MW tidal barrage (1966).
- ☐ Sihwa Lake (S. Korea) — 254 MW, world's largest barrage.
- ☐ SeaGen (N. Ireland) — first commercial tidal stream device.
- ☐ MeyGen (Scotland) — largest tidal stream array.

**Common Comparison Points (for 3-mark & 7-mark questions):**
- ☐ Barrage = high impact, high cost, low CF.
- ☐ Stream = low impact, modular, higher CF.
- ☐ Tidal energy is **predictable** (advantage over solar/wind).
- ☐ Capital cost is **high**; environmental concerns are significant.

**Valuation Traps to Avoid:**
- ☐ Always convert km² → m² (multiply by 10⁶).
- ☐ Always multiply stream power by $C_p$ (or Betz 16/27).
- ☐ Always multiply by efficiency $\eta$ for electrical output.
- ☐ Draw a labeled block diagram in every 7-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Energy Conversion Systems: Oscillating Water Columns, Point Absorbers, and Overtopping Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wave Energy Conversion Systems: Oscillating Water Columns, Point Absorbers, and Overtopping Devices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) Systems **Wave Energy Conversion (WEC)** is the process of capturing kinetic and potential energy from ocean surface waves and converting it into useful electrical or mechanical work. Ocean waves possess an enormous energy density — roughly **10–50 kW per meter of wave front** in moderately energetic seas — making them one of the most promising renewable resources. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The classification of WEC devices into **Oscillating Water Columns (OWC), Point Absorbers, and Overtopping Devices** is a **direct syllabus extract** from Module 3 of the 2024 Scheme. Students must remember the device name, its working principle, and at least one real-world example for each category. ### 1.2 Formal KTU Definition > **Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) System** — A mechanical–electrical system that harnesses the oscillatory motion of ocean waves (typically within the frequency range of **0.05 Hz to 0.5 Hz** with wavelengths of **2 m to 500 m**) by coupling a hydrodynamic absorber to a Power Take-Off (PTO) mechanism, which may be pneumatic, hydraulic, or direct-drive. ### 1.3 Three Primary WEC Architectures (KTU Classification) | # | WEC Type | Energy Capture Mode | Dominant Wave Parameter | |---|----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** | Pneumatic air-flow conversion | Wave period & height | | 2 | **Point Absorber** | Heaving/oscillating buoy | Buoy displacement (small in size relative to wavelength) | | 3 | **Overtopping Device** | Potential energy of stored water | Wave run-up height | --- ## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies ### 🔹 Oscillating Water Column (OWC) — "The Blowing Bottle" Imagine holding an empty plastic bottle upside down with its neck pointing into your mouth. As you push the bottle up and down, air is alternately forced out of and sucked back into the bottle. If you connect the neck to a small spinning pinwheel, the pinwheel turns. **This is exactly how an OWC works** — a wave pushes water up into a chamber, compressing trapped air; the air rushes through a turbine (Wells turbine) which spins in the same direction regardless of air-flow direction. ### 🔹 Point Absorber — "The Bobbing Cork" Throw a cork into wavy water. The cork moves up and down (heaves) with the passing waves. If you attach a rope from the cork to a generator on the sea floor, the rhythmic bobbing can produce electricity. **A point absorber is a much larger, engineered version of that cork** — small in horizontal extent (typically < 1/10 of the wavelength) but tuned to capture energy across a broad frequency range. ### 🔹 Overtopping Device — "The Seawater Reservoir" Picture a wide low wall on a beach. Waves crash and spill water over the wall into a raised reservoir behind it. The water trapped in the reservoir is then released through a conventional low-head hydro turbine. **An overtopping device is the ocean-engineering version** — it converts wave motion into a head of water for conventional hydro-electric conversion. > [!NOTE] > **Capture Width Concept:** A key performance metric for WECs is the **Capture Width (W)** in meters. It is the width of an incident wave front from which the device captures *all* the wave power. A device with capture width **W = 10 m** effectively "harvests" the energy of a **10 m wide** wave front, even if its physical structure is much smaller. The dimensionless **Capture Width Ratio (CWR)** is W divided by the device's physical characteristic length. --- ## 1.5 Why Wave Energy? (Engineering Justification) - **Predictability:** Waves can be forecast 24–48 hours in advance with high accuracy (unlike solar/wind). - **High Energy Density:** Water is ~**800 times denser than air**, so a small volume carries large energy. - **Capacity Factor:** Offshore wave systems routinely achieve **25–40%** capacity factors, exceeding most wind and solar PV installations. - **Low Visual Footprint:** Submerged or near-shore devices have minimal landscape impact. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants (Bolded as per protocol):** > - Density of seawater: **ρ_sw ≈ 1025 kg/m³** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Typical significant wave height: **H_s = 1 to 5 m** > - Typical wave period: **T = 5 to 20 s** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Oscillating Water Column (OWC) — Detailed Working Principle An **OWC** is a partially submerged hollow structure (chamber) open to the sea below the waterline. As ocean waves enter the chamber, the water column inside rises and falls, alternately compressing and decompressing the air trapped above. This bidirectional air flow is channeled through a self-rectifying **Wells turbine** coupled to a generator. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Wave enters the chamber** through a submerged aperture. 2. **Water column rises** → air is compressed → high-pressure air is expelled through the turbine duct. 3. **Wave retreats** → water column falls → low-pressure region forms → air is sucked back in through the same turbine. 4. **Wells turbine** (with symmetric airfoil blades, typically **NACA 0020 profile**) rotates in the same direction for both inward and outward flow, eliminating the need for rectification valves. 5. **PTO Generator** converts mechanical rotation to electricity. > [!NOTE] > **Wells Turbine Aerodynamic Equation:** > The torque coefficient C_T of a Wells turbine depends on the **flow coefficient (φ)**: > $$\phi = \frac{U}{c_a}$$ > where U is the blade tip speed and c_a is the axial air-flow velocity. The relationship is approximately: > $$C_T \approx K \phi$$ > with K being a turbine-specific constant (typically 0.4–0.6). This linearity is what makes the Wells turbine ideal for OWC applications. ### 2.1.2 Famous OWC Installations | Plant | Location | Rated Power | Status | |-------|----------|-------------|--------| | **LIMPET 500** | Islay, Scotland | 500 kW | Decommissioned (commercial demonstrator) | | **Mutriku OWC** | Basque Country, Spain | 296 kW | Operational (16 chambers) | | **Kujukuri OWC** | Japan (demonstration) | Pilot | Research | --- ## 2.2 Point Absorber — Detailed Theoretical Framework A **Point Absorber** is a floating buoy (or submerged body) with horizontal dimensions **small compared to the incident wavelength** (typically diameter **D ≤ λ/5**, where λ is the wavelength). Despite the small size, it can absorb a disproportionately large amount of wave energy when properly tuned. ### 2.2.1 Operating Modes - **Heave Mode:** Buoy moves vertically (up-down). Most common mode. - **Pitch Mode:** Buoy rotates about a horizontal axis. - **Surge Mode:** Buoy moves horizontally (less common, requires mechanical resistance). ### 2.2.2 Hydrodynamic Theory (Heaving Buoy) The equation of motion for a heaving buoy (linear potential theory) is: $$m \ddot{z} + B(\omega) \dot{z} + K_s z = F_{exc}(t) - F_{PTO}(t)$$ where: - **m** = total mass (buoy + added mass) [kg] - **B(ω)** = radiation damping coefficient [N·s/m] - **K_s** = hydrostatic stiffness [N/m] - **F_exc(t)** = wave excitation force [N] - **F_PTO(t)** = Power Take-Off force [N] - **z** = heave displacement [m] ### 2.2.3 Resonance Condition (Critical for Exam) Maximum power extraction occurs when the system is in **resonance**: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{m + m_a(\omega_n)}} = \omega_{wave}$$ For a typical buoy, **natural period ≈ 2–4 s**, ideally matched to the dominant wave period. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Small body, big appetite":** A point absorber tuned to resonance can capture energy from a wave front up to **5–10 times wider** than its own physical diameter. This is the famous "**lensing effect**" of wave energy. --- ## 2.3 Overtopping Device — Detailed Operating Principle The **Overtopping Device** is conceptually the simplest of the three. Waves are guided up a ramp into a reservoir above mean sea level. The trapped water is then released through **low-head Kaplan or Pelton turbines** back to the sea. ### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic 1. **Wave ramp / collector** channels incoming waves upward. 2. **Water spills over** the crest into an elevated storage reservoir (typical head: **2–6 m**). 3. **Reservoir** acts as a buffer — a constant head supply. 4. **Low-head hydro turbine** (Kaplan-type) discharges water back to the sea, generating electricity. 5. **Output smoothing** — the reservoir provides a degree of time-averaging, reducing power fluctuations. ### 2.3.2 Real-World Implementations | Device | Country | Concept | |--------|---------|---------| | **Wave Dragon** | Denmark | Floating offshore overtopping platform (rated prototype: 7 MW) | | **TAPCHAN** (Tapered Channel) | Norway | Shore-fixed tapered channel that funnels waves into a head-pond | | **SSG (Seawater Slot-Cone Generator)** | Norway | Multi-reservoir stepped design for incremental head generation | ### 2.3.3 Power Equation for Overtopping Devices The hydraulic power available from an overtopping device is: $$P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{res}$$ where: - **Q** = average overtopping discharge [m³/s] - **H_res** = mean head of the reservoir [m] The overtopping discharge per unit width, **q**, is given by the **Van der Meer / Kofoed-type empirical formula**: $$\frac{q}{\sqrt{g \cdot H_s^3}} = f\left(\frac{H_s}{L_0}, R_c/H_s, \tan\alpha\right)$$ where **R_c** is the crest freeboard and **α** is the ramp slope. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|--------------------| | 1 | Wave power per unit crest length (deep water) | $P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \cdot g^2}{64\pi} \cdot H_s^2 \cdot T$ | $P$ in W/m, $H_s$ in m, $T$ in s | | 2 | Wave energy density (per unit area) | $E = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H_s^2$ | $E$ in J/m² | | 3 | Capture Width (CWR) | $W = P_{absorbed} / (P_{incident}/m)$ | $W$ in m | | 4 | Capture Width Ratio | $CWR = W / D$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Buoy equation of motion | $m \ddot{z} + B(\omega)\dot{z} + K_s z = F_{exc} - F_{PTO}$ | SI units | | 6 | Natural frequency (heave) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s / (m + m_a)}$ | rad/s | | 7 | Overtopping hydraulic power | $P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} g Q H_{res}$ | Watts | | 8 | Wells turbine flow coefficient | $\phi = U / c_a$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | Hydrostatic stiffness (heaving cylinder) | $K_s = \rho_{sw} g A_w$ | $A_w$ = waterplane area, N/m | | 10 | Air pressure fluctuation in OWC chamber | $\Delta p(t) = \dfrac{\rho_a L_a}{A_c} \ddot{V}_{water}(t)$ | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas collectively allow an engineer to: > (a) estimate the **available wave resource** at a coastal site, > (b) size the **buoy/diameter** for a point absorber, > (c) determine the **reservoir volume** for an overtopping device, and > (d) select the **Wells turbine rating** for an OWC. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wave Power Per Unit Crest Width (Deep-Water Linear Wave Theory) This is one of the **most-asked derivations in KTU exams** for the Energy Systems paper. ### Step 1: Set up the velocity potential For a linear (Airy) deep-water wave of height **H** and wavelength **λ**, the velocity potential is: $$\phi(x, z, t) = \dfrac{H g}{2 \omega} \cdot e^{kz} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$ where **k = 2π/λ** is the wavenumber and **ω = 2π/T** is the angular frequency. ### Step 2: Derive the horizontal and vertical velocity components From $\phi$, the velocities are: $$u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{H g k}{2 \omega} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$ $$w = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \dfrac{H g k}{2 \omega} e^{kz} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$ ### Step 3: Compute the dynamic pressure Using the linearized Bernoulli equation: $$p_{dyn} = -\rho_{sw} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \rho_{sw} \dfrac{H g}{2} e^{kz} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$ ### Step 4: Express energy density (per unit horizontal area) For a linear wave, the **total energy density** is the sum of kinetic and potential: $$E = E_k + E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g a^2 \cdot 2 = \rho_{sw} g a^2$$ where amplitude **a = H/2**. Substituting: $$E = \rho_{sw} g \left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H^2$$ ### Step 5: Compute the energy flux (wave power per unit crest width) In deep water, the **group velocity** $c_g = g / (2\omega) = c/2$, where **c = gT/(2π)** is the phase velocity. Power per unit crest width is: $$P = E \cdot c_g = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H^2 \cdot \dfrac{g}{2\omega}$$ Substituting $\omega = 2\pi / T$: $$P = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H^2 \cdot \dfrac{g T}{4\pi} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$$ ### Step 6: Express in terms of significant wave height Using $H_s \approx \sqrt{2} H_{rms}$ and noting that the deep-water power is conventionally expressed using $H_s$, we obtain: $$\boxed{P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \, g^2}{64 \pi} \, H_s^2 \, T \;\; \text{[W/m]}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Check (KTU-style calculation):** For $H_s = 2$ m, $T = 8$ s, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²: > $$P = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81^2 \times 2^2 \times 8}{64 \pi} \approx \dfrac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 4 \times 8}{201.06} \approx 15{,}720 \text{ W/m} \approx 15.7 \text{ kW/m}$$ > This matches field-measured values for moderate Atlantic swells. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capture Width Definition for a Point Absorber The capture width is a critical performance index for WEC devices. We define it for a heaving buoy. ### Step 1: Define incident wave power Incident wave power per unit crest width, from Derivation 1: $$P_{inc} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g^2 H_s^2 T}{64\pi}$$ ### Step 2: Total incident power on capture width W $$P_{incident} = P_{inc} \cdot W$$ ### Step 3: Equate to absorbed power By definition, **capture width** is the value of W such that: $$P_{absorbed} = P_{inc} \cdot W$$ ### Step 4: Solve for W $$\boxed{W = \dfrac{P_{absorbed}}{P_{inc}}}$$ ### Step 5: Capture Width Ratio (CWR) For a buoy of characteristic length **D** (e.g., diameter): $$\boxed{CWR = \dfrac{W}{D}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** A **CWR > 1** is desirable — it means the device harvests energy from a wave front wider than its physical size. The theoretical maximum (thermodynamic limit) for a heaving axisymmetric buoy is **CWR_max ≈ λ/(2π D)**, which for typical sea states can reach **5–10**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Evaluation of WEC Types | Parameter | OWC | Point Absorber | Overtopping | |-----------|-----|----------------|--------------| | **Energy capture mechanism** | Pneumatic air compression | Heave/pitch motion of buoy | Hydraulic head via reservoir | | **WEC size relative to λ** | Large (extends across wave crest) | Small (D < λ/5) | Very large (collector ramp) | | **Power Take-Off (PTO)** | Wells turbine + generator | Linear generator / hydraulic motor | Kaplan/Pelton turbine | | **Visual impact** | Moderate (chamber visible) | Low (mostly submerged) | High (reservoir visible) | | **Survivability in storms** | Good (large, robust) | Challenging (mooring loads) | Moderate (ramp subject to overwash) | | **Power smoothing** | Inherent (air cushion) | Requires active control | Excellent (reservoir buffer) | | **Typical efficiency** | 30–50% | 40–70% (when tuned) | 25–40% | | **Grid readiness** | Mature (LIMPET, Mutriku) | Pilot (PowerBuoy, Archimedes) | Pilot (Wave Dragon) | | **Cost per kWh (LCOE est.)** | High (0.30–0.50 USD/kWh) | High (0.25–0.45 USD/kWh) | High (0.30–0.50 USD/kWh) | | **KTU Example** | LIMPET (Scotland) | PowerBuoy (USA) | Wave Dragon (Denmark) | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A coastal site has $H_s = 2.5$ m and $T = 7$ s. An OWC device with **Wells turbine rated 250 kW** is proposed. (a) Compute the incident wave power per metre of crest. (b) Estimate the required crest width of the device to fully utilize the turbine at a system efficiency of **η = 0.4**. (c) Comment on the feasibility. **Solution:** ### (a) Incident wave power per metre Using the deep-water formula: $$P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g^2 H_s^2 T}{64 \pi}$$ $$P = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81^2 \times 2.5^2 \times 7}{64 \pi}$$ Numerator: $1025 \times 96.24 \times 6.25 \times 7 = 4{,}317{,}217$ Denominator: $64 \pi = 201.06$ $$P \approx 21{,}470 \text{ W/m} \approx 21.5 \text{ kW/m}$$ ### (b) Required crest width Useful power delivered = η × incident power $$P_{useful} = 0.4 \times 21{,}470 = 8{,}588 \text{ W/m}$$ For 250 kW output: $$L = \dfrac{250{,}000}{8{,}588} \approx 29.1 \text{ m}$$ ### (c) Feasibility A **29 m wide OWC** is comparable to the LIMPET 500 (chamber width ≈ 21 m, rated 500 kW). With the higher waves in this site (2.5 m), 29 m is **physically achievable**, but civil engineering costs for such a coastal structure are very high. **Feasibility is marginal economically**; the device may be better suited to higher-energy sites. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic WEC System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ocean Waves
Kinetic + Potential] --> B[Wave Energy Converter
OWC / Point Absorber / Overtopping] B --> C[Power Take-Off
Wells Turbine / Linear Gen / Kaplan] C --> D[Generator / Alternator] D --> E[Power Conditioning
Rectifier + Inverter] E --> F[Grid / Load] B --> G[Mooring / Foundation
Substructure] G --> A style A fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#80bfff,stroke:#003366 style D fill:#4da6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#fff style E fill:#1a8cff,stroke:#003366,color:#fff style F fill:#0066cc,stroke:#003366,color:#fff style G fill:#999999,stroke:#333333 ``` ## 4.2 Oscillating Water Column (OWC) — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB W1[Incoming Wave Crest
Water Rises in Chamber] --> P1[Air Volume Compressed
High Pressure] P1 --> WT1[Wells Turbine
Spins Clockwise] WT1 --> G1[Generator] G1 --> EL1[Electrical Output] W2[Wave Trough
Water Falls] --> P2[Air Volume Expands
Low Pressure] P2 --> WT2[Air Drawn Back Through
Same Wells Turbine] WT2 --> WT1 style W1 fill:#aaccff,stroke:#003366 style W2 fill:#aaccff,stroke:#003366 style P1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style P2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 style WT1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style WT2 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000 style G1 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style EL1 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900 ``` ## 4.3 Point Absorber — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB SEA[Sea Surface
Wave Action] --> BUOY[Buoy
Heaves Up and Down] BUOY --> SPRING[Hesitating Rod /
Linear Generator Piston] SPRING --> PTO[Power Take-Off
Linear Generator] PTO --> CTRL[Control System
Phase & Damping Tuning] CTRL --> SPRING PTO --> INV[Rectifier + Inverter] INV --> GRID[Electrical Grid] ANCHOR[Mooring /
Sea-Bed Anchor] --> BUOY style SEA fill:#aaccff,stroke:#003366 style BUOY fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600 style SPRING fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300 style PTO fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style CTRL fill:#cc99ff,stroke:#660099,color:#fff style INV fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style GRID fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900 style ANCHOR fill:#999999,stroke:#333333,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Overtopping Device — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR OCEAN[Ocean Waves
Incoming] --> RAMP[Ramp / Collector
Funnels Wave Run-up] RAMP --> CREST[Crest Spillway
Water Overtops] CREST --> RES[Elevated Reservoir
Stored Head 2 to 6 m] RES --> TURB[Low-Head Kaplan
or Pelton Turbine] TURB --> GEN[Generator] GEN --> POW[Electrical Output to Grid] TURB --> SEA2[Water Discharged
Back to Sea] RES --> TURB style OCEAN fill:#aaccff,stroke:#003366 style RAMP fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300 style CREST fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#cc6600 style RES fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366 style TURB fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#fff style GEN fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600 style POW fill:#ffff99,stroke:#999900 style SEA2 fill:#aaccff,stroke:#003366 ``` ## 4.5 Comparative WEC Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Select WEC Type] --> Q1{Site is shore-fixed
with high wave run-up?} Q1 -- Yes --> OD[Overtopping Device
e.g., TAPCHAN] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Deep water offshore
with small buoy possible?} Q2 -- Yes --> PA[Point Absorber
e.g., PowerBuoy] Q2 -- No --> OWC[Oscillating Water Column
e.g., LIMPET, Mutriku] OD --> CHECK[Check: power smoothing, cost, environment] PA --> CHECK OWC --> CHECK style START fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style OD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style PA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style OWC fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099 style CHECK fill:#ffccff,stroke:#990099 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy conversion system. Mention the function of the Wells turbine in it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** is a wave energy conversion device in which a partially enclosed chamber is open to the sea below the waterline. As waves enter the chamber, the water column inside rises and falls, alternately compressing and expanding the air column above it. This bidirectional air flow is routed through a **Wells turbine** coupled to a generator. (2 marks) **Function of the Wells Turbine:** It is a self-rectifying axial-flow turbine with symmetric airfoil blades (e.g., NACA 0020) that rotates in the **same direction** for both inward and outward air flow, eliminating the need for flap valves or rectification. (1 mark) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is a "point absorber" in wave energy conversion? Why is its capture width ratio (CWR) often greater than unity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **point absorber** is a wave energy device whose horizontal dimensions are small compared to the incident wavelength (typically diameter D < λ/5). It captures energy through the heaving, pitching, or surging motion of a buoy driven by passing waves. (1.5 marks) **CWR > 1:** Because of the wave-radiation/diffraction interaction, a small tuned buoy can extract energy from a wave front that is much wider than its physical diameter — this is the **lensing effect**. The theoretical maximum CWR for a heaving axisymmetric body is on the order of λ/(2π D), which in typical sea states can reach 5–10. (1.5 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the working principle of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the Wells turbine and discuss its advantages over conventional turbines for this application. **(b) [7 marks]** A deep-water site has significant wave height $H_s = 3$ m and peak period $T = 9$ s. Calculate: (i) the wave energy density (per unit area), (ii) the wave power per unit crest width, (iii) the incident wave power on a 50 m wide OWC device. **Take ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s².** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** ### Part (a) — OWC Working Principle [7 marks] **Working Principle (4 marks):** An Oscillating Water Column consists of a hollow concrete or steel chamber that is open to the sea below the waterline. The upper part of the chamber contains a trapped air column. As ocean waves enter the submerged opening, the water surface inside the chamber rises and falls with the wave motion. - **Wave crest phase:** Water rises inside the chamber → air above is compressed → high-pressure air is expelled through a duct at the top, driving the **Wells turbine** and generator. - **Wave trough phase:** Water falls inside the chamber → air expands → low-pressure draws atmospheric air back in through the same turbine, which continues to rotate in the same direction. This bidirectional flow is converted to unidirectional rotation by the **Wells turbine**, which is then coupled to a generator. A control valve (throttle) regulates air flow to prevent turbine stall at high flow rates. **Role of Wells Turbine (2 marks):** - Symmetric airfoil blades give identical lift characteristics in both flow directions. - Self-rectifying — no flap valves required (lower maintenance, higher reliability). - Compact design suitable for the bidirectional, fluctuating air flow of an OWC. - Operates effectively across a wide range of flow coefficients. **Advantages (1 mark):** 1. Simple, robust design with no moving parts in seawater. 2. Self-rectifying, eliminating valve maintenance. 3. Good efficiency across a range of wave conditions. 4. Mature technology with proven commercial-scale installations (LIMPET, Mutriku). --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks] **Given:** $H_s = 3$ m, $T = 9$ s, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(i) Wave energy density (E):** $$E = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H_s^2$$ $$E = \dfrac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 3^2$$ $$E = \dfrac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9$$ $$E = \dfrac{1}{8} \times 90{,}485.25 \approx 11{,}310 \text{ J/m}^2$$ **(ii) Wave power per unit crest width (P):** $$P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g^2 H_s^2 T}{64 \pi}$$ $$P = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81^2 \times 3^2 \times 9}{64 \pi}$$ Numerator: $1025 \times 96.24 \times 9 \times 9 = 8{,}372{,}748$ Denominator: $64 \pi \approx 201.06$ $$P \approx \dfrac{8{,}372{,}748}{201.06} \approx 41{,}635 \text{ W/m} \approx 41.6 \text{ kW/m}$$ **(iii) Incident power on 50 m wide device:** $$P_{total} = P \times L = 41{,}635 \times 50 = 2{,}081{,}750 \text{ W} \approx 2.08 \text{ MW}$$ **Comment:** This 50 m wide OWC receives ~2 MW of incident wave power, of which typically 30–50% (i.e., 0.6–1.0 MW) can be converted to electrical output, depending on system efficiency and Wells turbine performance. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the working principle of a **point absorber** wave energy device with a suitable diagram. Discuss the concept of "resonance tuning" and its importance in maximizing power capture. **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **overtopping device**. Compare the three main WEC types (OWC, Point Absorber, Overtopping) in terms of power take-off mechanism, site suitability, and survivability. --- **Model Solution for Question B:** ### Part (a) — Point Absorber [7 marks] **Working Principle (3 marks):** A point absorber is a floating buoy (or submerged body) whose horizontal dimensions are small compared to the incident wavelength ($D \leq \lambda/5$). It captures wave energy through the relative motion between the buoy and a fixed reference (e.g., a reaction plate on the sea floor or a fixed spar). The buoy heaves (moves vertically) under wave action. The relative motion drives a **linear generator** (or hydraulic pump/motor), converting mechanical oscillation into electrical power. A **mooring system** keeps the device on station. **Resonance Tuning (3 marks):** Power absorption is maximized when the buoy's natural heave frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s/(m + m_a)}$ matches the dominant wave frequency $\omega_{wave}$. At resonance: - The displacement amplitude of the buoy is large. - The phase between buoy velocity and wave excitation force is optimum (~90°) for power transfer. - The capture width ratio (CWR) peaks. In practice, real seas are broadband, so **latching** (mechanical clamping) and **active PTO control** are used to broaden the effective bandwidth around resonance. **Importance (1 mark):** Without resonance tuning, the buoy's heave amplitude is small, and power capture drops dramatically. Resonance tuning is therefore the single most important design consideration for a point absorber. --- ### Part (b) — Overtopping Device & Comparison [7 marks] **Overtopping Device Working (3 marks):** - Waves approach a **ramp/collector** that funnels them upward. - Water overtops the crest into an **elevated reservoir** (head 2–6 m). - The reservoir acts as a buffer, providing quasi-steady head to a **low-head Kaplan (or Pelton) turbine**. - Discharge water returns to the sea; electricity is generated continuously, providing **power smoothing**. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | OWC | Point Absorber | Overtopping | |---------|-----|----------------|--------------| | **PTO** | Wells turbine + generator | Linear generator / hydraulic motor | Kaplan / Pelton turbine | | **Site suitability** | Shore-fixed or near-shore | Offshore deep water | Shore-fixed, high run-up sites | | **Survivability** | Good (robust chamber) | Challenging (mooring loads) | Moderate (reservoir overtopping risk) | | **Power smoothing** | Inherent (air cushion) | Requires active control | Excellent (reservoir buffer) | | **KTU example** | LIMPET (Scotland) | PowerBuoy (USA) | Wave Dragon (Denmark) | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Forgetting units:** In numerical problems, students often write the final answer without units (e.g., "P = 41,635" without "W/m"). Examiner deducts **0.5–1 mark** strictly. > 2. **Wrong value of ρ_sw:** Some students use ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³. Always use **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** for ocean wave problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. > 3. **Confusing H, H_s, and H_rms:** The deep-water power formula uses $H_s$ (significant wave height), not H. Substituting H directly leads to a **factor-of-2 error**. > 4. **OWC vs. Overtapping confusion:** Students often describe overtopping devices as "OWC with reservoirs." They are fundamentally different: OWC uses **pneumatic** conversion, Overtopping uses **hydraulic head** conversion. > 5. **Skipping the Wells turbine explanation:** In OWC questions, examiners expect at least 1–2 lines on **why** the Wells turbine is self-rectifying (symmetric airfoils). A bare mention costs marks. > 6. **No block diagram:** For 7-mark descriptive questions, a diagram is **mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks. Always draw a clear, labeled block diagram. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) ### ✅ **Must-Know Definitions** - ☐ Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) - ☐ Oscillating Water Column (OWC) - ☐ Point Absorber - ☐ Overtopping Device - ☐ Wells Turbine - ☐ Capture Width (W) and Capture Width Ratio (CWR) - ☐ Hydrostatic Stiffness ($K_s$) - ☐ Added Mass ($m_a$) - ☐ Radiation Damping ($B(\omega)$) ### ✅ **Must-Know Formulas** - ☐ Wave power per unit crest: $P = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} g^2 H_s^2 T}{64\pi}$ [W/m] - ☐ Wave energy density: $E = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho_{sw} g H_s^2$ [J/m²] - ☐ Hydraulic power (overtopping): $P_{hyd} = \rho_{sw} g Q H_{res}$ - ☐ Buoy natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s / (m + m_a)}$ - ☐ Capture width: $W = P_{absorbed} / P_{inc}$ - ☐ Hydrostatic stiffness: $K_s = \rho_{sw} g A_w$ - ☐ Wells turbine flow coefficient: $\phi = U / c_a$ ### ✅ **Must-Know Examples** - ☐ OWC: **LIMPET 500** (Scotland), **Mutriku** (Spain) - ☐ Point Absorber: **PowerBuoy** (USA), **Archimedes Waveswing** - ☐ Overtopping: **Wave Dragon** (Denmark), **TAPCHAN** (Norway) ### ✅ **Must-Know Physical Constants** - ☐ ρ_sw = **1025 kg/m³** - ☐ g = **9.81 m/s²** - ☐ H_s (typical sea) = **1–5 m** - ☐ T (typical sea) = **5–20 s** ### ✅ **Must-Know Comparative Facts** - ☐ OWC uses **pneumatic** energy conversion - ☐ Point Absorber uses **mechanical/hydraulic** conversion with resonance tuning - ☐ Overtopping uses **hydraulic head** conversion (essentially low-head hydro) - ☐ Point absorbers have small physical size but can have **CWR > 1** (lensing effect) - ☐ Overtopping devices provide the best **power smoothing** (reservoir buffer) - ☐ OWCs are the most **mature** of the three technologies ### ✅ **Must-Know Design Principles** - ☐ **Resonance tuning** is critical for point absorber efficiency. - ☐ **Wells turbine** symmetry enables self-rectification in OWC. - ☐ **Ramp geometry** (slope, crest freeboard) governs overtopping efficiency. - ☐ **Survivability** in storm conditions is the main techno-economic challenge. - ☐ **Active control** (latching, reactive PTO) can broaden the effective frequency bandwidth.

topics.title: Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC): Working Principles and Types (Closed-Cycle, Open-Cycle, Hybrid)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC): Working Principles and Types (Closed-Cycle, Open-Cycle, Hybrid)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy technology that exploits the **natural temperature gradient** between the **warm surface waters** of tropical/sub-tropical oceans (heated by solar radiation) and the **cold deep-ocean waters** (typically found at depths of **800 m to 1000 m**) to drive a heat engine and generate useful electrical or mechanical work. The system is fundamentally a **low-temperature, low-pressure thermal power cycle** constrained by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** OTEC is a classic application of a heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs (warm ocean surface ≈ 25–28 °C and cold deep ocean ≈ 4–8 °C). The minimum usable temperature differential (ΔT) required for a commercially viable OTEC plant is approximately **20 K (20 °C)** — a critical threshold you must remember for numerical problems.

The theoretical framework is rooted in the **Carnot efficiency** of an ideal heat engine:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

where $T_C$ and $T_H$ are the absolute temperatures (in **Kelvin**) of the cold and warm reservoirs, respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of OTEC as a **"giant natural refrigerator running in reverse"**. Imagine a swimming pool whose surface water feels lukewarm (~26 °C) on a sunny day, but if you jump into the deep end you feel a sharp chill (~5 °C). That *temperature difference* is a vast, continuous, free reservoir of thermal energy.

A practical analogy is the **steam engine invented by Thomas Newcomen**, but instead of burning coal to create a hot side, OTEC simply *borrows* the sun's stored heat from the ocean surface and uses the cold deep water as a sink. A working fluid (ammonia, propane, or even warm seawater itself) is evaporated by the warm water, expanded through a turbine to make electricity, and then re-condensed by the cold deep water — exactly like a refrigerator compressor cycle, but **power-producing** instead of cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics for OTEC:**
> - **Minimum ΔT (temperature difference):** ≥ **20 K** (20 °C)
> - **Warm surface temperature (T_H):** 25–28 °C → **298 K – 301 K**
> - **Cold deep temperature (T_C):** 4–8 °C → **277 K – 281 K**
> - **Typical theoretical Carnot efficiency:** ≈ **6 % – 8 %** (very low!)
> - **Practical (real) efficiency:** ≈ **2 % – 3 %**
> - **Cold-water pipe depth:** **800 m – 1000 m**
> - **Best geographic locations:** Within ± 20° of the equator (tropical belt)

## 1.3 Why OTEC is Thermodynamically Challenging

Because $T_H$ and $T_C$ are so close together (only a 20 K span), the Carnot efficiency ceiling is **drastically low**. For $T_H = 301$ K and $T_C = 281$ K:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{281}{301} \approx 6.64\%$$

This low efficiency means that an OTEC plant must process **enormous volumes of seawater** to generate meaningful power. This is why the technology favors floating or shore-based plants in deep-ocean coastal regions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The OTEC plant operates as a **heat engine** between two thermal reservoirs. The generalized operational logic is:

1. **Step 1 — Heat Absorption (Evaporator):** Warm surface seawater flows through a heat exchanger (called the *evaporator*). A working fluid with a low boiling point (e.g., **ammonia at –33 °C** or **seawater itself**) absorbs this heat and **boils/evaporates** at low pressure.
2. **Step 2 — Expansion (Turbine):** The high-pressure vapor expands through a **turbine**, producing rotational mechanical work that drives an **electrical generator**.
3. **Step 3 — Heat Rejection (Condenser):** The low-pressure vapor leaving the turbine is condensed back to liquid by passing cold deep-ocean water (pumped up through a long pipe) through a second heat exchanger (the *condenser*).
4. **Step 4 — Pumping (Recompression):** A **working-fluid pump** returns the condensed liquid to the high-pressure side, ready to be re-evaporated. Simultaneously, **warm-water** and **cold-water pumps** circulate the ocean reservoirs.
5. **Step 5 — Power Output:** Net useful power = (Turbine power) – (Pump power consumptions). Since pump work is significant (≈ 30 % of gross output), the **net efficiency is markedly lower** than the gross Carnot efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** The gross thermal efficiency of an OTEC cycle is bounded by Carnot. The **net plant efficiency** must subtract all parasitic loads (cold-water pumping, warm-water pumping, working-fluid pumping, and fluid friction in the long cold-water pipe). This is a common KTU 14-mark sub-question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Carnot (ideal) efficiency | dimensionless | Temperatures MUST be in Kelvin |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{real}} = (0.30 \text{ to } 0.45) \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$ | Actual cycle efficiency (multiplier) | dimensionless | Empirical; depends on working fluid and equipment |
| 3 | $Q_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{H,in} - T_{H,out})$ | Heat absorbed from warm water | kW | $T_{H,out} - T_{evap} \geq 2\text{ K}$ for pinch |
| 4 | $Q_C = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{C,out} - T_{C,in})$ | Heat rejected to cold water | kW | $T_{C,out} \leq T_{cond} + 2\text{ K}$ |
| 5 | $\dot{W}_{turb} = \dot{m}_f \cdot (h_3 - h_4)$ | Turbine power output | kW | $h_3$ = turbine inlet enthalpy; $h_4$ = outlet |
| 6 | $\dot{W}_{net} = \dot{W}_{turb} - \sum \dot{W}_{pump}$ | Net electrical power | kW | Sum of all parasitic loads subtracted |
| 7 | $h_{fg,\text{NH}_3} \approx 1367$ | Latent heat of ammonia | kJ/kg | At typical OTEC evaporator pressure |
| 8 | $P_{\text{evap}} \approx 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5$ | Evaporator pressure (closed cycle) | bar | For ammonia working fluid |
| 9 | $A_{\text{CWP}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_C}{U_{CWP} \cdot \Delta T_{LM,C}}$ | Cold-water pipe heat-exchanger area | m² | $U$ ≈ 2–4 kW/m²·K |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{\min} = T_H - T_C$ | Available temperature difference | K | **Must be ≥ 20 K** for economic viability |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often plug temperatures in **°C** directly into the Carnot formula. Always convert to **Kelvin** by adding 273.15. Losing this conversion costs full marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

OTEC is not just an academic curiosity. Practical applications include:
- **Continuous baseload renewable power** (24 × 7 × 365, unlike solar/wind).
- **Desalinated freshwater** as a valuable by-product (especially from the open and hybrid cycles).
- **Deep-ocean aquaculture** using the cold nutrient-rich water pumped up from depth.
- **Air-conditioning and refrigeration** of coastal buildings using the cold deep water (a *closed-loop cold-water district cooling* application).
- **Hydrogen production** via electrolysis using the baseload OTEC electricity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Theoretical (Carnot) Efficiency of OTEC

We start from the Clausius statement of the Second Law for a cyclic heat engine operating between two reservoirs:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$$

For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, equality holds. The net work output of any heat engine is the difference between heat absorbed and heat rejected:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= Q_H - Q_C \\
\eta_{th} &= \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a reversible Carnot cycle, entropy is conserved:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S_{\text{cycle}} &= 0 \\
\frac{Q_H}{T_H} &= \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th,\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H} \\
&= 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental efficiency ceiling** of any OTEC plant. For a typical tropical site with $T_H = 300$ K and $T_C = 280$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{280}{300} \\
&= 1 - 0.9333 \\
&= 0.0667 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 6.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Less than 7 % of the incoming solar energy stored in the warm surface layer can be converted to work, even in the ideal case.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Power Output of a Closed-Cycle OTEC Plant

The **closed (Anderson) cycle** uses a working fluid (typically **ammonia, NH₃**) that circulates continuously inside a sealed loop. The four principal thermodynamic states are:

- **State 1:** Saturated liquid leaving the condenser (low T, low P)
- **State 2:** Compressed liquid leaving the working-fluid pump (high P, slightly higher T)
- **State 3:** Saturated vapor leaving the evaporator (high P, warm T)
- **State 4:** Two-phase mixture leaving the turbine (low P, cold T)

The mass flow rate of the working fluid is determined from the **evaporator heat balance**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{evap} &= \dot{m}_f \,(h_3 - h_2) \\
\dot{m}_f &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{h_3 - h_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **turbine power** output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_{turb} &= \dot{m}_f \,(h_3 - h_4) \\
&= \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}\,(h_3 - h_4)}{h_3 - h_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **net plant power** subtracts the parasitic loads. The three principal pumps are:
1. Working-fluid pump: $\dot{W}_{p,f} = \dot{m}_f \,(h_2 - h_1)$
2. Warm seawater pump: $\dot{W}_{p,w} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w \cdot \Delta P_w}{\rho_w \cdot \eta_p}$
3. Cold seawater pump: $\dot{W}_{p,c} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \cdot \Delta P_c}{\rho_c \cdot \eta_p}$

The **net electrical output** is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_{net} &= \dot{W}_{turb} - \dot{W}_{p,f} - \dot{W}_{p,w} - \dot{W}_{p,c} \\
&= \dot{m}_f \,(h_3 - h_4) - \dot{m}_f \,(h_2 - h_1) - \dot{W}_{p,w} - \dot{W}_{p,c}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding **net thermal efficiency** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{net} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{evap}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Warm-Water Flow Rate (Numerical Pattern)

This derivation follows the **typical KTU 14-mark numerical pattern** for OTEC. Given a desired net power output, calculate the required warm and cold seawater flow rates.

**Given (typical KTU problem data):**
- $T_{H,in} = 28\,°C = 301$ K
- $T_{C,in} = 5\,°C = 278$ K
- $T_{evap} = 25\,°C$, $T_{cond} = 10\,°C$
- $\dot{W}_{net} = 1$ MW
- $c_{p,w} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 0.030$ (3 % practical)

**Step 1 — Heat input required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\eta_{net}} = \frac{1000\,\text{kW}}{0.030} = 33{,}333.33\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat absorbed by working fluid from warm water:**

Assume the warm water cools by $\Delta T_w = T_{H,in} - T_{H,out}$. With $T_{H,out} \approx T_{evap} + 2\,°C = 27\,°C$, we get $\Delta T_w = 1$ K (typical evaporator pinch is 2 K, but warm-side drop is often 3–5 K). Taking $\Delta T_w = 5$ K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_w} \\
&= \frac{33{,}333.33}{4.186 \times 5} \\
&= 1592.4 \;\text{kg/s} \quad \approx \;1.6 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected to cold water:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{out} &= \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{net} \\
&= 33{,}333.33 - 1000 = 32{,}333.33\;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Required cold-water flow rate:**

Assuming the cold water warms by $\Delta T_c = 4$ K (from 5 °C to 9 °C, leaving a 1 K approach to the 10 °C condenser):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_c &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{out}}{c_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c} \\
&= \frac{32{,}333.33}{4.186 \times 4} \\
&= 1931.1 \;\text{kg/s} \quad \approx \;1.9 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Conclusion:** A 1 MW OTEC plant requires roughly **1.6 m³/s of warm surface water** and **1.9 m³/s of cold deep water** — totalling **~3.5 m³/s**, illustrating why the seawater-handling infrastructure is *massive*.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix of the Three OTEC Cycles

| Parameter | **Closed Cycle (Anderson)** | **Open Cycle (Claude)** | **Hybrid Cycle** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Ammonia (NH₃) / Propane / R-134a | Warm seawater itself (vapor) | Warm seawater + NH₃ |
| Evaporation pressure | ~1.0–1.5 bar (above atm.) | < 0.04 bar (vacuum) | Vacuum for seawater + closed for NH₃ |
| Turbine type | Standard axial/turbo | Huge low-pressure axial (voluminous) | Combination |
| Condensation | Cold deep water in heat exchanger | Direct contact with cold deep water | Both modes |
| Fresh water by-product | No | **Yes** (a major advantage) | **Yes** |
| Net efficiency | 2.0 – 3.0 % | 1.7 – 2.5 % | 2.5 – 3.5 % |
| Equipment size | Compact | Very large (vacuum vessels) | Medium |
| Pilot plants | OTEC-1 (Hawaii, USA) | 210 kW plant in Hawaii | Nauru, Hawaii, Okinawa |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — The Three OTEC Cycles in One Schematic

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the interactions between the warm-water source, cold-water source, working-fluid loop, turbine-generator, and by-product streams, in compliance with the Mermaid safeguards specified in the engine rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    coldSource[Cold Deep Seawater 4 to 8 C from 800 to 1000 m]:::reservoir

    coldPump[Cold Water Pump]:::pump
    evap[Evaporator Heat Exchanger]:::heatex
    cond[Condenser Heat Exchanger]:::heatex
    turbine[Turbine Generator Set]:::machine
    wfPump[Working Fluid Pump]:::pump
    closedLoop[Closed Anderson Cycle NH3 working fluid]:::cycle
    openLoop[Open Claude Cycle warm seawater as fluid]:::cycle
    hybridLoop[Hybrid Cycle combined closed and open]:::cycle
    freshWater[Desalinated Fresh Water Output]:::output
    netPower[Net Electrical Power Output]:::output

    warmSource --> warmPump --> evap
    coldSource --> coldPump --> cond
    evap --> turbine --> cond
    wfPump --> closedLoop
    warmSource -.-> openLoop
    coldSource -.-> openLoop
    openLoop -.-> freshWater
    hybridLoop --> freshWater
    hybridLoop --> netPower
    closedLoop --> netPower
    openLoop --> netPower
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Evaporator Warm Surface Seawater heats NH3]:::heatex
```

```mermaid
    B --> C[Low Pressure Steam drives Turbine]:::machine
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Hybrid OTEC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    warmIn[Warm Surface Seawater Inlet]:::reservoir --> flashEvap[Flash Evaporator Vacuum]:::heatex
    flashEvap --> steamOut[Low Pressure Steam]:::state
    steamOut --> turb[Vacuum Turbine]:::machine
    turb --> cond[Surface Condenser using Closed Loop NH3]:::heatex
    cond --> freshOut[Fresh Water Output]:::output
    cond --> vent[Vacuum Vent]:::pump
    vent --> flashEvap
    coldIn[Cold Deep Seawater Inlet]:::reservoir --> nh3Cond[NH3 Condenser]:::heatex
    nh3Cond --> evapNH3[NH3 Evaporator using Warm Water]:::heatex
    evapNH3 --> turbNH3[NH3 Turbine Secondary Loop]:::machine
    turbNH3 --> nh3Pump[NH3 Recirculation Pump]:::pump
    nh3Pump --> evapNH3

    classDef reservoir fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heatex fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef state fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef machine fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Narrative Block Description of Each Cycle

**Closed-Cycle (Anderson Cycle):** Warm surface seawater circulates through the **evaporator** heat exchanger, where it transfers heat to a working fluid (typically **ammonia, NH₃**) that boils at low pressure. The ammonia vapor drives a turbine, then condenses in a second heat exchanger (the **condenser**) using cold deep seawater. The condensed liquid ammonia is repressurized by a pump and returned to the evaporator. The warm and cold seawater are *never in direct contact* with the working fluid — they only transfer heat through metal walls.

**Open-Cycle (Claude Cycle):** Warm seawater itself becomes the working fluid. It is admitted into a **vacuum flash evaporator** at very low pressure (≈ 0.03 bar), where it **boils spontaneously** at ambient temperature. The resulting low-pressure steam drives a turbine. The exhaust steam is **condensed by direct contact** with cold deep seawater. The condensate is **pure, desalinated fresh water** — a valuable co-product. The non-condensable gases (mainly air) are removed by a **vacuum pump**.

**Hybrid Cycle:** The hybrid cycle *combines* the open and closed loops. Warm seawater is flash-evaporated in a vacuum, and the resulting low-pressure steam is condensed on the *cold side of an ammonia evaporator*. This chills the ammonia, which then *itself* vaporizes and drives a secondary turbine-generator. The warm-seawater steam is condensed on the ammonia side, producing **fresh water**. The cold deep water is used to condense the ammonia vapor in the closed loop. This configuration **eliminates the need for a direct-contact condenser** in the steam loop and offers the highest net efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). State the minimum temperature difference required for a viable OTEC plant.**

**Model Answer:**

Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) is a method of generating electrical power by exploiting the **natural temperature difference** between the warm surface water (25–28 °C) and the cold deep-ocean water (4–8 °C) of tropical seas. A heat engine is operated between these two thermal reservoirs to produce useful work.

The **minimum temperature difference (ΔT_min)** required for a commercially viable OTEC plant is **20 K (20 °C)**. Below this value, the Carnot efficiency falls below ~6 %, making the capital and pumping costs uneconomical.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Compare the open-cycle and closed-cycle OTEC systems in terms of working fluid and by-product.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **Closed Cycle (Anderson)** | **Open Cycle (Claude)** |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Ammonia (NH₃) or propane in a sealed loop | Warm seawater itself (vaporized in a vacuum) |
| By-product | None (purely electrical power) | **Desalinated fresh water** (from condensed steam) |
| Turbine size | Compact, standard | Very large (handles huge volume of low-pressure steam) |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A — Alternative 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **working principle of a closed-cycle (Anderson) OTEC plant**. Discuss the role of the working fluid and the heat exchangers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An OTEC plant operates with warm surface water at **28 °C** and cold deep water at **6 °C**. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum (Carnot) efficiency.
- (ii) The expected practical net efficiency if the cycle achieves **40 % of Carnot**.
- (iii) The required heat-input rate to produce **500 kW** of net power. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Working of Closed-Cycle OTEC

A closed-cycle (Anderson) OTEC plant uses a **secondary working fluid** such as **ammonia (NH₃)**, which has a very low boiling point (–33 °C at atmospheric pressure). The four major components are:

1. **Evaporator (Heat Exchanger 1):** Warm surface seawater, after passing through a warm-water pump, flows through the shell side of the evaporator. The ammonia circulates on the tube side, absorbs heat, and **boils at approximately 1.0–1.5 bar**, even though its temperature rises only marginally above the warm seawater.
2. **Turbine:** The high-pressure ammonia vapor expands through the turbine, producing **rotational work** that drives the electrical generator. The vapor exits as a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture.
3. **Condenser (Heat Exchanger 2):** Cold deep seawater, pumped from 800–1000 m depth, flows through the condenser. It **absorbs the latent heat** of the ammonia vapor, condensing it back to saturated liquid.
4. **Working-Fluid Pump:** The condensed liquid ammonia is repressurized by a pump and sent back to the evaporator, completing the **sealed closed loop**.

The warm and cold seawater streams **never mix** with the working fluid — heat transfer occurs only through the **heat-exchanger walls**. This is why it is called a *closed* cycle.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $T_H = 28\,°C$, $T_C = 6\,°C$, $\eta_{\text{real}} = 0.40 \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}}$, $\dot{W}_{net} = 500$ kW.

**Step (i) — Convert to Kelvin:**

$$
T_H = 28 + 273.15 = 301.15\,\text{K}
$$

$$
T_C = 6 + 273.15 = 279.15\,\text{K}
$$

**Step (ii) — Carnot efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} \\
&= 1 - \frac{279.15}{301.15} \\
&= 1 - 0.9269 \\
&= 0.0731 \;\text{or}\; 7.31\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Practical efficiency (40 % of Carnot):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{real}} &= 0.40 \times 0.0731 \\
&= 0.02924 \;\text{or}\; 2.92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iv) — Required heat-input rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\eta_{\text{real}}} \\
&= \frac{500}{0.02924} \\
&= 17{,}099.86\,\text{kW} \\
&\approx 17.1\,\text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 7.31\%$, $\eta_{\text{real}} = 2.92\%$, $\dot{Q}_{in} = 17.1$ MW.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Alternative 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **working of an open-cycle (Claude) OTEC plant**. Mention its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1 MW OTEC plant uses warm seawater at **27 °C** and cold seawater at **5 °C**. If the warm water cools by **4 K** in the evaporator and the cold water warms by **3 K** in the condenser, calculate:
- (i) The required warm-water flow rate ($c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K).
- (ii) The required cold-water flow rate.
- (iii) The net thermal efficiency of the plant. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Open-Cycle (Claude) OTEC

In an open-cycle (Claude) OTEC plant, **warm seawater itself acts as the working fluid**. The principal components and their functions are:

1. **Flash Evaporator (Vacuum Chamber):** Warm surface seawater is sprayed into a large vacuum chamber maintained at a very low absolute pressure (≈ 0.03 bar). At this pressure, water boils at ~26 °C, so the warm seawater **flash-evaporates** partially, producing low-pressure steam.
2. **Vacuum Turbine:** The low-pressure steam drives a **large low-pressure turbine** coupled to an electrical generator. The turbine is physically very large because the steam volume per kilowatt is huge at near-vacuum conditions.
3. **Direct-Contact Condenser:** The exhaust steam is brought into **direct contact** with cold deep seawater inside a condenser. The steam condenses into **pure desalinated water**.
4. **Vacuum Pump:** A small continuous vacuum pump removes **dissolved air and non-condensable gases** that leak into the system, since these would otherwise destroy the vacuum and stop the flash evaporation.

**Advantages:**
- No separate working fluid required — seawater is free.
- Produces **fresh water** as a valuable by-product.
- Lower capital cost for working fluid inventory.

**Limitations:**
- Very **large equipment size** (vacuum chambers and turbine are bulky).
- Turbine is expensive due to enormous volume flow rate of low-pressure steam.
- Direct contact between seawater and steam can cause biofouling and corrosion.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $T_{H,in} = 27\,°C$, $T_{C,in} = 5\,°C$, $\dot{W}_{net} = 1000$ kW, $\Delta T_w = 4$ K, $\Delta T_c = 3$ K, $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Step (i) — Convert temperatures to Kelvin and find Carnot efficiency:**

$$
T_H = 300.15\,\text{K}, \quad T_C = 278.15\,\text{K}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{278.15}{300.15} \\
&= 1 - 0.9267 \\
&= 0.0733 \;\text{or}\; 7.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Assume a practical net efficiency of 3 % (typical for OTEC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} &= \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\eta_{net}} = \frac{1000}{0.03} = 33{,}333.33\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Required warm-water flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_w &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_w} \\
&= \frac{33{,}333.33}{4.186 \times 4} \\
&= \frac{33{,}333.33}{16.744} \\
&= 1990.4\;\text{kg/s} \\
&\approx 2.0\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iv) — Heat rejected to condenser:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{out} &= \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{net} \\
&= 33{,}333.33 - 1000 \\
&= 32{,}333.33\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (v) — Required cold-water flow rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_c &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{out}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T_c} \\
&= \frac{32{,}333.33}{4.186 \times 3} \\
&= \frac{32{,}333.33}{12.558} \\
&= 2574.6\;\text{kg/s} \\
&\approx 2.6\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (vi) — Net thermal efficiency (using 3 % assumption):**

$$
\eta_{net} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{1000}{33{,}333.33} = 0.03 = 3.0\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\dot{m}_w \approx 2.0$ m³/s, $\dot{m}_c \approx 2.6$ m³/s, $\eta_{net} = 3.0\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature unit trap:** Failing to convert °C → K before applying the Carnot formula. *Marks deducted: 1 to 2 out of 7.*
> 2. **Heat balance sign error:** Forgetting that $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{W}_{net} + \dot{Q}_{out}$ (energy balance). Students sometimes equate $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out}$, which is wrong.
> 3. **Using gross instead of net efficiency:** When the question gives a "practical efficiency" (e.g., 3 %), you must use that directly, not the Carnot efficiency, to find $\dot{Q}_{in}$.
> 4. **Confusing working fluids:** In part (a) of Question A, students write "seawater" as the working fluid for the closed cycle. Remember — closed cycle uses **ammonia**.
> 5. **Missing the by-product:** In Question B (a), the fresh-water by-product of the open cycle is a *2-mark standalone point*. Many examiners specifically hunt for it.
> 6. **Diagrams without labels:** KTU examiners demand **labeled arrows** showing warm/cold water flow and working fluid flow. Unlabeled block diagrams get only partial credit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — OTEC**

- ✅ **OTEC =** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion — a heat engine between warm surface and cold deep seawater.
- ✅ **Minimum ΔT for viability:** **20 K (20 °C)**. Always state this in 3-mark questions.
- ✅ **Carnot formula** uses **Kelvin** only. $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - T_C / T_H$.
- ✅ **Typical Carnot efficiency** of OTEC: **6 – 8 %**. Practical: **2 – 3 %**.
- ✅ **Cold-water pipe depth:** **800 – 1000 m**.
- ✅ **Three types:** Closed (Anderson) → ammonia; Open (Claude) → seawater + fresh water; Hybrid → best of both.
- ✅ **Closed cycle working fluid:** **Ammonia (NH₃)** — chosen for low boiling point (–33 °C) and high latent heat.
- ✅ **Open cycle by-product:** **Desalinated fresh water** — a major advantage.
- ✅ **Hybrid cycle** combines open and closed loops to eliminate direct-contact condenser and produce fresh water.
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{W}_{net} + \dot{Q}_{out}$ — never forget this.
- ✅ **Net power** = Turbine power − (warm-water pump + cold-water pump + working-fluid pump). Parasitic loads are significant (~30 % of gross).
- ✅ **Seawater handling is massive** — a 1 MW OTEC plant needs ~3.5 m³/s of combined seawater flow.
- ✅ **Geographical limit:** Within ±20° latitude of the equator (tropical belt only).
- ✅ **24 × 7 baseload power** — a unique advantage over solar and wind.
- ✅ **By-product uses** — fresh water, deep-ocean aquaculture, district cooling, hydrogen production via electrolysis.
- ✅ **KTU numerical pattern:** Always convert °C to K first → compute Carnot efficiency → apply practical factor → find $\dot{Q}_{in}$ → use $\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T_w$ to find mass flow rates.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy from Biomass: Principles of Bio-Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy from Biomass: Principles of Bio-Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass (as defined in the KTU 2024 Non-Conventional Energy Systems syllabus):** Any organic matter derived from living or recently living organisms — primarily plant and animal residues — that can be converted into useful forms of energy (heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous fuels) through biochemical, thermochemical, physico-chemical, or mechanical conversion processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bio-Conversion Principle:** Bio-conversion refers to the controlled transformation of solid/liquid biomass into usable energy carriers using **biological agents** (micro-organisms, enzymes) and **controlled chemical engineering unit operations**. The two principal pillars of bio-conversion recognised by KTU are:
> 1. **Biochemical Conversion** — driven by living micro-organisms (anaerobic digestion, alcoholic fermentation, composting).
> 2. **Thermochemical Conversion** — driven by heat (combustion, gasification, pyrolysis, liquefaction).

**Standard Energy Metrics (KREES/KTU standard reference values):**

| Metric | Value (Indicative) |
|---|---|
| Calorific value of dry wood | **~ 18 – 21 MJ/kg** |
| Calorific value of methane (CH₄) | **55.5 MJ/kg** |
| Density of air at STP | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Stoichiometric O₂ for CH₄ combustion | **2 mol O₂ / mol CH₄** |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Biomass is a Battery from the Sun"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy for the student:**
> Think of biomass as a **"solar battery"**. A plant is essentially a natural photocell. During photosynthesis, it uses sunlight to fix atmospheric CO₂ and water into carbohydrate chains (cellulose, starch, sugars). When we burn that plant material (or let it ferment), we are simply **discharging the stored solar energy**. The energy was never lost — it was *deposited* during the plant's life and is now being *withdrawn* through conversion.

**Why biomass matters (KTU energy-economics context):**
- India produces ~ **500 million tonnes** of agricultural residue per year (MNRE estimate).
- Kerala's coconut, paddy, and coir sectors alone generate significant **agroresidue** — a feed-stock for decentralized biogas plants.
- Unlike fossil fuels, biomass is **carbon-neutral** over its lifecycle (CO₂ released on combustion ≈ CO₂ absorbed during growth).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Biomass (KTU Taxonomy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification (4-tier hierarchy):**

1. **Wood & Woody Biomass** — fire-wood, saw-dust, bark, forest litter.
2. **Agro-residues** — rice husk, rice straw, bagasse, groundnut shell, coconut coir pith, jute sticks.
3. **Aquatic Biomass** — water hyacinth, marine algae (seaweed), duckweed.
4. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — kitchen waste, paper, sewage sludge, animal dung.

Each tier favours a different conversion route (see Section 4 flowchart).

---

## 1.4 Conversion Routes — Bird's-Eye View

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Bio-Conversion (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Route | Driving Force | Main Product |
|---|---|---|
| **Biochemical** | Micro-organisms / enzymes | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), ethanol, compost |
| **Thermochemical – Combustion** | Excess O₂ + heat | Heat, steam, electricity |
| **Thermochemical – Gasification** | Sub-stoichiometric O₂ (T > 800 °C) | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + N₂) |
| **Thermochemical – Pyrolysis** | Absence of air (T = 400 – 800 °C) | Char, bio-oil, pyro-gas |
| **Physico-Chemical** | Trans-esterification, hydrolysis | Bio-diesel, bio-ethanol |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biochemical Conversion — The Anaerobic Digestion (AD) Cycle

Anaerobic digestion is the **heart of biomass bio-conversion** in the KTU syllabus. It is the controlled, oxygen-free decomposition of organic matter by a consortium of bacteria, producing **biogas** (≈ 55 – 70 % CH₄, 30 – 45 % CO₂, trace H₂S).

### 2.1.1 The Four Microbial Stages

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes (cellulase, protease, lipase).
   - *Limiting step* for high-cellulose feed-stocks such as rice straw.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers are converted by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (VFAs: acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols are oxidised to acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Strict anaerobes (Methanosarcina, Methanococcus) produce CH₄.
   - **Acetoclastic reaction** (≈ 70 % of CH₄): CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂
   - **Hydrogenotrophic reaction** (≈ 30 % of CH₄): CO₂ + 4 H₂ → CH₄ + 2 H₂O

### 2.1.2 Operating Parameters of an AD Plant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Book-work Bullets (commonly tested in 3-mark questions):**
> - **pH range:** 6.8 – 7.4 (methanogens die below pH 6.2).
> - **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30 – 40 °C) preferred for Indian conditions; thermophilic (50 – 60 °C) gives higher rate but lower stability.
> - **Retention time (HRT):** 25 – 40 days for cattle-dung digesters.
> - **C/N ratio of feed:** 25 : 1 to 30 : 1 is ideal. Too high → slow digestion; too low → ammonia inhibition.
> - **Solid content:** 7 – 9 % TS (Total Solids) for continuous stirred-tank reactors.

### 2.1.3 Biochemistry — Why Methane?

A simple energy balance justifies why methanogenesis is favoured:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta G^\circ = -31.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The negative Gibbs free energy confirms that methanogens can **scavenge energy** from this otherwise thermodynamically marginal step — they are the "clean-up crew" of the consortium.

---

## 2.2 Thermochemical Conversion Principles

### 2.2.1 Combustion
- Reaction: C + O₂ → CO₂ ; ΔH = **-393.5 kJ/mol**
- Requires **excess air** (typically 50 – 100 % stoichiometric excess).
- The only route that uses the **gross calorific value** directly.

### 2.2.2 Gasification
- Sub-stoichiometric air: **ER (Equivalence Ratio) = 0.20 – 0.40**.
- Endothermic & exothermic reactions coexist; the gasifier operates at **800 – 1200 °C**.
- Reactions:
  - C + ½ O₂ → CO ; ΔH = -111 kJ/mol
  - C + H₂O → CO + H₂ ; ΔH = +131 kJ/mol (water-gas reaction)
  - C + CO₂ → 2 CO ; ΔH = +172 kJ/mol (Boudouard reaction)

### 2.2.3 Pyrolysis
- No air; T = **400 – 800 °C**; heating rate determines product distribution.
- Slow pyrolysis → more **char** (carbonaceous solid fuel).
- Fast pyrolysis → more **bio-oil** (liquid).

### 2.2.4 Carbonisation
- A specialised low-temperature (≤ 700 °C) pyrolysis used to make **charcoal**.
- Often cited separately by KTU under the thermochemical umbrella.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-tested. Memorise the variables, units, and the conditions of validity.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \times GCV$ | Energy released on combustion | $Q$ in kJ, $m$ in kg |
| 2 | $GCV = HCV + 9h \times 2.326$ | Gross CV from Hydrogen fraction $h$ | $GCV$ in MJ/kg, $h$ in fraction |
| 3 | $ER = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio for gasifier | dimensionless; **0.20 – 0.40** |
| 4 | $\eta_{gasifier} = \dfrac{Q_{gas}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | Cold-gas efficiency of gasifier | % |
| 5 | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Cumulative methane yield (modified Gompertz approximation used in lab); $\tau$ = retention time | m³ CH₄ / kg VS |
| 6 | $CH_4\% = \dfrac{V_{CH_4}}{V_{biogas}} \times 100$ | Methane fraction in biogas | % (typical **55 – 70 %**) |
| 7 | $\text{C/N}_{opt} \approx \dfrac{30}{1}$ | Optimum C/N ratio for AD | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Volatile Solids (VS)} = \text{TS} - \text{Ash}$ | Biodegradable fraction of sludge | % |
| 9 | $H_{u,gas} = \sum x_i \, H_{u,i}$ | Heating value of producer gas from volumetric fractions $x_i$ | MJ/Nm³ |
| 10 | $C_{biogas} = \dfrac{m_{feed} \times VS \times B_{0}}{HRT}$ | Biogas production rate (B₀ = methane yield coefficient, m³/kg VS) | m³/day |
| 11 | $LHV = GCV - h_{fg} \times (9h + w)$ | Lower Heating Value (water as vapour) | MJ/kg |
| 12 | $\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = \sum a_i \, \dot{n}_i$ | Stoichiometric O₂ for fuel CₐHᵦOᵧ | mol/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation safeguard (LaTeX/Markdown):** Absolute values and "such that" are written using `\vert` (e.g. $\text{ER} < \vert 0.40 \vert$) — never the unescaped pipe symbol `|` — to keep the markdown table intact.

---

## 2.4 Why the KTU Syllabus Cares About This Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility of these formulas:**
> 1. **Design of KVIC / Deenabandhu model biogas plants** for Kerala households — uses Equation (5) to size the digester.
> 2. **Downdraft gasifier selection** for rice-husk-based power plants in Kuttanad — Equations (3) and (4) decide whether a fixed-bed or fluidised-bed unit is appropriate.
> 3. **Bio-diesel blending calculations** (trans-esterification of pongamia / waste cooking oil) use Equation (11) to confirm that the LHV of B20 blend meets the **IS 15607** standard.
