# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion of a Generic Biomass Fuel

> **Problem context (typical KTU 14-mark part-(a) sub-question):**
> A typical woody biomass has the ultimate analysis **C : H : O : N = 50 : 6 : 43 : 1** (mass %, dry basis). Derive the stoichiometric air requirement per kg of fuel and the dry flue-gas composition (assume complete combustion, N₂ from air at 79/21 ratio).

### Step 1 — Convert mass % to kg-atoms per kg of fuel
$$\text{kg-atom C} = \frac{0.50}{12} = 0.04167 \text{ kmol/kg fuel}$$
$$\text{kg-atom H} = \frac{0.06}{1.0} = 0.0600 \text{ kmol/kg fuel}$$
$$\text{kg-atom O} = \frac{0.43}{16} = 0.02688 \text{ kmol/kg fuel}$$

*(N does not contribute to combustion enthalpy; it is carried to flue gas as N₂.)*

### Step 2 — Oxygen required for complete combustion

For every kmol of C → CO₂, we need 1 kmol O₂. For every kmol of H₂, we need ½ kmol O₂.

$$O_{2,\text{req}} = 0.04167 \times 1 + 0.0600 \times 0.5$$
$$O_{2,\text{req}} = 0.04167 + 0.03000 = 0.07167 \text{ kmol O}_2 \text{ / kg fuel}$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the oxygen already in the fuel
$$O_{2,\text{net}} = 0.07167 - 0.02688 = 0.04479 \text{ kmol O}_2 \text{ / kg fuel}$$

### Step 4 — Convert net O₂ to stoichiometric air (1 kmol O₂ comes with 79/21 = 3.76 kmol N₂)
$$n_{air,stoich} = 0.04479 \times (1 + 3.76) = 0.04479 \times 4.76$$
$$n_{air,stoich} = 0.2133 \text{ kmol air / kg fuel}$$

In mass terms (molar mass of air ≈ 28.97 kg/kmol):
$$m_{air,stoich} = 0.2133 \times 28.97 = 6.18 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

This is the **theoretical air (TA)** used in KTU combustion problems.

### Step 5 — Dry flue-gas composition per kg fuel (theoretical air basis)

Products per kg fuel:
- CO₂ produced = kmol C = **0.04167 kmol**
- H₂O produced = kmol H / 2 = **0.0300 kmol** (condensed out in dry basis)
- N₂ from air = O₂,net × 3.76 = 0.04479 × 3.76 = **0.1684 kmol**
- O₂ in flue gas = 0 (theoretical air basis)

Total dry flue gas = 0.04167 + 0.1684 = **0.2101 kmol**

Composition (mol %):
$$y_{CO_2} = \frac{0.04167}{0.2101} \times 100 = 19.83\%$$
$$y_{N_2} = \frac{0.1684}{0.2101} \times 100 = 80.17\%$$

This derivation is **routinely asked in KTU University Exams as a 7-mark sub-part** of a 14-mark module-3 question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Biogas Yield from Cattle Dung (Mass-Balance)

> **Problem context (typical 7-mark sub-question):**
> A small dairy farm in Thrissur produces **200 kg/day of fresh cattle dung** with **Total Solids (TS) = 18 %** and **Volatile Solids (VS) = 80 % of TS**. Using the standard methane yield coefficient B₀ = **0.25 m³ CH₄ / kg VS fed** and a methane fraction in biogas of **60 %**, calculate the **daily biogas production** and the **electricity generation** if a gas engine has an electrical efficiency of 30 % and the calorific value of methane is 55.5 MJ/m³.

### Step 1 — Volatile solids fed per day
$$m_{VS} = 200 \text{ kg} \times 0.18 \times 0.80 = 28.8 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

### Step 2 — Methane volume produced per day
$$V_{CH_4} = 28.8 \times 0.25 = 7.2 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{day}$$

### Step 3 — Total biogas volume (60 % methane)
$$V_{biogas} = \frac{7.2}{0.60} = 12.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

### Step 4 — Energy content of the methane
$$Q_{th} = 7.2 \times 55.5 = 399.6 \text{ MJ/day}$$

### Step 5 — Electrical output (η = 0.30)
$$W_{el} = 399.6 \times 0.30 = 119.88 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):
$$W_{el} = \frac{119.88}{3.6} = 33.30 \text{ kWh/day}$$

This is enough to **power ~ 10 rural households** for lighting (KTU textbook benchmarking value).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bio-Conversion Route Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision matrix directly in 14-mark essay-type answers:**

| Feed-stock Property | Recommended Route | Typical Yield | By-product |
|---|---|---|---|
| High moisture (> 60 %), e.g. cattle dung, kitchen waste | Anaerobic digestion | 0.2 – 0.5 m³ biogas/kg VS | Slurry (bio-fertiliser) |
| High cellulose, dry, e.g. rice straw | Slow pyrolysis | 30 – 35 % bio-oil + 25 % char | Char, gas |
| Granular, low ash, e.g. wood chips, coconut shells | Downdraft gasification | 70 – 80 % cold-gas efficiency | Tar, ash |
| Sugar / starch-rich, e.g. molasses, cassava | Fermentation (bio-ethanol) | 0.45 – 0.50 L ethanol/kg sugar | Stillage |
| Oil-rich seeds, e.g. pongamia, jatropha | Trans-esterification (bio-diesel) | 0.95 L bio-diesel/kg oil | Glycerol |
| Municipal solid waste | Combustion / RDF gasification | Energy from waste | Inert ash |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Cold-Gas Efficiency of a Downdraft Gasifier

> **Working formula (commonly required derivation in KTU 14-mark Module-3 questions):**

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{V_{gas} \times LHV_{gas}}{m_{fuel} \times GCV_{fuel}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{gas}$ = Nm³ of producer gas per kg of dry fuel
- $LHV_{gas}$ = Lower Heating Value of producer gas ≈ **4.5 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³**
- $GCV_{fuel}$ = Calorific value of the dry biomass, e.g. **17 MJ/kg** for wood chips.

For a well-designed downdraft gasifier running on coconut shell:
$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{2.5 \times 5.2}{1.0 \times 17.0} \times 100 = 76.5\%$$

This number matches the **MNRE benchmark of 70 – 80 %** for Indian downdraft gasifiers and is often cited as a design target in KTU viva-voce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams (KTU tip):** When a question asks to "draw a labelled schematic of a biogas plant," the **three sub-systems** examiners expect are:
> 1. **Digester** (where AD happens)
> 2. **Gas-holder** (stores biogas at constant pressure)
> 3. **Displacement / outlet chamber** (collects effluent and balances hydraulic pressure)
> Missing any one of these three in a labelled diagram is the most common reason for **2-mark deduction** in KTU 14-marker answers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 OEC — End Semester Exam):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - **Part B:** 2 questions × 14 marks (with internal choice) = 28 marks
> - Total for module-level question: **34 marks** across Part A + Part B
> - Cognitive levels tested: **L1 (Remember) → L4 (Analyse)** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> Define biomass. List **four major categories** of biomass with **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU 2024 valuation key):**
Biomass is any organic matter of plant or animal origin that can be converted into useful forms of energy. The four major categories recognised by the KTU syllabus are:
1. **Wood and woody biomass** — e.g., fire-wood, saw-dust.
2. **Agro-residues** — e.g., rice straw, bagasse.
3. **Aquatic biomass** — e.g., water hyacinth.
4. **Municipal solid waste** — e.g., kitchen waste, sewage sludge. *(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the four categories with examples = 3 marks)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> Write **two differences** between anaerobic digestion and gasification of biomass.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Parameter | Anaerobic Digestion | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Microbial action (no air) | Heat + sub-stoichiometric air |
| Operating temperature | 30 – 60 °C | 800 – 1200 °C |
| Main product | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate | Producer gas (CO + H₂) + tar + ash |
| Feedstock preference | Wet, high-moisture | Dry, granular |

*(1.5 marks per correct pair, full 3 marks for any two crisp differences.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled schematic**, describe the construction and working of a **KVIC-type continuous biogas plant**. List **two advantages** of the floating gas-holder design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cattle farm generates **300 kg/day of dung** with TS = 20 %, VS = 80 % of TS. The methane yield coefficient is **0.30 m³ CH₄ / kg VS** and the methane fraction in biogas is 60 %. Calculate the **daily biogas production** and the **electrical energy that can be generated** by a gas-engine set running at 28 % electrical efficiency (LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/m³). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) — 7 marks

> **Construction (3 marks):**
> 1. **Digester** — cylindrical underground masonry/brick tank of typical volume 4 – 6 m³.
> 2. **Inlet pipe** — sloped feeding chute, mixes dung with water (1 : 1 ratio by weight).
> 3. **Outlet / displacement chamber** — collects the spent slurry by hydraulic displacement.
> 4. **Floating gas-holder** — inverted mild-steel drum resting on water seal; rises/falls with gas volume.
> 5. **Gas outlet** — with water-cup manometer and H₂S scrubber.

> **Working (2 marks):**
> - Slurry is fed once or twice daily; the digester maintains a temperature of 30 – 40 °C (mesophilic range).
> - Methanogens convert the volatile solids to CH₄ + CO₂ over a hydraulic retention time (HRT) of 30 – 40 days.
> - Biogas collects under the floating dome at a pressure of ~ 5 – 10 cm of water column.

> **Advantages of floating gas-holder (2 marks):**
> 1. **Constant gas pressure** irrespective of volume of gas stored.
> 2. **Visual indication** of gas quantity by the height of the dome.
> 3. *Bonus point:* Acts as a safety pressure-relief — dome lifts if gas pressure exceeds design limit.

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Volatile solids fed per day**

$$m_{VS} = 300 \text{ kg/day} \times 0.20 \times 0.80 = 48.0 \text{ kg VS/day}$$

**Step 2 — Methane volume produced**

$$V_{CH_4} = 48.0 \times 0.30 = 14.4 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

**Step 3 — Biogas volume (60 % CH₄)**

$$V_{biogas} = \frac{14.4}{0.60} = 24.0 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal energy from methane**

$$Q_{th} = 14.4 \times 50 = 720 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 5 — Electrical energy (η = 0.28)**

$$W_{el} = 720 \times 0.28 = 201.6 \text{ MJ/day}$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$W_{el} = \frac{201.6}{3.6} = 56.0 \text{ kWh/day}$$

> **Final Answer:** Daily biogas production = **24.0 m³/day**, daily electrical energy = **56.0 kWh/day**. *(1.5 marks for step-1, 1 mark for step-2, 1.5 marks for step-3, 1 mark for step-4, 2 marks for step-5 & final answer.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** What is **gasification**? Explain the **principle and chemistry of a downdraft gasifier** with relevant reactions. Discuss why the **equivalence ratio (ER) is maintained between 0.20 and 0.40**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier is fed with **100 kg/h of wood chips** of GCV = 17 MJ/kg. It produces **240 Nm³/h of producer gas** having LHV = 5.0 MJ/Nm³. Calculate the **cold-gas efficiency** and the **thermal output of the gas**. If this hot gas drives a 100 kW IC engine at 32 % electrical efficiency, is the system **self-sustainable** in energy terms? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) — 7 marks

> **Definition (1 mark):** Gasification is the thermochemical conversion of solid biomass into a combustible gaseous fuel (producer gas) using a **sub-stoichiometric amount of air/oxygen** at elevated temperatures (800 – 1200 °C).

> **Principle (2 marks):** The biomass is partially oxidised — the exothermic C + O₂ → CO₂ supplies the heat needed to drive the endothermic C + H₂O → CO + H₂ and C + CO₂ → 2 CO reactions, sustaining the gasification zone autothermally.

> **Reactions (2 marks):**
> 1. Partial oxidation: $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -111 \text{ kJ/mol}$
> 2. Water-gas: $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131 \text{ kJ/mol}$
> 3. Boudouard: $\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172 \text{ kJ/mol}$

> **Why ER = 0.20 – 0.40 (2 marks):**
> - **ER < 0.20** → too little air → insufficient heat → temperature drops below 800 °C → tar formation, incomplete carbon conversion.
> - **ER > 0.40** → too much air → the gas is diluted with N₂ and CO₂ → LHV of producer gas falls below 4 MJ/Nm³, making it unsuitable as a fuel gas.
> - The narrow window **0.20 – 0.40** is the sweet-spot for **autothermal operation** with a producer-gas LHV of 4.5 – 6.0 MJ/Nm³.

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Energy input from fuel**

$$Q_{in} = 100 \text{ kg/h} \times 17 \text{ MJ/kg} = 1700 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Energy in producer gas**

$$Q_{gas} = 240 \text{ Nm}^3\text{/h} \times 5.0 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 1200 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Cold-gas efficiency**

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{1200}{1700} \times 100 = 70.59\%$$

**Step 4 — Electrical output**

$$W_{el} = 1200 \times 0.32 = 384 \text{ MJ/h} = \frac{384}{3.6} = 106.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Sustainability check**

Since the engine produces **106.67 kW** while rated at **100 kW**, the system has a **net surplus of ≈ 6.67 kW** to overcome auxiliary loads (pumps, fans, controls). Therefore, the system is **marginally self-sustainable** — the IC engine can comfortably deliver its rated 100 kW with ≈ 6 % surplus to feed parasitic loads. *(1 mark for each step + 2 marks for the conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Stoichiometry confusion:** Students often forget to subtract the **inherent oxygen** present in the biomass (here, the 43 % O from ultimate analysis). Forgetting this step leads to a **2-mark deduction** in 7-mark problems.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Using Nm³/h and MJ/Nm³ correctly is critical. A common mistake is to use SCF (standard cubic feet) without conversion — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Methane vs. biogas volume:** When the problem states "60 % methane", the **total biogas = V_CH₄ / 0.60**, not V_CH₄ × 0.60. Reversing this will halve your final energy output.
> 4. **ER vs. Excess-Air confusion:** ER = 0.20 – 0.40 corresponds to **150 – 400 % excess air** for gasification. Do not write "excess air = 30 %" — that is a combustion value, not a gasification value.
> 5. **Labelled diagram:** In the KVIC biogas plant question, omitting the **displacement chamber** alone can cost **2 marks**, because KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly checks for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — cover each bullet before walking into the exam hall.**

- [x] **Biomass definition** — organic matter of plant/animal origin, **carbon-neutral**, stored solar energy.
- [x] **Four classes of biomass** — woody, agro, aquatic, MSW.
- [x] **Four conversion routes** — biochemical, combustion, gasification, pyrolysis (and physico-chemical for oils/sugars).
- [x] **Anaerobic digestion stages** — hydrolysis → acidogenesis → acetogenesis → methanogenesis; **HRT 30 – 40 d**; **C/N 25–30:1**; **pH 6.8 – 7.4**; **TS 7 – 9 %**; **methanogens die < pH 6.2**.
- [x] **Acetoclastic reaction** (70 % of CH₄): $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$
- [x] **Hydrogenotrophic reaction** (30 % of CH₄): $\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- [x] **Combustion** — excess air, ΔH = -393.5 kJ/mol for C → CO₂.
- [x] **Gasification** — ER = **0.20 – 0.40**, T = 800 – 1200 °C, three key reactions (partial-oxidation, water-gas, Boudouard).
- [x] **Pyrolysis** — no air, T = 400 – 800 °C; fast → bio-oil; slow → char.
- [x] **GCV vs. LHV** — $GCV = HCV + 9h \times 2.326$; $LHV = GCV - h_{fg} \times (9h + w)$.
- [x] **Cold-gas efficiency** = $\frac{V_{gas} \times LHV_{gas}}{m_{fuel} \times GCV_{fuel}} \times 100$ (target 70 – 80 %).
- [x] **Biogas yield formula** — $V_{CH_4} = m_{feed} \times TS \times VS\% \times B_0$.
- [x] **KVIC biogas plant** has three mandatory sub-systems in the labelled diagram — digester, gas-holder, displacement chamber.
- [x] **Kerala context** — coconut coir, rice husk, paddy straw, and cattle dung are abundant feed-stocks; KVIC plants in Wayanad and Thrissur districts are reference installations.
- [x] **MNRE benchmark** — downdraft gasifier cold-gas efficiency **70 – 80 %**; Indian biogas plant target 0.5 m³/kg dung/day under mesophilic operation.
- [x] **Common valuation killers** — forgetting to subtract inherent O₂ from biomass, mixing up ER with excess air, missing the displacement chamber in the labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anaerobic/Aerobic Digestion, Pyrolysis, and Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anaerobic/Aerobic Digestion, Pyrolysis, and Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 | Anaerobic/Aerobic Digestion, Pyrolysis, and Gasification

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module covers the **Thermochemical and Biochemical Conversion pathways** of biomass into usable energy carriers. These four processes (Anaerobic Digestion, Aerobic Digestion, Pyrolysis, and Gasification) form the **backbone of biomass-to-energy engineering** and are compulsory for understanding decentralized renewable energy systems.

### 1.1.1 Anaerobic Digestion (AD)

**Formal Definition:** *Anaerobic Digestion is a biochemical process in which organic matter is decomposed by a consortium of microorganisms in the strict absence of oxygen, yielding a gaseous mixture of predominantly **methane (CH₄)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — collectively termed **biogas** — along with a nutrient-rich slurry called **digestate**.*

- **Operating Principle:** Sequential microbial breakdown through **four stages** — Hydrolysis, Acidogenesis, Acetogenesis, and Methanogenesis.
- **Primary Product:** Biogas with **~50–70% CH₄**, **~30–40% CO₂**, traces of H₂S, NH₃, and water vapor.
- **Calorific Value of Biogas:** **~18–25 MJ/m³** (depending on methane fraction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Point of View:** The four sequential stages (H-A-A-M) are a **favourite 7-mark question topic**. Memorize the bacteria involved at each stage — this is a guaranteed sub-part (a) ask in ESE.

### 1.1.2 Aerobic Digestion

**Formal Definition:** *Aerobic Digestion is a biochemical oxidation process in which aerobic microorganisms (in the presence of dissolved oxygen) mineralize biodegradable organic matter into stable end products such as **CO₂, H₂O, mineral salts**, and a reduced mass of microbial cells (biological sludge).*

- **Operating Principle:** Continuous supply of oxygen via mechanical aeration.
- **Primary Product:** Mineralized sludge, CO₂, H₂O, and heat.
- **Net Energy Output:** **Negative** (energy consuming) — used mainly for **wastewater/sludge treatment**, not energy generation.

### 1.1.3 Pyrolysis

**Formal Definition:** *Pyrolysis is the thermochemical decomposition of organic (carbonaceous) feedstocks at elevated temperatures (**300–900 °C**) in the complete absence of oxidizing agents (air/oxygen), producing three product fractions — **bio-char, bio-oil (pyrolytic liquid), and non-condensable pyrolysis gas**.*

- **Operating Principle:** Thermal cracking of long-chain hydrocarbons into smaller molecules.
- **Product Distribution:** Strongly dependent on **heating rate, residence time, and temperature**.

### 1.1.4 Gasification

**Formal Definition:** *Gasification is a thermochemical conversion process in which a carbonaceous solid fuel is partially oxidized at high temperatures (**700–1500 °C**) using a sub-stoichiometric quantity of oxidant (air, pure O₂, steam, or CO₂), producing a combustible gas mixture called **synthesis gas (syngas)** — primarily composed of **CO, H₂, CO₂, CH₄, and N₂**.*

- **Operating Principle:** Sub-stoichiometric combustion → starved O₂ prevents complete combustion → CO and H₂ dominate.
- **Primary Product:** Producer gas / Syngas with **calorific value 4–18 MJ/m³** (depending on gasifying agent).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogies & Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Hack for KTU Exams:** All four processes do the **same job** — *"convert dirty organic matter into clean energy"*. Their **difference lies in the agent that breaks the molecules**.

### Real-World Analogy: The "Wood-Fire Dinner" Metaphor

| Process | Dinner Analogy | What's Different |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerobic Digestion** | A **bonfire** in a windy field — oxygen plentiful, complete burning, ash + heat + light. | Microbes act as the wind; **everything burns to CO₂ + H₂O**. |
| **Anaerobic Digestion** | A **closed airtight pressure cooker** with decomposing food — the bacteria are "burped" with methane instead of breathing fire. | No oxygen → microbes extract energy by making **methane**. |
| **Pyrolysis** | Heating food in a **sealed clay oven (chulha)** — it chars, gives off vapors, and turns black. | No oxygen → solid + liquid + gas **all three products**. |
| **Gasification** | The **glowing red coal bed** in a barbeque — air is starved, so instead of full flames, you get a sizzling blue flame of CO and H₂. | Limited oxygen → mostly **gaseous fuel**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "A²PG":** **A**naerobic, **A**erobic, **P**yrolysis, **G**asification. In increasing order of operating temperature: **30 °C < 35 °C < 600 °C < 1000 °C**.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Anaerobic Digestion | Aerobic Digestion | Pyrolysis | Gasification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 30–55 °C | 15–35 °C | 300–900 °C | 700–1500 °C |
| **Oxygen** | Absent | Present (excess) | Absent | Sub-stoichiometric |
| **Nature** | Biochemical | Biochemical | Thermochemical | Thermochemical |
| **Main Product** | Biogas (CH₄) | CO₂, H₂O, sludge | Char, bio-oil, gas | Syngas (CO+H₂) |
| **Calorific Value** | 18–25 MJ/m³ | — | 17–22 MJ/kg (bio-oil) | 4–18 MJ/m³ |
| **Energy Yield** | Positive | Negative | Positive | Positive |
| **End Use** | Cooking, electricity | Sludge stabilization | Liquid fuel, soil amendment | Power, Fischer-Tropsch |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anaerobic Digestion — The Four-Stage Biochemistry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Topic:** The H-A-A-M cascade. **Always draw a 4-block block diagram** to score full marks.

The biochemical conversion of complex organics (e.g., carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) into methane proceeds through four sequential, interdependent microbial steps:

### Stage 1: Hydrolysis
- **Inputs:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids).
- **Action:** Extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases) secreted by fermentative bacteria break down polymers into **monomers** (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
- **Bacteria:** *Clostridium, Bacillus, Staphylococcus*.
- **Bottleneck:** This is the **rate-limiting step** for particulate feedstocks like sewage sludge.

### Stage 2: Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- **Inputs:** Monomers from Stage 1.
- **Action:** Acidogenic bacteria convert monomers into **volatile fatty acids (VFAs)**, alcohols, H₂, CO₂, NH₃.
- **Bacteria:** *Bacteroides, Lactobacillus, Clostridium*.
- **Output:** Acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid.

### Stage 3: Acetogenesis
- **Inputs:** VFAs and alcohols from Stage 2.
- **Action:** Acetogenic bacteria oxidize these intermediates into **acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂**.
- **Critical Note:** This stage is **thermodynamically unfavorable** unless H₂ partial pressure is kept low (methanogens consume H₂).

### Stage 4: Methanogenesis
- **Inputs:** Acetate, H₂, CO₂.
- **Action:** Methanogenic archaea produce **CH₄**.
- **Two Pathways:**
  - **Acetoclastic:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (~70% of CH₄)
  - **Hydrogenotrophic:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (~30% of CH₄)
- **Bacteria:** *Methanosarcina, Methanococcus, Methanobacterium*.

---

## 2.2 Aerobic Digestion — The Oxidation Pathway

$$ Organic\,Matter + O_2 \xrightarrow{Aerobic\,Microbes} CO_2 + H_2O + NH_3 + Biomass + Heat $$

- **Process Type:** **Endogenous respiration** — microbes consume their own cellular protoplasm in absence of external substrate.
- **Operating Modes:**
  - **Conventional Aerobic Digestion:** Retention time 20–30 days at ambient temperature.
  - **Autoheated Thermophilic Aerobic Digestion (ATAD):** Retention time 5–10 days at **45–65 °C** (self-heated by exothermic reactions).
- **Process Control Parameters:**
  - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** **1–2 mg/L** minimum.
  - **SRT (Solids Retention Time):** **20–40 days**.
  - **pH:** 6.5–8.0.
  - **VS (Volatile Solids) Reduction:** **35–50%**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Balance Reality:** Aerobic digestion is **net energy consuming** because the **theoretical heat of combustion of biomass (~22 MJ/kg)** is dissipated as low-grade heat, and the aeration system consumes **~0.5–1.0 kWh/m³** of air.

---

## 2.3 Pyrolysis — Thermochemical Decomposition Mechanics

The three competing pathways during pyrolysis:

### Pathway 1: Carbonization (Slow Pyrolysis)
- **Conditions:** Low heating rate (**0.1–1 °C/s**), long residence time, low temperature (**300–500 °C**).
- **Dominant Product:** **Bio-char (35–45%)**.
- **Historical Use:** Charcoal making.

### Pathway 2: Conventional (Intermediate) Pyrolysis
- **Conditions:** Moderate heating rate (**1–10 °C/s**), moderate temperature.
- **Product Mix:** Roughly equal char, bio-oil, gas.

### Pathway 3: Fast/Flash Pyrolysis
- **Conditions:** High heating rate (**>100 °C/s**), very short vapor residence time (**<2 s**), temperature **450–550 °C**.
- **Dominant Product:** **Bio-oil (60–75% wt)**.
- **Use:** Liquid biofuels for transport.

### General Stoichiometry of Pyrolysis

$$ Biomass \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, No\,O_2} Char + Bio\text{-}oil + Gas\,(CO,\, CO_2,\, CH_4,\, H_2) $$

For cellulose (a proxy for biomass):

$$ C_6H_{10}O_5 \xrightarrow{\Delta} xC\,(char) + yH_2O + zCO + wCO_2 + vCH_4 + uH_2 $$

---

## 2.4 Gasification — Reaction Engineering

The gasification reactor hosts **four coupled sub-reactions**:

| Reaction | Name | Enthalpy (ΔH @ 25°C) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | Combustion (Oxidation) | **−393.5 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |
| $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ | Water-Gas (Steam) | **+131.3 kJ/mol** | Endothermic |
| $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ | Boudouard | **+172.4 kJ/mol** | Endothermic |
| $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$ | Water-Gas Shift (WGS) | **−41.2 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |
| $C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4$ | Methanation | **−74.8 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Solve:** Air gasification always yields low CV gas (4–7 MJ/m³) because N₂ dilutes the product. **Oxygen or steam gasification** gives high CV gas (10–18 MJ/m³).

### Air Equivalence Ratio (ER)
$$ ER = \frac{Actual\,Air\,Supplied}{Stoichiometric\,Air\,Required} $$

- **ER < 0.25:** Pure pyrolysis
- **0.25 < ER < 0.40:** **Gasification zone (typical operation)**
- **ER ≈ 1.0:** Complete combustion
- **ER > 1.0:** Excess air (flame zone)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CH_4\,Yield = 0.25 \cdot VS_{added}$ | Empirical methane yield (Buswell equation) | m³ CH₄ / kg VS |
| 2 | $C/N\,Ratio = \frac{\%Carbon}{\%Nitrogen}$ | Optimum: **20–30 : 1** for AD | dimensionless |
| 3 | $HRT = \frac{V_{reactor}}{Q_{feed}}$ | Hydraulic Retention Time | days |
| 4 | $SRT = \frac{M_{biomass}}{Q_{waste} \cdot X_{waste}}$ | Solids Retention Time | days |
| 5 | $OLR = \frac{Q_{feed} \cdot S_{in}}{V_{reactor}}$ | Organic Loading Rate | kg COD / m³·day |
| 6 | $ER = \frac{Actual\,O_2}{Stoichiometric\,O_2}$ | Equivalence Ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CGE = \frac{CV_{gas} \cdot m_{gas}}{CV_{feed} \cdot m_{feed}} \times 100$ | Cold Gas Efficiency | % |
| 8 | $Q_{comb} = m \cdot CV$ | Heat released from combustion | kJ |
| 9 | $CV_{biogas} = 0.358 \cdot \%CH_4$ | Calorific value of biogas | MJ/m³ |
| 10 | $CV_{syngas} = (CV_{CO} \cdot y_{CO}) + (CV_{H_2} \cdot y_{H_2}) + (CV_{CH_4} \cdot y_{CH_4})$ | Calorific value of syngas | MJ/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** When using $CV_{biogas} = 0.358 \cdot \%CH_4$, ensure %CH₄ is expressed as **volume % (e.g., 60, not 0.60)**. Result will be in **MJ/m³**. Forgetting this costs **2 marks** easily.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Anaerobic Digestion:** Widely deployed in **cattle dung-based rural biogas plants** (e.g., the **Kerala Suchitwa Mission** promoted ~1,00,000+ household plants), **municipal solid waste (MSW) management** in cities like Bengaluru, and **industrial effluent treatment** for breweries, dairies, and distilleries.
- **Aerobic Digestion:** Integral to **Kerala Water Authority (KWA) sewage treatment plants** at Thiruvananthapuram and Kochi.
- **Pyrolysis:** Powers **slow-pyrolysis biochar units** in coffee plantations (Wayanad, Chikmagalur) and emerging **fast-pyrolysis bio-refineries**.
- **Gasification:** Foundation of **biomass-based decentralized power generation** (e.g., 1–10 MWe rice husk gasifier plants in Punjab and Tamil Nadu).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** Numerical problems on **biogas stoichiometry, calorific value calculations, and gasifier energy balances** appear almost every year. The three derivations below cover the highest-yield patterns.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Biogas Yield from Glucose (Buswell's Equation)

**Problem Statement:** A typical KTU 2019/2022/2024 style question — *"Calculate the theoretical yield of methane and carbon dioxide when 1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is completely digested anaerobically. Assume 100% conversion."*

### Step 1: Write the General Anaerobic Digestion Stoichiometry

For any carbohydrate $C_n H_a O_b$, the generalized reaction is:

$$ C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 $$

> **Origin:** This is the **Buswell–Mueller equation** (1932). It's derived by setting up two mass balances (C and H) and a redox balance (O). It applies to **all carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids** when organic nitrogen is converted to NH₃.

### Step 2: Apply the General Formula to Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)

Identify coefficients: $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$.

**Moles of H₂O consumed:**

$$ n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0 $$

**Interpretation:** Glucose digestion is **net zero in water balance** — neither produced nor consumed, a unique feature of the hexose stoichiometry.

**Moles of CH₄ produced:**

$$ \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 $$

**Moles of CO₂ produced:**

$$ \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3 $$

### Step 3: Write the Final Stoichiometric Equation

$$ C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2 $$

### Step 4: Calculate Masses

- **Molar mass of glucose:** $6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180$ g/mol.
- **Molar mass of CH₄:** $12 + 4(1) = 16$ g/mol.
- **Molar mass of CO₂:** $12 + 2(16) = 44$ g/mol.

**Methane produced per kg of glucose:**

$$ m_{CH_4} = 1000\,g \times \frac{3 \times 16}{180} = 266.67\,g $$

**Carbon dioxide produced per kg of glucose:**

$$ m_{CO_2} = 1000\,g \times \frac{3 \times 44}{180} = 733.33\,g $$

### Step 5: Volumetric Yield (at STP: 22.4 L/mol)

- **Moles of CH₄ per kg glucose:** $\frac{1000}{180} \times 3 = 16.67$ mol.
- **Volume of CH₄ at STP:** $16.67 \times 22.4 = 373.33$ L = **0.373 m³**.
- **Total biogas volume:** $0.373 \times 2 = 0.747$ m³/kg glucose.

### Step 6: Calorific Value Contribution

- **CV of CH₄:** $55.5$ MJ/kg.
- **Energy from methane:** $0.2667 \times 55.5 = 14.8$ MJ/kg glucose.
- **% CH₄ in biogas:** $\frac{3}{3+3} \times 100 = 50$%.
- **CV of biogas:** $0.358 \times 50 = 17.9$ MJ/m³ ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cold Gas Efficiency of a Downdraft Biomass Gasifier

**Problem Statement:** *"A biomass gasifier is fed with **500 kg/h of rice husk** (CV = 14 MJ/kg, moisture = 8% wt). The gasifier produces **850 m³/h of producer gas** (at STP) with a calorific value of **5.2 MJ/m³**. Calculate the (a) Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE), (b) Air-Fuel Ratio, and (c) Thermal Output."*

### Step 1: Cold Gas Efficiency

$$ CGE = \frac{CV_{gas} \cdot V_{gas}}{CV_{feed} \cdot m_{feed}} \times 100 $$

Substituting:

$$ CGE = \frac{5.2 \times 850}{14 \times 500} \times 100 $$

$$ CGE = \frac{4420}{7000} \times 100 = 63.14\,\% $$

**Interpretation:** A CGE of 60–70% is **typical for commercial downdraft gasifiers** (acceptable range 55–80%).

### Step 2: Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric vs Actual)

For a fuel of elemental composition $C_a H_b O_c N_d S_e$ (by mass), the **stoichiometric oxygen (O₂) requirement** is:

$$ O_{2,\,stoich} = \frac{32}{12}a + \frac{32}{4}b + 32e - c $$

For rice husk (approx.): $a = 0.39$, $b = 0.052$, $c = 0.385$, $d = 0.005$ (mass fractions).

$$ O_{2,\,stoich} = \frac{32}{12}(0.39) + \frac{32}{4}(0.052) + 32(0) - 0.385 $$

$$ O_{2,\,stoich} = 1.040 + 0.416 - 0.385 = 1.071\,kg\,O_2/kg\,fuel $$

$$ Air_{stoich} = \frac{1.071}{0.232} = 4.62\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel $$

**Actual air supplied in gasification** is typically **0.2–0.4** of stoichiometric:

$$ ER = 0.30\,(typical) $$

$$ Air_{actual} = 0.30 \times 4.62 = 1.386\,kg\,air/kg\,fuel $$

$$ Air\text{-}Fuel\,Ratio = 1.386 : 1 $$

### Step 3: Thermal Output (Q)

$$ Q_{thermal} = CV_{gas} \times V_{gas} = 5.2 \times 850 = 4420\,MJ/h $$

$$ Q_{thermal} = 4420\,MJ/h = \frac{4420}{3.6} = 1227.78\,kW $$

$$ Q_{thermal} = 1.228\,MW $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heating Value of Syngas from Gas Composition

**Problem Statement:** *"The syngas from an air-blown gasifier has the following dry volume composition: CO = 22%, H₂ = 18%, CH₄ = 4%, CO₂ = 10%, N₂ = 46%. Calculate the (a) Calorific Value of syngas and (b) Air-Fuel Ratio equivalence."*

### Step 1: Calorific Value of Syngas

CV of combustible components:
- **CO:** 12.64 MJ/m³
- **H₂:** 12.79 MJ/m³
- **CH₄:** 37.71 MJ/m³

$$ CV_{syngas} = \sum y_i \cdot CV_i $$

$$ CV_{syngas} = (0.22)(12.64) + (0.18)(12.79) + (0.04)(37.71) $$

$$ CV_{syngas} = 2.781 + 2.302 + 1.508 = 6.591\,MJ/m^3 $$

**Total (including inerts):** 6.591 MJ/m³ on same basis (CV formula is for combustible fraction only; on the **total** syngas basis: $6.591/1.0 = 6.591$ MJ/m³ since CO₂, N₂ contribute zero).

### Step 2: Cross-Verification with Heat of Combustion Equation

$$ CV_{syngas} = y_{CO} \cdot \Delta H_{CO} + y_{H_2} \cdot \Delta H_{H_2} + y_{CH_4} \cdot \Delta H_{CH_4} $$

(where $\Delta H$ values are per cubic meter at STP), giving **6.59 MJ/m³** ✓.

### Step 3: Stoichiometric Air Analysis

Combustion reactions in syngas:

$$ CO + 0.5O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 $$
$$ H_2 + 0.5O_2 \rightarrow H_2O $$
$$ CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O $$

**O₂ required (per m³ of syngas):**

$$ O_2 = 0.5(0.22) + 0.5(0.18) + 2(0.04) = 0.11 + 0.09 + 0.08 = 0.28\,m^3\,O_2/m^3\,syngas $$

**Air required:** $0.28/0.21 = 1.333$ m³ air/m³ syngas.

**Air-Fuel Mass Ratio (approximate):** 
- Density of air = 1.293 kg/m³.
- Air mass per m³ syngas: $1.333 \times 1.293 = 1.724$ kg.

**Final Air-Fuel Ratio:** $1.724 : 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Conclusion:** All three derivations represent **standard KTU 14-mark questions** that combine (a) a 7-mark theoretical/derivation sub-part and (b) a 7-mark numerical sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Block diagrams in answers are worth **1–2 bonus marks** (under "presentation skills") in KTU valuation. Mermaid-rendered diagrams are presented below; in handwritten exams, replicate them as labeled rectangles connected by arrows.

---

## 4.1 Anaerobic Digestion — H-A-A-M Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Complex Organic Waste] --> A2[Stage 1 Hydrolysis]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 2 Acidogenesis]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 3 Acetogenesis]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 4 Methanogenesis]
    A5 --> A6[Biogas CH4 plus CO2]
    A5 --> A7[Digestate Slurry]
    A2 -. VFAs .-> A4
    A3 -. H2 and CO2 .-> A4
```

> **Reading the Flow:** Solid arrows = primary flow; dotted arrows = intermediate byproducts recycled to next stage.

---

## 4.2 Pyrolysis Product Distribution — Function of Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Biomass Feedstock] --> P2{Pyrolysis Reactor}
    P2 --> P3[Bio-Char Solid]
    P2 --> P4[Pyrolytic Vapors]
    P4 --> P5[Quench Condenser]
    P5 --> P6[Bio-Oil Liquid]
    P5 --> P7[Non-Condensable Gas]
    P2 -.->|Cool Slow Pyrolysis 300-500 C| P8[More Char Output]
    P2 -.->|Hot Fast Pyrolysis 450-550 C| P9[More Bio-Oil Output]
    P2 -.->|High Temp 700-900 C| P10[More Gas Output]
```

---

## 4.3 Gasifier — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Biomass Feedstock] --> G2[Hopper and Feeder]
    G2 --> G3[Gasifier Reactor Core]
    G4[Air or O2 or Steam Inlet] --> G3
    G3 --> G5[Drying Zone]
    G5 --> G6[Pyrolysis Zone]
    G6 --> G7[Reduction Zone]
    G7 --> G8[Combustion Zone]
    G8 --> G9[Ash Discharge]
    G7 --> G10[Syngas Outlet]
    G10 --> G11[Gas Cleaning Cyclone]
    G11 --> G12[Cooling and Filtration]
    G12 --> G13[Engine or Turbine User]
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix — Aerobic vs Anaerobic Digestion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AD[Anaerobic Digestion]
        AD1[No Oxygen]
        AD2[Produces Biogas]
        AD3[Net Energy Positive]
        AD4[Methanogenic Archaea]
    end
    subgraph AE[Aerobic Digestion]
        AE1[Oxygen Required]
        AE2[Produces CO2 and H2O]
        AE3[Net Energy Negative]
        AE4[Aerobic Bacteria]
    end
    AD -.Favored for.-> F1[Energy Recovery]
    AE -.Favored for.-> F2[Sludge Stabilization]
```

---

## 4.5 Temperature vs Product Yield Pyramid (Pyrolysis)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PY1[Temperature Range] --> PY2[300-500 C Slow]
    PY1 --> PY3[500-700 C Intermediate]
    PY1 --> PY4[700-900 C Fast]
    PY2 --> PY5[Bio-Char Dominant 40 percent]
    PY3 --> PY6[Bio-Oil Balanced 50 percent]
    PY4 --> PY7[Pyrolysis Gas Dominant 70 percent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and is typically direct-concept recall (1 mark) + brief explanation (2 marks).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*"Differentiate between Aerobic and Anaerobic Digestion of biomass with respect to end products and energy recovery."*

### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Parameter | Anaerobic Digestion | Aerobic Digestion |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Requirement** | Strictly absent | Continuously supplied |
| **End Products** | **Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** + digestate | **CO₂ + H₂O** + mineralized sludge |
| **Energy Recovery** | **Positive** (biogas is a fuel) | **Negative** (energy consumed in aeration) |
| **Microorganisms** | Methanogenic archaea | Aerobic heterotrophs |
| **Net Reaction** | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{Energy}$ |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*"Define the Equivalence Ratio (ER) in a gasifier. What is its typical range during gasification?"*

### Model Answer (3 marks)

- **Definition (2 marks):** The Equivalence Ratio is the **ratio of the actual oxidant (air/oxygen) supplied to the stoichiometric oxidant required for complete combustion** of the fuel.

$$ ER = \frac{(O/A)_{actual}}{(O/A)_{stoichiometric}} $$

- **Typical Range (1 mark):** **0.20 to 0.40** (sub-stoichiometric); if **ER ≥ 1.0**, the process becomes **combustion** rather than gasification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question has **internal choice** — students attempt **ONE of the two alternatives**. Each carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four sequential stages** of Anaerobic Digestion. Name the microorganisms involved in each stage and identify the rate-limiting step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A household biogas digester of **8 m³ capacity** is fed daily with **40 kg of cattle dung** (VS = 80%, methane yield coefficient = 0.25 m³ CH₄ per kg VS added). The biogas produced has **55% methane** content. Calculate:
- (i) Daily methane volume at STP
- (ii) Daily biogas volume
- (iii) Useful thermal energy produced per day (CV of CH₄ = 37.71 MJ/m³) **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Four Stages of AD (7 marks)

**Stage 1 – Hydrolysis (1.5 marks):** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down by **extracellular enzymes** (amylases, proteases, lipases) into soluble monomers. Bacteria: *Clostridium, Bacillus*. **This is the rate-limiting step** for particulate feedstocks.

**Stage 2 – Acidogenesis (1.5 marks):** Monomers are fermented by **acidogenic bacteria** (*Bacteroides, Lactobacillus*) into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂, and ammonia.

**Stage 3 – Acetogenesis (1.5 marks):** VFAs and alcohols are oxidized by **acetogenic bacteria** into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂. The reaction is **thermodynamically unfavorable** unless H₂ partial pressure is low.

**Stage 4 – Methanogenesis (2 marks):** Methanogenic archaea (*Methanosarcina, Methanobacterium*) produce methane via:
- **Acetoclastic pathway:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (~70% of CH₄).
- **Hydrogenotrophic pathway:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (~30% of CH₄).

> [Block diagram to be drawn as in SECTION 4.1]

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Reactor volume: $V = 8$ m³
- Daily feed: $m_{feed} = 40$ kg
- Volatile Solids (VS) fraction: $0.80$
- Methane yield: $0.25$ m³ CH₄/kg VS added
- CH₄ content in biogas: $55\%$

**Step 1: VS added per day**
$$ VS_{added} = 40 \times 0.80 = 32\,kg\,VS/day $$

**Step 2: Daily methane volume (i)**
$$ V_{CH_4} = 32 \times 0.25 = 8.0\,m^3\,CH_4/day $$

**Step 3: Daily total biogas volume (ii)**
$$ V_{biogas} = \frac{V_{CH_4}}{0.55} = \frac{8.0}{0.55} = 14.55\,m^3/day $$

**Step 4: Useful thermal energy per day (iii)**
$$ Q = V_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} = 8.0 \times 37.71 = 301.68\,MJ/day $$

$$ Q = \frac{301.68}{3.6} = 83.8\,kWh/day $$

**Results Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Daily CH₄ volume | **8.0 m³** |
| Daily biogas volume | **14.55 m³** |
| Daily thermal energy | **301.68 MJ ≈ 83.8 kWh** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Compare **Pyrolysis and Gasification** based on: (i) operating temperature, (ii) oxygen requirement, (iii) primary products, (iv) calorific value of products, and (v) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft biomass gasifier processes **1000 kg/h of woody biomass** (CV = 18 MJ/kg, moisture = 12% wt) with an **Air-Fuel Ratio of 1.6** and **equivalence ratio of 0.30**. The producer gas has the following composition (by volume): CO = 22%, H₂ = 15%, CH₄ = 3%, CO₂ = 10%, N₂ = 50%. Calculate:
- (i) The **calorific value of the producer gas**.
- (ii) The **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** of the gasifier, if 1800 m³/h of gas is produced.
- (iii) The **thermal power output** in kW. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Pyrolysis vs Gasification (7 marks)

| Parameter | **Pyrolysis** | **Gasification** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Temperature | **300–900 °C** | **700–1500 °C** |
| (ii) Oxygen | **Completely absent** | **Sub-stoichiometric (ER = 0.2–0.4)** |
| (iii) Primary Products | Bio-char, bio-oil, pyrolysis gas | **Syngas (CO + H₂)** as dominant product |
| (iv) Calorific Value | Bio-oil: **17–22 MJ/kg** | Producer gas: **4–18 MJ/m³** |
| (v) Applications | Biochar, biofuel, chemicals | Power generation, Fischer-Tropsch diesel |

> **Key difference (0.5 mark):** Pyrolysis deliberately avoids oxygen and yields three product phases; gasification uses controlled sub-stoichiometric oxygen and targets **gaseous fuel production**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1: CV of producer gas (i)**

$$ CV_{gas} = (0.22)(12.64) + (0.15)(12.79) + (0.03)(37.71) $$

$$ CV_{gas} = 2.781 + 1.919 + 1.131 = 5.83\,MJ/m^3 $$

**Step 2: Cold Gas Efficiency (ii)**

$$ CGE = \frac{CV_{gas} \times V_{gas}}{CV_{feed} \times m_{feed}} \times 100 $$

$$ CGE = \frac{5.83 \times 1800}{18 \times 1000} \times 100 $$

$$ CGE = \frac{10494}{18000} \times 100 = 58.3\,\% $$

**Step 3: Thermal power output (iii)**

$$ Q = CV_{gas} \times V_{gas} = 5.83 \times 1800 = 10494\,MJ/h $$

$$ Power = \frac{10494}{3.6} = 2915\,kW \approx 2.92\,MW $$

**Results Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Calorific value of gas | **5.83 MJ/m³** |
| Cold Gas Efficiency | **58.3%** |
| Thermal power output | **2915 kW ≈ 2.92 MW** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % CH₄ to a fraction** when applying $CV = 0.358 \times \%CH_4$ — the constant is calibrated for volume %, not mole fraction. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing ER (Equivalence Ratio) with AFR (Air-Fuel Ratio).** ER is dimensionless; AFR has units. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Not naming the microorganisms** in anaerobic digestion stages — examiners look for keywords *Clostridium, Bacteroides, Methanosarcina*. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Using CV of CH₄ (37.71 MJ/m³)** when the question asks for the energy from the **total biogas** — must either divide by 0.55 (CH₄ fraction) or explicitly state the assumption. Loss: **3 marks**.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram** for the four stages of AD — KTU valuation policy awards 1–2 marks for "neat diagrams with proper labels". Loss: **2 marks**.
>
> **Valuation Key Pattern:** In a 14-mark question, expect **1 mark for definition, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for principle/explanation, 2 marks for tabulation/comparison, and 7 marks for numerical** (most Part B questions have a numerical sub-part worth 7 marks).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ☐ **Anaerobic Digestion** is **biochemical**, requires **no O₂**, produces **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**, and is **net energy positive**.
- ☐ **Four stages of AD:** **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis. Rate-limiting stage = **Hydrolysis** (for particulate substrates).
- ☐ **Aerobic Digestion** is **biochemical**, requires **O₂**, produces **CO₂ + H₂O + sludge**, and is **net energy negative** (used for sludge stabilization, not energy).
- ☐ **C/N ratio optimum for AD:** **20–30 : 1**. pH optimum: **6.8–7.4**. Temperature: **Mesophilic (35–40 °C)** or **Thermophilic (50–55 °C)**.
- ☐ **HRT** for mesophilic AD: **20–40 days**. Hydraulic retention time $HRT = V_{reactor}/Q_{feed}$.
- ☐ **Pyrolysis** is **thermochemical**, requires **no O₂**, temperature **300–900 °C**, yields three products: **char + bio-oil + gas**. **Fast pyrolysis** maximizes **bio-oil**; **slow pyrolysis** maximizes **char**.
- ☐ **Gasification** is **thermochemical**, uses **sub-stoichiometric O₂**, temperature **700–1500 °C**, produces **syngas (CO + H₂)** as the main product. **ER = 0.2–0.4**.
- ☐ **Buswell equation** for carbohydrate AD: $C_nH_aO_b \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2$.
- ☐ **Biogas equation** for glucose: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ (50:50 CH₄:CO₂).
- ☐ **CV of biogas** ≈ **$0.358 \times \%CH_4$** in MJ/m³. Typical value: **18–25 MJ/m³**.
- ☐ **CV of syngas** = sum of volume fractions × individual component CVs: $CV_{CO} = 12.64$, $CV_{H_2} = 12.79$, $CV_{CH_4} = 37.71$ MJ/m³.
- ☐ **CGE (Cold Gas Efficiency)** = $\frac{CV_{gas} \cdot V_{gas}}{CV_{feed} \cdot m_{feed}} \times 100$. Typical range: **60–80%** for downdraft gasifiers.
- ☐ **Five gasification reactions** to memorize: **Combustion, Water-Gas, Boudouard, Water-Gas Shift, Methanation**. Know their ΔH signs.
- ☐ **Air gasification** → low CV gas (**4–7 MJ/m³**) due to N₂ dilution. **Oxygen/steam gasification** → high CV gas (**10–18 MJ/m³**).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodiesels: Manufacture and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biodiesels: Manufacture and Characteristics # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biodiesel** is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from renewable biological feedstocks such as **vegetable oils, animal fats, and used cooking oils (UCO)**. It is produced via a catalyzed chemical reaction with an alcohol (typically **methanol** or **ethanol**) and is used as a **drop-in or blended alternative fuel** for compression ignition (CI / diesel) engines. The most common production pathway is **transesterification**, in which a **triglyceride** molecule (the dominant constituent of vegetable oil) reacts with a short-chain alcohol in the presence of a catalyst (acid, base, or enzymatic) to form **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)** — which is biodiesel — and **glycerol (glycerin)** as a valuable by-product. $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Soap-Making Cousin" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine vegetable oil as a tightly packed, heavy three-legged stool — a *triglyceride*. Each leg is a long fatty acid chain. This stool is too bulky and viscous to burn efficiently in a diesel engine. **Transesterification is like a chemist who cuts off all three legs and gives each one a small methyl "handle"** (FAME), leaving behind the original stool-top as glycerol. The three small methylated legs (FAME) flow easily, ignite predictably, and burn cleanly — that's biodiesel. The leftover glycerol is sold to pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries, making the process economically attractive. ## 1.3 Why Biodiesel Matters in KTU Context | Parameter | Conventional Diesel | Biodiesel (FAME) | |---|---|---| | Source | Fossil (finite) | Renewable (biological) | | Sulfur content | 10–500 ppm | < 10 ppm (essentially zero) | | Aromatic content | High | Negligible | | Biodegradability | Slow | > 90% in 21 days | | Net CO₂ footprint | High | ~78% lower (closed loop) | | Cetane number | 45–55 | 48–65 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** Biodiesel is classified as a **first-generation biofuel** in the KTU syllabus. Its study is essential for understanding the broader theme of *sustainable transportation fuels* and forms a regular 14-mark Part B question in End Semester Examinations (ESE). ## 1.4 Key Feedstock Categories - **Edible vegetable oils:** Soybean (USA), Rapeseed/Canola (Europe), Palm (SE Asia), Sunflower. - **Non-edible oils:** Jatropha curcas, Karanja (Pongamia pinnata), Mahua, Neem, Castor. - **Waste oils:** Used cooking oil (UCO), yellow grease, tallow. - **Algal oils:** Microalgae (third-generation, future scope). > [!NOTE] > For India (and hence KTU Kerala context), **Jatropha and Karanja** are the strategic non-edible feedstocks because they grow on marginal lands and do not compete with the food chain. ## 1.5 Standardized Quality Benchmarks - **ASTM D6751** (USA) — Specification for biodiesel (B100) blend stock. - **EN 14214** (European) — Automotive fuels — fatty acid methyl esters (FAME). - **IS 15607** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — Indian biodiesel specification. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Chemistry of Triglycerides A **triglyceride (TG)** is the triester of **glycerol (C₃H₅(OH)₃)** with three long-chain fatty acids (RCOOH). General molecular formula: $$ \text{TG} = \text{C}_{3}\text{H}_{5}(\text{OOCR}_1)(\text{OOCR}_2)(\text{OOCR}_3) $$ The fatty acid chains $\text{R}_1, \text{R}_2, \text{R}_3$ are typically C12–C24, with **C16 (palmitic)**, **C18:0 (stearic)**, **C18:1 (oleic)**, **C18:2 (linoleic)** being the most abundant in nature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the chain length matters:** Longer chains → higher viscosity, higher cetane number, higher calorific value per kg. More double bonds (unsaturation) → lower cloud point, better cold-flow, but lower oxidative stability. This **trade-off** is the central design challenge of biodiesel formulation. ## 2.2 Manufacturing Routes — The Three Pillars ### 2.2.1 Transesterification (Alcoholysis) — *Dominant Commercial Route* - Reactant stoichiometry: **1 mole TG : 3 moles alcohol** (molar ratio). - In practice, an **excess of alcohol (6:1 to 12:1)** is used to push equilibrium toward FAME. - **Catalyst options:** - **Homogeneous base** (NaOH, KOH, NaOCH₃) — fast, 60 °C, 1 h, but sensitive to FFA. - **Homogeneous acid** (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) — slower, tolerates FFA, used as pre-treatment. - **Heterogeneous** (CaO, MgO, hydrotalcites) — reusable, cleaner separation. - **Enzymatic (lipases)** — green, mild, but expensive. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The reaction is **reversible and endothermic**. Excess alcohol shifts Le Chatelier's principle to the right. The **biphasic product** (FAME on top, glycerol on bottom due to density difference ~ 880 kg/m³ vs 1260 kg/m³) enables easy separation by a *settling tank / decanter*. ### 2.2.2 Esterification (Acid Pre-treatment) Free fatty acids (FFA) react with methanol in presence of acid catalyst to form FAME: $$ \text{RCOOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{ }]{\text{H}^+} \text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical for low-quality feedstocks:** When FFA content > 2 wt%, base catalyst undergoes **saponification** (soap formation), consuming catalyst and reducing yield. Therefore, an **acid pre-esterification step** is mandatory for feedstocks such as UCO and animal fats before the base transesterification stage. This is a **two-step acid-base catalysis** process. ### 2.2.3 Blending (Direct Use / Dilution) — *Simplest Route* - Mixing raw vegetable oil with conventional diesel (e.g., **B20 = 20% oil + 80% diesel**). - **Disadvantages:** high viscosity (~ 10× diesel), injector coking, incomplete combustion, ring sticking. - **Verdict:** Acceptable for short-term emergency use only; **not recommended** by KTU syllabus as a long-term strategy. ### 2.2.4 Pyrolysis / Microemulsion — *Emerging Routes* - **Pyrolysis (thermal cracking)** of TG in absence of air yields alkanes, alkenes, aromatics — chemistry similar to petroleum refining. High temperature (300–900 °C) needed. - **Microemulsion:** Surfactant + co-surfactant + alcohol + oil forms a clear, low-viscosity microemulsion. Co-solvents like *n-butanol* or *ethanol* are used. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Stoichiometric Molar Ratio | TG : MeOH = 1 : 3 | — | Practical: 1 : 6 to 1 : 12 | | Mass yield of FAME | $Y = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical 90–98% | | Theoretical glycerol mass | $m_{\text{gly}} = m_{\text{oil}} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{gly}}}{3\,M_{\text{TG/avg oil}}} \times 1000$ | kg | $M_{\text{gly}} = 92.09$ g/mol | | Calorific value (approx.) | $CV \approx 39{,}500$ to $41{,}000$ | kJ/kg | Lower than diesel (~ 43,000) | | Cetane Number (CN) | Increases with chain length, decreases with unsaturation | — | Typical 48–65 | | Kinematic viscosity (40 °C) | $\nu = 3.5$ to $5.0$ | mm²/s | ASTM D445; EN ISO 3104 | | Flash point | $T_{fp} \approx 423$ to $453$ | K (150–180 °C) | Safer storage than diesel | | Density (15 °C) | $\rho \approx 860$ to $900$ | kg/m³ | | | Acid value | $AV = \dfrac{56.1 \times V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg KOH/g | Should be < 0.5 | | Saponification value | $SV = \dfrac{56.1 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N}{m}$ | mg KOH/g | Average molecular weight basis | | Iodine value (unsaturation) | $IV = \dfrac{126.9 \times (V_b - V_s) \times N}{m}$ | g I₂/100 g | < 120 per EN 14214 | | Cloud Point / Pour Point | Empirical; depends on saturation | °C | Winter operability | | Molar ratio for % excess | $R_{\text{excess}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{actual}} - n_{\text{stoich}}}{n_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100\%$ | % | | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility Biodiesel bridges three engineering domains: **chemical reaction engineering** (catalysis, kinetics), **mechanical engineering** (engine performance, emissions), and **environmental engineering** (carbon cycle, life-cycle analysis). KTU expects students to be able to discuss at least one industrial process flow — the **batch / continuous transesterification plant** — and to defend biodiesel on **renewable + biodegradable + non-toxic** grounds. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometry of Base-Catalyzed Transesterification The reaction proceeds in **three sequential, reversible steps**, each producing a diglyceride, monoglyceride, and finally glycerol with one FAME released at each step. ### Step 1 — Triglyceride → Diglyceride $$ \text{TG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{ }]{\text{NaOH}} \text{DG} + \text{R}_1\text{COOCH}_3 $$ ### Step 2 — Diglyceride → Monoglyceride $$ \text{DG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{ }]{\text{NaOH}} \text{MG} + \text{R}_2\text{COOCH}_3 $$ ### Step 3 — Monoglyceride → Glycerol $$ \text{MG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{ }]{\text{NaOH}} \text{GLY} + \text{R}_3\text{COOCH}_3 $$ **Net overall reaction:** $$ \text{TG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{ }]{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Transition logic:** Each step consumes 1 mole of methanol and liberates 1 mole of FAME. The diglyceride and monoglyceride intermediates are short-lived. The final glycerol layer (denser, ~ 1.26 g/cm³) settles below the FAME layer (~ 0.88 g/cm³), enabling phase separation. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — FAME Yield from Soybean Oil > **Problem:** 1000 kg of refined soybean oil (average molecular weight **M_oil = 885 g/mol**, assumed pure TG) is reacted with **250 kg of methanol** (M = 32.04 g/mol) in presence of **1% NaOH** catalyst at 60 °C for 1 h. Given conversion **X = 95%** and that 100% of methanol in excess is recovered, calculate: > (i) the stoichiometric methanol requirement, > (ii) the percentage excess methanol, > (iii) the mass of FAME produced, > (iv) the mass of glycerol co-produced. ### Solution **(i) Stoichiometric methanol** Moles of oil: $$ n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 1000 \text{ g}}{885 \text{ g/mol}} = 1129.94 \text{ mol} $$ Stoichiometric methanol required (3 mol per mol oil): $$ n_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} = 3 \times 1129.94 = 3389.83 \text{ mol} $$ Mass: $$ m_{\text{MeOH, stoich}} = 3389.83 \times 32.04 = 108{,}609.0 \text{ g} = 108.61 \text{ kg} $$ **(ii) Percentage excess methanol** Actual methanol used = 250 kg. $$ \% \text{ excess} = \dfrac{250 - 108.61}{108.61} \times 100\% = 130.17\% $$ > This is well within the typical industrial operating range of 100–200 % excess. **(iii) Mass of FAME produced** For **oleic acid methyl ester (C₁₉H₃₆O₂)** the molecular weight is $M_{\text{FAME}} = 296.49$ g/mol (using R₁ = C₁₇H₃₃ for oleic). Theoretical FAME (3 mol per mol oil): $$ m_{\text{FAME, theor}} = 3 \times 1129.94 \times 296.49 / 1000 = 1005.18 \text{ kg} $$ At 95 % conversion: $$ m_{\text{FAME, actual}} = 0.95 \times 1005.18 = 954.92 \text{ kg} $$ **(iv) Mass of glycerol co-produced** Theoretical glycerol: $$ n_{\text{gly}} = n_{\text{oil}} = 1129.94 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{\text{gly, theor}} = 1129.94 \times 92.09 / 1000 = 104.05 \text{ kg} $$ At 95 % conversion: $$ m_{\text{gly, actual}} = 0.95 \times 104.05 = 98.85 \text{ kg} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass balance check:** Oil (1000 kg) + MeOH reacted = 1000 + (3 × 32.04 × 1129.94 × 0.95 / 1000) ≈ 1000 + 103.13 = 1103.13 kg input. FAME + Glycerol = 954.92 + 98.85 = 1053.77 kg. Difference (≈ 49.36 kg) = unreacted TG + catalyst residue + dissolved methanol in glycerol phase — consistent with industrial data. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters and Their Effect | Parameter | Increased Effect on Conversion | Increased Effect on Yield | KTU Exam Tip | |---|---|---|---| | Molar ratio (MeOH : oil) | Positive up to 12:1, then plateau | Positive | Mention equilibrium shift | | Catalyst concentration (1 % w/w) | Optimum at 0.5–1.5 % | Optimum then decreases (saponification) | Cite "Golden range" | | Reaction temperature (°C) | Positive up to ~ 65 °C (above MeOH bp) | Positive | Above 65 °C MeOH vaporizes | | Reaction time | Positive, saturates ~ 90 min | Positive | Mention kinetics | | Water content | Strongly negative (deactivates base) | Negative | Soap formation | | FFA content | Negative for base catalyst | Negative (saponification) | Justifies pre-esterification | | Mixing intensity (rpm) | Positive (mass-transfer limited) | Positive | Two-phase liquid system | ## 3.4 Derivation: Calorific Value Estimation (Knothe Method) A semi-empirical formula approximating the gross heat of combustion (HHV) of FAME from its iodine value (IV) and saponification value (SV) is: $$ HHV = 49.43 - \dfrac{0.041 \times IV + 0.015 \times SV}{1} \quad \text{[MJ/kg]} $$ > For methyl oleate (IV ≈ 85, SV ≈ 190): $HHV \approx 49.43 - 3.49 - 2.85 = 43.09$ MJ/kg — close to experimental 39.9 MJ/kg net. Useful as a first-order estimate only. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Industrial Batch Transesterification Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[oilFeedT1
Vegetable Oil Feedstock
Preheated to 60 C] --> B[filterT2
Filtration Unit
Removes solids and gums] B --> C[acidPreT3
Acid Pre Esterification
H2SO4 1 to 2 percent
