(3 Marks) **Define biodiesel. List any four important fuel properties used to characterize biodiesel.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids, typically produced by the **transesterification of vegetable oils or animal fats with a short-chain alcohol** (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a catalyst. Chemically, it is a mixture of **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)**, with the general formula **R–COO–CH₃**, where R is a C15–C21 alkyl chain. **Four important properties (½ mark each, 1 mark total):** 1. **Cetane number (CN):** Indicator of ignition quality; higher CN → shorter ignition delay. Biodiesel has CN 48–65 vs diesel 45–55. 2. **Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C:** Affects atomization in injectors. Specified range 3.5–5.0 mm²/s (ASTM D445). 3. **Flash point:** Biodiesel ~ 150–180 °C (much higher than diesel ~ 55–65 °C) → safer storage and transport. 4. **Acid value:** Should be < 0.5 mg KOH/g to prevent engine corrosion (ASTM D664). Other acceptable properties: density, calorific value, iodine value, cloud/pour point, oxidative stability, water content. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q2. (3 Marks) **What is transesterification? Write the overall reaction and name the catalyst commonly used.** **Model Answer:** Transesterification is the **chemical reaction in which an ester (triglyceride) reacts with an alcohol in the presence of a catalyst** to form a new ester and a by-product alcohol. In biodiesel context, the triglyceride ester of glycerol reacts with methanol to give fatty acid methyl esters (FAME = biodiesel) and glycerol. **Overall reaction:** $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH or KOH}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 $$ **Commonly used catalyst:** Homogeneous base catalyst — **Sodium hydroxide (NaOH)** or **Potassium hydroxide (KOH)** at ~ 1 wt% of oil. Sodium methoxide (NaOCH₃) is also widely used industrially because it eliminates water formation. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (1 × 14 Marks; Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Q7]** --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain in detail the **manufacture of biodiesel by transesterification** with a neat process flow diagram. Discuss the role of catalyst, temperature, and molar ratio. (7 Marks) **(b)** Discuss the **important fuel properties of biodiesel** and compare them with conventional diesel. Mention the relevant IS/ASTM/EN standards. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Definition & Chemistry (1 mark):** Transesterification is the alcoholysis of triglycerides using methanol/ethanol to yield FAME + glycerol. Reaction is reversible, base-catalyzed, and conducted at 60 °C for 1 hour. **2. Step-by-step process flow (4 marks):** - **Feedstock preparation:** filtration, degumming (H₃PO₄), neutralization to remove gums and FFA. - **Acid pre-esterification (optional):** if FFA > 2 %, treat with 1–2 % H₂SO₄ + methanol at 60 °C for 1 h → reduces FFA below 1 %. - **Base transesterification:** Mix oil + methanol (6:1 molar) + 1 % NaOH at 60 °C, 600 rpm, 1 h. - **Phase separation:** Settle in decanter for 2–8 h. FAME (top, ~ 880 kg/m³) and glycerol (bottom, ~ 1260 kg/m³) separate by gravity. - **FAME purification:** Water wash (3×) → vacuum drying → polishing filter. - **Glycerol purification:** Neutralize acid → distill → pharma grade. **3. Role of key parameters (2 marks):** - **Catalyst (NaOH/KOH):** Provides methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) which attacks carbonyl carbon; rate increases with [Cat] up to 1.5 %; above which saponification reduces yield. - **Temperature:** Reaction rate doubles per 10 °C rise; optimum 60 °C (close to MeOH boiling point 64.7 °C). Higher temperatures cause MeOH vaporization. - **Molar ratio:** Stoichiometric 3:1 insufficient; industrial use 6:1 to 12:1 pushes equilibrium toward FAME. Excess MeOH later recovered by distillation. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Important properties (5 marks):** Tabulate and explain as in Section 2.3. Highlight: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cetane Number}_{\text{biodiesel}} &= 48\text{–}65 \quad \text{vs} \quad 45\text{–}55 \text{ (diesel)} \\ \text{Kinematic Viscosity}_{40\,°\text{C}} &= 3.5\text{–}5.0 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s} \quad \text{vs} \quad 2.0\text{–}4.5 \text{ (diesel)} \\ \text{Flash Point} &= 150\text{–}180\,°\text{C} \quad \text{vs} \quad 55\text{–}65\,°\text{C} \text{ (diesel)} \\ \text{Calorific Value} &\approx 37\text{–}40 \text{ MJ/kg} \quad \text{vs} \quad 42\text{–}45 \text{ (diesel)} \\ \text{Density}_{15\,°\text{C}} &= 860\text{–}900 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{vs} \quad 820\text{–}860 \text{ (diesel)} \end{aligned} $$ **2. Standards (1 mark):** ASTM D6751 (USA), EN 14214 (Europe), IS 15607 (India). **3. Comparative advantage (1 mark):** Biodiesel is renewable, biodegradable, ultra-low sulfur, higher CN, safer flash, lower CO/HC emissions, slightly higher NO_x. Drawback: ~ 8–10 % lower calorific value, slight NO_x increase, cold-flow limitation, hygroscopic. --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Describe the **chemistry of biodiesel synthesis** with a stepwise reaction mechanism. What is the function of the **catalyst and why is methanol preferred over ethanol**? (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain **two-step acid-base catalyzed transesterification**. Under what conditions is this process preferred? Discuss the **effect of free fatty acids (FFA) and water** on base-catalyzed reaction. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **1. Reaction sequence (3 marks):** The transesterification proceeds in three consecutive reversible steps — triglyceride → diglyceride → monoglyceride → glycerol, with one FAME released at each step (refer to Section 3.1). **2. Mechanism (role of base catalyst) (2 marks):** - NaOH + CH₃OH → CH₃O⁻ Na⁺ + H₂O (methoxide ion formation) - Methoxide ion attacks electrophilic carbonyl carbon of TG → tetrahedral intermediate. - Intermediate collapses → diglyceride anion + FAME. - Diglyceride anion deprotonates methanol → regenerates methoxide; equilibrium continues. - Net: catalytic cycle repeats for DG and MG. **3. Why methanol preferred over ethanol (2 marks):** - **Lower cost** (~$ 350/ton vs $700/ton for ethanol). - **Shorter chain → less steric hindrance → faster reaction** kinetics. - **Easier separation** from FAME and glycerol (lower boiling point 64.7 °C vs 78.4 °C for ethanol). - **Lower water co-production** → less hydrolysis. - **Established global supply chain** for biodiesel. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **1. Two-step process (3 marks):** - **Step 1 — Acid pre-esterification:** Oil + Methanol + 1–2 % H₂SO₄, 60 °C, 1 h → FFA → FAME; reduces FFA from 5–15 % down to < 1 %. - **Step 2 — Base transesterification:** Same as standard route with 1 % NaOH at 60 °C, 1 h. **2. When preferred (2 marks):** Used for **low-cost, high-FFA feedstocks** such as used cooking oil, tallow, animal fats, non-edible crude oils (Jatropha, Karanja, yellow grease) where FFA > 2 %. **3. FFA effect on base catalyst (1 mark):** $$ \text{RCOOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ FFA neutralizes NaOH to form **soap (saponification)**, consuming catalyst, emulsifying products, blocking phase separation → drastically reduces FAME yield. **4. Water effect (1 mark):** Water hydrolyses FAME back to FFA, deactivates base catalyst (forms NaOH·H₂O complexes), promotes soap formation. Hence, oil must be dried to < 0.06 % water before reaction. --- ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common reasons students LOSE marks in biodiesel questions:** > 1. **Confusing transesterification with esterification** — they are different. Transesterification is TG + alcohol → FAME + glycerol (used for FAME production). Esterification is FFA + alcohol → FAME + water (used as pre-treatment). Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if these are mixed up. > 2. **Forgetting the three-step nature** of transesterification (TG → DG → MG → GLY) and jumping directly to the net equation. Always write the stepwise equations in Part B (a) questions. > 3. **Wrong stoichiometry:** writing "1 mole methanol produces 1 mole FAME" is incorrect. The ratio is **3 : 3 (TG : MeOH : FAME)**. > 4. **Omitting units** in property tables (especially kinematic viscosity in mm²/s, not m²/s). > 5. **Not stating the catalyst type clearly** — saying "catalyst" without specifying NaOH/KOH/H₂SO₄ loses marks. > 6. **Missing the practical molar ratio** (6:1 to 12:1) vs stoichiometric 3:1 — frequently asked as a 2-mark sub-part. > 7. **Neglecting the role of FFA and water** — a high-yield KTU topic often worth 3–4 marks. > 8. **Process flow diagrams without temperatures, catalyst %, and molar ratio** — examiners want process parameters on the diagram, not just arrows. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **Biodiesel = FAME = RCOOCH₃** (mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids). - **Dominant production route:** Base-catalyzed **transesterification** of triglyceride + methanol (6:1 to 12:1) at 60 °C, 1 h, 1 % NaOH. - **Three-step mechanism:** TG → DG → MG → GLY with FAME released at each step. - **By-product:** Glycerol (10 wt% of oil) — commercially valuable, ~ 1 kg glycerol per 9 kg FAME. - **Two-step acid-base route** is mandatory when FFA > 2 wt% (e.g., Jatropha, UCO, tallow). - **Saponification** is the *enemy* — caused by FFA + water reacting with base catalyst; consumes catalyst, emulsifies products. - **Key fuel properties to memorize:** - **Cetane number:** 48–65 (B100) > 45–55 (diesel) - **Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C:** 3.5–5.0 mm²/s - **Flash point:** 150–180 °C (much safer than diesel) - **Calorific value:** ~ 37–40 MJ/kg (≈ 8–10 % lower than diesel) - **Density:** 860–900 kg/m³ at 15 °C - **Cloud/Pour point:** Higher than diesel → cold-flow limitation - **Acid value:** < 0.5 mg KOH/g - **Iodine value:** < 120 g I₂/100 g (EN 14214) - **Standards to mention:** ASTM D6751, EN 14214, IS 15607. - **Common feedstocks for India:** Jatropha curcas, Karanja (Pongamia), Mahua, Used Cooking Oil (UCO). - **Advantages:** renewable, biodegradable (> 90 % in 21 days), ultra-low sulfur, lower CO/HC/particulate emissions, higher CN. - **Disadvantages:** higher NOₓ, lower calorific value, poor cold-flow, hygroscopic, slightly higher viscosity, marginally higher cost. - **Stoichiometric shortcut:** 1 mol oil → 3 mol FAME → 1 mol glycerol. - **Methanol preferred** over ethanol — cheaper, faster, easier separation. - **Process flow:** Oil prep → (acid pre-treatment) → Base reactor → Settler → FAME wash → Dry → Storage; Glycerol → Refine → Pharma. - **Industrial yield range:** 90–98 % under optimized conditions. - **Knothe calorific formula** (semi-empirical): $HHV = 49.43 - 0.041\,IV - 0.015\,SV$ in MJ/kg.

topics.title: Hydrogen Production Methods: Electrolysis and Thermochemical Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Production Methods: Electrolysis and Thermochemical Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen (H₂)** is the lightest, most abundant chemical element in the universe. As an energy carrier, it stores and delivers energy in a usable form, distinguished from a primary energy source because it must be produced from compounds containing hydrogen (water, hydrocarbons, biomass).

**Molecular Weight:** 2.016 g/mol | **Energy Density (LHV):** 120 MJ/kg | **Energy Density (HHV):** 142 MJ/kg

### 1.2 Hydrogen Production: Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen Production** refers to the family of industrial, electrochemical, thermochemical, biological, and photonic processes used to extract molecular hydrogen (H₂) from a hydrogen-rich source — most commonly **water (H₂O)** or **hydrocarbons (CH₄, etc.)** — and isolate it as a usable, separable gas.

In the context of **KTU Module 4**, the syllabus narrows this to two dominant non-fossil pathways:
1. **Electrolysis of Water** (Electrochemical route)
2. **Thermochemical Water-Splitting Cycles** (Thermal route)

### 1.3 Electrolysis — The Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrolysis** is the process of using **direct electrical current (DC)** to drive a **non-spontaneous chemical reaction** — specifically, splitting water (H₂O) into hydrogen (H₂) at the cathode and oxygen (O₂) at the anode.

**Real-World Analogy — "The Battery in Reverse":**
Imagine a rechargeable battery being **charged**. When you charge a battery, electricity forces chemical reactions to happen in reverse — storing energy as chemical bonds. **Electrolysis does the exact same thing to water:** you pour in electrical energy, and the water "absorbs" it by breaking its H–O bonds. Hydrogen bubbles rise from the negative plate (cathode), oxygen bubbles from the positive plate (anode). You are literally **storing electricity inside hydrogen molecules** for later use in a fuel cell.

### 1.4 Thermochemical Processes — The Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermochemical Hydrogen Production** uses a **closed-loop series of chemical reactions** at high temperatures (often 500 °C–2500 °C) to split water into H₂ and O₂. The intermediate chemicals are *regenerated* and recycled — only water and heat are consumed, with hydrogen and oxygen as outputs.

**Real-World Analogy — "The Recyclable Chemical Relay Race":**
Think of a relay race where the baton is a chemical element. Water (H₂O) hands its oxygen "baton" to a helper chemical (say, iodine or a metal oxide). The helper chemical becomes oxidized and is separated. It then passes the baton back in a separate reaction chamber, regaining its original form, ready to run again. The runner never gets tired — the chemicals keep cycling forever. **At the finish line, you have pure H₂ and pure O₂**, and all the helpers are back at the start.

### 1.5 Classification Map (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Production Method | Energy Input | Maturity | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline Electrolysis | Electricity (DC) | Commercial | High |
| PEM Electrolysis | Electricity (DC) | Commercial | High |
| Solid Oxide Electrolysis (SOEC) | Electricity + Heat | Pilot / Demonstrating | High |
| Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle | High-Temp Heat (≥ 800 °C) | Demonstrated | High |
| Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle | Medium-Temp Heat (≤ 550 °C) | Pilot | Medium |
| Hybrid Sulfur (Westinghouse) Cycle | Heat + Electricity | Pilot | Medium |
| Direct Thermal Decomposition | Very High Heat (> 2500 °C) | Lab | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Focus:** *Alkaline Electrolysis*, *PEM Electrolysis*, *Sulfur-Iodine Cycle*, and the *thermodynamic/efficiency analysis* of each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Water Electrolysis — The Core Chemistry

The overall stoichiometric reaction of water electrolysis is:

$$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2} O_{2(g)}$$

This overall reaction is the sum of two half-reactions occurring at opposite electrodes in an electrolyte:

- **At the Cathode (Reduction — gain of electrons):**
$$2H_2O_{(l)} + 2e^- \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + 2OH^-_{(aq)} \quad E^0 = 0.00\,V \,vs.\,SHE \text{ (in alkaline)}$$

- **At the Anode (Oxidation — loss of electrons):**
$$2OH^-_{(aq)} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} O_{2(g)} + H_2O_{(l)} + 2e^- \quad E^0 = +0.40\,V \,vs.\,SHE$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does electrolysis require energy?**
> Water is thermodynamically *stable*. The Standard Gibbs Free Energy of formation of liquid water is **ΔG°f = –237.13 kJ/mol**, meaning water *prefers* to exist as H₂O. To tear it apart, we must inject at least 237.13 kJ of electrical work per mole — this is the *minimum theoretical* energy barrier.

### 2.2 The Three Industrial Electrolyzer Technologies

#### A. Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL)

- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous KOH (typically 25–30 wt%) or NaOH.
- **Operating Temperature:** 60–90 °C.
- **Operating Pressure:** Atmospheric to ~30 bar.
- **Electrodes:** Nickel-coated steel; often activated with catalysts.
- **Separator:** Diaphragm (asbestos historically; now PPS, Zirfon, or polymer membranes).
- **Maturity:** Most mature; commercial since the 1920s.

**Step-by-step logic of why it works:**
1. DC voltage is applied across two nickel electrodes immersed in KOH solution.
2. Hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) migrate toward the anode (positive electrode).
3. At the anode, OH⁻ is oxidized → O₂ gas + water + electrons.
4. The released electrons travel through the external circuit to the cathode.
5. At the cathode, water is reduced → H₂ gas + OH⁻ ions.
6. The OH⁻ ions migrate back through the diaphragm toward the anode, completing the loop.

**Electrical Efficiency:** 60–70% (LHV basis). **Cell Voltage:** 1.8–2.4 V. **Current Density:** 0.2–0.4 A/cm².

#### B. Proton Exchange Membrane Electrolyzer (PEMEL)

- **Electrolyte:** Solid polymer membrane (Nafion™-type perfluorosulfonic acid).
- **Operating Temperature:** 50–80 °C.
- **Operating Pressure:** Up to 80 bar (high-pressure capability).
- **Electrodes:** Precious metal catalysts — Platinum (Pt) at cathode, Iridium oxide (IrO₂) at anode.
- **Maturity:** Commercial; rapidly scaling.

**Half-reactions (acidic PEM environment):**
- Cathode: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$
- Anode: $H_2O \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$

**Electrical Efficiency:** 70–82% (LHV basis). **Cell Voltage:** 1.8–2.2 V. **Current Density:** 1.0–3.0 A/cm² (much higher than alkaline).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PEM is preferred for renewables:** It handles **dynamic / intermittent loads** (solar/wind) far better than alkaline, because it tolerates rapid current changes without performance loss.

#### C. Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (SOEC)

- **Electrolyte:** Solid ceramic — typically Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ).
- **Operating Temperature:** 700–900 °C (high-temp operation).
- **Maturity:** Pilot / demonstration stage.

**Why high temperature is an advantage:**
At elevated temperatures, a portion of the required energy can be supplied as **heat (Q)** instead of expensive **electricity (W)**. The total enthalpy ΔH = ΔG + TΔS. Since the TΔS term is supplied thermally, the **electrical work requirement drops dramatically**.

**Electrical Efficiency:** 75–85% (LHV, system); total (HHV) efficiency can approach **> 95%** when waste heat is integrated.

### 2.4 Thermochemical Water-Splitting Cycles

#### A. Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle — The Most Studied

The S-I cycle (also called the **General Atomics cycle**) uses three reactions over a temperature range of **800–950 °C**:

**Reaction 1 — Bunsen Reaction (exothermic, ~120 °C):**
$$SO_2 + I_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 + 2HI$$

**Reaction 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (endothermic, ~850 °C):**
$$H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

**Reaction 3 — Hydrogen Iodide Decomposition (endothermic, ~450 °C):**
$$2HI \rightarrow I_2 + H_2$$

**Net Reaction:**
$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **I₂ and SO₂ are completely regenerated** — they are catalysts, not consumables. Only water is consumed, and only H₂ and O₂ are produced.

**Theoretical Thermal Efficiency:** 50–52% | **Demonstrated Efficiency:** 40–47% in lab/pilot.

#### B. Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle

Operates at lower temperatures (**≤ 550 °C**) — compatible with lower-grade industrial waste heat, solar thermal collectors, and Generation IV nuclear reactors.

**Key reactions (simplified 5-step form):**
1. **Hydrolysis:** $2CuCl_2 + H_2O \rightarrow Cu_2OCl_2 + 2HCl$
2. **Drying/decomposition:** $Cu_2OCl_2 \rightarrow 2CuCl + \frac{1}{2}O_2$
3. **Electrolysis (electrochemical step):** $2CuCl + 2HCl \rightarrow 2CuCl_2 + H_2$
4. **Recycling:** CuCl and HCl regenerated.

**Why Cu-Cl matters for KTU:** It blends thermochemical + electrochemical steps — a **hybrid cycle** — and is the most likely cycle for near-term deployment because its peak temperature is achievable with current solar thermal or advanced nuclear technology.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities use **SI units** unless noted. Standard conditions: $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $P_0 = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa, Faraday constant **F = 96485 C/mol**, Universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**.

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Thermoneutral Voltage | $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Minimum voltage if **all** energy supplied as electricity. $E_{tn} \approx 1.481$ V (HHV) at 25 °C. |
| Reversible (Equilibrium) Voltage | $E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Minimum electrical work required. $E_{rev} \approx 1.229$ V (HHV) at 25 °C. |
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Actual reversible voltage under non-standard T, P, and concentration. |
| Thermoneutral Voltage @ T | $E_{tn}(T) = \dfrac{\Delta H(T)}{nF}$ | Used in SOEC high-T calculations. |
| Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = \dfrac{M \cdot Q}{nF}$ | Mass of substance produced; $M$ molar mass, $I$ current, $t$ time, $Q$ charge, $n$ electrons transferred. |
| Volumetric H₂ Production Rate | $\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot V_m}{nF} = \dfrac{I \cdot RT}{nFP}$ | Volume of H₂ per second. $V_m$ = molar volume = 22.414 L/mol at STP. |
| Energy Consumed per kg H₂ | $E_{specific} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{cell}}{M_{H_2} \cdot 1000}$ | kWh per kg H₂, function of operating cell voltage. |
| LHV of H₂ | $LHV_{H_2} = 33.33\,kWh/kg$ | Lower Heating Value — used for electrolyzer efficiency. |
| HHV of H₂ | $HHV_{H_2} = 39.39\,kWh/kg$ | Higher Heating Value. |
| Electrolyzer Efficiency (LHV) | $\eta = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{E_{specific}} = \dfrac{33.33}{E_{specific}(kWh/kg)}$ | Practical metric — typically 60–80%. |
| Carnot Efficiency (Heat Engine) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Upper bound for any heat-driven cycle. |
| Thermal-to-Hydrogen Efficiency | $\eta_{th-H_2} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot,\,ideal}}$ | Used for thermochemical cycle analysis. |
| First Law: Energy Balance | $Q_{in} + W_{in} = \Delta H_{reaction}$ | For SOEC at high T, $Q$ and $W$ split the enthalpy demand. |
| Faradaic / Current Efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical H₂ produced. |
| Gibbs–Helmholtz (T-dependence) | $\dfrac{\partial(\Delta G/T)}{\partial T} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{T^2}$ | Used to derive Nernst and voltage-temperature curves for SOEC. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The *Reversible Voltage* (1.229 V) is **not** the operating voltage. The *operating voltage* is always **higher** (1.8–2.4 V) due to **overpotentials** (activation, ohmic, concentration). This gap defines the *irreversibility losses*.

### 2.6 The Three Sources of Overpotential (Voltage Loss)

1. **Activation Overpotential ($\eta_{act}$):** Energy to overcome reaction activation barrier — dominant at low current densities.
2. **Ohmic Overpotential ($\eta_{ohm} = iR$):** Ionic/electronic resistance in electrolyte, membrane, electrodes — dominant at medium current densities.
3. **Concentration Overpotential ($\eta_{conc}$):** Mass-transport limitation when reactants can't reach the electrode fast enough — dominant at very high current densities.

**Total Cell Voltage:**
$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} + \vert \eta_{act} \vert + \vert \eta_{ohm} \vert + \vert \eta_{conc} \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering significance:** Minimizing these overpotentials is the central design goal of every modern electrolyzer — it directly determines the **$/kg H₂** levelized cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermoneutral Voltage from Thermodynamics

**Goal:** Show that the minimum cell voltage (if 100% electrical) equals $\Delta H / nF$.

**Starting Point — First Law for a Constant-Pressure Electrolytic Cell:**

For water electrolysis at constant temperature and pressure, the enthalpy change is supplied as electrical work (since $\Delta V \approx 0$ at constant P):

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$$

Divide both sides by the charge transferred per mole of H₂ (n = 2 electrons, F = Faraday constant):

$$\frac{\Delta H}{nF} = \frac{\Delta G}{nF} + \frac{T\Delta S}{nF}$$

By definition:
- $\dfrac{\Delta G}{nF} = E_{rev}$ (reversible / equilibrium voltage)
- $\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF} = E_{tn}$ (thermoneutral voltage)
- $\dfrac{T\Delta S}{nF} = E_{thermal}$ (thermal-equivalent voltage)

Therefore:

$$E_{tn} = E_{rev} + E_{thermal}$$

**Numerical evaluation at Standard Conditions (25 °C = 298.15 K):**
- $\Delta H_{H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2} = +285.83\,kJ/mol$ (HHV basis, liquid water)
- $\Delta G^0 = +237.13\,kJ/mol$
- $T\Delta S = \Delta H - \Delta G = 285.83 - 237.13 = 48.70\,kJ/mol$

$$E_{rev} = \frac{237130\,J/mol}{2 \times 96485\,C/mol} = 1.229\,V$$

$$E_{tn} = \frac{285830\,J/mol}{2 \times 96485\,C/mol} = 1.481\,V$$

$$E_{thermal} = \frac{48700}{2 \times 96485} = 0.252\,V$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** If you operate at the thermoneutral voltage 1.481 V, the cell is *thermally self-sustaining* — no net heat is absorbed or released. Operate *below* 1.481 V and the cell cools (needs external heat). Operate *above* 1.481 V and the cell generates waste heat.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy Consumption of an Electrolyzer

**Goal:** Express the electricity required (kWh) to produce 1 kg of H₂.

**Step 1 — Moles of H₂ in 1 kg:**
$$n_{H_2} = \frac{1000\,g}{M_{H_2}} = \frac{1000}{2.016} = 496.03\,mol$$

**Step 2 — Charge required (n = 2 electrons per H₂):**
$$Q = n_{H_2} \cdot n \cdot F = 496.03 \times 2 \times 96485 = 9.571 \times 10^7\,C$$

**Step 3 — Energy at cell voltage $V_{cell}$:**
$$E_{total} = Q \cdot V_{cell} = 9.571 \times 10^7 \times V_{cell}\,[J]$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh (÷ 3.6 × 10⁶):**
$$E_{specific} = \frac{9.571 \times 10^7 \times V_{cell}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 26.59 \cdot V_{cell}\,[kWh/kg\,H_2]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compact formula:**
> $$E_{specific}\,[kWh/kg] = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot V_{cell}}{M_{H_2} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^6} = 26.59 \times V_{cell}\,[V]$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency (LHV basis):**
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{E_{specific}} = \frac{33.33}{26.59 \cdot V_{cell}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Operating at $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V:
$$E_{specific} = 26.59 \times 2.0 = 53.18\,kWh/kg$$
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{33.33}{53.18} = 62.7\%$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Faraday's Law and Current-to-Hydrogen Mass

**Goal:** Derive the production rate of H₂ (kg/day) from a known current.

**Step 1 — Coulombs per second (current I in A) over time t in seconds:**
$$Q = I \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Moles of electrons:**
$$n_{e^-} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂ (2 electrons per H₂):**
$$n_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t}{2F}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of H₂ (multiply by molar mass M = 2.016 g/mol):**
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kg per day (t = 86400 s, g → kg ÷ 1000):**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{I \times 86400 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96485 \times 1000}$$

**Compact formula:**
$$\dot{m}_{H_2}\,[kg/day] = 9.024 \times 10^{-3} \times I\,[A]$$

**Numerical Example:** I = 1000 A:
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 9.024 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 9.024\,kg/day$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering check:** A 1 MW PEM electrolyzer operating at ~500 mA/cm² and ~250 V stack typically produces ~360–400 kg H₂/day. This matches industry benchmarks.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermodynamic Upper Limit of a Thermochemical Cycle (Carnot Bound)

**Goal:** Show that thermochemical cycles cannot exceed Carnot efficiency and bound the practical efficiency.

**Step 1 — Maximum work extractable from heat source at $T_H$ with sink at $T_C$:**
$$W_{max} = Q_{in}\left(1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}\right)$$

**Step 2 — For an *ideal* thermochemical cycle, the work must equal the LHV of H₂ produced:**
$$W_{max} = LHV_{H_2} = 33.33\,kWh/kg$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q_{in}/kg\,H_2$:**
$$Q_{in} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{1 - T_C/T_H}$$

**Step 4 — Ideal thermal-to-H₂ efficiency:**
$$\eta_{ideal} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

**Numerical Example:** $T_H = 1000\,K$ (727 °C), $T_C = 300\,K$ (27 °C):
$$\eta_{ideal} = 1 - \frac{300}{1000} = 0.70 = 70\%$$

For the Sulfur-Iodine cycle (real efficiency ~50%), the *gap to Carnot* is about 20 percentage points, attributed to:
- Kinetic irreversibilities
- Heat recovery limitations
- Separation work (distillation of H₂SO₄, HI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The **lower** the cycle's peak temperature, the **lower** the Carnot ceiling, but also the **lower** the materials and capital cost. Cu-Cl cycle trades efficiency ceiling for practical deployability. This is a classic engineering trade-off.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electrolysis vs Thermochemical

| Parameter | Alkaline Electrolysis | PEM Electrolysis | SOEC Electrolysis | S-I Thermochemical | Cu-Cl Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating T (°C)** | 60–90 | 50–80 | 700–900 | 800–950 | 450–550 |
| **Energy Input** | Electrical | Electrical | Elec + Heat | Heat (thermal) | Heat + Elec |
| **Efficiency (LHV)** | 60–70% | 70–82% | 75–85% | 40–52% (thermal) | 40–50% (thermal) |
| **Current Density (A/cm²)** | 0.2–0.4 | 1.0–3.0 | 0.3–1.0 | N/A | N/A |
| **H₂ Purity** | 99.5–99.9% | 99.99% | 99.99% | 99.99% | 99.99% |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (Commercial) | 8 (Early commercial) | 6 (Pilot) | 6 (Pilot) | 5 (Lab-pilot) |
| **Dynamic Load (Renewables)** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Poor | Poor |
| **Capital Cost (relative)** | Low | High | Very High | Very High | Very High |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | High | Medium | Very High | Medium |
