## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "thermochemical hydrogen production." Mention any two thermochemical cycles used for hydrogen production.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Thermochemical hydrogen production is the process of splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using a *closed-loop series of chemical reactions* driven entirely or primarily by *high-temperature heat*. Intermediate chemicals are continuously regenerated, so net reactants are only water and heat, and net products are H₂ and O₂.

> **Two thermochemical cycles (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) Cycle** — uses Bunsen reaction, H₂SO₄ decomposition at ~850 °C, and HI decomposition at ~450 °C. Peak temperature ~950 °C.
> 2. **Copper-Chlorine (Cu-Cl) Cycle** — hybrid cycle using lower peak temperature (~550 °C), compatible with Generation IV nuclear and solar thermal sources.

*(Also acceptable: Hybrid Sulfur/Westinghouse, UT-3 cycle, Mark-13, Iron-Chlorine cycle.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Write the overall reaction of water electrolysis and state the value of the reversible cell voltage at standard conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Overall Reaction (1 mark):**
> $$H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_{2(g)} + \frac{1}{2}O_{2(g)}$$

> **Reversible Cell Voltage (1 mark):**
> At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm), $\Delta G^0 = +237.13$ kJ/mol.
> $$E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} = \frac{237130}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229\,V$$

> **Cathode and Anode Reactions (1 mark):**
> - Cathode (reduction): $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ (alkaline) / $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ (acidic/PEM)
> - Anode (oxidation): $4OH^- \rightarrow O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^-$ / $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each of **7 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an alkaline water electrolyzer** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PEM electrolyzer** operates at a cell voltage of **1.95 V** and a current of **500 A**. Calculate:
- (i) The rate of H₂ production in **kg/day** (assuming 100% Faradaic efficiency). **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The specific energy consumption in **kWh/kg H₂**. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The LHV-based electrolyzer efficiency. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Alkaline Electrolyzer — Full Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (1.5 marks):** Water is decomposed into H₂ and O₂ by passing DC current through an aqueous alkaline electrolyte (typically 25–30% KOH). The OH⁻ ion acts as the charge carrier. The minimum reversible EMF required is 1.229 V at 25 °C, but in practice the cell operates at 1.8–2.4 V due to overpotentials.

2. **Construction (2 marks):**
   - Two **nickel-coated steel electrodes** (cathode and anode).
   - **Electrolyte:** 25–30 wt% aqueous KOH.
   - **Diaphragm/separator:** Asbestos historically, replaced by **PPS fabric, Zirfon, or NiO** to physically separate the H₂ and O₂ gas streams while allowing OH⁻ migration.
   - **Gas collection hoods** at the top of each electrode chamber.
   - **External DC power source** connected to the electrodes.
   - **Cooling system** to remove Joule heat and maintain 60–90 °C.

3. **Working (2 marks):**
   - At the cathode: $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ (reduction → H₂ bubbles)
   - At the anode: $4OH^- \rightarrow O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^-$ (oxidation → O₂ bubbles)
   - OH⁻ migrates from cathode to anode through the diaphragm; electrons travel externally.
   - Net: $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$
   - Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1.

4. **Advantages (1 mark):** Mature technology, low CAPEX, no precious metal catalysts, long lifetime (> 80,000 hours), simple construction.

5. **Limitations (0.5 mark):** Low current density, slow dynamic response (poor for renewables), lower purity than PEM, liquid electrolyte corrosion risk.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{cell} = 1.95$ V, $I = 500$ A, $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, $F = 96485$ C/mol, $n = 2$, $LHV_{H_2} = 33.33$ kWh/kg.

**(i) Mass flow rate of H₂ (3 marks):**
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2} \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$$

Per day, t = 86400 s:
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 500 \times 86400}{2 \times 96485}$$
$$m_{H_2} = \frac{8.709 \times 10^7}{192970}$$
$$\boxed{m_{H_2} = 451.3\,kg/day}$$

**(ii) Specific Energy Consumption (2 marks):**
$$E_{specific} = \frac{nF \cdot V_{cell}}{M_{H_2} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^6} = 26.59 \times V_{cell}$$
$$E_{specific} = 26.59 \times 1.95 = 51.85\,kWh/kg$$
$$\boxed{E_{specific} = 51.85\,kWh/kg\,H_2}$$

**(iii) LHV Efficiency (2 marks):**
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{E_{specific}} = \frac{33.33}{51.85}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{LHV} = 64.3\%}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of reaction equations, explain the **Sulfur-Iodine (S-I) thermochemical cycle** for hydrogen production. Discuss its efficiency limits and the source of irreversibilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermochemical cycle operates between a heat source at **900 °C (1173 K)** and a sink at **30 °C (303 K)**. Calculate:
- (i) The **Carnot (ideal) efficiency** of conversion of heat to hydrogen LHV. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The **minimum heat input** (Q_in) per kg of H₂ produced, in kWh/kg. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) If the actual thermal-to-H₂ efficiency is **42%**, compute the **actual Q_in** per kg H₂. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) S-I Cycle — Full Answer (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The Sulfur-Iodine cycle (also called Ispra Mark-6 / General Atomics cycle) is a closed-loop thermochemical process that splits water using only heat (and no net electricity). It is the most studied thermochemical cycle for coupling with high-temperature nuclear reactors or concentrated solar thermal.

2. **Three Reaction Steps (3 marks):**

   **Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (exothermic, ~120 °C):**
   $$SO_2 + I_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 + 2HI$$
   *(Sulfur dioxide reacts with iodine and water to form sulfuric acid and hydrogen iodide.)*

   **Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (highly endothermic, ~850 °C):**
   $$H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$
   *(Sulfuric acid is decomposed at high temperature, releasing oxygen and regenerating SO₂.)*

   **Step 3 — Hydrogen Iodide Decomposition (endothermic, ~450 °C):**
   $$2HI \rightarrow I_2 + H_2$$
   *(Hydrogen iodide is decomposed to produce hydrogen gas and regenerate iodine.)*

   **Net Reaction:**
   $$H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$$
   All intermediate species (SO₂, I₂, H₂SO₄, HI) are fully recycled.

3. **Efficiency Limits (1.5 marks):** The maximum theoretical efficiency is bounded by the **Carnot efficiency** between the highest process temperature (~1173 K) and ambient (~300 K):
   $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{300}{1173} = 74.4\%$$
   Practical demonstrated efficiency in pilot plants is **40–52%**, with the gap to Carnot due to heat recovery, separation work, and kinetic losses.

4. **Sources of Irreversibility (1.5 marks):**
   - **Heat recovery losses** in product coolers and preheaters.
   - **Separation work** — distillation of H₂SO₄/H₂O and HI/I₂/H₂O mixtures.
   - **Kinetic and equilibrium limitations** in acid decomposition at feasible temperatures.
   - **Material degradation** at 850 °C requiring exotic alloys.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T_H = 1173$ K, $T_C = 303$ K, $LHV_{H_2} = 33.33$ kWh/kg, $\eta_{actual} = 0.42$.

**(i) Carnot Efficiency (3 marks):**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{303}{1173}$$
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.2583$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 0.7417 = 74.17\%}$$

**(ii) Minimum Heat Input per kg H₂ (2 marks):**
$$Q_{in,\,ideal} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{\eta_{Carnot}} = \frac{33.33}{0.7417}$$
$$\boxed{Q_{in,\,ideal} = 44.94\,kWh/kg\,H_2}$$

**(iii) Actual Heat Input at 42% Efficiency (2 marks):**
$$Q_{in,\,actual} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{\eta_{actual}} = \frac{33.33}{0.42}$$
$$\boxed{Q_{in,\,actual} = 79.36\,kWh/kg\,H_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering observation:** The actual heat input is **~76% higher** than the Carnot ideal, reflecting the practical irreversibility penalty. This is why thermochemical cycles need very cheap, abundant high-temperature heat (e.g., from nuclear or solar concentrators) to be economical.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Hydrogen Production Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_{rev}$ (1.229 V) with operating voltage.** Students write "operating voltage is 1.229 V" — this loses 1–2 marks. Operating voltage is always higher (1.8–2.4 V) due to overpotentials.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Faraday's Law calculations.** Always show $M$ in g/mol, $I$ in A, $t$ in s, $m$ in g, and convert to kg/day explicitly. Marks are reserved for the unit-conversion line.
>
> 3. **Writing the S-I cycle as a "two-step" process.** It is **three reactions** (Bunsen, H₂SO₄ decomposition, HI decomposition). Missing even one reaction costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing LHV and HHV.** AEM/PEM efficiencies are conventionally reported on **LHV** (33.33 kWh/kg). If the question does not specify, default to LHV but state your assumption.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Carnot upper bound in thermochemical questions.** Always state $\eta_{actual} \leq \eta_{Carnot}$ and calculate the Carnot value to show you understand the thermodynamic limit.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to recycle I₂ and SO₂ in the S-I block diagram.** Arrows must show the loop closure — full marks require the recycling arrows.
>
> 7. **Writing the S-I Bunsen reaction with wrong stoichiometry.** It is $SO_2 + I_2 + 2H_2O$ (note: 2 H₂O, not 1). Examiners check the coefficients.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Hydrogen Production Methods**

- **Hydrogen is an *energy carrier*, not a primary energy source** — must be produced from water or hydrocarbons.
- **Net reaction of water electrolysis:** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$
- **Reversible voltage @ STP:** $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V
- **Thermoneutral voltage @ STP:** $E_{tn} = 1.481$ V (HHV basis)
- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ C/mol
- **LHV of H₂:** 33.33 kWh/kg | **HHV of H₂:** 39.39 kWh/kg
- **Faraday's law:** $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$
- **Specific energy formula:** $E_{specific} = 26.59 \times V_{cell}$ kWh/kg H₂
- **Electrolyzer LHV efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{33.33}{E_{specific}}$
- **Operating voltage > 1.229 V** due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.

- **Alkaline Electrolyzer:** KOH electrolyte, 60–90 °C, Ni electrodes, efficiency 60–70%, mature, **poor dynamic response**.
- **PEM Electrolyzer:** Nafion membrane, 50–80 °C, Pt + IrO₂ catalysts, efficiency 70–82%, **excellent dynamic response** (best for renewables).
- **SOEC Electrolyzer:** YSZ ceramic electrolyte, 700–900 °C, efficiency up to 85% (HHV > 95%), uses heat + electricity.

- **Thermochemical cycles** use **heat (not electricity)** to split water; intermediates are recycled.
- **S-I Cycle:** three reactions (Bunsen, H₂SO₄ decomposition, HI decomposition); peak T ~ 850 °C; efficiency 40–52%.
- **Cu-Cl Cycle:** hybrid thermochemical-electrochemical; peak T ~ 550 °C; more deployable.
- **Carnot efficiency upper bound:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$
- **Net reaction of any thermochemical cycle:** $H_2O \rightarrow H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2$ (same as electrolysis).

- **Color codes for H₂ applications:** Gray (fossil), Blue (fossil + CCS), Green (renewable electricity), Pink (nuclear), Yellow (grid-mix).
- **Green hydrogen = electrolyzer + renewable electricity** — the most studied KTU-relevant combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Transportation: Compressed Gas, Liquid Hydrogen, and Metal Hydrides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storage and Transportation: Compressed Gas, Liquid Hydrogen, and Metal Hydrides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrogen Storage and Transportation** refers to the engineered set of processes, vessels, and logistics required to safely confine, preserve, and deliver molecular hydrogen (H₂) from its point of production (electrolysis, steam methane reforming, biomass gasification) to its end-use application (fuel cells, ICE vehicles, industrial reactors). Because hydrogen has an extremely low ambient density (**0.0899 kg/m³** at STP), it must be physically compressed, liquefied, or chemically bound to be transported economically. The three principal methods mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Compressed Gas Hydrogen (CGH₂)** – gas stored at high pressure (350 – 700 bar) in carbon-fiber composite vessels.
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** – gas cryogenically cooled below its critical temperature of **−252.87 °C (20.27 K)**.
3. **Metal Hydride Storage (MH)** – hydrogen absorbed into the interstitial lattice of selected metals/alloys via reversible chemisorption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Board explicitly tests the *gravimetric* (mass) and *volumetric* (volume) energy densities of each storage mode, plus the thermodynamic work required to PACK hydrogen into that form. Always quote both densities when answering any 14-mark question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of hydrogen molecules as **hyperactive, ultra-lightweight children** at a birthday party:
- **Compressed Gas (CGH₂):** You stuff them into a small, very strong play-pen and pressurize the room (like a scuba tank). They bounce around furiously, but the pen must withstand enormous force.
- **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** You cool the room to almost absolute zero (−253 °C) until the children "fall asleep" and stack neatly like a liquid. Very compact, but the refrigerator must run continuously to keep them asleep, or they "wake up" and escape (boil-off).
- **Metal Hydrides (MH):** You give each child a magnetized suit and let them stick to iron filing walls inside a special box. When you heat the box, the suits lose their grip and release the children on demand. Safe and dense, but the box is heavy because of all the iron.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Anchors to Memorize:**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂: **120 MJ/kg**
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂: **142 MJ/kg**
> - 1 kg H₂ ≈ **11.12 Nm³** at STP
> - Density of liquid H₂: **70.8 kg/m³**
> - Critical point: T_c = **20.27 K**, P_c = **12.93 bar**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Compressed Gas Hydrogen (CGH₂) — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Compression Stage:** A multi-stage reciprocating or diaphragm compressor pressurizes H₂ from atmospheric pressure to the storage pressure (typically 350 bar for older vehicles, **700 bar** for modern Type IV tanks).
- **Step 2 — Filling Cascade:** The cylinder is filled in stages, with intermediate cooling between stages to avoid adiabatic heat build-up that would reduce the mass of H₂ actually stored (the "mass lost to heating" effect).
- **Step 3 — Vessel Classification:** Four pressure-vessel categories exist, distinguished by the material that bears the structural load.
- **Why this matters:** Gravimetric density is limited by tank mass; volumetric density is limited by the 700-bar ceiling of composite materials.

**Vessel Classification (KTU High-Yield):**

| Type | Material Bearing Load | Typical Use | Weight Penalty |
|------|----------------------|-------------|----------------|
| Type I | All-metal (steel/aluminium) | Stationary bulk storage | Very heavy |
| Type II | Metal liner + hoop-wound composite | Bulk transport trailers | Heavy |
| Type III | Metal liner + fully wrapped composite | Older bus/truck fleets | Moderate |
| Type IV | Polymer liner + fully wrapped carbon-fiber | Modern FCEV (Mirai, Nexo) | **Lightest (DOE target)** |

### 2.2 Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂) — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Liquefaction:** H₂ is first ortho-para converted (catalyst, ~90% conversion releases ~527 kJ/kg as heat) and then cooled through the Joule–Thomson expansion cycle (Linde-Hampson) or the more efficient Claude cycle.
- **Step 2 — Cryogenic Storage:** Held in double-walled, vacuum-insulated (Dewar) spherical or cylindrical tanks. Boil-off gas (BOG) is the principal loss.
- **Step 3 — Handling:** LH₂ is dispensed by pressurizing the inner tank with a small amount of boiled-off gas.

> [!WARNING]
> The ortho-para conversion is exothermic. **Normal hydrogen (n-H₂)** is 75% ortho / 25% para at room temp; **equilibrium at 20 K is 100% para**. Without this conversion, the LH₂ would continuously boil off as the conversion heat is released.

### 2.3 Metal Hydride Storage — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Activation:** The metal alloy is first exposed to high-pressure H₂ at elevated temperature to "crack" the surface oxide layer (activation cycle).
- **Step 2 — Absorption (Hydriding):** H₂ dissociates on the metal surface, H atoms diffuse into the lattice, and the metal expands (typically 10–25% volumetrically) to form a hydride (e.g., LaNi₅H₆, FeTiH₂, MgH₂).
- **Step 3 — Desorption:** Heat (waste heat from a fuel cell, ~80–300 °C) is supplied; the equilibrium pressure rises, releasing H₂.
- **Why it matters:** The Pressure-Composition-Temperature (P-C-T) plateau dictates operating temperature and pressure.

**Van't Hoff Equation (Plateau Pressure vs. Temperature):**

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{-\Delta H}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

where ΔH = enthalpy of hydride formation (J/mol H₂), ΔS = entropy change, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** All equations below are KTU-board favourites. Master units and sign conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Valid For |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Isothermal compression work | $W = nRT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | n in mol, T in K | Ideal gas, slow compression |
| 2 | Adiabatic compression work | $W = \frac{nRT_1}{\gamma-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}-1\right]$ | γ = 1.4 for H₂ (diatomic) | Fast / industrial compression |
| 3 | Polytropic compression | $W = \frac{nRT_1}{n_p-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n_p-1}{n_p}}-1\right]$ | 1 < n_p < γ | Real compressors |
| 4 | Real-gas compressibility | $Z = \frac{PV}{nRT}$ | Z ≈ 1.45 for H₂ at 700 bar, 25°C | High-pressure filling |
| 5 | Gravimetric density | $\rho_g = \frac{m_{H_2}}{m_{H_2}+m_{tank}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Compare systems by weight |
| 6 | Volumetric density | $\rho_v = \frac{m_{H_2}}{V_{tank}}$ | kg H₂/m³ | Compare systems by space |
| 7 | Liquefaction work (ideal) | $W_L^{ideal} = T_1 \Delta S - \Delta H$ | Min ~3.9 kWh/kg | Reversible Carnot limit |
| 8 | Real liquefaction work | $W_L^{real} \approx 10-15\,\text{kWh/kg}$ | ≈ 30-40% of LHV | Industrial plants |
| 9 | Boil-off rate (BOG) | $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \frac{Q_{heat}}{L_{vap}}$ | L_vap ≈ 446 kJ/kg | Dewar losses |
| 10 | Van't Hoff (hydride) | $\ln P_{eq} = \frac{-\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$ | ΔH in J/mol | P-C-T plateau |
| 11 | Energy in tank | $E = m_{H_2} \times LHV$ | LHV = 120 MJ/kg | Useful work available |
| 12 | Volume at STP from LH₂ | $V_{STP} = m_{H_2}/\rho_{LH_2} \times 11.12$ | m³ STP | Transportation accounting |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CGH₂ at 700 bar** is the **de-facto automotive standard** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) because of fast refuelling and mature pressure-vessel manufacturing.
- **LH₂** dominates **long-distance and bulk transport** (truck trailers, ships, rockets — SpaceX Starship uses subcooled LH₂/LOX).
- **Metal hydrides** are favoured for **stationary backup power** and **portable/military applications** where safety and high volumetric density outweigh weight penalty (e.g., LaNi₅ for submarines, FeTi for fuel-cell forklifts).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a 700-bar Type IV Tank

**Problem Statement:** A Type IV composite tank has an internal volume of 122 L and is filled with H₂ at 700 bar, 25 °C. Using the real-gas compressibility factor Z, calculate (a) the mass of hydrogen stored, (b) the energy content, and (c) the equivalent volume at STP.

**Given:**
- V = 122 L = 0.122 m³
- P = 700 bar = 70 MPa = 7.0 × 10⁷ Pa
- T = 298.15 K
- Z ≈ 1.45 for H₂ at 700 bar, 25 °C (from NIST REFPROP)
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- M_H₂ = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol
- LHV = 120 MJ/kg

**Step (a) — Mass of hydrogen:**

Starting from the real-gas equation:

$$PV = ZnRT$$

Solve for n:

$$n = \frac{PV}{ZRT}$$

Substitute the values:

$$n = \frac{(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.122)}{(1.45)(8.314)(298.15)}$$

Compute denominator:

$$(1.45)(8.314)(298.15) = 3595.3\,\text{J}$$

Compute numerator:

$$(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.122) = 8.54 \times 10^6\,\text{J}$$

Therefore:

$$n = \frac{8.54 \times 10^6}{3595.3} = 2375.4\,\text{mol}$$

Convert to mass:

$$m = n \cdot M = 2375.4 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 4.79\,\text{kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board answer: **m_H₂ ≈ 4.79 kg** stored at 700 bar in 122 L. (Toyota Mirai's actual tank stores ~5.0 kg in 122 L — perfect agreement.)

**Step (b) — Energy content:**

$$E = m \times LHV = 4.79 \times 120 = 574.4\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step (c) — Equivalent volume at STP:**

$$V_{STP} = \frac{n \times R \times T_{STP}}{P_{STP}} = \frac{2375.4 \times 8.314 \times 273.15}{101325}$$

$$V_{STP} = \frac{5.395 \times 10^6}{101325} = 53.25\,\text{m}^3$$

Cross-check via shortcut:
$$V_{STP} = m \times 11.12 = 4.79 \times 11.12 = 53.27\,\text{m}^3 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 700-bar tank compresses 53.27 m³ of gas into 0.122 m³ — a volumetric compression factor of **~437×**, but at the cost of Z = 1.45 (15% storage loss) compared to ideal-gas prediction.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Comparison of Energy Density in CG, LH and Metal Hydride

**Problem Statement:** A 100-L storage container is used in each of the three modes. Determine the stored energy in each case and the volumetric energy density.

| Storage Mode | H₂ Density (kg/m³) | Mass in 100 L | Energy (MJ) | Energy Density (MJ/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compressed (700 bar) | 40.2 | 4.02 | 482.4 | 4.82 |
| Liquid (LH₂) | 70.8 | 7.08 | 849.6 | 8.50 |
| Metal Hydride (LaNi₅H₆) | ~88 | 8.80 | 1056.0 | 10.56 |

**Sample calculation (CG, 700 bar):**

$$\rho_{CGH_2} = \frac{P \cdot M}{ZRT} = \frac{(70 \times 10^6)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.45)(8.314)(298.15)} = 39.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

Rounded to **40.2 kg/m³** (Z = 1.43 at 65 °C filling temperature).

**Energy:**
$$E = \rho \cdot V \cdot LHV = 40.2 \times 0.1 \times 120 = 482.4\,\text{MJ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At equal tank volume, **metal hydrides store 2.2× more energy than CGH₂** but weigh 5–10× more due to the metal mass. **LH₂ offers the best compromise** for transport: high density and no exotic metallurgy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Compression Power for a Refuelling Station

**Problem Statement:** A 700-bar refuelling station must compress H₂ from 20 bar (delivery trailer) to 700 bar at a flow rate of 5 kg/h. Estimate the ideal adiabatic compression power.

**Given:**
- P₁ = 20 bar, P₂ = 700 bar
- Mass flow: ṁ = 5 kg/h = 5/3600 kg/s = 1.389 × 10⁻³ kg/s
- T₁ = 298 K, γ = 1.41 (H₂)
- M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol

**Molar flow rate:**

$$\dot{n} = \frac{\dot{m}}{M} = \frac{1.389 \times 10^{-3}}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.689\,\text{mol/s}$$

**Adiabatic work per mole:**

$$W = \frac{RT_1}{\gamma-1}\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}-1\right]$$

Compute pressure ratio:
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{700}{20} = 35$$

Compute exponent:
$$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.41}{1.41} = 0.2908$$

Compute bracket:
$$35^{0.2908} = e^{0.2908 \times \ln 35} = e^{0.2908 \times 3.555} = e^{1.0339} = 2.812$$

Substitute:
$$W = \frac{(8.314)(298)}{0.41}(2.812 - 1) = 6041.6 \times 1.812 = 10947\,\text{J/mol}$$

**Power required:**

$$\dot{W} = \dot{n} \cdot W = 0.689 \times 10947 = 7542\,\text{W} \approx 7.54\,\text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real multi-stage intercooled compressors, the actual shaft power is **~2.0–2.5×** this ideal value → **~15–19 kW** for the station, which is why H₂ stations have a 50–100 kW electrical service.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Van't Hoff Analysis for FeTi Hydride

**Problem Statement:** For the FeTi-H₂ system, ΔH = −28.1 kJ/mol H₂ and ΔS = −104.6 J/(mol·K). Determine the equilibrium plateau pressure at 25 °C.

**Given:**
- ΔH = −28,100 J/mol
- ΔS = −104.6 J/(mol·K)
- T = 298.15 K
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

**Van't Hoff Equation:**

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{-\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

Compute the enthalpy term:
$$\frac{-\Delta H}{RT} = \frac{-(-28100)}{(8.314)(298.15)} = \frac{28100}{2478.8} = 11.336$$

Compute the entropy term:
$$\frac{\Delta S}{R} = \frac{-104.6}{8.314} = -12.582$$

Sum:
$$\ln P_{eq} = 11.336 - 12.582 = -1.246$$

Exponentiate (P in atm):
$$P_{eq} = e^{-1.246} = 0.288\,\text{atm} = 2.92 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa} = 0.292\,\text{bar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** At 25 °C, FeTi releases H₂ at only 0.29 bar (gently), making it ideal for **near-ambient PEM fuel-cell applications**. To release at 1 bar, simply heat to ~31 °C.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Storage Methods — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydrogen Source\nElectrolysis / SMR] --> B{Storage Mode Selection}
    B -- High pressure gas --> C[CGH2 System]
    B -- Cryogenic liquid --> D[LH2 System]
    B -- Chemical absorption --> E[Metal Hydride System]
    C --> C1[Compressor 1 to 350 bar]
    C1 --> C2[Compressor 2 to 700 bar]
    C2 --> C3[Type IV Composite Tank]
    C3 --> C4[Dispense via PRD]
    D --> D1[Ortho-Para Converter]
    D1 --> D2[Claude / Linde Liquefier]
    D2 --> D3[Vacuum Insulated Dewar]
    D3 --> D4[Boil-off Recovery Loop]
    E --> E1[Alloy Reactor Bed]
    E1 -- Heat in --> E2[Desorption H2 out]
    E1 -- Pressure in --> E3[Adsorption exotherm]
    E2 --> F[End Use: PEMFC / ICE / Burner]
    E3 --> E1
    C4 --> F
    D4 --> F
```

### 4.2 P-C-T Diagram and Hydriding/Dehydriding Cycle (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Vacuum Start Point] --> S2[Activate Alloy\nHigh P and High T]
    S2 --> S3{PC Isotherm}
    S3 -- T1 Low --> S4[Low Plateau P\nLow H content]
    S3 -- T2 Mid --> S5[Mid Plateau P\nMid H content]
    S3 -- T3 High --> S6[High Plateau P\nHigh H content]
    S5 -- Heat to T3 --> S7[Desorption Branch\nH2 released]
    S5 -- Cool to T1 --> S8[Adsorption Branch\nH2 absorbed]
    S7 --> S9[Storage Bed Empty]
    S8 --> S10[Storage Bed Full]
    S9 --> S11[Refill via Compressed H2]
    S10 --> S12[Discharge to Fuel Cell]
    S11 --> S3
    S12 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Liquid Hydrogen Plant — Process Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Compressor 20 bar] --> P2[Ortho-Para Catalyst Bed\nReleases 527 kJ/kg]
    P2 --> P3[JT First Stage Cooler]
    P3 --> P4[Inter-stage Heat Exchanger]
    P4 --> P5[JT Expansion Valve]
    P5 --> P6[Liquid Collection 20 K]
    P6 --> P7[Vacuum Insulated Storage Tank]
    P7 --> P8[Boil-off Gas Recovery Compressor]
    P8 --> P1
    P7 --> P9[Road Tanker or Pipeline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above intentionally abstract physical layouts into **functional process architecture** — exactly the level of schematic the KTU 2024 examiner expects in the 14-mark "explain with neat block diagram" questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the four types of compressed hydrogen storage vessels and state the material composition of each. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Type I:** All-metal (steel or aluminium) — heavy, used for stationary bulk storage.
- **Type II:** Metal liner hoop-wrapped with fibre-composite — moderate weight.
- **Type III:** Metal liner fully wrapped with composite — used in older vehicle fleets.
- **Type IV:** Polymer (HDPE) liner fully wrapped with carbon-fibre composite — **lightest**, used in modern FCEVs.

(½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks; concluding remark that Type IV is the KTU/DOE 2025 target = 1 mark.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is ortho-para hydrogen conversion necessary before liquefaction? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Normal hydrogen at room temperature is 75% ortho / 25% para. At 20 K the equilibrium is ~100% para. The conversion from ortho to para is **exothermic**, releasing ~527 kJ/kg even at 20 K. Without catalytic conversion (Fe(OH)₃ or Cr₂O₃ gel catalyst in the liquefier), this heat would be liberated *inside* the LH₂ storage tank, causing continuous boil-off. Therefore ortho-para conversion is a **mandatory pre-cooling step** in any LH₂ plant. (1 mark each for ortho-para definition, exothermicity, and boil-off consequence.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a Pressure-Composition-Temperature (P-C-T) diagram, explain the operation of a metal-hydride hydrogen storage system. Derive the Van't Hoff equation and calculate the plateau pressure of LaNi₅-H₂ at 50 °C given ΔH = −30.8 kJ/mol and ΔS = −108 J/(mol·K).

**(b) [7 marks]** A 700-bar Type IV composite tank of volume 100 L is filled with hydrogen. Using Z = 1.45, calculate the mass of hydrogen stored, its energy content at LHV, and the equivalent volume at STP. Comment on the volumetric compression ratio.

---

**Model Solution to Q1(a):**

**P-C-T Description (3 marks):**
- The P-C-T (Pressure-Composition-Temperature) diagram plots equilibrium pressure (log scale) versus hydrogen-to-metal atomic ratio (H/M) at a fixed temperature.
- It exhibits three regions: **(i) α-phase** (solid solution of H in metal at low H/M), **(ii) plateau region** (α + β coexistence, where the pressure remains constant as H is absorbed), and **(iii) β-phase** (saturated metal hydride).
- The flat plateau is the engineering operating region — long plateaus indicate high usable storage capacity.
- Increasing T shifts the plateau to higher P (Van't Hoff behaviour).
- Hysteresis: absorption plateau is at higher P than desorption plateau (energy loss).

**Van't Hoff Derivation (2 marks):**
At equilibrium, the Gibbs free energy of hydride formation is zero:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0$$

For an ideal gas of H₂ at standard pressure P₀:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S + RT \ln \frac{P_{eq}}{P_0} = 0$$

Solving for the equilibrium pressure ratio:

$$\ln \frac{P_{eq}}{P_0} = -\frac{\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

Choosing P₀ = 1 atm:

$$\ln P_{eq} = \frac{-\Delta H}{RT} + \frac{\Delta S}{R}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
- T = 50 °C = 323.15 K
- ΔH = −30,800 J/mol; ΔS = −108 J/(mol·K)
- R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)

$$\frac{-\Delta H}{RT} = \frac{30800}{(8.314)(323.15)} = \frac{30800}{2686.7} = 11.464$$

$$\frac{\Delta S}{R} = \frac{-108}{8.314} = -12.990$$

$$\ln P_{eq} = 11.464 - 12.990 = -1.526$$

$$P_{eq} = e^{-1.526} = 0.217\,\text{atm} = 0.220\,\text{bar} \approx 22\,\text{kPa}$$

**Result:** At 50 °C, LaNi₅H₆ releases hydrogen at ~0.22 bar — confirming its applicability for low-temperature fuel cells.

---

**Model Solution to Q1(b):**

**Given:** V = 0.1 m³, P = 700 bar = 7.0 × 10⁷ Pa, T = 298 K, Z = 1.45, M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol, LHV = 120 MJ/kg.

**Mass of H₂ (3 marks):**

$$n = \frac{PV}{ZRT} = \frac{(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.1)}{(1.45)(8.314)(298)} = \frac{7.0 \times 10^6}{3591.6} = 1949\,\text{mol}$$

$$m = nM = 1949 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3} = 3.93\,\text{kg}$$

**Energy content (2 marks):**

$$E = m \times LHV = 3.93 \times 120 = 471.6\,\text{MJ}$$

**Equivalent volume at STP (1 mark):**

$$V_{STP} = m \times 11.12 = 3.93 \times 11.12 = 43.7\,\text{m}^3$$

**Compression ratio (1 mark):**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{V_{STP}}{V_{tank}} = \frac{43.7}{0.1} = 437$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls (Q1b):**
> 1. Forgetting Z = 1.45 → gives 5.7 kg instead of 3.93 kg (board cuts 2 marks).
> 2. Using P = 700 psi (a unit confusion) instead of 700 bar.
> 3. Reporting energy in kJ instead of MJ — units carry ½ mark in KTU.
> 4. Not stating the "compression ratio" explicitly — the 1-mark interpretive comment is mandatory.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4, Choice 2]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare Compressed Gas (CGH₂), Liquid (LH₂), and Metal Hydride hydrogen storage on the basis of gravimetric density, volumetric density, energy required for storage, safety, and typical application.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the Claude cycle for hydrogen liquefaction. Compute the theoretical minimum work of liquefaction starting from 25 °C and comment on why real plants need 10–15 kWh/kg.

---

**Model Solution to Q2(a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | CGH₂ (700 bar) | LH₂ (−253 °C) | Metal Hydride (LaNi₅) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gravimetric density** | 5–7 % | 6–8 % (system) | 1–2 % (system) |
| **Volumetric density** | 30–40 kg/m³ | 70.8 kg/m³ | 80–100 kg/m³ |
| **Energy to store** | 2–3 kWh/kg | 10–15 kWh/kg | ~0.5 kWh/kg (heat) |
| **Safety** | High-pressure hazard | Cryogenic + flammability | Low pressure, intrinsically safe |
| **Boil-off / loss** | Minimal (leakage) | 0.1–0.3 %/day BOG | None if sealed |
| **Refuelling time** | 3–5 min | 10–20 min (cool-down) | 30–60 min (heat/cool) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (cryogenic plant) | High (alloy cost) |
| **Typical application** | FCEV cars, buses | Trucks, ships, rockets | Stationary backup, submarines |

(Minimum 5 rows for full 7 marks. Each row carries equal weight; "best" choice depends on application — board wants explicit "which is best for what" comment.)

---

**Model Solution to Q2(b) — Claude Cycle (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Compressor → After-cooler → First expansion engine (does work, recovers ~80% as shaft power) → Second heat exchanger → Second expansion engine (or JT valve) → Liquid collection → Flash-gas recycle to compressor suction.

**Description (2 marks):**
The Claude cycle is a modification of the Linde-Hampson cycle that uses **expansion engines** (instead of only a JT valve) to extract work from the gas, dramatically improving efficiency from ~10% (Linde) to ~30–35% (Claude). The cold expanded gas pre-cools the incoming high-pressure gas through a multi-stream heat exchanger.

**Theoretical Minimum Work (2 marks):**
From 25 °C (298.15 K) to 20.27 K, treating H₂ as an ideal gas with average c_p ≈ 14.3 kJ/(kg·K):

$$W_{min} = T_1 \Delta S_{H_2} - \Delta H$$

For H₂: ΔS ≈ −46.5 kJ/(kg·K) from 298 K to 20 K.

$$W_{min} = 298.15 \times (-46.5) - 0 = -13.86\,\text{MJ/kg} = -3.85\,\text{kWh/kg}$$

The negative sign indicates work must be supplied to the system. Real liquefiers need 10–15 kWh/kg (3–4× theoretical) due to:
- Irreversibilities in compression and heat exchange.
- Incomplete expansion-engine isentropic efficiency (~75–80%).
- Ortho-para conversion heat removal (527 kJ/kg).
- Heat leaks into the cryogenic sections (despite multilayer insulation).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for the Full Question Set:**
> 1. **Failing to convert units** (bar ↔ Pa, °C ↔ K). KTU board deducts 1 mark per incorrect unit pair.
> 2. **Skipping the P-C-T sketch** in metal-hydride answers — examiners allot a *minimum* 2 marks just for the diagram. Even a hand-drawn approximation earns those marks.
> 3. **Forgetting ortho-para conversion** in LH₂ derivation. Board considers this a *core concept* for the 2024 scheme and a missing mention costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing LHV and HHV** — always state which one you are using; default is LHV = 120 MJ/kg.
> 5. **Omitting the heat-removal step** in metal-hydride desorption — students often write the reaction but forget to mention that waste heat from the fuel cell (~60–80 °C) is the *driving input*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **CGH₂ at 700 bar + Type IV tank** = automotive standard; mass stored in 100 L ≈ 3.9–4.0 kg (use Z = 1.45).
- **LH₂ storage is dominated by boil-off losses** (~0.1–0.3 %/day); the *ortho-para conversion* is mandatory to prevent runaway boil-off.
- **Liquefaction consumes 30–40 % of the LHV** of the stored H₂ — a critical efficiency penalty for LH₂.
- **Metal hydride storage trades gravimetric density for safety**; Van't Hoff equation governs the operating P-T window.
- **Three famous alloys to memorize:** **FeTi** (low T, ~0.3 bar at 25 °C), **LaNi₅** (low T, ~0.2 bar at 25 °C), **MgH₂** (high T, requires 300 °C to release).
- **Compression work isothermal < adiabatic**; the isothermal value is the *theoretical minimum* and is approached in slow / intercooled multi-stage compressors.
- **Pipeline transport** of H₂ is the cheapest over long distances (~0.1–0.3 $/kg per 1000 km), comparable to natural gas but at lower energy density per m³.
- **Tube trailers** (350 bar, ~400 kg H₂ per trailer) are the workhorse for short-haul (<300 km) gaseous delivery.
- **Cryo-compressed (CcH₂)** at 350 bar + LH₂ combines both storage modes; this is a *bonus KTU point* if mentioned.
- **Always use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol** unless the question says otherwise.
- **DOE 2025 targets (KTU 2024 syllabus is aligned):** 7.5 % gravimetric, 70 g/L volumetric for on-board light-duty FCEV.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Infrastructure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety and Infrastructure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen Safety – Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Safety** refers to the comprehensive set of engineering practices, codes, detection systems, and operational protocols designed to manage the unique hazards associated with hydrogen production, storage, transport, and utilization. Because hydrogen is colourless, odourless, highly flammable across a wide concentration range, and prone to material embrittlement, safety engineering becomes a non-negotiable pillar of the entire hydrogen economy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** Hydrogen safety encompasses leak detection, material compatibility, explosion prevention, ventilation, flame detection, pressure relief, and compliance with standards such as **NFPA 2**, **ISO 19880**, and **IEC 60079**.

## 1.2 Hydrogen Infrastructure – Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Infrastructure** is the integrated network of physical assets, including production plants (electrolyzers, reformers), storage facilities (tanks, caverns, hydrides), transportation networks (pipelines, tube trailers, LH₂ tankers), and end-use dispensing stations, required to deliver hydrogen from its source to the point of application in a reliable, economic, and safe manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 4 places equal weight on safety codes and on infrastructure components (electrolyzers, compressors, storage vessels, and dispensing units). Both are high-yield areas for ESE questions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy – The "Sneaky Gas" Problem:**
Imagine a helium balloon released in a room. Helium rises quickly and escapes through cracks. Hydrogen behaves similarly, except it is **highly flammable** and burns with an almost invisible flame. Now, imagine scaling this up to a city-scale gas network: tiny leaks can travel long distances, accumulate in ceilings or enclosed spaces, and ignite from the tiniest spark (static electricity from a sweater can ignite it!). 

Hydrogen safety engineering is therefore like **building a "smart fire alarm system" for an invisible, fast-moving gas** — combining sensors, ventilation, leak-resistant joints, and automatic shutoff valves.

**Analogy – The Hydrogen Highway:**
Think of today's CNG (Compressed Natural Gas) refueling stations. Hydrogen infrastructure is conceptually identical, but with stricter materials (no carbon steel), cryogenic tanks for liquid hydrogen (-253 °C), and compressors capable of **700 bar** pressure.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Value (Bolded) |
|---|---|
| Flammability range in air | **4 – 75 %** (by volume) |
| Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) | **4 %** |
| Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) | **75 %** |
| Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) | **0.02 mJ** |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **500 °C** |
| Detonation range in air | **18 – 59 %** |
| Diffusion coefficient in air | **0.61 cm²/s** (≈ 4× methane) |
| Buoyancy relative to air | **14× lighter** (ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³) |
| Flame temperature in air | **2,045 °C** |
| Flame visibility | Nearly invisible (pale blue) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Hydrogen Demands Special Safety Engineering

Hydrogen's atomic simplicity creates four safety challenges unseen in hydrocarbons:

1. **Wide Flammability Range (4–75 %):** Almost any leak can form an explosive mixture with air, unlike gasoline (1.4–7.6 %).
2. **Low Minimum Ignition Energy (0.02 mJ):** One-tenth of methane's MIE; even static discharge can ignite it.
3. **High Diffusivity:** Leaks disperse rapidly, reducing ground-level accumulation but creating ceiling-level pockets.
4. **Material Embrittlement:** Hydrogen atoms diffuse into metals, causing Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE) — loss of ductility and fracture toughness in steels and alloys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because hydrogen rises rapidly, sensors should be mounted at the **ceiling level**, the opposite of propane detectors, which sit near the floor.

## 2.2 Hydrogen Leak Detection Methods

| Detection Principle | Sensor Type | Response Time | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catalytic bead | Pellistor | 5–30 s | Industrial plants |
| Electrochemical | Amperometric | 10–60 s | Stationary storage |
| Thermal conductivity | TCD | <5 s | Pipelines |
| Metal-oxide semiconductor (MOS) | Resistive | 1–10 s | Refueling stations |
| Acoustic Emission | AE | <1 s | High-pressure vessels |
| Optical (TDLAS) | Laser spectroscopy | <1 s | Process monitoring |

## 2.3 Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE) – Mechanism

Hydrogen atoms enter the metal lattice through:
- **Cathodic reactions** during electroplating or corrosion
- **High-pressure molecular dissociation** at crack tips
- **High-temperature exposure** (sour service)

Once inside, they reduce the bond strength at grain boundaries, leading to:
- **Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (HIC)**
- **Stress-Oriented Hydrogen-Induced Cracking (SOHIC)**
- **Sulfide Stress Cracking (SSC)** — relevant in petrochemical service

**Mitigation strategies:**
- Use austenitic stainless steels (SS 304L, 316L) instead of carbon steel
- Use **composite overwrapped pressure vessels (COPV)** for storage
- Apply **nickel, copper, or aluminium barriers** on internal surfaces
- Post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) to relieve residual stress

## 2.4 Hydrogen Infrastructure – Four Pillars

### Pillar 1: Production
- **Centralized:** Large-scale Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) or water electrolysis near renewable sources
- **Distributed:** On-site electrolyzers at refueling stations
- **By-product:** Chlor-alkali, coke-oven gas recovery

### Pillar 2: Storage
- **Compressed Gas:** 350 bar (Type III) and **700 bar (Type IV)** for vehicles
- **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Cryogenic storage at -253 °C
- **Underground:** Salt caverns, depleted gas fields
- **Solid-State:** Metal hydrides (LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂), Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)

### Pillar 3: Transportation
- **Pipelines:** Existing natural gas pipelines (blended) or dedicated H₂ lines
- **Tube Trailers:** 200–500 bar compressed H₂
- **Cryogenic Tankers:** Liquid H₂ ships and trucks
- **LOHC Carriers:** Ambient temperature, oil-based

### Pillar 4: Dispensing
- Refueling stations with cascade storage, compressors, pre-cooling, and dispensers
- Typical SAE J2601 protocol for fast fills (3–5 minutes for 700 bar)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) of H₂ | $\text{LFL} = 4\,\%$ | vol % | KTU frequently tests this value |
| Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) of H₂ | $\text{UFL} = 75\,\%$ | vol % |  |
| Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE) | $\text{MIE} = 0.02\,\text{mJ}$ | mJ | Compare: CH₄ = 0.29 mJ |
| Compressed H₂ storage energy | $E = \dfrac{P V}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right]$ | J | γ = 1.41 (diatomic gas) |
| Energy in LH₂ (latent + sensible) | $Q = m \cdot L + m \cdot c_p \Delta T$ | J | L = 445 kJ/kg (vaporization) |
| Hydrogen Embrittlement Index | $\text{HEI} = \dfrac{\sigma_{th}}{\sigma_{UTS}}$ | dimensionless | < 0.5 → susceptible |
| Compressor work (polytropic) | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_1\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$ | J/kg | n ≈ 1.35 for H₂ |
| Density of H₂ at STP | $\rho_{H_2} = 0.0899\,\text{kg/m}^3$ | kg/m³ | 14× lighter than air |
| Leakage rate (ideal orifice) | $\dot{m} = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{RT_1(\gamma-1)}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | kg/s | Subsonic / choked flow |
| Refueling mass flow | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho A v}{1}$ | kg/s | Used in J2601 protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Hydrogen safety and infrastructure engineers are critical for the **Hydrogen Council**, **Green H₂ Valley** projects in Kerala, India's **National Green Hydrogen Mission (₹19,744 crore allocation)**, and ISO/SAE certified refueling networks worldwide.

## 2.6 Safety Codes and Standards (High-Yield)

| Code | Scope |
|---|---|
| **NFPA 2** | Hydrogen Technologies Code (USA) |
| **ISO 19880-1** | Gaseous hydrogen fuelling stations — general requirements |
| **ISO 14687** | Hydrogen fuel quality specification |
| **SAE J2601** | Fueling protocols for light-duty vehicles |
| **IEC 60079** | Explosive atmospheres — equipment classification |
| **CGA G-5.5** | Standard for bulk hydrogen supply systems |
| **DOT 49 CFR** | Transportation of hydrogen in USA |
| **PESO (India)** | Petroleum and Explosives Safety Organisation — India |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compressed Hydrogen Energy Stored in a Cylinder

A Type IV hydrogen cylinder stores gas at **P₂ = 700 bar**, with a residual pressure of **P₀ = 1 bar** when emptied. The volume is **V = 0.122 m³** (typical 700 bar tank).

**Goal:** Compute the mass of usable hydrogen and its energy content.

### Step 1 — Apply the Real Gas Equation
For high-pressure hydrogen, we use the compressibility factor **Z**:
$$m = \frac{PV}{ZRT}$$

### Step 2 — Mass at 700 bar
Using **Z ≈ 1.4** (at 700 bar, 298 K):
$$m_{\text{full}} = \frac{700 \times 10^5 \times 0.122}{1.4 \times 4.124 \times 10^3 \times 298}$$

$$m_{\text{full}} = \frac{8.54 \times 10^6}{1.72 \times 10^6} = 4.96\,\text{kg}$$

> **Logic:** Numerator converts Pa·m³ → J; denominator uses R = 4.124 kJ/kg·K (specific gas constant for H₂).

### Step 3 — Mass at 1 bar
$$m_{\text{empty}} = \frac{1 \times 10^5 \times 0.122}{1 \times 4.124 \times 10^3 \times 298}$$

$$m_{\text{empty}} = \frac{1.22 \times 10^7}{1.229 \times 10^6} = 9.93 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg}$$

### Step 4 — Usable Mass
$$\Delta m = 4.96 - 0.00993 \approx 4.95\,\text{kg}$$

### Step 5 — Energy Content
Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = **120 MJ/kg**:
$$E = m \times \text{LHV} = 4.95 \times 120 = 594\,\text{MJ}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is equivalent to **16.5 kWh**, enough to drive a Toyota Mirai ~650 km.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polytropic Compression Work for Hydrogen

A 3-stage compressor compresses H₂ from **P₁ = 20 bar** to **P₂ = 700 bar** at suction temperature **T₁ = 298 K**. Polytropic index **n = 1.35**.

**Step 1 — Write the polytropic work equation per kg:**
$$W = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure ratio:**
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{700}{20} = 35$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent:**
$$\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{0.35}{1.35} = 0.2593$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracketed term:**
$$35^{0.2593} = e^{0.2593 \times \ln 35} = e^{0.2593 \times 3.555} = e^{0.9217} = 2.514$$

**Step 5 — Substituting R = 4.124 kJ/kg·K:**
$$W = \frac{1.35}{0.35} \times 4.124 \times 298 \times (2.514 - 1)$$

$$W = 3.857 \times 4.124 \times 298 \times 1.514$$

$$W = 3.857 \times 1228.95 \times 1.514$$

$$W = 3.857 \times 1860.6 = 7{,}176\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

> **Interpretation:** For a 1 kg/s station (≈ 86.4 tonnes/day), the theoretical compressor power is **7.18 MW**. Real compressors with intercooling and 3 stages achieve ~75% isentropic efficiency, requiring **~9.6 MW input power**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Liquid Hydrogen Boil-Off Loss

A 1,000 L LH₂ tank at **T = 20 K** loses heat through insulation at a rate of **q = 50 W**.

**Step 1 — Energy required to vaporize 1 kg LH₂:**
$$L_{vap} = 445\,\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Boil-off mass rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{bo} = \frac{q}{L_{vap}} = \frac{50}{445 \times 10^3} = 1.124 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Daily boil-off mass:**
$$m_{day} = 1.124 \times 10^{-4} \times 86{,}400 = 9.71\,\text{kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage of full tank (density of LH₂ = 70.8 kg/m³):**
$$m_{tank} = 1000 \times 10^{-3} \times 70.8 = 70.8\,\text{kg}$$

$$\%\text{ boil-off} = \frac{9.71}{70.8} \times 100 = 13.7\%\,\text{per day}$$

> **Conclusion:** This is why **multi-layer insulation (MLI)** with **vacuum-jacketed dewars** is mandatory, and why boil-off gas is often **re-liquefied or vented safely**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hydrogen vs Methane vs Gasoline Safety

| Parameter | Hydrogen (H₂) | Methane (CH₄) | Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **0.0899** | 0.717 | 750 (liquid) |
| Flammability range (%) | **4 – 75** | 5 – 15 | 1.4 – 7.6 |
| MIE (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.29 | 0.24 |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | **500** | 540 | 280 |
| Flame temperature (°C) | **2,045** | 1,950 | 2,200 |
| Flame speed (m/s) | **2.65** | 0.4 | 0.4 |
| Buoyancy | Rises rapidly | Rises slowly | Sinks (vapor) |
| Energy density (MJ/kg) | 120 | 50 | 44 |
| Embrittlement risk | **High** | Low | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The wide flammability range of H₂ is a **double-edged sword**: in confined spaces, it's dangerous, but in open atmosphere, the rapid dispersion actually reduces explosion risk compared to gasoline pools.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
