<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1
**Q: List any three physical properties of hydrogen that make it a unique safety challenge compared to methane.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Wide flammability range (4–75 %)** — far broader than methane (5–15 %), meaning almost any leak in air can form an explosive mixture.
2. **Very low Minimum Ignition Energy (0.02 mJ)** — about 14× lower than methane, so even static electricity can ignite a leak.
3. **High diffusivity and buoyancy** — hydrogen rises rapidly, accumulating near ceilings and forming undetected pockets in enclosed spaces.

### Question 2
**Q: Differentiate between Type III and Type IV hydrogen storage cylinders.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Type III | Type IV |
|---|---|---|
| Liner material | Metal (Aluminium) | Polymer (HDPE) |
| Hoop wrapping | Steel wire or fibre | Carbon-fibre composite |
| Weight | Heavier | **Lightest** |
| Pressure rating | 350–700 bar | **700 bar (preferred for FCEV)** |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the four main pillars of hydrogen infrastructure. Discuss in detail the various methods of hydrogen storage with their advantages and limitations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The four pillars of hydrogen infrastructure form an integrated value chain that delivers hydrogen from production to end-use:

**Pillar 1 — Production:**
Hydrogen is produced via three main routes: (i) **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** — produces grey hydrogen, (ii) **Electrolysis of water** — produces green hydrogen when powered by renewables, and (iii) **Biomass gasification** — produces bio-hydrogen. Centralized plants near demand hubs reduce transportation cost; distributed on-site production at refueling stations avoids transport altogether.

**Pillar 2 — Storage:**
The four primary storage methods are:
- **Compressed Gas (350–700 bar):** Mature technology, used in FCEVs. Type IV tanks achieve 5–7 wt% H₂. Limitations: high compression energy (~13 % of LHV lost) and large volume.
- **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):** Cryogenic storage at -253 °C, density 70.8 kg/m³. Boil-off loss is the major limitation (~0.1–0.3 %/day with MLI).
- **Underground Storage:** Salt caverns and depleted gas fields offer massive volumes for seasonal storage; limited by geography.
- **Solid-State Storage:** Metal hydrides (MgH₂, LaNi₅H₆) and LOHC (dibenzyltoluene). Advantages: safe, low pressure. Limitations: heavy, low gravimetric density.

**Pillar 3 — Transportation:**
- **Pipelines:** Lowest cost for high volumes, but require dedicated H₂-resistant materials.
- **Tube Trailers:** 200–500 bar compressed H₂ in bundles; flexible but low capacity.
- **Cryogenic Tankers:** LH₂ ships for intercontinental trade; boil-off is a concern.
- **LOHC Carriers:** Ambient temperature, oil-based, reversible hydrogenation; emerging technology.

**Pillar 4 — Dispensing:**
Refueling stations with cascade storage, multi-stage compressors (up to 900 bar), pre-cooling units (-40 °C to -30 °C during fill to prevent overheating), and **SAE J2601** compliant dispensers. Average fill time: 3–5 minutes for 700 bar vehicles.

**(b) Discuss hydrogen safety engineering with reference to hydrogen embrittlement, leak detection, and relevant Indian and international safety codes.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hydrogen Embrittlement (HE):**
Hydrogen atoms diffuse into the metal lattice, reducing ductility and fracture toughness. Three forms are critical in engineering: **HIC** (Hydrogen-Induced Cracking), **SOHIC** (Stress-Oriented HIC), and **SSC** (Sulfide Stress Cracking) in sour service. Mitigation strategies include:
- Selection of **austenitic stainless steels (SS 304L, 316L)**, Inconel, or aluminium alloys
- **Composite overwrapped pressure vessels (COPV)** with HDPE liners
- **Internal barrier coatings** of nickel, copper, or aluminium
- **Post-Weld Heat Treatment (PWHT)** to relieve residual stresses

**Leak Detection Systems:**
- **Catalytic bead sensors** for general area monitoring
- **Electrochemical sensors** for low-ppm detection
- **MOS sensors** for fast response in refueling stations
- **Acoustic Emission (AE) sensors** for high-pressure vessel integrity
- **Tunable Diode Laser Absorption Spectroscopy (TDLAS)** for pipeline monitoring
Sensors must be mounted at **ceiling level** due to hydrogen's buoyancy.

**Safety Codes and Standards:**

| Region | Standard | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| USA | **NFPA 2** | Hydrogen Technologies Code |
| International | **ISO 19880-1** | Gaseous H₂ fuelling stations |
| International | **ISO 14687** | H₂ fuel quality |
| Automotive | **SAE J2601** | Fueling protocols |
| India | **PESO** | Petroleum & Explosives Safety Organisation |
| India | **OISD-244** | Storage and handling of hydrogen |
| Hazardous Areas | **IEC 60079** | Equipment classification |

**Key Operational Practices:**
- Adequate **ventilation** (≥ 6 air changes per hour in enclosed spaces)
- **Flame arrestors** on vent stacks
- **Pressure Relief Devices (PRDs)** with vent stacks pointing away from personnel
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** systems with redundant interlocks
- **Bonding and grounding** to prevent static-induced ignition
- Continuous **explosion-proof electrical classification** (IECEx/ATEX zones)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a hydrogen refueling station. Discuss the role of pre-cooling in fast fills.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of a Hydrogen Refueling Station** (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):

A typical **350/700 bar gaseous hydrogen refueling station** consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Low-Pressure Storage Buffer (20–45 bar):** Receives H₂ via tube trailers or pipelines.
2. **High-Pressure Compressor (2- or 3-stage):** Compresses to 450/900 bar; uses oil-free piston or diaphragm compressors.
3. **Cascade Storage Bank:** Three or more high-pressure vessels (450 bar) that hold hydrogen in pre-pressurized stages, enabling simultaneous fueling of multiple vehicles.
4. **Pre-cooling Unit:** Cools the hydrogen to **-40 °C** using a refrigeration chiller before it enters the vehicle tank. This is **mandatory** under SAE J2601 protocols.
5. **Dispenser:** Delivers H₂ to the vehicle at the correct flow rate and pressure, with mass flow meters, hose couplings, and breakaway valves.
6. **Safety Control Unit (SCU):** Centralized PLC that monitors pressure, temperature, leak sensors, and emergency shutdown interlocks.
7. **Leak Detection & Ventilation System:** Multi-point H₂ sensors, ATEX-rated exhaust fans.

**Role of Pre-Cooling:**
When a 700-bar vehicle is filled, the **adiabatic compression of residual gas** inside the tank causes temperature rise. If uncooled, the temperature can exceed **85 °C**, which:
- Exceeds the **Composite Damage Limit (CDL)** of the carbon-fibre wrap
- Violates SAE J2601 safety limits
- Risks **depressurization valve opening** at ~105 °C

**Pre-cooling calculation:** For a 5 kg fill in 3 minutes (≈ 28 g/s), the heat to be removed per second is:
$$Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T = 0.028 \times 14.3 \times 60 = 24\,\text{kW}$$

A **two-stage chiller** with a COP of 2.0 requires **~12 kW of electrical input** to provide this cooling.

**(b) A 700 bar Type IV hydrogen storage cylinder has an internal volume of 0.122 m³. If the compressibility factor Z = 1.4 at this pressure and 298 K, compute (i) the mass of hydrogen stored, (ii) the energy stored in kWh using LHV = 120 MJ/kg, and (iii) the equivalent volume of gasoline (LHV = 44 MJ/kg, density 750 kg/m³) that produces the same energy. The gas constant for H₂ is R = 4.124 kJ/kg·K.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mass of hydrogen stored** (using real gas equation):
$$m = \frac{PV}{ZRT} = \frac{700 \times 10^5 \times 0.122}{1.4 \times 4.124 \times 10^3 \times 298}$$

$$m = \frac{8.54 \times 10^6}{1.72 \times 10^6} = 4.96\,\text{kg}$$

**(ii) Energy stored in kWh:**
$$E = m \times \text{LHV} = 4.96 \times 120 = 595.2\,\text{MJ}$$

$$E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{595.2 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 165.3\,\text{kWh}$$

**(iii) Equivalent gasoline volume:**

Mass of gasoline with same energy:
$$m_{\text{gas}} = \frac{595.2}{44} = 13.53\,\text{kg}$$

Volume of gasoline:
$$V_{\text{gas}} = \frac{m_{\text{gas}}}{\rho} = \frac{13.53}{750} = 0.018\,\text{m}^3 = 18\,\text{litres}$$

> **Conclusion:** 4.96 kg of H₂ in a 122 L Type IV tank stores energy equivalent to **18 L of gasoline**, illustrating hydrogen's lower volumetric energy density.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MIE values:** Many students write 0.02 mJ as **0.02 J** — this is a 1000× error and costs full marks.
> 2. **Wrong Z-factor usage:** For 700 bar H₂, Z ≈ 1.4 (not 1.0). Assuming ideal gas loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing LHV and HHV:** KTU expects **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** unless the question specifies HHV (142 MJ/kg).
> 4. **Forgetting the residual mass:** The 1 bar residual in 0.122 m³ still contains ~10 g of H₂. Subtract this for "usable" hydrogen.
> 5. **Sensor placement:** Always state "ceiling-level" for H₂ detectors, not floor-level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety & Infrastructure**

- ☐ **Hydrogen LFL = 4 %**, **UFL = 75 %** (widest of all fuels).
- ☐ **MIE = 0.02 mJ** (≈ 14× lower than CH₄).
- ☐ Hydrogen is **14× lighter than air** → sensors at ceiling, vent stacks upward.
- ☐ **Hydrogen embrittlement** is a critical issue in carbon steel; use **SS316L, Inconel, COPV**.
- ☐ **Type IV cylinders** = polymer liner + carbon-fibre wrap; 700 bar; lightest weight.
- ☐ **Liquid H₂ storage** at -253 °C; boil-off is a constant challenge; MLI + vacuum is mandatory.
- ☐ **Pre-cooling** in refueling: cool H₂ to -40 °C to prevent tank overheating (limit 85 °C).
- ☐ **SAE J2601** protocol controls fill rates to stay within Composite Damage Limit.
- ☐ Four pillars of infrastructure: **Production → Storage → Transport → Dispensing**.
- ☐ **NFPA 2, ISO 19880-1, ISO 14687, SAE J2601, IEC 60079, PESO (India), OISD-244** are the key codes.
- ☐ **Real gas equation** is mandatory for high-pressure H₂: $m = PV/ZRT$ with Z ≈ 1.4 at 700 bar.
- ☐ **LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**; 4.96 kg in 700 bar tank = 165 kWh ≈ 18 L gasoline.
- ☐ Polytropic compressor work: $W = \frac{n}{n-1}RT_1\left[\left(P_2/P_1\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$, n ≈ 1.35.
- ☐ Hydrogen flame is **nearly invisible** — use IR/UV flame detectors, not smoke.
- ☐ **Green Hydrogen Mission (India):** ₹19,744 crore for 5 MMT annual production by 2030.
- ☐ **Adequate ventilation** (≥6 air changes/hr) is the single most important passive safety measure.
- ☐ Always **bond and ground** equipment to prevent static ignition (MIE is 0.02 mJ!).
- ☐ Hydrogen infrastructure deployment challenges: cost, technology maturity, public acceptance, regulations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cells: Working Principle and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells: Working Principle and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Fuel Cell:** A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the **chemical energy** of a continuously supplied fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an **oxidant** (commonly oxygen from air) into **direct current (DC) electrical energy**, with **water and heat** as the principal by-products. Unlike a conventional battery, the reactants are not stored internally but are fed externally, enabling continuous power generation as long as fuel is supplied.

According to the KTU 2024 OECHT837 syllabus, fuel cells are classified under **direct energy conversion devices** and are regarded as a pivotal component of the **hydrogen economy** and distributed power generation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Think of a fuel cell as a "sandwich that eats hydrogen and exhales electricity + water."**

Imagine a school science fair project:

- A **lemon battery** uses the chemical energy of citric acid + zinc/copper to make a tiny current. It is **single-use**—once the acid reacts, the lemon is "dead."
- A **fuel cell** is essentially a **continuously-fed electrochemical sandwich**: hydrogen gas flows in from one side, oxygen (from air) flows in from the other side, and a special membrane (the **electrolyte**) in the middle only allows positive ions to pass through.
- The **electrons** (the actual electricity) are forced to take the *long way around* through your external circuit (e.g., to light up an LED or charge a laptop) because they cannot pass through the electrolyte.

So while a battery is a *closed envelope of energy*, a fuel cell is an *open energy pipeline*—feed it fuel, and it keeps producing power without ever "running out" internally.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard values must be memorized for KTU 2024 numerical problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Cell EMF (H₂–O₂, liquid H₂O, 25 °C) | $E^{\circ}$ | **1.229 V** |
| Standard Cell EMF (H₂–O₂, water vapour, 25 °C) | $E^{\circ}$ | **1.18 V** |
| Number of electrons transferred (H₂ → H₂O) | $n$ | **2** |
| Theoretical Efficiency (H₂–O₂, HHV basis) | $\eta_{th}$ | **83 %** |
| Theoretical Efficiency (H₂–O₂, LHV basis) | $\eta_{th}$ | **94 %** |

### 1.4 Core Concept Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT] > **The "cardinal rule" of a fuel cell:** Electricity is generated *without combustion*. The chemical bonds of H₂ are broken electrochemically (not by fire), which is why fuel cells are **2–3× more efficient** than internal combustion engines and produce **zero CO₂** when fuelled by pure hydrogen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell consists of three principal components stacked together:

1. **Anode (Fuel Electrode / Negative Terminal):**
   - **Why:** This is where the *oxidation* of fuel occurs. We need a porous electrode with a platinum-based catalyst so H₂ gas can dissociate efficiently at low overpotentials.
   - **How:** Hydrogen gas is fed through flow channels. The catalyst (typically Pt/C) splits H₂ into protons and electrons:
     $$\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$$

2. **Electrolyte (Ion-Conducting Medium):**
   - **Why:** It physically separates the two half-reactions (otherwise H₂ and O₂ would simply combust to water) while permitting selective ion transport to maintain charge balance.
   - **How:** Different electrolytes conduct different ions (H⁺ in PEMFC/PAFC, O²⁻ in SOFC, CO₃²⁻ in MCFC, OH⁻ in AFC).

3. **Cathode (Oxidant Electrode / Positive Terminal):**
   - **Why:** This is where *reduction* of the oxidant takes place, completing the circuit.
   - **How:** Oxygen (from air) is reduced at the cathode surface. The exact reaction depends on the migrating ion:
     - **For H⁺ conduction (PEMFC, PAFC):**
       $$\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{H^+} + 2\mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}$$
     - **For O²⁻ conduction (SOFC):**
       $$\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O^{2-}}$$
     - **For CO₃²⁻ conduction (MCFC):**
       $$\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + \mathrm{CO_2} + 2\mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}$$
     - **For OH⁻ conduction (AFC):**
       $$\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + \mathrm{H_2O} + 2\mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{OH^-}$$

4. **External Circuit (Load):**
   - **Why:** The electrons released at the anode cannot pass through the electrolyte; they are forced to travel through the external wires—**this is the usable DC current**.
   - **How:** Power delivered $P = V_{cell} \times I$, where $V_{cell}$ is the practical terminal voltage (typically 0.6–0.8 V per cell at operating load).

### 2.2 Net Cell Reaction (Hydrogen–Oxygen Cell)

Adding the two half-reactions for the PEMFC case:

$$\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \, \mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] > **Memorize the following equations—these appear in nearly every fuel-cell numerical.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^{\circ} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Standard cell EMF from Gibbs free energy | V |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}{P_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}\right)$ | **Nernst Equation** for fuel cell EMF | V |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}{P_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}\right)$ at 25 °C | Nernst Equation in log₁₀ form | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{\mathrm{thermo}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \times 100\,\%$ | Thermodynamic (theoretical) efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{\mathrm{voltage}} = \dfrac{V_{\mathrm{cell}}}{E_{\mathrm{thermo}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Voltage efficiency (polarization losses) | % |
| 6 | $\eta_{\mathrm{faradaic}} = \dfrac{I_{\mathrm{actual}}}{I_{\mathrm{theoretical}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Coulombic / Faradaic efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{\mathrm{overall}} = \eta_{\mathrm{thermo}} \times \eta_{\mathrm{voltage}} \times \eta_{\mathrm{faradaic}}$ | Overall fuel cell efficiency | % |
| 8 | $V_{\mathrm{stack}} = N_{\mathrm{cells}} \times V_{\mathrm{cell}}$ | Stack voltage for series-connected cells | V |
| 9 | $E_{\mathrm{HHV}} = 1.481 \, \mathrm{V} \;,\quad E_{\mathrm{LHV}} = 1.253 \, \mathrm{V}$ | Thermoneutral voltages (textbook reference) | V |
| 10 | $P = V_{\mathrm{cell}} \cdot I$ | DC electrical power output | W |

**Boundary conditions / key signs to remember:**

- $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$ (spontaneous reaction) → $E^{\circ} > 0$
- All partial pressures expressed in **atm** when using $R = 8.314 \, \mathrm{J/(mol\cdot K)}$.
- Water partial pressure appears in the denominator **only for PEMFC/PAFC/AFC**. For SOFC/MCFC, water is produced at the *anode* and the term is rearranged accordingly.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fuel cells are no longer "laboratory curiosities"—they power:

- **Spacecraft** (Apollo, Space Shuttle orbiter) using AFC technology.
- **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity** — passenger FCEVs (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles) using PEMFC.
- **Stationary combined-heat-and-power (CHP)** units from companies like Bloom Energy (SOFC) and Doosan (MCFC).
- **Backup power** for telecom towers, data centres, hospitals.
- **Portable / military applications** — soldier-wearable PEMFC power packs.
- **Submarines** — Type 212A German U-boats use PEMFC + methanol reformer for weeks of silent underwater running.

### 2.5 KTU-Examiner's Conceptual Check-Points

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual trap:** Students often confuse **electrolysis** (uses electricity to split water → H₂) with a **fuel cell** (uses H₂ + O₂ to produce electricity). They are *reversible* processes. Examiners love to test this difference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for a Hydrogen–Oxygen Fuel Cell

> **Goal:** Show that the cell EMF depends on the partial pressures of the reactants and the temperature.

For the overall reaction:

$$\mathrm{H_2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2(g)} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O(l)}$$

The change in Gibbs free energy at any state is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where the reaction quotient $Q$ is:

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}$$

Assuming ideal-gas behaviour, activities equal partial pressures (in atm) and $a_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = 1$ for liquid product.

We also know from thermodynamics that:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Substituting into the Gibbs equation:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

Plugging $Q$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}\right)$$

Using the identity $-\ln(1/x) = \ln(x)$:

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}\right)\,}$$

**Conversion to base-10 logarithm at 25 °C (298 K):**

$$\dfrac{RT}{F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569 \, \mathrm{V}$$

Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$: $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$

$$2.303 \times 0.02569 = 0.0592 \, \mathrm{V}$$

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10}\!\left(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}\right) \quad \text{(at 25 °C)}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Solved Numerical Problem — Nernst-Based Cell EMF (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern):**
> A hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell operates at **40 °C (313 K)**. The partial pressures are:
> $P_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 1.5 \, \mathrm{atm},\; P_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.8 \, \mathrm{atm}$.
> The standard EMF at 25 °C is $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \, \mathrm{V}$ and $n = 2$.
> **(a)** Calculate the cell EMF at 40 °C.
> **(b)** Find the percentage change in EMF compared with the standard condition.
> **Given:** $R = 8.314 \, \mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)},\; F = 96485 \, \mathrm{C/mol}$.

#### Part (a): Cell EMF at 40 °C

**Step 1 — Calculate the temperature term coefficient:**

$$\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 313}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$= \dfrac{2602.282}{192970} = 0.013485 \, \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the logarithmic argument:**

$$P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2} = 1.5 \times (0.8)^{0.5}$$

$$(0.8)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.8} = 0.8944$$

$$1.5 \times 0.8944 = 1.3416$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Nernst equation:**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2})$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.013485 \times \ln(1.3416)$$

$$\ln(1.3416) = 0.2942$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.013485 \times 0.2942$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.003968$$

$$\boxed{\,E = 1.2329 \, \mathrm{V}\,}$$

#### Part (b): Percentage Change in EMF

$$\% \text{ change} = \dfrac{E - E^{\circ}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{1.2329 - 1.229}{1.229} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{0.003968}{1.229} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\approx 0.323\,\% \text{ increase}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing the operating temperature *and* raising reactant partial pressures above 1 atm slightly boosts the open-circuit voltage, but the gain is small.

---

### 3.3 Solved Numerical — Theoretical Efficiency of a Fuel Cell

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 pattern):**
> For a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell, $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \, \mathrm{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \, \mathrm{kJ/mol}$ (Higher Heating Value, HHV).
> **(a)** Compute the standard cell EMF.
> **(b)** Determine the theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency.
> **(c)** If the cell operates at 0.7 V under load, find the voltage efficiency.

#### Part (a): Standard EMF

$$E^{\circ} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\dfrac{-237200}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$= \dfrac{237200}{192970}$$

$$\boxed{\,E^{\circ} = 1.229 \, \mathrm{V}\,}$$

#### Part (b): Theoretical (Thermodynamic) Efficiency

$$\eta_{\mathrm{thermo}} = \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} \times 100 = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{\mathrm{thermo}} \approx 83.0\,\%\,}$$

#### Part (c): Voltage Efficiency at 0.7 V

$$\eta_{\mathrm{voltage}} = \dfrac{V_{\mathrm{cell}}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.7}{1.229} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{\mathrm{voltage}} \approx 56.96\,\%\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight:** The **thermodynamic ceiling** of 83 % is the *maximum possible* electrical efficiency of an H₂–O₂ fuel cell on the HHV basis. Real systems run at 40–60 % electrical efficiency (PEMFC) and 50–60 % (SOFC) due to activation, ohmic, and concentration polarization losses (the **"polarization curve"**).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE] > *Physical cross-section drawings of fuel cells are difficult to render textually; therefore, the following Mermaid block diagrams convey the **functional architecture and electrochemical pathway** with maximum clarity for KTU 2024 examination purposes.*

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Generic H₂–O₂ Fuel Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET["FUEL INLET SIDE"]
        H2TANK["H2 Gas Storage / Reformer"]
        H2BPR["Blower / Pressure Regulator"]
    end

    subgraph ANODE["ANODE COMPARTMENT (Oxidation)"]
        ANCHAN["H2 Flow Channels"]
        ANELEC["Porous Anode Catalyst Layer Pt/C"]
        ANRXN["Anode Half Reaction H2 to 2H plus 2e minus"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["ELECTROLYTE CORE (Ion Conduction)"]
        ELECMEM["Ion Selective Membrane or Liquid Electrolyte"]
    end

    subgraph CATHODE["CATHODE COMPARTMENT (Reduction)"]
        CATHAN["O2 Flow Channels from Air"]
        CATELEC["Porous Cathode Catalyst Layer Pt/C"]
        CATRXN["Cathode Half Reaction O2 plus H plus e minus to H2O"]
    end

    subgraph OUTLET["BYPRODUCT AND EXHAUST"]
        WATER["Water and Heat Removal"]
        AIR["O2 Depleted Air Vent"]
    end

    subgraph CIRCUIT["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT"]
        LOAD["DC Load LED, Motor, Inverter"]
        WIRE["Electron Path through Wire"]
    end

    H2TANK --> H2BPR --> ANCHAN --> ANELEC --> ANRXN
    ANRXN -->|"2 e minus released"| WIRE
    WIRE --> LOAD
    LOAD -->|"e minus return"| CATRXN
    CATHAN --> CATELEC --> CATRXN
    CATRXN -->|"H plus crosses membrane"| ELECMEM
    ELECMEM -->|"H plus migrates to cathode"| CATRXN
    ANRXN --> WATER
    CATHAN --> AIR
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Types of Fuel Cells

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FC["FUEL CELLS"]

    FC --> AFC["Alkaline Fuel Cell AFC"]
    FC --> PEMFC["Proton Exchange Membrane FC PEMFC"]
    FC --> DMFC["Direct Methanol FC DMFC"]
    FC --> PAFC["Phosphoric Acid FC PAFC"]
    FC --> MCFC["Molten Carbonate FC MCFC"]
    FC --> SOFC["Solid Oxide FC SOFC"]

    AFC -->|"Electrolyte KOH"| AFC1["Low Temp 60 to 120 C"]
    PEMFC -->|"Electrolyte Nafion Membrane"| PEMFC1["Low Temp 60 to 100 C"]
    DMFC -->|"Electrolyte Polymer Membrane"| DMFC1["Low Temp 60 to 90 C Fuel Methanol"]
    PAFC -->|"Electrolyte H3PO4"| PAFC1["Mid Temp 150 to 200 C"]
    MCFC -->|"Electrolyte Molten Li2CO3 K2CO3"| MCFC1["High Temp 600 to 700 C"]
    SOFC -->|"Electrolyte YSZ Yttria Stabilized Zirconia"| SOFC1["Very High Temp 800 to 1000 C"]

    AFC1 --> AFCAPP["Apollo Spacecraft, Shuttle"]
    PEMFC1 --> PEMFCAPP["Cars Toyota Mirai, Buses, Backup Power"]
    DMFC1 --> DMFCAPP["Laptops, Portable Electronics"]
    PAFC1 --> PAFCAPP["Stationary CHP 200 kW Units"]
    MCFC1 --> MCFCAPP["Large Scale Power Plants MW"]
    SOFC1 --> SOFCAPP["Bloom Energy, Stationary Microgrid"]
```

### 4.3 Polarization Curve — Why Real Voltage Drops Below $E^{\circ}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Current Density I in A per cm2"]:::axis
    Y["Cell Voltage V"]:::axis

    subgraph POL["TYPICAL POLARIZATION CURVE H2 O2 PEMFC"]
        A["Region 1 Activation Loss Steep Drop at Low I due to Slow Kinetics"]
        B["Region 2 Ohmic Loss Linear Drop due to Membrane plus Contact Resistance"]
        C["Region 3 Concentration Loss Sharp Drop at High I due to Mass Transport Limit"]
    end

    A --> B --> C
    EOCV["Open Circuit Voltage approx 1.0 V for PEMFC at I = 0"] --> A
    THV["Thermodynamic Limit E degree = 1.229 V"] --- EOCV
```

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Major Fuel-Cell Types

| Parameter | **AFC** | **PEMFC** | **DMFC** | **PAFC** | **MCFC** | **SOFC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | KOH (aq) | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | Polymer membrane | H₃PO₄ | Molten (Li, K)₂CO₃ | YSZ ceramic |
| Charge Carrier | OH⁻ | H⁺ | H⁺ | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| Operating Temp. | 60–120 °C | 60–100 °C | 60–90 °C | 150–200 °C | 600–700 °C | 800–1000 °C |
| Electrical Eff. | 50–60 % | 40–60 % | 30–40 % | 40–50 % | 50–60 % | 50–60 % |
| Power Range | 1–100 kW | 1 W – 250 kW | < 1 kW | 50 kW – 1 MW | 100 kW – 10 MW | 1 kW – 10 MW |
| Fuel (typical) | Pure H₂ | Pure H₂ | Methanol (CH₃OH) | H₂ (from NG reformer) | H₂, NG, biogas | H₂, NG, biogas |
| Catalyst | Pt, Ag, NiO | Platinum | Pt/Ru | Platinum | Nickel | Perovskite (LSM) |
| CO₂ Sensitivity | High (poisoning) | Low (mild) | None | None | None (actually uses CO₂) | None |
| Key Advantage | Fast kinetics, high efficiency | Quick start, compact | No reformer needed | Higher tolerance to CO | High efficiency, internal reforming | Highest efficiency, solid-state |
| Key Limitation | Sensitive to CO₂, needs pure H₂ & O₂ | Expensive Pt catalyst | Methanol crossover | Heavy, expensive Pt | Slow start, electrolyte corrosion | Slow start, ceramic fragility |
| Major Application | Spacecraft (Apollo, Shuttle) | FCEV cars, buses, backup | Portable electronics | Stationary CHP | Large power plants | Stationary microgrids |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define a fuel cell. With the help of a neat sketch, explain its working principle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (H₂) and an oxidant (O₂) into DC electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products.

**Construction & Working:**
- It consists of two porous electrodes (**anode** and **cathode**) separated by an **electrolyte**.
- **At the anode:** $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$ (oxidation)
- **At the cathode:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{H^+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}$ (reduction)
- The **electrons** released at the anode flow through the external circuit, providing the **electrical current**.
- The **ions** (H⁺) migrate through the electrolyte to the cathode to complete the reaction.
- **Net reaction:** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O}$ + electrical energy + heat.

*(Marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark working + 1 mark diagram/sketches.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*List any **four types** of fuel cells. Write any **two differences** between a fuel cell and a battery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four types of fuel cells:**
1. Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)
2. Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)
3. Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)
4. Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)

| S. No. | Fuel Cell | Battery |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fuel and oxidant are **supplied continuously from outside**; it does not run out as long as fuel is fed. | Reactants are **stored internally**; once exhausted, the battery must be recharged or discarded. |
| 2 | **No combustion** takes place; energy is released electrochemically. | Energy is also released electrochemically, but no continuous feeding is possible. |
| 3 | Operates at **higher efficiencies (40–60 %)** and lower emissions. | Typically **lower round-trip efficiency**, especially for lead–acid types. |

*(Marks: 1 mark list + 1 mark difference + 1 mark second difference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the construction and working of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** with a neat diagram. Mention its **advantages, limitations, and applications**.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A PEMFC consists of a **solid polymer electrolyte membrane** (typically **Nafion**) sandwiched between two **porous carbon electrodes** coated with a **platinum catalyst** (Pt/C).
- **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA):** Anode + Membrane + Cathode laminated together.
- **Bipolar plates** on either side distribute the reactant gases (H₂ and air) and conduct current to the external load.
- **Operating temperature:** 60–100 °C.

**Working:**
- **At anode:** $\mathrm{H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-}$ (catalysed by Pt)
- The H⁺ ions migrate through the **proton-conducting membrane** (Nafion) to the cathode side.
- The electrons are forced through the **external circuit** (DC load).
- **At cathode:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{H^+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}$ (water is produced here).
- Water is removed from the cathode side (humidification management is critical).

**Advantages:**
1. **Quick start-up** (suitable for vehicles).
2. **Compact and lightweight** (high power density).
3. **Solid electrolyte** — no leakage/corrosion issues.
4. **Operates at low temperature** — quick response.
5. Zero emissions (only water released).

**Limitations:**
1. **Expensive platinum catalyst** drives up cost.
2. **Sensitive to CO poisoning** (even 10 ppm CO degrades performance).
3. **Water management** in the membrane is critical.
4. **Low-grade waste heat** cannot be used for cogeneration.

**Applications:**
- Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
- Backup power for telecom towers, data centres.
- Submarines, buses, forklifts, residential CHP.

*(Marks: 2 construction + 2 working + 1 advantages + 1 limitations + 1 applications.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Derive the **Nernst equation** for the EMF of an H₂–O₂ fuel cell. A fuel cell operates at **50 °C** with $P_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 2.0 \, \mathrm{atm}$ and $P_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.5 \, \mathrm{atm}$. Given $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \, \mathrm{V}$, $n = 2$, $R = 8.314 \, \mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$, $F = 96485 \, \mathrm{C/mol}$, calculate the cell EMF.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation of Nernst Equation:**

For the overall cell reaction: $\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O(l)}$

From thermodynamics, the change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q = \dfrac{a_{\mathrm{H_2O}}}{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}$. For liquid water, $a_{\mathrm{H_2O}} = 1$.

We also know:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Substituting:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

Since $Q = \dfrac{1}{P_{\mathrm{H_2}} P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}}$, we get:

$$\boxed{\,E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}\right)\,}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $T = 50 + 273 = 323 \, \mathrm{K}$, $P_{\mathrm{H_2}} = 2.0 \, \mathrm{atm}$, $P_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 0.5 \, \mathrm{atm}$, $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \, \mathrm{V}$, $n = 2$.

**Step 1:** Temperature term:
$$\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 323}{2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{2685.4}{192970} = 0.01392 \, \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 2:** Logarithmic argument:
$$P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2} = 2.0 \times (0.5)^{0.5} = 2.0 \times 0.7071 = 1.4142$$

**Step 3:** Apply Nernst equation:
$$E = 1.229 + 0.01392 \times \ln(1.4142)$$
$$\ln(1.4142) = 0.3466$$
$$E = 1.229 + 0.01392 \times 0.3466 = 1.229 + 0.00482$$
$$\boxed{\,E = 1.2338 \, \mathrm{V}\,}$$

*(Marks: 3 derivation + 1 setup + 1 calculation step 1 + 1 calculation step 2 + 1 final answer.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)**. Discuss its **advantages, limitations, and applications**.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- The SOFC uses a **solid ceramic electrolyte**, typically **Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ)**.
- The **anode** is a porous **nickel–YSZ cermet**.
- The **cathode** is a porous **perovskite** such as **Lanthanum Strontium Manganite (LSM)**.
- **Operating temperature:** 800–1000 °C (very high).
- Interconnects are made of **lanthanum chromite (LaCrO₃)** or high-temperature alloys.

**Working (using O²⁻ ion conduction):**
- **At the cathode (air side):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} + 2\mathrm{e^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O^{2-}}$
- The **O²⁻ ions** migrate through the **YSZ electrolyte** (which conducts oxygen ions at high T) to the anode.
- **At the anode (fuel side):** $\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O^{2-}} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O} + 2\mathrm{e^-}$
  *(or for CO: $\mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{O^{2-}} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO_2} + 2\mathrm{e^-}$)*
- The electrons flow through the external circuit, producing DC power.
- **Net reaction:** $\mathrm{H_2} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_2O}$ + electricity + heat.

**Advantages:**
1. **Highest electrical efficiency** (50–60 %, up to 85 % with CHP).
2. **Solid-state** — no liquid electrolyte, hence no leakage or corrosion.
3. **Fuel flexibility** — can use H₂, natural gas, biogas, syngas.
4. **High-grade waste heat** (800–1000 °C) suitable for gas turbines and CHP.
5. **No precious-metal catalyst** required (uses nickel and ceramics).
6. CO and CO₂ are *not poisons* but actually *fuels* for SOFC.

**Limitations:**
1. **Very slow start-up** due to high operating temperature.
2. **Ceramic brittleness** — mechanical shock sensitivity.
3. **High-temperature material degradation** (sintering, inter-diffusion).
4. **Expensive interconnect materials** (LaCrO₃).
5. **Thermal cycling** reduces lifetime.

**Applications:**
- Large stationary power plants (1 kW – multi-MW).
- Bloom Energy Servers (data centres, hospitals, microgrids).
- Combined cycle systems with gas turbines.
- Industrial CHP systems.

*(Marks: 2 construction + 2 working + 1.5 advantages + 1 limitations + 0.5 applications.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare the following fuel cells on the basis of (i) electrolyte, (ii) operating temperature, (iii) efficiency, and (iv) major applications: **PEMFC, PAFC, MCFC, and SOFC**.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Feature | **PEMFC** | **PAFC** | **MCFC** | **SOFC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrolyte** | Polymer membrane (Nafion, hydrated) | Concentrated phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄) | Molten mixture of Li₂CO₃ + K₂CO₃ held in a porous LiAlO₂ matrix | Solid ceramic — Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia (YSZ) |
| **Charge carrier** | H⁺ (proton) | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ (carbonate ion) | O²⁻ (oxide ion) |
| **Operating Temperature** | 60–100 °C | 150–200 °C | 600–700 °C | 800–1000 °C |
| **Electrical Efficiency** | 40–60 % | 40–50 % | 50–60 % | 50–60 % (up to 85 % with CHP) |
| **Fuel** | Pure H₂ | H₂ (from natural gas reformer) | H₂, NG, biogas, coal gas | H₂, NG, biogas, syngas |
| **Catalyst** | Platinum | Platinum | Nickel (cheap) | Nickel (anode) / Perovskite (cathode) |
| **Water management** | Critical (must keep membrane humid) | Less critical | Produced at cathode (gas) | Produced at anode (gas) |
| **Start-up time** | Seconds to minutes | ~1 hour | Several hours | Several hours |
| **Major Applications** | FCEVs (cars, buses), backup power, portable | Stationary CHP 200 kW | Large power plants (MW) | Stationary microgrids, MW power |
| **CO₂ tolerance** | High | High | Uses CO₂ (advantage) | Uses CO₂ (advantage) |
| **CO tolerance (at operating T)** | Very poor (10 ppm) | Poor (< 1 %) | Good (fuel) | Excellent (fuel) |

**Concluding Remarks (Board-Examiner Favourite):**
- Low-temperature cells (PEMFC, PAFC) need **expensive Pt catalyst** but offer **quick start-up** → preferred for **transportation**.
- High-temperature cells (MCFC, SOFC) tolerate **impure fuels**, enable **internal reforming** of natural gas, and yield **high-grade waste heat** for **cogeneration** → preferred for **stationary power**.

*(Marks: 1 mark each for the four comparison points × 4 cells = 4 marks + 2 marks for CO tolerance/charge-carrier rows + 1 mark for concluding remark.)*

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of "½"** in the O₂ term of the Nernst equation. The Nernst equation for fuel cells has $\ln(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2})$, **not** $\ln(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} \cdot P_{\mathrm{O_2}})$. Losing this "½" gives a 0.5–1 % error and may be penalised.
> 2. **Mixing up anode and cathode.** Mnemonic: **AnOx** (Anode = Oxidation) and **Red Cat** (Reduction = Cathode). Confusing these swaps the half-reactions and loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $E^{\circ} = 1.18$ V instead of 1.229 V** (or vice versa) without specifying whether the water product is liquid or vapour. **Always state the assumption.**
> 4. **Writing "fuel cells are 100 % efficient"** — this is wrong. The thermodynamic limit is **83 % (HHV)** or **94 % (LHV)**; real cells achieve 40–60 % electric efficiency.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Nernst-equation problems. KTU examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit errors.
> 6. **Calling SOFC and MCFC the same thing** — they have *different* electrolytes, charge carriers, and operating temperatures.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Exam:**

- ☐ A **fuel cell** is a *direct* electrochemical converter (no combustion) of chemical energy → electrical energy.
- ☐ **Generic half-reactions (PEM-type):** Anode: $\mathrm{H_2 \to 2H^+ + 2e^-}$; Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2O}$.
- ☐ **Net cell reaction:** $\mathrm{H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \to H_2O}$ with $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \, \mathrm{kJ/mol}$.
- ☐ **Standard EMF:** $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \, \mathrm{V}$ (liquid H₂O) / $1.18 \, \mathrm{V}$ (vapour H₂O).
- ☐ **Nernst equation:** $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(P_{\mathrm{H_2}} P_{\mathrm{O_2}}^{1/2}\right)$.
- ☐ **Constants:** $F = 96485 \, \mathrm{C/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \, \mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$, $n = 2$ for H₂–O₂.
- ☐ **Theoretical efficiency:** $\eta_{\mathrm{th}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$ = **83 %** (HHV) or **94 %** (LHV).
- ☐ **Voltage efficiency** captures the *real* performance loss to **activation, ohmic, and concentration** polarization.
- ☐ **AFC** — KOH electrolyte, OH⁻ ion, 60–120 °C, used in space; sensitive to CO₂.
- ☐ **PEMFC** — Nafion membrane, H⁺ ion, 60–100 °C, used in **cars and buses**; Pt catalyst.
- ☐ **PAFC** — H₃PO₄ electrolyte, H⁺ ion, 150–200 °C, used in **stationary CHP 200 kW**.
- ☐ **MCFC** — Molten carbonates, CO₃²⁻ ion, 600–700 °C, **MW-scale power plants**, internal reforming.
- ☐ **SOFC** — YSZ ceramic electrolyte, O²⁻ ion, 800–1000 °C, **highest efficiency**, Bloom Energy servers.
- ☐ **DMFC** — Uses **liquid methanol** directly, no reformer, low power density.
- ☐ **Charge-carrier mnemonic:** **"PEM & PAFC carry Protons; SOFC carries Oxide; MCFC carries Carbonate; AFC carries Hydroxide."**
- ☐ **Temperature mnemonic:** **"SOFC > MCFC > PAFC > PEMFC > AFC"** (SOFC hottest, AFC coolest).
- ☐ **Fuel cell vs battery:** Fuel cell = *open* system (continuous fuel feed); Battery = *closed* system (finite reactants).
- ☐ **Key advantages:** Zero emissions (if H₂ from renewables), high efficiency, modular, silent operation.
- ☐ **Key challenges:** Cost (Pt catalyst), hydrogen storage & infrastructure, durability, water management.
- ☐ **Polarization curve** has three regions: **activation (initial drop), ohmic (linear), concentration (sharp fall)**.
- ☐ **Faraday's constant 96485 C/mol** is sacred for KTU numericals — never use 96500 unless the question specifies the rounded value.
- ☐ **Stack voltage** = $N_{\text{cells}} \times V_{\text{cell}}$ for series-connected cells in a fuel-cell stack.
- ☐ Always **convert °C to Kelvin** before substituting $T$ in the Nernst equation.
- ☐ Real practical cell voltage: **0.6–0.8 V/cell** under load, far below 1.229 V due to polarization.
- ☐ For PEM-type cells, water is produced at the **cathode**; for SOFC/MCFC, water is produced at the **anode** (different exhaust handling).
- ☐ **Applications recap:** Spacecraft (AFC) → Cars (PEMFC) → Buildings (PAFC) → Power plants (MCFC/SOFC) → Portable (DMFC).
- ☐ KTU examiner loves asking: *"Why is the theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell limited to 83 %?"* → Answer: Because some energy is *always* liberated as **heat (TΔS)**, not as electrical work. $\Delta H = \Delta G + T\Delta S$, and the $T\Delta S$ portion cannot be converted to electricity isothermally.
- ☐ Another favourite: *"Why does increasing temperature decrease the cell EMF of a fuel cell?"* → Answer: From $\left(\partial E/\partial T\right)_P = -\Delta S / (nF)$. For H₂–O₂, $\Delta S < 0$ (entropy decreases as 1.5 mol of gas → 1 mol of liquid), so $\partial E/\partial T > 0$. Wait — *increase* in $T$ actually *increases* $E$ for H₂–O₂. (Counter-intuitive but a common KTU trick question!)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization and Durability of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterization and Durability of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Characterization of a fuel cell** refers to the systematic experimental and analytical evaluation of a fuel cell's electrochemical performance through standardized metrics such as polarization behavior, power density, impedance response, and efficiency, expressed as a function of operating conditions (current density, temperature, pressure, and reactant utilization).

**Durability of a fuel cell** is defined as the ability of the fuel cell to sustain its performance (voltage, power output) within acceptable degradation limits over its operational lifetime under defined stress conditions, typically quantified as the rate of voltage decay ($\mu V/h$) or as the total hours of operation before reaching End-of-Life (EOL) criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 OBE framework, students must be able to **identify, plot, and interpret** the polarization curve, distinguish between the three primary loss mechanisms, and explain the dominant degradation modes affecting Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFCs) and Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFCs).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Battery vs. Fuel Cell Analogy (The "Tiffin Box" vs. The "Restaurant Kitchen"):**
> A **battery** is like a sealed tiffin box — it carries a fixed amount of food (stored chemical energy) and once consumed, it must be recharged or thrown away. A **fuel cell**, however, is like a restaurant kitchen — as long as you keep supplying groceries (hydrogen and oxygen) and removing the cooked food (water + electricity + heat), it never stops serving.
>
> **Characterization** = measuring how efficiently the kitchen converts groceries into meals (its performance).
> **Durability** = how long the kitchen's pots, pans, and burners stay functional before wearing out (its lifetime).

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The key metrics, each used in KTU problems, are:

- **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV):** $\approx$ **1.2 V** for an H₂/O₂ PEMFC (theoretical), but practically **1.0–1.1 V** due to fuel crossover and mixed potentials.
- **Operating Voltage Range:** **0.5 V to 0.8 V** per cell at practical current densities.
- **Power Density:** measured in **W/cm²** or **kW/m²** of active electrode area.
- **Faradaic (Coulombic) Efficiency:** ideally **100%**, since the electrochemical reaction is direct.
- **Energy Efficiency:** ratio of electrical energy output to the heating value of the fuel supplied, typically **40–60%** for PEMFC, up to **85%** with CHP.
- **Durability Target (U.S. DOE 2030):** **$8{,}000$ hours** for light-duty vehicles; **$30{,}000$–$40{,}000$ hours** for stationary applications; decay rate **$< 10\ \mu V/h$**.

### 1.4 Fuel Cell Types Recap (for Context)

| Fuel Cell Type | Operating Temperature | Electrolyte | Charge Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| PEMFC | 60–80 °C | Polymer membrane | H⁺ |
| AFC | 60–220 °C | KOH solution | OH⁻ |
| PAFC | 150–220 °C | H₃PO₄ | H⁺ |
| MCFC | 600–700 °C | Molten carbonate | CO₃²⁻ |
| SOFC | 600–1000 °C | YSZ ceramic | O²⁻ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Polarization Curve (V–I Curve) — The Heart of Characterization

The polarization curve is the *single most important* characterization plot in fuel cell engineering. It plots **cell voltage ($V_{cell}$)** on the y-axis against **current density ($j$ in A/cm²)** on the x-axis.

The actual cell voltage at any operating point is the **theoretical (reversible) voltage** minus **three categories of irreversible losses (overpotentials)**:

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

Where:
- $E_{rev}$ = Reversible (Nernst) open-circuit voltage
- $\eta_{act}$ = Activation overpotential (kinetic losses)
- $\eta_{ohmic}$ = Ohmic overpotential (resistive losses)
- $\eta_{conc}$ = Concentration overpotential (mass transport losses)

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of the Three Loss Regions

**Region I — Activation Losses (Low Current, $j < 0.1$ A/cm²):**
- Caused by the **sluggish kinetics** of the Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the cathode.
- Described by the **Butler–Volmer equation**, simplified to the **Tafel equation** at high overpotentials:
  $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = a + b \log(j)$$
  where $b$ is the Tafel slope (~60 mV/dec for PEMFC cathode).
- The cathode is the dominant contributor because ORR is ~6 orders of magnitude slower than HOR.

**Region II — Ohmic Losses (Mid Current, $0.1 < j < 1.0$ A/cm²):**
- Caused by **ionic resistance of the electrolyte** and **electronic resistance of electrodes, bipolar plates, and contact interfaces**.
- Strictly **linear** with current (Ohm's law):
  $$\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot R_{ohmic}$$
- Dominant contributor: **proton conduction resistance of the Nafion membrane**, strongly dependent on **humidification**.

**Region III — Concentration Losses (High Current, $j > 1.0$ A/cm²):**
- Caused by **mass transport limitations** — reactants cannot reach the catalyst sites fast enough.
- Modeled as a limiting current density $j_L$:
  $$\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$$
- As $j \rightarrow j_L$, voltage collapses sharply to zero.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-essential. Memorize the units and applicability.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Application | Key Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Theoretical reversible voltage from Gibbs free energy | $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V (H₂/O₂, 25 °C, 1 atm) |
| 2 | $E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst equation — voltage vs. partial pressures | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol |
| 3 | $\eta_{act} = a + b\log_{10}(j)$ | Tafel equation (simplified activation loss) | Valid for $j \gg j_0$ |
| 4 | $\eta_{ohmic} = j \cdot ASR$ | Ohmic loss using Area-Specific Resistance | ASR in $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$ | Concentration loss | $j < j_L$ always |
| 6 | $P_{density} = V_{cell} \cdot j$ | Power density curve (peak = max power point) | Peak is at knee of V-I curve |
| 7 | $\eta_{Faradaic} = \frac{I_{actual}}{nF \cdot \dot{n}_{fuel,in}}$ | Faradaic efficiency | $\le 100\%$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{energy} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E_{th}} \times \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta G}$ | Energy efficiency (electrical) | $E_{th} = 1.48$ V (HHV) or $1.229$ V (LHV) |
| 9 | $V(t) = V_0 - r_{decay} \cdot t$ | Linear durability decay model | $r_{decay}$ in $\mu V/h$ |
| 10 | $ECSA = \frac{Q_{desorption}}{[Pt] \cdot v_{scan} \cdot 210\ \mu C/cm^2_{Pt}}$ | Electrochemical Surface Area from CV | Used for catalyst degradation studies |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The polarization curve is the **fingerprint of a fuel cell**. It is used in:
> - **Stack design:** determining number of cells for a target voltage.
> - **System integration:** sizing BOP (boP = Balance of Plant: humidifier, compressor, heat exchanger).
> - **Quality control:** comparing membrane/MEA batches.
> - **Failure diagnosis:** an unexpectedly high $\eta_{act}$ indicates catalyst poisoning; a steep $\eta_{ohmic}$ slope indicates membrane drying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamic First Principles

We derive the reversible cell voltage $E_{rev}$ as a function of operating partial pressures.

**Step 1 — Gibbs free energy change defines the maximum work:**

For the half-reactions:
- Anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$

**Step 2 — Overall reaction:**

$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

**Step 3 — Standard Gibbs free energy at 25 °C, 1 atm:**

$$\Delta G^0 = -237.2\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Electrical work per mole of electrons:**

The maximum non-PV electrical work equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{elec,max} &= -\Delta G = nFE_{rev} \\
E_{rev} &= -\frac{\Delta G}{nF} \\
E^0_{rev} &= -\frac{(-237{,}200\ \text{J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485\ \text{C/mol})} \\
E^0_{rev} &= 1.229\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Generalize using the reaction quotient $Q$:**

At non-standard conditions, the change in Gibbs free energy is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT\ln Q$$

**Step 6 — For our reaction, $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
-\Delta G &= nF E_{Nernst} \\
E_{Nernst} &= -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right) \\
E_{Nernst} &= E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Nernst equation for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell** — the most-asked equation in KTU Module 4 derivations.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Activation Overpotential using Tafel Kinetics

**Step 1 — Start from the Butler–Volmer equation:**

$$j = j_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — At large cathodic overpotentials (fuel cell operation), the anodic term is negligible:**

$$j \approx j_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_{act}}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\eta_{act}$ by taking the natural logarithm:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{j}{j_0} &= \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_{act}}{RT}\right) \\
\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) &= -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_{act}}{RT} \\
\eta_{act} &= -\frac{RT}{\alpha_c F}\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) \\
\eta_{act} &= \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F}\ln(j) - \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F}\ln(j_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (Tafel form):**

$$\eta_{act} = \underbrace{\frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F}}_{b\ (\text{Tafel slope})}\log_{10}(j) - \underbrace{\frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F}\log_{10}(j_0)}_{a\ (\text{Tafel intercept})}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Tafel slope for PEMFC cathode ORR:**

At $T = 353$ K (80 °C), $\alpha_c = 1$, $n = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{1 \times 96{,}485} \\
b &= \frac{6756.5}{96{,}485} \\
b &\approx 0.0700\ \text{V/decade} = 70\ \text{mV/dec}
\end{aligned}$$

(Measured values are typically **60–70 mV/dec** for Pt/C cathode in PEMFC.)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause–Symptom–Fix for Each Loss Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** A 7-mark question may show a polarization curve and ask: "Identify and explain the dominant loss at point X." Use this matrix as your diagnostic template.

| Region | Dominant Loss | Physical Cause | Diagnostic Signature | Engineering Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I (low j) | Activation | Slow ORR kinetics on Pt | Steep initial drop; Tafel-like | Higher Pt loading; Pt-alloy catalysts; elevated T & P |
| II (mid j) | Ohmic | Membrane proton resistance + contact resistance | Linear, gentle slope | Thinner membrane; better humidification; metallic bipolar plates |
| III (high j) | Concentration | Reactant starvation | Sharp voltage cliff | Higher GDL porosity; optimized flow fields; higher stoich ratios |
| OCV < 1.0 V | Fuel crossover + internal currents | H₂ permeation through membrane; e⁻ shorting | OCV below theoretical | Thicker membrane; lower H₂ pressure; reinforced membrane |

### 3.4 Durability Calculation — Linear Decay Model

**Given:** A PEMFC starts at $V_0 = 0.70$ V at rated current, and after $t = 2000$ h, voltage has dropped to $V = 0.65$ V.

**Step 1 — Apply the linear decay model:**

$$V(t) = V_0 - r_{decay} \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the decay rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
r_{decay} &= \frac{V_0 - V(t)}{t} \\
r_{decay} &= \frac{0.70 - 0.65}{2000\ \text{h}} \\
r_{decay} &= \frac{0.05}{2000} \\
r_{decay} &= 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{V/h} \\
r_{decay} &= 25\ \mu V/h
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Predict lifetime to 10% voltage drop (EOL = 0.63 V):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{EOL} &= \frac{V_0 - V_{EOL}}{r_{decay}} \\
t_{EOL} &= \frac{0.70 - 0.63}{25\ \mu V/h} = \frac{0.07}{2.5\times 10^{-5}} = 2800\ \text{h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with DOE 2030 light-duty target of $8{,}000$ h:**

This cell is **3× below target** — design changes (lower catalyst degradation, membrane reinforcement) are mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Polarization Curve Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Cell Input: H2 and O2] --> B[Reversible Voltage Erev = 1.229 V]
    B --> C[Subtract Activation Loss eta_act]
    C --> D[Subtract Ohmic Loss eta_ohmic]
    D --> E[Subtract Concentration Loss eta_conc]
    E --> F[Actual Cell Voltage Vcell]
    F --> G[Multiply by Current Density j]
    G --> H[Power Density Curve P = Vcell x j]
    H --> I[Peak Power Point: dP/dj = 0]
    I --> J[Operating Point Selection]
    J --> K{Below Acceptable Threshold?}
    K --> L[Yes: Replace Membrane or Catalyst]
    K --> M[No: Continue Operation]
    L --> N[Degradation Diagnosis]
    M --> O[Long-Term Durability Tracking]
```

### 4.2 Durability Degradation Pathway Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh MEA: ECSA High] --> B[Operating Stress]
    B --> C[Catalyst Degradation]
    B --> D[Membrane Degradation]
    B --> E[Bipolar Plate Corrosion]
    B --> F[GDL Carbon Oxidation]
    C --> C1[Pt Dissolution]
    C --> C2[Ostwald Ripening]
    C --> C3[Pt Nanoparticle Detachment]
    D --> D1[Chemical Attack by Radicals]
    D --> D2[Mechanical Pinhole Formation]
    D --> D3[Thermal Cycling Damage]
    E --> E1[Passive Layer Growth]
    F --> F1[Loss of Hydrophobicity]
    C1 --> G[ECSA Loss and Activation Increase]
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> H[Increased H2 Crossover and Ohmic Resistance]
    D2 --> H
    E1 --> I[Higher Contact Resistance]
    F1 --> J[Flooding and Mass Transport Loss]
    G --> K[Polarization Curve Shift Downward]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Decay Rate Microns V per hour]
    L --> M[End of Life: V less than 80 percent of Rated]
```

### 4.3 Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) — Diagnostic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EIS Input: Small AC Perturbation] --> B[Frequency Sweep 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz]
    B --> C[Measure Complex Impedance Z]
    C --> D[Plot Nyquist: Z Imaginary vs Z Real]
    D --> E[High Frequency Arc: Ohmic plus Charge Transfer]
    D --> F[Low Frequency Arc: Mass Transport]
    E --> G[Extract Rct: Charge Transfer Resistance]
    F --> H[Extract Diffusion Coefficient]
    G --> I[Diagnose Activation Loss]
    H --> J[Diagnose Concentration Loss]
    I --> K[Targeted Mitigation Strategy]
    J --> K
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Fuel Cell System Architecture (for Durability Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        H2[Hydrogen Supply Tank]
        AIR[Air Blower and Filter]
        H2O_IN[Coolant Deionized Water]
    end
    subgraph Stack
        MEA[MEA: Membrane Electrode Assembly]
        BP[Bipolar Plates Graphite or Metal]
        GDL[Gas Diffusion Layer Carbon Paper]
        SEAL[Gaskets and Sealing]
    end
    subgraph BOP_Balance_of_Plant
        HUM[Humidifier]
        PUMP[Re-circulation Pump]
        RAD[Radiator and Cooling Loop]
        COND[Power Conditioner DC to DC]
    end
    subgraph Monitoring
        V_MON[Voltage Telemetry per Cell]
        T_MON[Temperature Sensors]
        ECSA_MON[Periodic EIS and CV Tests]
    end
    H2 --> MEA
    AIR --> MEA
    MEA --> BP
    BP --> GDL
    GDL --> SEAL
    HUM --> MEA
    PUMP --> HUM
    RAD --> BP
    COND --> BP
    V_MON --> MEA
    T_MON --> RAD
    ECSA_MON --> MEA
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. What is a polarization curve? Name and briefly explain the three regions of voltage loss in a fuel cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A polarization curve is a plot of **cell voltage ($V_{cell}$)** versus **current density ($j$)** that characterizes the electrical performance of a fuel cell. The three regions of voltage loss are:

1. **Activation Loss ($\eta_{act}$):** At low current densities, caused by the slow kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) at the cathode. Follows Tafel behavior.
2. **Ohmic Loss ($\eta_{ohmic}$):** In the mid-current range, caused by ionic resistance of the electrolyte and electronic contact resistance. Linear with current ($V = IR$).
3. **Concentration Loss ($\eta_{conc}$):** At high current densities, caused by mass-transport limitations where reactants cannot reach the catalyst sites. Voltage drops sharply to zero as $j \rightarrow j_L$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List four major degradation mechanisms affecting the durability of a PEMFC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four major degradation mechanisms in a PEMFC are:

1. **Catalyst (Pt) Degradation:** Pt nanoparticle dissolution, Ostwald ripening, and detachment, leading to loss of Electrochemical Surface Area (ECSA).
2. **Membrane Degradation:** Chemical attack by hydroxyl (·OH) and hydroperoxyl (·OOH) radicals, mechanical pinhole formation, and thermal cycling damage.
3. **Bipolar Plate Corrosion:** Surface passivation layer growth increasing contact resistance, especially in metallic plates.
4. **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL) Degradation:** Carbon oxidation and loss of hydrophobicity, leading to flooding.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Full Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for an H₂/O₂ fuel cell starting from the Gibbs free energy definition. Clearly state the standard value of $E^0$ and the operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm (air), and $P_{H_2O} = 0.5$ atm. Calculate the Nernst (reversible) voltage. Also compute the cell voltage at $j = 0.5$ A/cm² if the activation overpotential (Tafel) is 0.05 V, the ohmic loss is 0.08 V, and the concentration loss is 0.02 V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Starting from $\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT\ln Q$ and $E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$:

$$E_{Nernst} = -\frac{\Delta G^0}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

For the reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, $Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$:

$$E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

With $E^0 = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, 1 atm, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol. (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for the substitution, 2 marks for the final form, 2 marks for stating $E^0$ value and conditions.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute Nernst voltage at $T = 353$ K:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{Nernst} &= 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{2(96{,}485)}\ln\left(\frac{1.5 \times (0.21)^{0.5}}{0.5}\right) \\
&= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times \ln\left(\frac{1.5 \times 0.4583}{0.5}\right) \\
&= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times \ln(1.375) \\
&= 1.229 + 0.01521 \times 0.3185 \\
&= 1.229 + 0.00485 \\
E_{Nernst} &\approx 1.234\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract all overpotentials:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cell} &= E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc} \\
V_{cell} &= 1.234 - 0.05 - 0.08 - 0.02 \\
V_{cell} &= 1.084\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Power density:**

$$P = V_{cell} \times j = 1.084 \times 0.5 = 0.542\ \text{W/cm}^2$$

(Marks: 3 for correct Nernst evaluation, 2 for voltage subtraction, 2 for power density.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the Butler–Volmer and Tafel equations for fuel cell kinetics. Define exchange current density ($j_0$), transfer coefficient ($\alpha$), and Tafel slope ($b$). Derive the relationship between Tafel slope and temperature for an $\alpha = 0.5$ PEMFC cathode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC stack is rated at 50 kW. After 3,000 hours of operation, its voltage at rated current has dropped from 0.70 V to 0.62 V per cell. Calculate: (i) the decay rate in $\mu V/h$, (ii) the predicted lifetime until the cell reaches 0.55 V (EOL criterion), and (iii) compare your answer with the DOE 2025 light-duty vehicle target of $5{,}000$ h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Butler–Volmer equation** describes the net current density across an electrode as a function of overpotential:

$$j = j_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F\eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

For large cathodic overpotentials (fuel cell regime), the anodic exponential is negligible, yielding the **Tafel equation**:

$$\eta_{act} = a + b\log_{10}(j)$$

where $b = \dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_c F}$ is the Tafel slope (V/decade), $j_0$ is the exchange current density (A/cm²), and $\alpha$ is the charge transfer coefficient (typically 0.5).

For $\alpha = 0.5$ at $T = 353$ K:

$$b = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96{,}485} = 0.140\ \text{V/dec} = 140\ \text{mV/dec}$$

(Marks: 3 for B-V equation, 2 for Tafel derivation, 2 for numerical Tafel slope.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Decay rate:**

$$r_{decay} = \frac{0.70 - 0.62}{3{,}000} = \frac{0.08}{3{,}000} = 2.67 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{V/h} = 26.7\ \mu V/h$$

**Step 2 — Time to reach 0.55 V from 0.62 V:**

$$t_{EOL} = \frac{0.62 - 0.55}{26.7\ \mu V/h} = \frac{0.07}{2.67\times 10^{-5}} = 2{,}622\ \text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total lifetime (from 0.70 V to 0.55 V):**

$$t_{total} = \frac{0.70 - 0.55}{26.7\ \mu V/h} = \frac{0.15}{2.67\times 10^{-5}} = 5{,}618\ \text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with DOE 2025 target of 5,000 h:**

This stack **meets the 5,000 h target** (5,618 h) but is **below the 2030 target of 8,000 h**. The decay rate of 26.7 μV/h is **higher than the DOE threshold of 10 μV/h** — improvements in catalyst and membrane stability are needed. (Marks: 2 + 2 + 3.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse HHV and LHV.** Use LHV ($\Delta H = 286$ kJ/mol) for $E^0 = 1.229$ V (electrical), and HHV ($\Delta H = 286$ kJ/mol water formed as liquid) for efficiency calculations involving combustion heat.
> - **Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin** in the Nernst equation is a common 1-mark loss.
> - **Mixing up anode and cathode transfer coefficients** ($\alpha_a$ vs $\alpha_c$) in the Tafel equation is a recurring error.
> - **Linear extrapolation** of decay rate is only valid for short time horizons; real decay is non-linear (acceleration near EOL).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Polarization curve** = *V vs. j* plot; the **fingerprint** of fuel cell performance.
- ✅ **Three loss regions**: Activation (low j, Tafel), Ohmic (mid j, linear), Concentration (high j, mass transport).
- ✅ **Nernst equation**: $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$; $E^0 = 1.229$ V at 25 °C.
- ✅ **Tafel equation**: $\eta_{act} = a + b\log(j)$; Tafel slope $b \approx 60$–$70$ mV/dec for PEMFC cathode.
- ✅ **Power density peak** = knee of the polarization curve, found by $\dfrac{dP}{dj} = 0$.
- ✅ **OCV < theoretical** indicates fuel crossover, internal short, or catalyst contamination.
- ✅ **Durability decay** quantified in $\mu V/h$; DOE targets: $5{,}000$–$8{,}000$ h automotive, $30{,}000$–$40{,}000$ h stationary.
- ✅ **Four degradation mechanisms**: Catalyst (Pt loss), Membrane (radical attack + pinholes), Bipolar plate (corrosion), GDL (flooding).
- ✅ **EIS (Nyquist plot)** is the standard non-destructive diagnostic for separating $R_{ohmic}$, $R_{ct}$, and mass-transport resistance.
- ✅ **ECSA** from cyclic voltammetry ($\text{under-peak charge} / 210\ \mu C/cm^2_{Pt}$) tracks catalyst degradation.
- ✅ **Accelerated Stress Tests (ASTs)** are used to predict long-term durability in compressed timeframes.
- ✅ **Faraday constant**: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; **Universal gas constant**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — memorize these.
- ✅ **Linear decay model** is a first-order approximation; non-linear models (e.g., bilinear, exponential) are used near EOL.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
