topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT837 - NON-CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Non-Conventional Energy Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> *Non-conventional energy applications* refer to the **practical end-use deployment** of energy harnessed from renewable, naturally replenishing sources — such as **solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave, and ocean thermal energy** — converted via appropriate conversion technologies (thermal, mechanical, electrical, or electrochemical) into useful forms like electricity, heat, mechanical work, or storable fuels (e.g., **hydrogen**).

The broad classification into two conversion families is essential for KTU:

| Conversion Route | Energy Form Change | Typical Devices |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Conversion** | Sunlight/Radiation → Electricity/Heat | PV Cells, Solar Thermal Collectors |
| **Indirect Conversion** | Kinetic/Potential → Mechanical → Electrical | Wind Turbines, Tidal Barrages |
| **Electrochemical** | Chemical → Electrical | Fuel Cells, Batteries |
| **Thermochemical** | Biomass → Fuel/Gas | Biogas Digesters, Gasifiers |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Buffet" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of the Earth's energy ecosystem as a **massive multi-cuisine buffet**. The sun is the master chef continuously cooking, the wind is the waiter circulating air, the oceans are the cold storage, and biomass is the pre-prepared pantry. **Non-conventional energy applications are the recipes** — different technologies (cookware) that turn these raw natural "ingredients" into a digestible meal (usable electricity, heat, or fuel). Just as you wouldn't use a wok to make ice cream, each renewable source needs its specific "recipe" (technology) for conversion.

## 1.3 Why "Applications" is a High-Yield KTU Module

> [!NOTE]
> Module 4 of OECHT837 is typically the **last technical module** of the syllabus and consolidates all earlier theoretical learning (solar, wind, biomass, hydrogen, fuel cells) into **real-world engineering deployment scenarios**. Questions from this module frequently test the **ability to map a source to its best-suited application**, justify technology selection, and discuss hybrid systems. Expected cognitive level per KTU 2024: **Apply / Analyze (Bloom Level 3–4)**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Solar constant (G\_sc) = 1367 W/m²**
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC) for PV: 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, 25 °C**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂: 120 MJ/kg**
- **Energy density of gasoline: 44 MJ/kg** (for hydrogen comparison)
- **Round-trip efficiency of fuel cell: 40–60%**
- **Power coefficient (Betz limit) C\_p,max = 0.593 (16/27)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application Classification by Energy Source

### A. Solar Energy Applications (Direct & Indirect)

Solar energy is deployed in **four major application families**, each governed by distinct physical laws:

**1. Solar Thermal — Low Temperature (T < 100 °C)**
- Flat Plate Collectors (FPC) for water heating, space heating, swimming pools.
- Operating principle: greenhouse effect + selective coating absorption.
- Useful for domestic and small-commercial water heating in Kerala climate (avg. 4–5 kWh/m²/day insolation).

**2. Solar Thermal — High Temperature (T > 100 °C)**
- Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Solar Tower (Concentrated Solar Power — CSP).
- Uses focused sunlight to drive steam Rankine / Brayton cycles.
- Applications: industrial process heat, utility-scale power generation.

**3. Solar Photovoltaic (PV)**
- Direct sunlight → electricity via semiconductor p-n junction.
- Applications: rooftop solar, solar farms, solar pumps, streetlights, satellites, calculators, EVs (vehicle-integrated PV).

**4. Solar Passive Architecture**
- Building orientation, thermal mass, ventilation — no active devices.
- Application: zero-energy buildings, daylighting design.

### B. Wind Energy Applications

- **Onshore wind farms** — utility-scale electricity (1.5–8 MW turbines).
- **Offshore wind farms** — higher, steadier wind speeds; floating turbines for deep waters.
- **Standalone / Hybrid systems** — wind + diesel + battery for remote electrification.
- **Wind-powered water pumping** — mechanical Multiblade / Savonius pumps for irrigation.
- **Distributed micro-wind** — building-integrated turbines (urban context, low C_p).

### C. Biomass & Biogas Applications

- **Direct combustion** — bagasse cogeneration in sugar mills (common in Kerala coir/sugar industries).
- **Biogas (anaerobic digestion)** — cooking fuel, electricity via gas engines, fertilizer slurry as byproduct.
- **Biofuels** — biodiesel (transesterification) for transport; bioethanol for blending with petrol (E10, E20).
- **Gasification** — producer gas for industrial boilers and IC engines.

### D. Hydrogen Energy Applications

Hydrogen is an **energy carrier**, not a primary source. KTU-relevant application areas:

- **Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCVs)** — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo; zero tailpipe emissions (only water).
- **Stationary fuel cells** — PAFC, MCFC, SOFC for residential/industrial CHP (Combined Heat & Power).
- **Hydrogen blending** — H₂ injection into natural gas pipelines (5–20% by volume) for cleaner combustion.
- **Energy storage** — Power-to-Gas (P2G): surplus RE electricity → electrolyze water → H₂ → store → reconvert.
- **Industrial feedstock** — ammonia (NH₃) production, petroleum refining, steel manufacturing (DRI process).
- **Rocket propulsion** — cryogenic LH₂/LOX for space launch vehicles.

### E. Fuel Cell Applications (Electrochemical Cross-Cutting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fuel cells convert **hydrogen (or hydrocarbon fuel) + oxidant → electricity + heat + water** continuously, as long as fuel is supplied. They are central to the hydrogen economy and appear in nearly every KTU end-sem paper on applications.

| Fuel Cell Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PEMFC** | Polymer membrane | 60–100 °C | Vehicles, portable electronics |
| **AFC** | Alkaline KOH | 50–200 °C | Space (Apollo, Shuttle) |
| **PAFC** | Phosphoric acid | 150–220 °C | Stationary CHP (200 kW units) |
| **MCFC** | Molten carbonate | 600–700 °C | Large stationary power plants |
| **SOFC** | Solid ceramic (YSZ) | 800–1000 °C | Industrial CHP, hybrid gas turbine |

### F. Ocean Energy Applications

- **Tidal Energy** — Tidal Stream (kinetic, like underwater wind turbines) and Tidal Barrage (potential, like hydro dam).
- **Wave Energy** — Point absorbers, oscillating water columns (OWC), attenuators.
- **OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion)** — uses 20 °C surface-to-deep temperature differential for Rankine cycle; suitable only for tropical coasts (Kerala coast has potential).

### G. Geothermal Applications

- **Direct use** — space heating, greenhouse cultivation, balneology (spas).
- **Ground-Source Heat Pumps (GSHP)** — shallow geothermal for HVAC, 4–6 COP typical.
- **Electricity generation** — dry steam, flash steam, binary cycle (only in geothermally active zones; India: Puga valley, Ladakh; Manikaran, HP).

## 2.2 Hybrid & Integrated Application Systems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to **justify hybrid system design** in 14-mark questions. The core idea: a single renewable source is **intermittent**, so combining sources + storage yields **reliable, dispatchable power**.

**Common hybrid topologies tested:**
- **PV + Wind + Battery** — solar-wind hybrid streetlights (very common exam question).
- **PV + Diesel Generator** — remote village electrification.
- **Biomass gasifier + Solar dryer** — agro-processing.
- **Fuel cell + PV electrolyzer + H₂ tank** — closed-loop renewable energy storage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in ≥ 1 numerical every KTU end-sem paper.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Units | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G_t$ | $P_{PV}$ = PV output power; $\eta_{PV}$ = module efficiency; $A$ = area; $G_t$ = incident irradiance | W, m², W/m² | Solar PV sizing |
| 2 | $Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \cdot [S - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)]$ | $Q_u$ = useful heat gain; $A_c$ = collector area; $F_R$ = heat removal factor; $S$ = absorbed radiation; $U_L$ = loss coefficient | W, m², W/m²·K | Solar FPC water heater |
| 3 | $P_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot V^3 \cdot C_p$ | $P_w$ = wind power; $\rho_{air}$ = 1.225 kg/m³; $A$ = swept area $\pi R^2$; $V$ = wind speed; $C_p$ = power coefficient | W, m/s | Wind turbine output |
| 4 | $V_{cut\text{-}in}, V_{rated}, V_{cut\text{-}out}$ | Cut-in: turbine starts; Rated: max efficiency; Cut-out: safety shutdown | m/s | Wind turbine operation |
| 5 | $C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit — theoretical max fraction of kinetic energy extractable from wind | dimensionless | Wind energy bound |
| 6 | $\eta_{cell} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | $\eta_{cell}$ = cell efficiency; $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit V; $I_{sc}$ = short-circuit I; $FF$ = fill factor | % | Solar PV performance |
| 7 | $E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$ | Chemical energy content of hydrogen | J, kg, J/kg | Hydrogen storage |
| 8 | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \times 100\%$ | Fuel cell efficiency (thermodynamic, HH⁄HV basis) | % | Fuel cell design |
| 9 | $P_{tidal} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \eta$ | $\rho$ = seawater density; $g$ = 9.81; $A$ = basin area; $h$ = tidal range; $\eta$ = turbine efficiency | W | Tidal barrage sizing |
| 10 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Factor — measures plant utilization | dimensionless | Plant economics |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In modern grids, **Variable Renewable Energy (VRE)** sources (solar, wind) require **firming** via storage. Hydrogen + fuel cells serve as **long-duration, seasonal** storage — a key exam-relevant insight. The Indian National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023) targets **5 million tonnes/year by 2030**, making H₂ applications a current KTU-relevant industry topic.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available from a Wind Turbine (Betz Limit Derivation)

> **Starting Premise:** Wind is moving air with kinetic energy. A turbine extracts a fraction of this kinetic energy.

**Step 1 — Kinetic energy flux of wind through swept area A:**

The mass of air passing per second through area A is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot V$$

where $\rho_{air}$ is the air density and $V$ is the upstream wind velocity.

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy of this air per second (= power in wind):**

$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot V^3$$

> This is the **raw power available** in the wind — note the cubic dependence on velocity, which is why a small increase in wind speed produces a dramatic rise in extractable power.

**Step 3 — Apply continuity with upstream ($V_1$) and downstream ($V_2$) velocities:**

The turbine cannot extract all the kinetic energy; air leaves slower but not stopped. So we use average velocity $\tfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot \tfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Power extracted by the turbine:**

$$P_{extracted} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

Substituting $\dot{m}$:

$$P_{extracted} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot A \cdot \tfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \cdot (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

**Step 5 — Express as fraction of upstream wind power (C_p):**

$$C_p = \dfrac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot \tfrac{V_1+V_2}{2} \cdot (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V_1^3}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{(V_1+V_2)(V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2 V_1^3}$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate with respect to V₂ and set dC_p/dV₂ = 0:**

Let $x = V_2 / V_1$. Then:

$$C_p = \dfrac{(1+x)(1-x^2)}{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+x)(1-x)(1+x) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1+x)^2(1-x)$$

Differentiate and equate to zero:

$$\dfrac{dC_p}{dx} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left[ 2(1+x)(1-x) + (1+x)^2(-1) \right] = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$2(1-x) - (1+x) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2 - 2x - 1 - x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - 3x = 0$$

$$\boxed{x = \tfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \tfrac{1}{3}}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back to find maximum C_p:**

$$C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^2 \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{16}{9} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3} = \tfrac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ (Betz Limit)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** No wind turbine — however well-designed — can convert more than **59.3%** of wind kinetic energy into mechanical energy. Modern three-blade HAWTs achieve **75–80% of Betz limit**, i.e., C_p ≈ 0.45–0.48.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Useful Heat Gain of a Flat Plate Solar Collector

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the absorber plate:**

The absorbed solar radiation minus losses to ambient = useful heat transferred to fluid:

$$Q_u = A_c \cdot S - A_c \cdot U_L \cdot (T_{pm} - T_a)$$

where $S$ = absorbed radiation per unit area, $U_L$ = overall loss coefficient, $T_{pm}$ = mean plate temp, $T_a$ = ambient temp.

**Step 2 — Introduce Heat Removal Factor F_R:**

Real collectors have a finite heat transfer coefficient between plate and fluid, so the fluid inlet temp $T_{fi}$ is lower than $T_{pm}$. The empirical **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** is:

$$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \cdot \left[ S - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]}$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous efficiency:**

$$\eta_{coll} = \dfrac{Q_u}{A_c \cdot G_t} = F_R \left[ \dfrac{S}{G_t} - \dfrac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_t} \right]$$

Defining $(\tau\alpha)$ as the transmittance–absorptance product:

$$\eta_{coll} = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) - \dfrac{U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{G_t} \right]$$

> This linear relation between efficiency and $(T_{fi}-T_a)/G_t$ is the **collector performance curve** universally asked in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fuel Cell Efficiency (HHV Basis)

**Step 1 — Energy released by hydrogen oxidation:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol (HHV, liquid water product)}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum useful work (Gibbs free energy change):**

$$\Delta G = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol (at 25 °C, 1 atm)}$$

**Step 3 — Thermodynamic (ideal) efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ideal} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.83 \;\; (83\%)$$

**Step 4 — Actual cell efficiency with operating voltage V\_cell:**

$$\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.229} \times 100\%$$

since 1.229 V is the reversible open-circuit voltage for the H₂/O₂ cell. A typical PEMFC operates at **0.6–0.7 V**, giving:

$$\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{0.7}{1.229} \approx 57\%$$

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three practical applications of hydrogen energy and explain the role of hydrogen as an energy carrier.**

**Model Answer:**

Hydrogen is a **secondary energy carrier** (energy vector) — it must first be produced using primary energy. Its three major application areas are:

1. **Transport — Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCVs):** Hydrogen is stored in high-pressure (350–700 bar) tanks and electrochemically combined with oxygen in a PEM fuel cell, producing electricity to drive an electric motor. The only tailpipe emission is water. Example: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

2. **Stationary Power — Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** PAFC/MCFC/SOFC systems supply 50–250 kW to commercial buildings or industrial sites, simultaneously producing electricity and useful heat, achieving 80–90% total efficiency.

3. **Industrial Feedstock:** Hydrogen is essential for **ammonia synthesis (Haber–Bosch)**, **petroleum hydrocracking**, and **direct reduced iron (DRI) steelmaking**. Replacing grey H₂ (from fossil fuels) with green H₂ (from electrolysis using RE) decarbonizes these heavy industries.

4. **Energy Storage — Power-to-Gas (P2G):** Surplus renewable electricity is converted to H₂ via electrolysis, stored for weeks/months, and reconverted to electricity via fuel cells or gas turbines during peak demand — solving the intermittency problem of solar and wind.

> **Role as energy carrier:** H₂ decouples **energy generation** from **energy use in time and space**, similar to electricity but with much higher mass energy density (120 MJ/kg LHV) and long-duration storage capability.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Betz's Law. What is its significance in wind energy applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Betz's Law states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3%) of the kinetic energy** of the wind passing through its swept area.

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593}$$

**Significance in wind energy applications:**

1. **Design Benchmark:** The law sets the **theoretical upper bound** for aerodynamic-to-mechanical conversion efficiency. Engineers use it to gauge how close a real turbine is to the ideal — modern three-blade HAWTs achieve **75–80% of the Betz limit** (C_p ≈ 0.45–0.48).

2. **Rotor Sizing:** Knowing the maximum extractable fraction lets designers compute the **minimum swept area** required for a target power output, preventing wasteful oversizing.

3. **Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR) Optimization:** The Betz optimum occurs at **TSR ≈ 8/3 ≈ 2.67** for an ideal turbine, guiding blade pitch and generator coupling design.

4. **Economic Justification:** Combined with capacity factor, the Betz limit enables **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** calculations and feasibility studies for wind farm installation.

5. **Multi-turbine Spacing:** In a wind farm, the Betz limit combined with wake effects dictates the minimum inter-turbine spacing (typically 5–7 rotor diameters) to avoid mutual aerodynamic losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) Discuss in detail the major applications of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems in the domestic, industrial, and utility sectors. Include a labelled system architecture and mention at least four application categories in each sector. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A standalone PV system powers a remote farmhouse. The daily load is: 4 LED lights × 10 W × 6 h, 1 fan × 60 W × 8 h, 1 TV × 80 W × 4 h, and 1 water pump × 500 W × 1 h. The location receives 5 peak sun hours (PSH)/day. PV module efficiency is 15%, and overall system (battery + inverter) efficiency is 75%. Design the PV array area required. Assume standard test irradiance of 1 kW/m². (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Solar PV applications are categorized by **deployment scale** and **purpose**:

**1. Domestic Sector Applications:**

| Application | Typical Size | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Rooftop PV (Grid-tied)** | 1–10 kWp | Net-metering; zero export limit bills |
| **Solar Home Lighting** | 50–200 Wp | Battery-backed LED DC system |
| **Solar Water Pumping** | 0.5–5 HP | Replaces diesel pumps; PMW or AC submersible |
| **Solar Lantern / Torches** | 1–5 Wp | Pico-PV; portable |

**2. Industrial Sector Applications:**

| Application | Typical Size | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Captive Power Plant** | 100 kW – 5 MW | Open-Access / Captive; reduces peak demand charges |
| **Solar Cold Storage** | 5–50 kW + battery | Powers Vapour Absorption / DC refrigeration |
| **Solar Telemetry / Telecom Towers** | 1–5 kWp | Hybrid with diesel genset |
| **Solar EV Charging** | 10–100 kWp | DC fast charging with MPPT |

**3. Utility Sector Applications:**

| Application | Typical Size | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar Power Plant (Ground-mounted)** | 1 MW – 2000 MW | Single-axis tracking; string vs central inverter |
| **Floating Solar (Floatovoltaics)** | 5–100 MW | Reservoir / canal / dam based; reduces evaporation |
| **Solar Parks** | 500 MW+ | Government-tendered; Plug-and-Play model |
| **Solar Canals** | 1–50 MW | Above irrigation canals (e.g., Gujarat model) |

**Block Diagram — Grid-Tied PV System Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Solar Radiation] --> ARRAY[PV Array Modules]
    ARRAY --> DCB[DC Combiner Box]
    DCB --> INV[Grid Tie Inverter MPPT]
    INV --> ACB[AC Distribution]
    ACB --> LOAD[Load]
    ACB --> METER[Bidirectional Meter]
    METER --> GRID[Utility Grid]
    ARRAY --> INVR[Inverter Protection Surge SPD]
    GRID --> BATT[Anti Islanding Protection]
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute total daily energy demand E_d:**

Energy of LED lights:
$$E_{LED} = 4 \times 10 \text{ W} \times 6 \text{ h} = 240 \text{ Wh/day}$$

Energy of fan:
$$E_{fan} = 1 \times 60 \text{ W} \times 8 \text{ h} = 480 \text{ Wh/day}$$

Energy of TV:
$$E_{TV} = 1 \times 80 \text{ W} \times 4 \text{ h} = 320 \text{ Wh/day}$$

Energy of pump:
$$E_{pump} = 1 \times 500 \text{ W} \times 1 \text{ h} = 500 \text{ Wh/day}$$

**Total daily energy demand:**
$$E_d = 240 + 480 + 320 + 500 = 1540 \text{ Wh/day} = 1.54 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective daily insolation on tilted surface:**

Effective sun-hours after system losses consideration:
$$G_{eff} = PSH \times G_{STC} = 5 \text{ h} \times 1000 \text{ W/m}^2 = 5000 \text{ Wh/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute energy that must be generated by PV (accounting for system losses):**

PV output per m² per day:
$$E_{PV,unit} = \eta_{PV} \cdot G_{eff} \cdot \eta_{system} = 0.15 \times 5000 \times 0.75$$

$$E_{PV,unit} = 562.5 \text{ Wh/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Compute required PV area:**

$$A_{PV} = \dfrac{E_d}{E_{PV,unit}} = \dfrac{1540}{562.5}$$

$$\boxed{A_{PV} \approx 2.74 \text{ m}^2}$$

> This is the **net active cell area**. Practically, one would oversize by 20–30% accounting for dust, temperature derating (panels lose ~0.5%/°C above 25 °C), wiring losses, and aging — so install ≈ **3.5 m² of PV module surface**.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a) Describe the principle of operation, classification, and applications of fuel cells. Compare the five major types based on electrolyte, operating temperature, and application. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A fuel cell power plant uses pure hydrogen and oxygen. The cell voltage under load is 0.7 V. Compute: (i) the thermodynamic ideal efficiency (HHV basis), (ii) the actual electrical efficiency, and (iii) the actual efficiency if the waste heat is also recovered and 60% of it is converted to useful thermal energy. The hydrogen LHV may be ignored; use ΔH = 285.8 kJ/mol, ΔG = 237.2 kJ/mol, and 2 electrons per H₂ molecule. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Principle of Operation:**

A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly H₂) and an oxidant (O₂) directly into electricity and heat, **without combustion**. It operates as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied — analogous to a battery that doesn't discharge.

**Half-reactions (acidic PEM fuel cell example):**

Anode (oxidation):
$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

Cathode (reduction):
$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall:**
$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The electrons released at the anode flow through the external circuit (delivering useful work) and reach the cathode, where they reduce oxygen. The proton-exchange membrane (electrolyte) allows only H⁺ ions to migrate, completing the circuit internally.

**Classification & Comparison Table:**

| Feature | PEMFC | AFC | PAFC | MCFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrolyte** | Polymer (Nafion) | KOH liquid | H₃PO₄ liquid | Molten Li/K carbonate | YSZ ceramic |
| **Charge Carrier** | H⁺ | OH⁻ | H⁺ | CO₃²⁻ | O²⁻ |
| **Operating Temp** | 60–100 °C | 50–200 °C | 150–220 °C | 600–700 °C | 800–1000 °C |
| **Efficiency** | 50–60% | 50–70% | 40–50% | 50–60% | 55–65% |
| **Power Range** | 1 W – 250 kW | 1 – 100 kW | 50 – 1000 kW | 100 kW – 10 MW | 1 kW – 10 MW |
| **Fuel** | Pure H₂ | Pure H₂ | H₂ (impure OK) | H₂, CH₄, CO | H₂, CH₄, CO |
| **Applications** | Cars, buses, drones | Spacecraft, military | Stationary CHP, hospitals | Large power plants, industrial | Industrial CHP, hybrid cycles |

**Key Applications by Scale:**

- **Portable (Watt-class):** Laptops, mobile phones, soldier portable power → **PEMFC, DMFC**.
- **Transport (kW–MW):** Passenger cars, trucks, forklifts, submarines, trains → **PEMFC**.
- **Stationary (kW–MW):** Residential micro-CHP, commercial buildings, backup power → **PAFC, SOFC, MCFC**.
- **Space:** Apollo, Space Shuttle Orbiter, future Mars missions → **AFC** (regenerative systems).

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- Cell voltage under load: $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V
- $\Delta H = 285.8$ kJ/mol (HHV)
- $\Delta G = 237.2$ kJ/mol
- 2 electrons per H₂ molecule

**Part (i) — Thermodynamic ideal efficiency (HHV basis):**

$$\eta_{ideal} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.8298$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ideal} \approx 82.98\% \approx 83\%}$$

**Part (ii) — Actual electrical efficiency:**

The reversible cell voltage (HHV basis) is calculated using $\Delta G$:
$$E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E_{rev} = \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> This is the **standard reversible voltage** for the H₂/O₂ cell on the HHV basis.

Actual electrical efficiency:
$$\eta_{elec} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E_{rev}} = \dfrac{0.7}{1.229} = 0.5696$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{elec} \approx 56.96\% \approx 57\%}$$

**Part (iii) — Combined efficiency with heat recovery:**

Heat fraction released (loss fraction) per mole of H₂:
$$q_{loss} = \dfrac{\Delta H - nFE_{cell}}{\Delta H} = 1 - \eta_{elec} = 1 - 0.5696 = 0.4304$$

Recovered useful heat:
$$q_{recovered} = q_{loss} \times 0.60 = 0.4304 \times 0.60 = 0.2583 \;(25.83\%)$$

Total (combined) efficiency:
$$\eta_{combined} = \eta_{elec} + q_{recovered} = 0.5696 + 0.2583$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{combined} \approx 0.8279 \approx 82.8\%}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Even a single PEMFC at 0.7 V can approach **83% total CHP efficiency** when waste heat is captured — surpassing most heat engines (Carnot-limited). This is why fuel cells are central to **Combined Heat, Hydrogen, and Power (CHHP)** systems in the hydrogen economy.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Betz Limit without "Betz" or derivation context:** Many students write $C_p = 0.593$ without stating that it is the *theoretical maximum* extractable fraction, missing the conceptual clarity expected in 7-mark derivations.
>
> 2. **PV sizing — forgetting the system efficiency factor:** A frequent error is computing $A_{PV} = E_d / (\eta \cdot G_{eff})$ and ignoring battery + inverter (BOS — Balance of System) losses. Always multiply by $1/\eta_{system}$.
>
> 3. **Fuel cell efficiency confusion:** Students often confuse the **voltage efficiency** ($V_{cell}/E_{rev}$) with the **Faradaic efficiency** (ratio of actual H₂ consumption to theoretical). They are different concepts; the former is the correct measure for fuel cell performance.
>
> 4. **Misclassifying H₂ as a "renewable energy source":** H₂ is an **energy carrier** — produced using primary energy. Writing "hydrogen is a renewable energy" loses 1 mark in any application question.
>
> 5. **Confusing "Capacity Factor" with "Efficiency":** Capacity factor is the ratio of actual annual energy generated to the maximum possible, while efficiency is energy-out / energy-in. They are **not interchangeable**.
>
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always state **units** for area (m²), power (W/kW/MW), and energy (Wh/kWh). Unit-less answers are flagged for partial deduction.
>
> 7. **Answering Hybrid Systems without justifying source selection:** When asked to design a hybrid system, students list components without justifying *why* that specific combination fits the load profile, solar resource, and wind resource of the location — this costs ~2 marks in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Applications Module 4**

- **Solar PV** applications span 4 broad sectors: domestic, industrial, utility, space. Rooftop, solar pumps, solar farms, satellites, and BIPV are high-frequency terms.
- **Solar Thermal** applications depend on temperature: FPC (T < 100 °C), ETC (T < 200 °C), Parabolic Trough (T < 400 °C), Solar Tower (T > 500 °C).
- **Betz Limit** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 = 0.593$ is the **theoretical ceiling** for wind energy extraction — achievable in 1-D actuator disk theory.
- **Wind turbine operation zones:** Cut-in (3–4 m/s), Rated (12–15 m/s), Cut-out (25 m/s) — outside cut-out the turbine shuts down for safety.
- **Biomass applications** include direct combustion, anaerobic digestion (biogas), gasification (producer gas), and transesterification (biodiesel).
- **Hydrogen is an energy carrier**, not a source. Key applications: FCVs, stationary CHP, industrial feedstock, seasonal storage (P2G).
- **Fuel cell types** — remember the **5-type family** (PEMFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC) sorted by temperature and electrolyte.
- **Fuel cell efficiency** = $\eta = V_{cell} / E_{rev}$ where $E_{rev} = 1.229$ V (HHV) or 1.481 V (LHV).
- **Tidal energy** has two forms: tidal stream (kinetic) and tidal barrage (potential). Suitable only where tidal range > 5 m.
- **OTEC** needs ΔT ≥ 20 °C between surface and deep ocean (~1000 m depth) — tropical seas only.
- **Geothermal grades:** Low (< 90 °C) — direct use, GSHP; Medium (90–150 °C) — binary cycle; High (> 150 °C) — flash & dry steam.
- **Hybrid systems** (PV + Wind + Battery / PV + DG / Biogas + Solar) address **intermittency** and are key to off-grid / microgrid applications.
- **Capacity Factor (CF)** = actual energy / (rated power × 8760 h) — typical CF: solar 15–25%, wind 25–40%, tidal 25–35%, geothermal 70–90%.
- **Standalone PV sizing formula:** $A_{PV} = E_d / (\eta_{PV} \cdot G_t \cdot \eta_{system})$ — always account for BOS losses.
- **Green Hydrogen** (produced via electrolysis powered by renewables) is the focus of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission — high industrial relevance.
- **Combined Heat, Hydrogen, and Power (CHHP)** using fuel cells can achieve total efficiencies > 80%, beating Carnot-limited heat engines.
- **Kerala-specific applications:** Coir industry biomass cogeneration, rooftop solar (high insolation 4–5 kWh/m²/day), tidal potential at Gulf of Kutch analog, OTEC feasibility off Thiruvananthapuram coast.
- **Pumped hydro + battery + hydrogen storage = three-tier storage hierarchy:** short-term (battery), mid-term (pumped hydro), long-term (hydrogen).

> **Final Examiner Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions on this module favour **scenario-based 14-mark questions** (e.g., "Design a hybrid renewable system for a remote village" or "Justify fuel cell selection for a specific application"). Practice framing your answer as: (i) Identify the load demand, (ii) Map the available RE resource, (iii) Select the conversion technology with justification, (iv) Size the components with formulas, and (v) Discuss storage / hybrid integration.
